fixed-titan.scenario_text: I took out a personal loan with your bank in March 2024. At no point during the application process or at the point of sale was I given any information about your complaints procedure or told how I could complain if something went wrong. I've since had a problem with an overcharge on my account and when I tried to find out how to complain, there was nothing on my loan agreement, nothing in the paperwork I was given, and I had to spend over an hour on the phone just to find out where to send my complaint. I also notice your website has no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service anywhere. I believe you have failed in your duty to make me aware of my rights and I want this addressed.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
The customer is complaining about two distinct failures: (1) not being given information about the firm's internal complaints procedure at or around the point of sale of a personal loan in March 2024; and (2) the firm's website not mentioning the Financial Ombudsman Service.

Regulatory position based on retrieved provisions:

1. DISP 1.2.1R (chunk: disp1s2) requires that summary details of the firm's internal complaints handling procedures be provided to eligible complainants. This obligation applies at, or immediately after, the point when contact is first made with an eligible complainant. If the customer received no such information at or around the point of sale of the loan, this is a potential breach of a binding rule.

2. DISP 1.2.3G (chunk: disp1s2) provides guidance that the summary details should cover at least: how the firm handles and seeks to resolve complaints; and (where the complaint falls within FOS jurisdiction) that if unresolved, the complainant may be entitled to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service. This is guidance, not a rule, but it informs what adequate disclosure looks like.

3. DISP 1.1A.11R (chunk: disp1s20) requires MiFID investment firms specifically to provide information about the FOS on their website and in general conditions of contracts. However, this provision is expressly limited to MiFID investment firms. A personal loan is not a MiFID product, so this specific rule does not directly apply here. You should check whether any equivalent website disclosure obligation applies to your firm under a different provision — the retrieved context does not supply one for non-MiFID retail lending. Flag this gap.

4. DISP 1.6.7R (chunk: disp1s6) — the final response rule — requires that a final response encloses the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provides the FOS website address, and informs the complainant of their right to refer to FOS. This is relevant to how you now respond to this complaint, not to the original sale.

Recommended action:
- Acknowledge the failure to provide complaints procedure information at point of sale, which on the face of it engages DISP 1.2.1R.
- Do not admit a specific regulatory breach in the customer letter — that is a legal/compliance determination — but acknowledge the customer's experience and apologise.
- Ensure this final response itself complies with DISP 1.6.7R: enclose the FOS leaflet, provide the FOS website address, and inform the customer of their referral right.
- The website disclosure point: the retrieved provisions do not supply a binding rule requiring FOS information on the website for non-MiFID retail lending products. Flag this to compliance — do not make a regulatory admission on this point in the customer letter.
- Human review is required before sending: this complaint involves a potential regulatory breach (DISP 1.2.1R), possible remedial action or goodwill gesture for the hour spent on the phone, and a compliance question about website disclosure that the retrieved context does not fully resolve.

Assumptions to confirm: (a) This is a personal loan, not a MiFID product — confirm so DISP 1.1A.11R does not apply directly. (b) Confirm whether the firm did in fact provide any complaints information at point of sale (e.g. in a welcome pack or online journey) — if it did, the factual basis of the complaint changes. (c) Confirm whether a goodwill gesture for the customer's wasted time is being considered — if so, this must be reviewed by the appropriate authority before the letter goes out. (d) Confirm this letter will serve as the final response, so that the DISP 1.6.7R requirements (FOS leaflet, website address, referral wording) are correctly included.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you experienced, and I want to assure you that we take your concerns seriously.

You have raised two concerns: first, that you were not given information about how to make a complaint when you took out your personal loan in March 2024; and second, that you could not find any reference to the Financial Ombudsman Service on our website.

We are sorry that when you needed to raise a concern, you were not able to find the information you needed easily, and that you had to spend significant time on the phone simply to find out how to complain. We recognise this was frustrating and fell short of the standard you should expect. Our regulator requires us to make information about our complaints procedure available to customers, and we take that obligation seriously. We will be reviewing what happened in your case.

We have now looked into the overcharging issue you mentioned and [INSERT OUTCOME OF INVESTIGATION / NEXT STEPS ON THE SUBSTANTIVE OVERCHARGE COMPLAINT — to be completed by the handler].

If you are not satisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. The Financial Ombudsman Service is an independent service that resolves disputes between consumers and financial businesses. Their website address is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk. I have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, which sets out how to contact them and the time limits that apply to referrals.

[INSERT DISP 1 Annex 3R wording on time limit waiver, as applicable — to be confirmed by handler.]

Thank you again for bringing this to our attention.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Firm name]

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I've been trying to call my bank to make a formal complaint about an incorrect fee they charged me. The only number they provide for complaints is an 0871 number, which is costing me a fortune every time I ring. I've called three times now and been on hold for extended periods, racking up charges. I'm on a basic state pension and these call costs are adding up significantly. I shouldn't have to pay premium rates just to complain. I want the bank to reimburse me for the call charges I've incurred and to provide a proper free or local-rate number for complaints.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises a clear regulatory issue under GEN 7.2. The customer is being required to use an 0871 number to contact the bank about an existing contract (the fee dispute relates to their account). GEN 7.2.1R requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate. GEN 7.2.5R explicitly lists 0871, 0872 and 0873 numbers as non-compliant with the call charges rule. This appears to be a straightforward breach of GEN 7.2.1R.

Recommended action:
1. The bank should immediately review whether the 0871 number is the only complaints contact number — if so, this is a systemic compliance issue that should be escalated beyond this individual complaint.
2. The customer is seeking reimbursement of call charges incurred. This involves a financial/redress decision and must be reviewed by a human before any commitment is made. However, the regulatory position strongly supports the customer's position that they should not have been charged above basic rate.
3. The customer has indicated vulnerability (basic state pension, significant financial impact of call costs). This reinforces the need for human review before sending any response.
4. You should confirm: (a) the exact call charges incurred (the customer mentions three calls with extended hold times — obtain records if possible); (b) whether the bank has any alternative geographic/freephone number that was available but not publicised; (c) whether this is a systemic issue affecting other customers.

Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft: I have assumed the calls relate to an existing contractual relationship (the fee complaint relates to their account), which brings GEN 7.2.1R into scope. If for any reason the call was not in relation to an existing contract, the position would need to be reconsidered — though this seems unlikely given the context. The customer draft below acknowledges the complaint and the regulatory position without committing to a specific redress figure, pending human review.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for contacting us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced in trying to reach us, and I want to assure you that we are taking your concerns seriously.

You have raised two issues: the fee that was charged to your account, and the cost of the telephone calls you have had to make in order to contact us about it.

On the telephone charges: our regulator's rules require that where a firm provides a telephone line for customers to contact it about an existing contract, the cost of those calls must not exceed the basic rate. Numbers beginning with 0871 do not meet this standard. We are therefore reviewing the telephone contact arrangements for complaints as a matter of urgency.

We also recognise that you have already incurred call costs as a result of using the number we provided, and we are sorry for that. We are looking into the charges you have incurred and will write to you again shortly with our findings and next steps on this point.

Regarding the underlying fee complaint, we will investigate this fully and impartially, and we will write to you with our conclusions.

If you need to contact us in the meantime, please do so in writing at [insert address] or by email at [insert email address], so that you do not incur any further call costs while we resolve this.

If you are not satisfied with how we handle your complaint, you have the right to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with full details of how to do this when we send you our final response.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

Defines 'basic rate' as the simple cost of connection, which must not provide the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues — an 0871 number does not meet this definition.
Explicitly lists 0871, 0872 and 0873 numbers as non-compliant with the call charges rule, directly applicable to the number the customer has been given.

retrieved_chunks.content: # GEN — GEN 7.2 Call charges ## GEN 7.2.1 (Rules) A firm which operates a telephone line for the purpose of enabling a consumer to contact the firm in relation to a contract that has been entered into with the firm, must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate for the telephone call. ## GEN 7.2.2 (Guidance) The contract entered into with the firm may be in writing or otherwise. ## GEN 7.2.3 (Rules) For the purposes of the call charges rule, the basic rate is the simple cost of connection and must not provide the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues. ## GEN 7.2.4 (Rules) The following numbers, if used by firms, would comply with the call charges rule: (1) geographic numbers or numbers which are always set at the same rate, which usually begin with the prefix 01, 02 or 03; (2) calls which can be free of charge to call, for example 0800 and 0808 numbers; and (3) standard mobile numbers, which usually begin with the prefix 07, provided that the firm ordinarily uses a mobile number to receive telephone calls. ## GEN 7.2.5 (Rules) The following numbers, if used by firms, would not comply with the call charges rule: (1) premium rate numbers that begin with the prefix 09; (2) other revenue sharing numbers in which a portion of the call charge can be used to either provide a service or make a small payment to the firm, such as telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 084 or 0871, 0872 or 0873; and (3) telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 0870 as the cost of making a telephone call on such numbers can be higher than a geographic cost and will vary depending on the consumer’s telephone tariff.

metadata.section_name: GEN 7.2 Call charges

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I took out a personal loan with your bank in 2021 and was sold payment protection insurance at the same time. In March this year, I submitted a formal complaint explaining that the PPI was mis-sold because I already had employer sickness cover and was never asked about existing protection. Your complaints team sent me a final response letter four weeks later rejecting my complaint, but the letter simply stated 'we have reviewed your complaint and do not believe it should be upheld' with no further explanation. There was no assessment of the specific points I raised about my existing employer cover, no consideration of whether redress might be appropriate, and no explanation of how you reached your decision. I feel my complaint has not been properly investigated or fairly assessed, and the response I received was neither clear nor informative. I would like my complaint to be reconsidered properly.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
The customer is complaining that their PPI mis-selling complaint was rejected without adequate reasoning — specifically, no assessment of their existing employer sickness cover, no consideration of redress, and no explanation of how the decision was reached. This raises a significant concern under DISP App 3 and the final response requirements in DISP 1.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Adequacy of the final response (DISP 1 chunk):** The retrieved provisions set out what a final response must contain. A rejection must 'give reasons for doing so' (DISP 1.6.2R equivalent wording in the retrieved chunk). A bare statement that the complaint is not upheld, with no reasoning, does not satisfy this requirement. The firm's letter appears deficient on its face.

2. **Assessment of the mis-selling complaint (DISP App 3):** Under DISP App 3.1.1G, the firm must handle PPI mis-selling complaints by working through the prescribed steps. DISP App 3.3.11G and 3.3.12G require the firm to consider whether information was communicated fairly, clearly and not misleadingly, having regard to the complainant's individual circumstances — including existing cover. The complainant's specific point about existing employer sickness cover is directly relevant to whether the sale was appropriate. DISP App 3.6.3E notes that relevant evidence includes the complainant's demands, needs and intentions at the time of sale. None of this appears to have been addressed in the rejection letter.

3. **Redress:** DISP App 3.7 sets out evidential provisions on how redress should be calculated if a complaint is upheld. The firm has not reached that stage, but the customer's complaint should be properly re-assessed before any redress question arises.

4. **PPI time limits:** The sale took place in 2021, which is after the 29 August 2017 cut-off in DISP 2.8.9R, so the August 2019 PPI deadline does not apply here. The standard FOS referral time limits under DISP 2.8.2R will apply. The final response letter must inform the customer of their right to refer to FOS and enclose the standard explanatory leaflet — check whether the original letter did this; if not, the new response must remedy that too.

**Recommended action:**
- Reopen and properly investigate the complaint, specifically addressing the customer's existing employer sickness cover and whether the firm took reasonable steps to establish the customer's existing protection needs before the sale.
- Issue a new final response that: (a) addresses the specific grounds raised; (b) either accepts, offers redress without accepting, or rejects with full reasons; (c) encloses the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (d) provides the FOS website address; (e) informs the customer of their right to refer to FOS; and (f) addresses the DISP 2.8.2R/2.8.7R time limit waiver question.
- If the reinvestigation leads to an upheld outcome, redress calculation under DISP App 3.7 will need to be considered separately.

**Human review required:** Yes — this complaint involves a potentially deficient prior final response, a possible redress outcome, and a reinvestigation of a PPI mis-selling allegation. A senior complaints handler or compliance oversight should review before any new final response is sent.

**Assumptions to confirm:**
- Confirm the exact date of the original final response letter and whether it included FOS referral information and the explanatory leaflet.
- Confirm whether the customer has already referred the matter to FOS (if so, the firm's ability to issue a new final response may need to be coordinated with FOS).
- The draft below is an acknowledgement/holding response confirming reinvestigation — it does not constitute a new final response, which must follow a proper reinvestigation.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for writing to us to ask us to reconsider your complaint about the sale of payment protection insurance alongside your personal loan in 2021.

We are sorry that our previous response did not address the specific concerns you raised. You told us that you already had employer sickness cover at the time of the sale and that you were not asked about existing protection before the policy was sold to you. These are important points that should have been considered and explained in our response, and we recognise that our letter did not do that.

We are reopening your complaint and will carry out a proper review. As part of that review, we will consider the circumstances of the sale, including the information that was — and was not — communicated to you at the time, and your individual situation, including any existing cover you held. We will write to you again with our findings.

We aim to provide you with a full response as quickly as possible. If we are not able to do so within eight weeks of your original complaint, we will write to explain why and let you know when we expect to be able to respond.

In the meantime, we want to make sure you are aware of your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge to you. You do not need to wait for our further response before doing so. We have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, which explains how to contact them and the time limits that apply. You can also find information on their website at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Complaints handler name]
[Job title]
[Bank name]

The firm should consider whether it communicated information to the complainant in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading — relevant to whether the customer was properly informed about the policy given their existing cover.
In assessing the information communicated, the firm should have regard to the complainant's individual circumstances at the time of sale, including any evidence of existing cover or protection needs.
Relevant evidence includes the complainant's demands, needs and intentions at the time of sale — directly applicable to the customer's point about existing employer sickness cover.
The August 2019 PPI deadline applies only to sales on or before 29 August 2017; as this sale was in 2021, that deadline does not apply and standard FOS time limits govern instead.

retrieved_chunks.content: The firm should consider the available evidence and assess whether or not it gave advice or made a recommendation (explicitly or implicitly) to the complainant. ## DISP App 3.3.11 (Guidance) The firm should consider in all situations whether it communicated information to the complainant in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading and with due regard to the complainant's information needs. ## DISP App 3.3.12 (Guidance) In considering the information communicated to the complainant and the complainant's information needs, the evidence to which a firm should have regard includes: (1) the complainant's individual circumstances at the time of the sale (for example, the firm should take into account any evidence of limited financial capability or understanding on the part of the complainant); (2) the complainant's objectives and intentions at the time of the sale; (3) whether, from a reasonable customer's perspective, the documentation provided to the complainant was sufficiently clear, concise and presented fairly (for example, was the documentation in plain and intelligible language?); (4) in a sale that was primarily conducted orally, whether sufficient information was communicated during the sale discussion for the customer to make an informed decision (for example, did the firm give an oral explanation of the main characteristics of the policy or specifically draw the complainant's attention to that information on a computer screen or in a document and give the complainant time to read and consider it?); (5) any evidence about the tone and pace of oral communication (for example, was documentation read out too quickly for the complainant to have understood it?); and (6) any extra explanation or information given by the firm in response to questions raised (or information disclosed) by the complainant. ## DISP App 3.3.13 (Guidance) The firm should not reject a complaint solely because the complainant had held a payment protection contract previously.

retrieved_chunks.content: If the complainant has received any rebate, for example if the customer cancelled a single premium payment protection contract before it ran full term and received a refund, the firm may deduct the value of this rebate from the amount otherwise payable to the complainant. ## DISP App 3.7.4 (Evidential Provisions) Additionally, where a single premium was added to a loan: (1) for live policies: (a) subject to DISP App 3.7.5 E, where there remains an outstanding loan balance, the firm should, where possible, arrange for the loan to be restructured (without charge to the complainant but using any applicable cancellation value) with the effect of: (i) removing amounts relating to the payment protection contract (including any interest and charges); and (ii) ensuring the number and amounts of any future repayments (including any interest and charges) are the same as would have applied if the complainant had taken the loan without the payment protection contract; or (b) where the firm is not able to arrange for the loan to be restructured (e.g. because the loan is provided by a separate firm), it should pay the complainant an amount equal to the difference between the actual loan balance and what the loan balance would have been if the payment protection contract (including any interest and charges) had not been added, deducting the current cancellation value. The firm should offer to pay any charges incurred if the complainant uses this amount to reduce his loan balance; and (2) for cancelled policies, the firm should pay the complainant the difference between the actual loan balance at the point of cancellation and what the loan balance would have been if no premium had been added (plus simple interest) minus any applicable cancellation value. ## DISP App 3.7.5 (Evidential Provisions) Where a claim was previously paid on the policy, the firm may deduct this from redress paid in accordance with DISP App 3.7.3 E.

retrieved_chunks.content: # DISP — DISP App 3.1 Introduction ## DISP App 3.1.1 (Guidance) (1) This appendix sets out how: (a) a firm should handle complaints relating to the sale of a payment protection contract by the firm which express dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling); and (b) a firm that is a CCA lender and which has received such a complaint should consider whether there was a failure to disclose commission in relation to the sale of a payment protection contract which covers or covered or purported to cover a credit agreement (this includes partial coverage). (2) It relates to the sale of any payment protection contract whenever the sale took place and irrespective of whether it was on an advised or non-advised basis; conducted through any sales channel; in connection with any type of loan or credit product, or none; whether the insurer was in the same group as the firm or not; whether the premium was financed by the credit product or not; and for a regular premium or single premium payment. It applies whether the policy is currently in force, was cancelled during the policy term or ran its full term. (3) It does not set out how a firm which has received a complaint referred to in (1)(a) should assess: (a) whether the firm’s conduct of the sale was in breach of a fiduciary duty where there has been a failure to disclose either the existence of, or the level of, any commission and/or profit share paid; (b) whether any omission (other than the omission referred to in DISP App 3.3A.2E) to disclose either the existence of, or level of, commission and/or profit share made the relationship unfair under section 140A of the CCA; (c) any other issue not dealt with in step 1 or step 2 set out in this appendix.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us to ask us to reconsider your complaint about the sale of payment protection insurance alongside your personal loan in 2021. ... You told us that you already had employer sickness cover at the time of the sale and that you were not asked about existing protection before the policy was sold to you.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately reflects its subject matter — the PPI mis-selling allegation, the existing employer sickness cover, and the inadequacy of the prior response.

The customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, reject, or partially uphold) on the complaint. It acknowledges the prior response was deficient and states the complaint is being reopened for reinvestigation, but no substantive decision or reasons are provided. This is labelled as a final response stage but the draft is effectively a holding response with no decision.

verdicts.evidence: we want to make sure you are aware of your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge to you. You do not need to wait for our further response before doing so. We have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, which explains how to contact them and the time limits that apply. You can also find information on their website at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer-facing text clearly states the right to refer to the FOS, provides the website, and mentions the enclosed explanatory leaflet. This satisfies the requirement.

The customer draft does not state that the complainant has six months from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. It mentions 'the time limits that apply' in reference to the enclosed leaflet, but does not itself state the six-month deadline anchored to the final response date.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry that our previous response did not address the specific concerns you raised. ... These are important points that should have been considered and explained in our response, and we recognise that our letter did not do that. We are reopening your complaint and will carry out a proper review.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the substance of the complaint, acknowledges the firm's prior failing, uses clear and non-dismissive language, and commits to a proper reinvestigation addressing the specific points raised. It treats the complainant's concerns seriously and even-handedly.

The customer draft does not address whether redress or remedial action is appropriate. It defers the entire question to a future reinvestigation without communicating any position on redress to the customer. The handler answer discusses redress but only as a future consideration, and nothing is reflected in the customer-facing text.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us to ask us to reconsider your complaint about the sale of payment protection insurance alongside your personal loan in 2021.
verdicts.reasoning: The opening sentence directly acknowledges the complaint and accurately restates its subject.
The draft only states the complaint is being reopened and a review will be carried out; no firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) or reasons for a decision are provided.

verdicts.evidence: We want to make sure you are aware of your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge to you... We have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, which explains how to contact them and the time limits that apply. You can also find information on their website at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing text clearly informs the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS and provides contact details and a leaflet.

verdicts.evidence: You have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, which explains how to contact them and the time limits that apply.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft mentions that time limits apply but does not specify the six‑month period from the date of the final response, so it only partially satisfies the requirement.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry that our previous response did not address the specific concerns you raised... We are reopening your complaint and will carry out a proper review... We will consider the circumstances of the sale... and your individual situation, including any existing cover you held.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is apologetic, acknowledges the complainant’s points, and commits to a thorough, impartial re‑investigation, demonstrating fair treatment.
The draft does not address whether any redress or remedial action will be offered, nor does it explain why none would be due.

claims.claim: A final response that rejects a complaint must give reasons for doing so.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision from DISP 1.6 explicitly states that a final response may reject the complaint and 'gives reasons for doing so'.

claims.claim: Under DISP App 3.1.1G, the firm must handle PPI mis-selling complaints by working through the prescribed steps.
claims.reasoning: DISP App 3.1.1G sets out how a firm should handle complaints relating to the sale of a payment protection contract, referencing step 1 and step 2 of the appendix.

claims.claim: DISP App 3.3.11G and 3.3.12G require the firm to consider whether information was communicated fairly, clearly and not misleadingly, having regard to the complainant's individual circumstances — including existing cover.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision from dispapp3s3 includes DISP App 3.3.11 requiring the firm to consider whether it communicated information fairly, clearly and not misleadingly, and DISP App 3.3.12 requiring regard to the complainant's individual circumstances at the time of sale.

claims.claim: DISP App 3.6.3E notes that relevant evidence includes the complainant's demands, needs and intentions at the time of sale.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision from dispapp3s6 explicitly states: 'Relevant evidence might include the complainant's demands, needs and intentions at the time of the sale and any other relevant evidence.'

claims.claim: DISP App 3.7 sets out evidential provisions on how redress should be calculated if a complaint is upheld.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision from dispapp3s7 sets out detailed evidential provisions on redress calculation for PPI complaints.

claims.claim: The sale took place in 2021, which is after the 29 August 2017 cut-off in DISP 2.8.9R, so the August 2019 PPI deadline does not apply here.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.9R(1)(a) states the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which 'relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017'. A 2021 sale is after this date, so the restriction does not apply.

claims.claim: The standard FOS referral time limits under DISP 2.8.2R will apply.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.2R is referenced in the retrieved provision but its full content (the standard time limits) is not fully set out in the retrieved text. The provision references it in context but does not reproduce the rule in full.

claims.claim: The final response letter must inform the customer of their right to refer to FOS and enclose the standard explanatory leaflet.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision from DISP 1.6 explicitly requires the final response to enclose a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet and inform the complainant of their right to refer to FOS.

claims.claim: A new final response must: (a) address the specific grounds raised; (b) either accept, offer redress without accepting, or reject with full reasons; (c) enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (d) provide the FOS website address; (e) inform the customer of their right to refer to FOS; and (f) address the DISP 2.8.2R/2.8.7R time limit waiver question.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision from DISP 1.6 sets out that a final response must accept/offer redress/reject with reasons, enclose the FOS leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform of the right to refer, and indicate whether the respondent consents to waive relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R.

claims.claim: The firm's previous response did not address the customer's specific concerns about existing employer sickness cover at the time of sale.
claims.reasoning: DISP App 3.3.12 requires the firm to have regard to the complainant's individual circumstances at the time of sale, which would include existing cover. However, the specific factual claim about what the firm's previous response did or did not address is a factual assertion about the firm's conduct, not directly a regulatory provision.

claims.claim: As part of the review, the firm will consider the circumstances of the sale, including the information that was and was not communicated to the customer at the time, and the customer's individual situation, including any existing cover held.
claims.reasoning: DISP App 3.3.11 and 3.3.12 require the firm to consider whether information was communicated fairly, clearly and not misleadingly, having regard to the complainant's individual circumstances at the time of sale.

claims.claim: If the firm is not able to provide a full response within eight weeks of the original complaint, it will write to explain why and let the customer know when it expects to be able to respond.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision from DISP 1.6 describes the alternative written response that explains why the firm is not in a position to make a final response and indicates when it expects to provide one.

claims.claim: The customer has the right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision requires the firm to inform the complainant that they may refer the complaint to FOS. The 'free of charge' element is a well-known fact about FOS but is not explicitly stated in the retrieved provisions.

claims.claim: The customer does not need to wait for the firm's further response before referring to FOS.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision for the holding response (written response (2)) states the firm must inform the complainant 'that he may now refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service', indicating the customer need not wait.

claims.claim: The firm has enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision requires enclosing a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service standard explanatory leaflet in both final responses and holding responses.

claims.claim: The FOS website address is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.
claims.reasoning: The provision requires providing the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service, but does not itself state the URL. The URL is factually correct but not grounded in the retrieved text.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I called my bank on Monday to complain about an incorrect £15 fee on my current account. The adviser I spoke to agreed it was an error, reversed the charge immediately, and I told her I was happy with that resolution. However, it's now been over two weeks and I haven't received anything in writing from the bank confirming that my complaint was resolved or telling me about my rights to go to the Ombudsman if I change my mind. I feel the bank hasn't followed proper procedure — I'd like confirmation of what should have been sent to me and why it wasn't.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
Note: The message appears to have been submitted from the customer's perspective rather than a handler's perspective. I am treating this as a handler presenting a customer's complaint for a response to be drafted.

Regulatory position: The customer complained verbally about an incorrect £15 fee. The bank's adviser agreed it was an error, reversed the charge immediately, and the customer confirmed they were satisfied at that point. This looks like a complaint resolved by close of business on the next business day after receipt, which would ordinarily qualify for a summary resolution communication (SRC) rather than a full final response under DISP 1.6.4R. However, the retrieved provisions do not include the full text of DISP 1.6.4R or DISP 1.6.2AR, so I cannot confirm the precise SRC requirements from the retrieved context alone.

What is clear from the retrieved provisions is that DISP 1.6.2R requires that where a final response is issued, it must: (a) accept, partially accept, or reject the complaint with reasons; (b) enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (c) provide the FOS website address; (d) inform the customer of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and (e) address the time-limit waiver question. The bank's internal policy (section 7.5) also requires that FOS referral rights and contact details be included in any final response or eight-week holding response.

The customer has not received any written confirmation of the resolution or their FOS referral rights. Regardless of whether this should have been an SRC or a final response, the absence of any written communication is a procedural gap. The bank should now issue a written response — most likely a summary resolution communication or final response — confirming the resolution and the customer's rights.

Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft:
1. Confirm the exact date the complaint was received (Monday of two weeks ago) and the date it was resolved — if it was resolved by close of business the next business day, an SRC route may apply; if not, a full final response is required.
2. Confirm whether the £15 fee has in fact been reversed and is visible on the account.
3. The draft below is written as a final response (which is the safer, more complete option) rather than an SRC, given the two-week gap. If the SRC route is confirmed as applicable, the letter should be adjusted accordingly.
4. This complaint involves financial redress (the £15 reversal). Under Crest Bank's internal policy, complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more require countersignature by the Head of Customer Operations — this is below that threshold, but the handler should confirm no other escalation criteria apply.
5. Human review is flagged because the draft depends on the assumption that the new letter will serve as the remedying written response, and because a regulatory deadline (written response at point of resolution or within required timeframe) may already have been missed.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for your patience, and I am sorry that you have not received written confirmation of the outcome of your complaint until now.

I am writing to confirm that we have investigated the concern you raised on [date of call] regarding an incorrect £15 fee applied to your current account. Having reviewed the matter, we uphold your complaint. We agree that the fee was applied in error, and we can confirm that it has been reversed in full. We apologise for the inconvenience this caused you.

We recognise that you should have received written confirmation of this outcome sooner, and we are sorry that this did not happen.

If you are satisfied with this response, no further action is needed. However, if you remain dissatisfied with any aspect of our handling of your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You have six months from the date of this letter to do so. You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567. I have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet for your reference.

Thank you again for bringing this to our attention.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Crest Bank]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the final response to inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, enclose the FOS explanatory leaflet, and provide the FOS website address — none of which the customer received at the point of resolution.
Establishes the six-month time limit from the date of the final response within which the customer may refer the complaint to the FOS, which must be communicated to the customer.
Crest Bank's internal policy requires all final response letters to include FOS contact details and confirm the six-month referral window, which was not done at the point of resolution.

(2) the statement will require the complainant to notify the Ombudsman , before the date specified in the statement, whether he accepts or rejects the determination; (3) if the complainant notifies the Ombudsman that he accepts the determination within that time limit, it is final and binding on both parties; (4) subject to paragraph (4A), if the complainant does not notify the Ombudsman that he accepts the determination within that time limit, the complainant will be treated as having rejected the determination, and neither party will be bound by it; (4A) the complainant is not to be treated as having rejected the determination under paragraph (4) if all the following conditions are met: (a) the complainant notifies the Ombudsman after the specified date of the complainant's acceptance of the determination; (b) the complainant has not previously notified the Ombudsman of the complainant's rejection of the determination; (c) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit for acceptance was as a result of exceptional circumstances; (5) the Ombudsman will notify the respondent of the outcome and, if the complainant is treated as having rejected the determination under paragraph (4), the effect of paragraph (4A). ## DISP 3.6.7 (Rules) (1) An Ombudsman may correct any clerical mistake in the written statement of an Ombudsman's determination, whether or not the determination has already been accepted or rejected. (2) Any failure to comply with any provisions of the procedural

retrieved_chunks.content: The final response must: 1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received. 2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons. 3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated. 4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5). 5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer. All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. ### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS: - **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected. - **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right. All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. Crest Bank will cooperate fully with the FOS in respect of any referred complaints, providing all requested documentation within the timeframes set by the FOS. ### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows: 1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure. 2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes. 3.

retrieved_chunks.content: # DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time? ## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules) The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if: (1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or (2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or (2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint: (a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or (b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or (3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme: (a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or (b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme; unless: (4) the respondent consents and: (a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and (b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint. ## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules) Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint. ## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules) The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service: (1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or (2) more than: (a) six years

verdicts.evidence: I am writing to confirm that we have investigated the concern you raised on [date of call] regarding an incorrect £15 fee applied to your current account.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer-facing text opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately reflects its subject matter — the incorrect £15 fee and the lack of written confirmation. It also apologises for the delay in providing written confirmation, which addresses the second aspect of the complaint.

verdicts.evidence: Having reviewed the matter, we uphold your complaint. We agree that the fee was applied in error, and we can confirm that it has been reversed in full.
verdicts.reasoning: A clear decision ('we uphold your complaint') is stated with reasons that engage with the specific points raised — the fee was applied in error and has been reversed. The response also acknowledges the procedural failure ('you should have received written confirmation of this outcome sooner'). This addresses both the fee issue and the lack of written communication.

verdicts.evidence: We agree that the fee was applied in error, and we can confirm that it has been reversed in full.

claims.claim: A complaint resolved by close of business on the next business day after receipt would ordinarily qualify for a summary resolution communication (SRC) rather than a full final response under DISP 1.6.4R.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.10B.13 references DISP 1.6.4R in the context of a complainant accepting an earlier response, implying the existence of a summary resolution route, but the full text of DISP 1.6.4R is not retrieved so the precise conditions (next business day) cannot be confirmed from the retrieved provisions.

claims.claim: DISP 1.6.2R requires that where a final response is issued, it must: (a) accept, partially accept, or reject the complaint with reasons; (b) enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (c) provide the FOS website address; (d) inform the customer of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and (e) address the time-limit waiver question.
claims.reasoning: Provision 4 (DISP 1.6) sets out the final response requirements including accepting/rejecting with reasons, enclosing the FOS leaflet, providing the FOS website, informing of the right to refer, and indicating consent to waive time limits. This matches the claim.

claims.claim: The bank's internal policy (section 7.5) requires that FOS referral rights and contact details be included in any final response or eight-week holding response.
claims.reasoning: Provision 2 (Crest Bank's internal policy section 7.5) explicitly states that FOS referral rights must be notified in the final response letter and in a written response issued at eight weeks, including FOS contact details.

claims.claim: Under Crest Bank's internal policy, complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more require countersignature by the Head of Customer Operations.
claims.reasoning: Provision 2 states: 'All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.'

claims.claim: The final response must be issued within eight weeks of the date the complaint was received.
claims.reasoning: Provision 2 explicitly states the final response must be issued within eight weeks of the date the complaint was received. DISP 2.8.1(2) also references the eight-week period.

claims.claim: We uphold your complaint.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R(1)(a) requires the final response to accept the complaint where appropriate, and Provision 2 (internal policy) requires the response to state clearly whether the complaint is upheld. The draft's statement of upholding is consistent with these requirements.

claims.claim: The fee was applied in error and has been reversed in full.
claims.reasoning: The internal policy requires that where a complaint is upheld, the redress or remedy offered must be set out. The factual claim about the fee reversal is a case-specific fact not directly supported by any provision, but the requirement to state the remedy is grounded in the policy.

claims.claim: If you remain dissatisfied with any aspect of our handling of your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R(1)(e) requires the final response to inform the complainant that if they remain dissatisfied they may refer the complaint to the FOS. The 'free of charge' element is standard FOS information though not explicitly stated in the retrieved text.

claims.claim: You have six months from the date of this letter to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.2(1) states the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the final response. This supports the six-month time limit claim.

claims.claim: You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567.
claims.reasoning: Provision 2 (section 7.5) explicitly states that FOS contact details must include the website www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk and telephone 0800 023 4567.

claims.claim: I have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet for your reference.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R(1)(d) requires the final response to enclose a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I made a formal complaint to my bank on 14 March about an incorrect fee applied to my current account. I received a brief email the same day saying 'thanks, we've got your complaint' but it didn't tell me anything about when I could expect a response or what the process would be. It's now been three weeks and I've heard absolutely nothing since that initial email — no updates, no indication of progress, nothing. I feel like my complaint has just been forgotten about. I want to know what's happening and when I'll get a proper answer.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have been submitted by the customer directly rather than by a complaints handler — please confirm whether you are the handler or the customer before sending any response. I will proceed on the basis that a handler is asking you to draft a response to this customer's situation.

Regulatory position:
1. Acknowledgement obligation: Under DISP 1.6.2R (as referenced in Crest Bank's internal procedure at section 7.2), a written acknowledgement must be sent within five business days of receipt, confirming the complaint reference number, the handler's name and contact details, and the expected resolution timescale. The initial 'thanks, we've got your complaint' email sent on 14 March does not appear to have met this standard — it did not confirm the expected resolution timescale or handler contact details. This is a potential breach of DISP 1.6.2R that should be reviewed.
2. Eight-week deadline: Under DISP 2.8.1R, the Financial Ombudsman Service can consider a complaint if eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received it (for a standard complaint that is not an EMD or PSD complaint). The complaint was received on 14 March; eight weeks will expire around 9 May. The bank must issue either a final response or a holding response before that date.
3. Holding response: Under DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in the retrieved provision from disp1s6), if the bank is not in a position to issue a final response, it must send a written response explaining why it cannot yet respond and indicating when it expects to be able to do so. It must also inform the complainant that they may now refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, and provide the FOS website address.
4. Final response requirements: When a final response is issued, it must (a) accept or reject the complaint with reasons, or offer redress without accepting; (b) enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (c) provide the FOS website address; (d) inform the customer of their right to refer to FOS if dissatisfied; and (e) indicate whether the bank consents to waive the relevant DISP 2.8.2R or 2.8.7R time limits, using the wording in DISP 1 Annex 3R.

Recommended action: Issue a holding response to the customer immediately, acknowledging the gap in communication, confirming the complaint reference, providing handler contact details, explaining that the investigation is ongoing, giving an expected response date, and informing the customer of their FOS referral rights. You should also check internally whether the initial acknowledgement was compliant and whether the complaint was properly logged in the CMS on 14 March.

Assumptions to confirm: (1) This is a standard complaint (not an EMD/PSD complaint), so the eight-week rule applies. (2) No final response has yet been issued. (3) The complaint concerns an incorrect fee on a current account — not a PPI matter. (4) The customer is an eligible complainant under DISP 2.7. Human review is required before sending given the potential acknowledgement breach and the need to confirm the above assumptions.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for your patience, and I am sorry that you have not heard from us since our initial email on 14 March. I understand how frustrating it must be to feel that your complaint has not been progressed, and I want to assure you that is not the case.

Your complaint about the incorrect fee applied to your current account has been logged and is being investigated. Your complaint reference number is [REFERENCE NUMBER]. If you have any questions in the meantime, please contact [Handler Name] at [contact details].

We are still working through the details of your complaint and are not yet in a position to provide you with our final response. We expect to be able to write to you with our findings by [DATE]. We will keep you updated if anything changes.

In the meantime, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service at any time. The Financial Ombudsman Service is a free and independent service for resolving disputes between customers and financial businesses. I have enclosed their explanatory leaflet, which explains how to contact them and what they can do for you. You can also find information on their website at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.

Thank you again for bringing this to our attention. We take all complaints seriously and are committed to resolving this for you as quickly as possible.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the bank to send a written acknowledgement within five business days confirming the complaint reference, handler contact details, and expected resolution timescale, and to issue either a final response or a compliant holding response within eight weeks.

Sets out that the FOS can consider a complaint once eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received it (for non-EMD/PSD complaints), establishing the outer deadline by which the bank must respond.

Sets the six-month time limit within which the complainant must refer their complaint to the FOS after receiving a final response; the final response must indicate whether the bank consents to waive this limit.

Where the bank cannot yet issue a final response, it must explain why, indicate when it expects to respond, inform the complainant of their FOS referral right, enclose the FOS leaflet, and provide the FOS website address.

retrieved_chunks.content: --- ## 7. Procedures ### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination. All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted. ### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must: 1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected. 2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer. 3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale. ### 7.3 Investigation Process The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include: 1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems. 2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made. 3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure, and referring to the DPO, MLRO, or CTO respectively where applicable. 4. Documenting all investigative steps and evidence reviewed in the CMS. Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target. ### 7.4 Final Response Letters Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must: 1.

put the complainant back into the position the complainant should have been in had the act or omission not occurred. (4) If a respondent reports on the amount of redress paid under DISP 1.10B.7D(2)(b) the redress should not include the amount of a non-executed, defective or unauthorised payment transaction but should include any redress paid as a result of losses incurred by the complainant as a result of the non-executed, defective or unauthorised payment transaction. ## DISP 1.10B.9 (Directions) The relevant reporting period is the year immediately following: (1) where the respondent has an accounting reference date, its accounting reference date; and (2) where the respondent does not have an accounting reference date, 31 December each year. ## DISP 1.10B.10 (Directions) Reports are to be submitted to the FCA within 30 business days of the end of the relevant reporting periods through, and in the electronic format specified in, the FCA complaints reporting system or the appropriate section of the FCA website. ## DISP 1.10B.11 (Directions) If a respondent is unable to submit a report in electronic format because of a systems failure of any kind, the respondent must notify the FCA, in writing and without delay, of that systems failure. ## DISP 1.10B.12 (Rules) (1) If a respondent does not submit a complete report by the date on which it is due, in accordance with DISP 1.10B.10D, the respondent must pay the administrative fee specified in SUP 16.3.14R.(2) The administrative fee in (1) does not apply if the respondent has notified the FCA of a systems failure in accordance with DISP 1.10B.11R. ## DISP 1.10B.13 (Directions) A closed complaint is a complaint where: (1) the respondent has sent a final response; or (2) the complainant has indicated in writing acceptance of the respondent’s earlier response under DISP 1.6.4R (where applicable). ## DISP 1.10B.14 (Guidance) (1) To improve consumer awareness and to help respondents compare their performance against their peers, the FCA may publish: (a) complaints data about the payment services and electronic money sector as a whole; and (b) respondent level complaints data and information giving context to the complaints

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I made a complaint to my bank, Westfield Banking PLC, six weeks ago about a payment protection insurance policy that was sold to me through their branch but was actually underwritten by a separate insurer. Three weeks after I complained, the bank wrote to me saying they were forwarding my complaint to the insurer because the insurer was solely responsible. However, the bank didn't tell me the insurer's contact details in that letter — just said 'the relevant insurer' without a name, address, or phone number. I also feel that three weeks was not 'prompt' — I was left waiting with no update for all that time. I've now heard nothing from the insurer either, and I don't even know who to chase. I want to know why the bank took so long to forward my complaint and why they failed to give me the insurer's details so I could follow up myself.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
IMPORTANT NOTE — THIS MESSAGE APPEARS TO COME FROM A CUSTOMER, NOT A HANDLER. The system prompt states that the user is a complaints handler (a member of staff). The message as written reads as though it is from a customer describing their own complaint against 'Westfield Banking PLC'. Please clarify whether: (a) you are a handler at Westfield Banking PLC dealing with this complaint and summarising the customer's position, or (b) this has been submitted in error from a customer-facing channel.

Setting that aside, I have assessed the complaint on its merits using only the retrieved provisions, so you can act on this analysis once the above is clarified.

**Regulatory position on the substance:**

1. **Forwarding / redirecting the complaint to the insurer.** The retrieved provisions do not contain an explicit rule on the mechanics of redirecting a PPI complaint to a co-respondent insurer (e.g. a rule requiring the firm to name the insurer and provide contact details when forwarding). The provisions retrieved cover: the content requirements for a final response (DISP 1.6.2R); the content of an 'unable to issue final response at eight weeks' letter (also DISP 1.6.2R); PPI time-bar rules (DISP 2.8.8G, DISP 2.8.9R); PPI mis-selling evidential provisions (DISP App 3.6.3E); and PPI commission-disclosure written communication obligations (DISP App 3.11.3R–3.11.5R). None of these directly address the obligation to name and provide contact details for a co-respondent when redirecting a complaint. Accordingly, I cannot ground a regulatory finding on that specific point from the retrieved context alone.

2. **Timeliness — the three-week silence.** DISP 1.6.2R (as retrieved) requires that within eight weeks of receiving a complaint, the respondent must send either (a) a final response, or (b) a written response explaining why it is not yet in a position to issue a final response and indicating when it expects to do so — and that holding response must also inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS and enclose the FOS explanatory leaflet. The complaint was received six weeks ago; the bank wrote at three weeks but that letter (as described) did not appear to constitute a compliant holding response under DISP 1.6.2R(2) because it did not explain why a final response could not yet be issued or indicate when one would follow. The eight-week deadline has not yet passed (six weeks elapsed), so no deadline has been missed yet — but it is imminent.

3. **Eight-week deadline.** The bank has approximately two weeks remaining to issue either a final response or a compliant eight-week holding response. If it fails to do so, DISP 1.6.2R will be breached and the customer will be entitled to refer to the FOS immediately.

4. **PPI-specific FOS time limits.** If this complaint relates to the sale of a PPI policy on or before 29 August 2017, the DISP 2.8.9R deadline of 29 August 2019 for referring to the FOS will be relevant. The handler should check whether that deadline applies and, if so, include the appropriate wording in any response (per DISP App 3.13 and DISP 1.6.2R(1)(f) note).

**Recommended action:**
1. Confirm whether Westfield Banking PLC is the selling firm. If so, it cannot simply redirect the complaint to the insurer without taking responsibility for its own role in the sale — the retrieved provisions on PPI mis-selling (DISP App 3.6.3E) place evidential weight on the conduct of the selling firm.
2. Issue a compliant holding response immediately (within the remaining two weeks) that: (a) explains why a final response has not yet been issued; (b) gives an expected date; (c) informs the customer of their FOS referral right; (d) encloses/links the FOS explanatory leaflet; and (e) provides the FOS website address — all as required by DISP 1.6.2R(2).
3. The failure to provide the insurer's name and contact details cannot be grounded in the retrieved provisions — flag this for human review and consider whether internal policy or other handbook provisions (not retrieved here) impose that obligation.
4. **Human review is required** before any response is sent — see flags below.

`insufficient_context` is set to true because the retrieved provisions do not cover the specific obligation to name a co-respondent insurer when redirecting a complaint.

Imposes a separate time limit for PPI sale complaints (referral by 29 August 2019) and requires the respondent to address that limit in its response wording where applicable.

Evidential provision bearing on whether the selling firm's conduct at the point of sale constitutes a PPI mis-sale, relevant to whether the bank as selling firm can properly redirect the complaint entirely to the insurer.

retrieved_chunks.content: The final response must: 1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received. 2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons. 3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated. 4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5). 5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer. All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. ### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS: - **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected. - **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right. All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. Crest Bank will cooperate fully with the FOS in respect of any referred complaints, providing all requested documentation within the timeframes set by the FOS. ### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows: 1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure. 2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes. 3.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I took out a payment protection insurance policy with your bank in 2002 and cancelled it in 2005. I recently heard from a friend that I might have been mis-sold PPI and decided to complain. Your bank has sent me a letter saying my complaint is 'time-barred' and that you won't look into whether I was mis-sold the policy. However, the letter simply states my complaint is rejected due to time limits — it doesn't explain my right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service or give me the six-month deadline for doing so. I feel this is unfair as I believe I should at least have been told about my options, even if you consider the complaint too old.
fixed-titan.handler_answer: The customer's complaint raises a legitimate procedural concern. Under DISP 1.8.1R, a respondent may reject a time-barred complaint without considering its merits, but must do so by issuing a final response that complies with DISP 1.6.2R. DISP 1.6.2R requires that a final response (among other things): encloses a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provides the FOS website address, informs the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied, and includes the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R indicating whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R. Additionally, given this is a PPI complaint, DISP 1.6.2R also notes the obligation under regulation 19 of the ADR Regulations to provide equivalent messaging in respect of the DISP 2.8.9R PPI time limit (29 August 2019 deadline). If the bank's rejection letter omitted the FOS referral right, the six-month referral window, the FOS leaflet, and the Annex 3R waiver wording, the letter was non-compliant with DISP 1.8.1R read with DISP 1.6.2R. The bank should issue a remedying letter that includes all required elements. ASSUMPTION TO CONFIRM: (1) Please confirm the exact contents of the original rejection letter — specifically whether any FOS referral information, the explanatory leaflet, or Annex 3R wording was included, as the customer's account suggests these were all absent. (2) Confirm whether the bank consents or does not consent to waive the time limits in DISP 2.8.2R and/or DISP 2.8.9R, as this determines which Annex 3R prescribed wording to use. (3) Note that the PPI complaint relates to a sale in 2002 and the customer did not complain by 29 August 2019, so DISP 2.8.9R would likely bar FOS jurisdiction over the PPI mis-sale complaint itself — but the procedural failure in the rejection letter is a separate issue that must be remedied regardless. Human review is required before sending given the PPI time-bar complexity and the need to select the correct Annex 3R wording.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch and for raising your concerns about the letter we sent you regarding your payment protection insurance complaint.

We are sorry that our previous letter did not set out your options clearly. When we reject a complaint on the basis that it falls outside the relevant time limits, we are required to explain your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), and our letter should have done so. We recognise that this was not done, and we apologise for any confusion this caused.

To be clear about where matters stand:

**Our position on your complaint**
Your PPI policy was sold in 2002 and cancelled in 2005. We consider your complaint to have been made outside the time limits that apply to PPI mis-sale complaints. On that basis, we are not in a position to investigate the merits of whether the policy was mis-sold.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**
Even though we have rejected your complaint on time-limit grounds, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. The FOS is an independent service that can look at complaints about financial businesses.

Please be aware that the FOS operates its own time limits and eligibility rules. Whether the FOS is able to consider your complaint — including whether the time limits that apply to PPI complaints affect its ability to do so — is a matter for the FOS to determine. [INSERT APPROPRIATE ANNEX 3R WORDING HERE ONCE WAIVER POSITION IS CONFIRMED.]

If you wish to refer your complaint to the FOS, you should do so within six months of the date of this letter.

I have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, which sets out how the service works and the time limits for referring complaints. You can also find further information at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.

If you have any questions about this letter, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Name]
[Job title]
[Bank name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Permits rejection of a time-barred complaint without considering the merits, but requires the rejection to be communicated in a final response complying with DISP 1.6.2R — the basis for the bank's obligation to include FOS referral information even in a time-bar rejection.

Sets out the mandatory contents of a final response, including enclosing the FOS explanatory leaflet, providing the FOS website address, informing the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and including the Annex 3R waiver wording — all of which the customer states were absent from the bank's letter.

Establishes the 29 August 2019 deadline for PPI mis-sale complaints to be referred to the respondent or FOS, which is relevant to the FOS's jurisdiction over this complaint and to the messaging required under DISP 1.6.2R.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry that our previous letter did not set out your options clearly. When we reject a complaint on the basis that it falls outside the relevant time limits, we are required to explain your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), and our letter should have done so. We recognise that this was not done, and we apologise for any confusion this caused. ... Your PPI policy was sold in 2002 and cancelled in 2005. We consider your complaint to have been made outside the time limits that apply to PPI mis-sale complaints. On that basis, we are not in a position to investigate the merits of whether the policy was mis-sold.

It gives reasons engaging with the specific points raised — the failure to include FOS referral information and the time-limit basis for rejection.
