fixed-titan.scenario_text: I opened a new savings account with your bank last month after seeing your promotional email comparing your interest rates to those of three competitor banks. The email claimed your rates were 'market-leading' and showed a comparison table, but it didn't mention that your rate was only a temporary introductory rate for 3 months, after which it drops to 0.5%. The competitors' rates shown were their standard ongoing rates. I feel this comparison was unfair and misleading — I wouldn't have switched if I'd known the rate would drop so dramatically. I transferred £40,000 from my previous provider and have now missed their loyalty bonus as a result. When I raised this issue at my branch three weeks ago, the branch manager told me it wasn't their responsibility because the email was sent by your 'digital marketing partner' and forwarded my complaint to them. I've heard nothing since from either party. I want to know who is actually dealing with my complaint and I want compensation for the interest I've lost by switching.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious regulatory concerns that require careful handling and human review before any response is sent.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Misleading comparison / omission of introductory rate:** BCOBS 4 (chunk bcobs4s1) and BCOBS 2 (chunk bcobs2s5, bcobs2s6) contain guidance that where an introductory, promotional or preferential rate applies until a specified date or end of a fixed period, this should be disclosed — including in the summary box for savings accounts. BCOBS 2 Annex 1R (referenced in bcobs2s5 and bcobs2s6) requires the summary box to include details of introductory or promotional rates. The promotional email appears to have compared the bank's introductory rate against competitors' standard ongoing rates without disclosing the temporary nature of the bank's rate. This is likely to have been misleading by omission. BCOBS 4 guidance (bcobs4s1) also indicates that notice of the expiry of an introductory rate should be provided within a reasonable period before it ceases to apply.

2. **Responsibility for the financial promotion:** The branch manager's suggestion that the digital marketing partner is responsible does not resolve the bank's own regulatory obligations. The retrieved provisions do not explicitly address agency/outsourcing liability, so I cannot make a definitive regulatory statement on that point from the retrieved context alone — flag this for your compliance/legal team. However, the complaint is clearly one the bank must own and handle.

3. **Complaint handling delay:** The customer raised this at branch three weeks ago and has heard nothing from either party. You will need to check your internal complaints handling timelines (DISP provisions are not in the retrieved context, so I cannot cite specific deadlines here — this is a gap in the retrieved provisions). Flag this urgently: a regulatory deadline may already be at risk.

4. **Compensation claim:** The customer is claiming quantifiable financial loss (lost loyalty bonus, interest differential on £40,000). This is a redress matter that must be assessed by a human reviewer — I cannot determine or recommend a compensation figure.

**Assumptions and uncertainties to confirm before sending:**
- Confirm the exact date the complaint was first received (branch visit, three weeks ago) and whether any acknowledgement was sent within the required timeframe.
- Confirm whether the digital marketing partner was acting as the bank's agent for the purposes of the financial promotion — if so, the bank is likely responsible for the content of the promotion.
- Confirm whether the promotional email constituted a 'direct offer financial promotion' under BCOBS 2.2A.1R, which would trigger the summary box requirements.
- Confirm the actual rate drop timeline and whether the customer received any advance notice of the rate change per BCOBS 4 guidance.
- Obtain the original promotional email for review before responding.
- Involve compliance and potentially legal before committing to any position on redress.

**Recommended action:** Send the customer a prompt acknowledgement confirming the bank is handling the complaint, who their point of contact is, and that a full response will follow. Do not address the merits or compensation in the acknowledgement. A substantive response should only follow after internal investigation and human review.

The draft below is an acknowledgement only — it does not address the merits or make any commitment on redress.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for raising your concerns with us. I am writing to confirm that we have received your complaint and that we are taking responsibility for investigating it.

I understand that you are concerned about the promotional email you received comparing our savings rate to those of competitor banks, and in particular that the email did not make clear that our rate was a temporary introductory rate. I also understand that you feel this affected your decision to transfer £40,000 to us, and that you have suffered a financial loss as a result. I am sorry that you have been left without a clear point of contact since raising this at your branch.

To be clear: your complaint is being handled by [Bank Name], and your point of contact is [Handler Name/Team Name] who can be reached at [contact details]. You do not need to pursue this separately with any third party.

We take concerns about the accuracy and fairness of our promotional communications very seriously. We are now investigating the matters you have raised, including the content of the email and the circumstances of your account switch, and we will write to you again with our findings and our response to your request for compensation.

We aim to provide you with a full response as soon as possible. If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us using the details above.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Bank Name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the summary box for a savings account to include the rate or rates of interest, including details of any introductory or promotional rate — relevant to whether the promotional email and account opening materials adequately disclosed the temporary nature of the rate.

Requires the summary box to include details where an introductory, promotional or preferential rate applies until a specified future date or end of a fixed period — directly relevant to the omission of the introductory rate expiry in the promotional comparison.

Indicates that where an introductory, promotional or preferential rate applies until a specified future date or end of a fixed period, a firm should provide notice of the expiry of that rate within a reasonable period before it ceases to apply — relevant to whether the customer was warned of the rate drop.

retrieved_chunks.content: When providing a notice under (3)(c) relating to a decrease in the rate of interest, for example, a firm should ensure that the heading of the notice clearly indicates that the rate of interest is decreasing. (4) Where a firm notifies a banking customer of a material change to a rate of interest that applies to a retail banking service and that will be to the disadvantage of a banking customer, this notification should, where applicable: (a) refer to the fact that the firm offers a comparable retail banking service for which the banking customer is eligible; (b) indicate that the banking customer may move to that retail banking service or a retail banking service provided by another firm; and (c) indicate that the firm will assist the banking customer to move to another retail banking service if he wishes to do so. (5) Subject to (5A), where , under a contract for a retail banking service, an introductory, promotional or preferential rate of interest applies to the retail banking service until a specified future date or the end of a fixed period, a firm should provide notice of the expiry of the application of that rate of interest to the banking customer on paper or in another durable medium within a reasonable period before that rate of interest ceases to apply. (5A) Paragraph (5) does not apply where the balance of the account is less than £100 at the time when the firm would otherwise provide the notice. (6) [deleted] (6A) In relation to a fixed-term savings account, a firm should provide notice of the expiry of the fixed term to the banking customer on paper or in another durable medium in good time before the end of the fixed term.

Note 3: ‘Reference interest rate’ has the same meaning as in the Payment Services Regulations.

(3) This rule does not apply to a financial promotion: (a) for an authorised non-business overdraft agreement provided by a firm of a type listed in CONC 5C.1.2R(2) ; or (b) for a credit agreement in respect of which the APR is 0%; or (c) for a credit agreement to be entered into by a community finance organisation as lender. ## CONC 3.5.8 (Guidance) (1) A firm’s trading name, website address or logo could trigger the requirements in CONC 3.5.7R(1). (2) For the purposes of CONC 3.5.7R(1)(b), a comparison with another person, product or service includes a reference (whether stated or implied) to: (a) the terms on which, or the way in which, credit is offered or made available; or (b) the nature or quality or any other aspect of the service relating to the credit that the person offers or provides (or does not offer or provide). The financial promotion does not need to specify a particular person, product or service for there to be a comparison. (3) A financial promotion does not necessarily include a comparison where it merely refers to a person, product or service in a factual manner, but there will be an implied comparison for the purposes of CONC 3.5.7R(1)(b) if it may reasonably be inferred that a comparison is being made. (3) Where the rate of interest that applies to a savings account may change depending on the period that has elapsed since it was opened or on whether certain conditions are met, a firm should incorporate the table set out in BCOBS 2 Annex 2G in the row of the summary box that includes the heading “What is the interest rate?” unless it would be misleading to do so. (4) The effect of BCOBS 2.2A.1R(1) is that the summary box must be incorporated in the direct offer financial promotion itself. It is not sufficient, for example, to include in a direct offer financial promotion that appears on a website a link to a separate page containing the summary box.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: My mother, who is 78 and has been diagnosed with vascular dementia, was granted a £5,000 credit card limit increase by your bank in March 2021. I am her son and hold lasting power of attorney for her financial affairs. At the time of the limit increase, she was already showing significant signs of confusion — she couldn't remember her own address when speaking to your staff on the phone, and her GP had written to you in February 2021 confirming her diagnosis. Despite this, your bank proceeded with the credit limit increase without taking any steps to help her understand the decision or contacting me as her attorney. She has since accumulated £4,800 in debt she cannot repay on a state pension. I raised this complaint with you in November 2024. Your branch manager told me verbally that because the credit limit increase happened over three years ago, you won't look into it and said it's 'time-barred'. I believe you should have recognised her lack of mental capacity and never increased her limit. I want the debt written off and a proper explanation of why you are refusing to investigate.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This is a serious complaint involving potential regulatory breaches under CONC 6.7.7R and CONC 2.10.10G, as well as possible failure to act on clear indicators of mental capacity limitation under CONC 2.10.8G (indicators listed in conc2s10 chunk). The customer's son holds LPA and is the complainant.

**Key regulatory points from the retrieved provisions:**

1. **CONC 6.7.7R** prohibits a firm from increasing a credit limit where a customer is at risk of financial difficulties. The complaint alleges the customer was already showing signs of confusion and had a GP letter confirming vascular dementia diagnosis in February 2021 — one month before the increase. This is directly relevant.

2. **CONC 2.10.9G and CONC 2.10.10G** indicate that firms should take reasonable steps to ensure suitable practices for fair treatment of customers with mental capacity limitations, and should not inappropriately deny or grant credit where the firm reasonably believes the agreement would be voidable. The GP letter and the customer's inability to recall her own address are strong indicators under CONC 2.10.8G that the firm ought reasonably to have been aware of a mental capacity limitation.

3. **The 'time-bar' position stated verbally by the branch manager is not supported by any provision in the retrieved context.** You must not rely on that position in the customer response without proper legal/compliance review. The FCA's DISP rules on time limits are not in the retrieved provisions, so I cannot confirm or deny whether a time-bar applies — this must be verified by your compliance team before any position is taken with the customer.

4. **CONC 7.10.1R** requires suspension of debt recovery where the firm has been notified or ought reasonably to be aware that the customer lacks mental capacity. Given the LPA holder has now raised this formally, this rule is engaged for any ongoing recovery activity.

**Recommended actions before sending any response:**
- Compliance must review whether the complaint is in fact time-barred under DISP rules (not covered by retrieved provisions — do not assert this to the customer without verification).
- The file must be reviewed for the GP letter dated February 2021 and any call recordings from the time of the limit increase.
- Any ongoing debt recovery activity against the customer should be suspended immediately pending review (CONC 7.10.1R).
- The request for debt write-off involves a financial/redress decision — this cannot be committed to in the customer letter and requires senior/compliance sign-off.
- There are clear signs of customer vulnerability (elderly, dementia diagnosis, LPA in place). Human review is mandatory.

**Assumption flagged:** The customer draft below does NOT accept or deny the time-bar position, as this has not been verified. It acknowledges the complaint and confirms investigation. Do not send this letter if the bank intends to maintain a time-bar position — that would require a different, carefully reviewed response.

**Do not send this draft without human review and compliance sign-off.**
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer's name / addressed to LPA holder as appropriate],

Thank you for contacting us and for setting out your concerns in detail. We are sorry to hear about your mother's situation, and we want to assure you that we take this complaint very seriously.

We understand that you hold a lasting power of attorney for your mother's financial affairs and that you are raising this complaint on her behalf. We are treating you as the authorised representative for all correspondence relating to this matter.

You have raised concerns that in March 2021, your mother's credit card limit was increased to £5,000 at a time when she had been diagnosed with vascular dementia, and that your bank had received a letter from her GP confirming this diagnosis before the increase was made. You have also told us that your mother was showing clear signs of confusion at the time, including being unable to recall her own address when speaking with our staff. You have further told us that you, as her attorney, were not contacted before the increase was applied.

We want to be clear: we have a responsibility to consider a customer's circumstances carefully before increasing a credit limit, and we are required not to increase a credit limit where a customer is at risk of financial difficulties. We also have obligations to treat customers with mental capacity limitations fairly and to take reasonable steps to ensure our practices are appropriate for their needs. The concerns you have raised go to the heart of whether we met those obligations in your mother's case.

We are now investigating your complaint fully. As part of that investigation, we will review the information that was available to us at the time of the credit limit increase, including any correspondence from your mother's GP and records of her interactions with our staff.

Regarding the suggestion made verbally by our branch manager that your complaint cannot be investigated because of the time that has passed: we are reviewing that position as part of our investigation and will address it in our formal response to you. We do not consider it appropriate to close this complaint without a proper examination of the facts.

In the meantime, please be assured that we have noted your mother's circumstances and will ensure that any ongoing activity on her account is handled appropriately in light of what you have told us.

We will write to you again with our findings. If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,
[Name]
[Job title]
[Contact details]

cited_provisions.relevance: Prohibits a firm from increasing a customer's credit limit on a credit card where the customer is at risk of financial difficulties — directly engaged by the allegation that the limit was increased despite known indicators of vulnerability and mental capacity limitation.

Indicates that it would not be inappropriate to decline to significantly increase credit where the firm reasonably believes the agreement would be voidable — relevant to whether the limit increase should have been refused given the GP letter and observed confusion.

Indicates firms should take reasonable steps to ensure suitable practices for fair treatment of customers with mental capacity limitations — relevant to whether the bank had adequate procedures in place at the time of the limit increase.

Requires a firm to suspend pursuit of debt recovery where it has been notified or ought reasonably to be aware that the customer lacks mental capacity — engaged now that the LPA holder has formally raised the complaint and the customer's diagnosis is known.

Indicates a firm should allow the customer or a person acting on their behalf a reasonable period to provide evidence of the impact of any mental capacity limitation — relevant to how the bank should now engage with the LPA holder.

Indicates that CONC 7.10.1R does not prevent pursuit of debt through a responsible third party acting under a registered lasting power of attorney — relevant to the appropriate channel for all future engagement on this account.

retrieved_chunks.content: [Note: paragraph 6.5 of ILG] ## CONC 6.7.7 (Rules) A firm must not increase, nor offer to increase, a customer's credit limit on a credit card or retail revolving credit agreement where: (1) the firm has been advised that the customer does not wish to have any credit limit increases; or (2) a customer is at risk of financial difficulties. [Note: paragraphs 6.6 and 6.7 of ILG] ## CONC 6.7.8 (Rules) A firm under a retail revolving credit agreement, or a regulated credit agreement for a credit card, must: (1) permit a customer at any time to reduce or decline offers to increase the credit limit; and (2) permit a customer to decline to receive offers of credit limit increases.[Note: paragraphs 6.8 and 6.9 of ILG] ## CONC 6.7.9 (Rules) (1) This rule applies to a regulated credit agreement for a credit card and to a retail revolving credit agreement. (2) A firm must notify the customer of a proposed increase in the credit limit under the agreement: (a) in the case of a regulated credit agreement for a credit card or a store card, at least 30 days before the increase comes into effect; and (b) in the case of a retail revolving credit agreement (other than an agreement for a store card), at least 28 days before the increase comes into effect, except in the circumstances described in (3). (3) The notification in (2) is not required where: (a) the increase is at the express request of the customer; or (b) the increase is proposed by the firm, but the customer agrees to it at that time and wishes it to come into effect in less than 30 days or 28 days (as the case may be). [Note: paragraph 6.17 of ILG] ## CONC 6.7.10 (Rules) Where a customer is at risk of financial difficulties, a firm under a retail revolving credit agreement or a regulated credit agreement for a credit card must, other than where a promotional rate of interest ends, not increase the rate of interest under the agreement.

retrieved_chunks.content: Examples (amongst others) of indications might include: (1) where a firm has an existing relationship with a customer, the customer making a decision that appears to the firm to be unexpected or out of character; (2) a person who is likely to have an informed view of the matter, such as a relative, close friend, carer or clinician raising a concern with the firm as to the capacity of the customer to make a decision about borrowing; (3) the firm understands or has reason to believe the customer has been diagnosed as having an impairment which led to the customer not having had mental capacity for similar decisions in the past; (4) the firm understands or has reason to believe the customer does not understand what the customer is applying for; (5) the firm understands or has reason to believe the customer is unable to understand the information and explanations provided by the firm, in particular concerning the key risks of entering into the agreement; (6) the firm understands or has reason to believe the customer is unable to retain information and explanations provided by the firm to enable the customer to make the decision to borrow; (7) the firm understands or has reason to believe the customer is unable to weigh up the information and explanations provided by the firm to enable the customer to make the decision to borrow; (8) the customer is unable to communicate a decision to borrow by any reasonable means; (9) the customer being confused about the personal information that the firm requires, such as date of birth or address. [Note: paragraphs 3.14 and 3.15 of MCG] ## CONC 2.10.9 (Guidance) (1) A firm should not unfairly discriminate against a customer who it understands, or reasonably suspects, has a mental capacity limitation, in particular, by inappropriately denying the customer access to credit. [Note: paragraph 4.8 of MCG] (2) It would not be inappropriate not to grant credit nor significantly increase the amount of credit under an agreement nor set a credit limit for running account credit where the firm reasonably believes the agreement or decision would be voidable at the instance of the customer or the agreement is void. ## CONC 2.10.10 (Guidance) (1) In accordance with Principle 6, firms should take reasonable steps to ensure they have suitable business practices and procedures in place for the fair treatment of customers who they understand, or reasonably suspect, have or may have a mental capacity limitation.

retrieved_chunks.content: # CONC — CONC 7.10 Treatment of customers with mental capacity limitations ## CONC 7.10.1 (Rules) A firm must suspend the pursuit of recovery of a debt from a customer when: (1) the firm has been notified that the customer might not have the mental capacity to make relevant financial decisions about the management of the customer's debt and/or to engage in the debt recovery process at the time; or (2) the firm understands or ought reasonably to be aware that the customer lacks mental capacity to make relevant financial decisions about the management of the customer's debt and/or to engage in the debt recovery process at the time. [Note: paragraphs 3.7r of DCG and 7.13 of ILG] ## CONC 7.10.2 (Guidance) A firm should allow a customer or a person acting on behalf of the customer a reasonable period of time to provide evidence as to the likely impact of any mental capacity limitation on the customer's ability to engage with the firm. [Note: paragraph 3.7r (box) of DCG] ## CONC 7.10.3 (Guidance) CONC 7.10.1 R does not prevent a firm from pursuing the debt through a responsible third party acting on behalf of the customer, where the customer has given prior consent, for example, pursuant to a registered lasting power of attorney. [Note: paragraph 3.7r (box) of DCG] ## CONC 7.10.4 (Guidance) Firms should note CONC 7.2.1 R (and its accompanying guidance) which requires firms to establish and implement policies and procedures for the fair and appropriate treatment of vulnerable customers.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I opened an execution-only share dealing account with your firm last year to buy and sell shares listed on the London Stock Exchange. When I signed up, I was never told anything about your complaints procedure — no leaflet, no mention of it at all. I wasn't even told about the Financial Ombudsman Service. More recently, I purchased units in a structured product through the same account. The product turned out to be far more complex than I expected — it embeds a derivative and I don't understand the risks at all. Nobody at your firm asked me any questions about my knowledge or experience before letting me buy it, and I wasn't warned that you weren't assessing whether it was appropriate for me. I've now lost a significant amount of money. I believe your firm failed in its obligations both by not assessing appropriateness for a complex product and by never informing me of my rights to complain or access the ombudsman.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues. I can partially address them from the retrieved provisions, but there are significant gaps that require human review before any response is sent.

**Issue 1: Failure to provide complaints procedure information at point of sale**
DISP 1.2.1R (retrieved in chunk disp1s2.md) requires respondents to provide eligible complainants with information about their internal complaints handling procedures and the availability of the Financial Ombudsman Service. This obligation applies at or immediately after the point of first contact with an eligible complainant. If the customer was not provided with this information when they opened the account, there is a prima facie breach of DISP 1.2.1R. DISP 1.2.3G further indicates that the summary details should cover how the firm handles complaints and that unresolved complaints may be referred to the FOS. You should check your records to confirm whether any complaints information was provided at account opening (e.g. in a welcome pack, terms and conditions, or online journey).

**Issue 2: Appropriateness assessment for the structured product / complex instrument**
The customer alleges that no appropriateness assessment was conducted before they purchased a structured product embedding a derivative, and that they were not warned that no such assessment was being made. The retrieved provisions do not include the specific COBS rules on appropriateness (COBS 10 or COBS 10A) that would govern this obligation for execution-only transactions in complex instruments. MCOB 4.8A provisions retrieved relate to regulated mortgage contracts, not share dealing or structured products, so they are not directly applicable here. The COBS 4 chunk retrieved relates to risk warnings for certain investments but does not set out the appropriateness assessment framework. I therefore cannot ground a regulatory position on the appropriateness failure from the retrieved context alone — `insufficient_context` is set to true for this limb.

**Financial loss / redress**
The customer has suffered a significant financial loss and is seeking redress. This requires human review before any commitment or position is taken.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending any customer response:**
1. Were complaints procedure details (including FOS information) provided at account opening or in any subsequent written communication? If yes, the DISP 1.2.1R point may be answered; if no, there is a likely breach.
2. Was an appropriateness assessment conducted for the structured product purchase? If not, was a warning given that no assessment was being made? You will need to retrieve the applicable COBS appropriateness rules to assess this limb properly.
3. Is the customer an eligible complainant under DISP? (Almost certainly yes for a retail customer, but confirm.)
4. Are there any vulnerability indicators for this customer given the significant financial loss?
5. What is the current date relative to when the complaint was received — are there any DISP response deadlines already running?

Do not send the draft below without human sign-off. The customer draft acknowledges the complaint and the complaints procedure point, but is deliberately non-committal on the appropriateness issue pending retrieval of the relevant COBS provisions.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. We are sorry to hear about your experience and we take your concerns seriously. We are writing to acknowledge your complaint and to explain what happens next.

Your complaint raises two main concerns: first, that you were not given information about our complaints procedure or your right to refer matters to the Financial Ombudsman Service when you opened your account; and second, that we did not assess whether the structured product you purchased was appropriate for you, and did not warn you that no such assessment was being made.

We are required to provide customers with information about how to complain to us and about the Financial Ombudsman Service at the point we first make contact with them. We are investigating whether this was done in your case, and we will address this fully in our final response.

We are also investigating your concerns about the purchase of the structured product, including whether the correct steps were taken before that transaction was completed. We will set out our findings on this point in our final response as well.

We aim to send you our final response as soon as possible. If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us using the details below.

Yours sincerely,
[Complaints handler name]
[Firm name]
[Contact details]

retrieved_chunks.content: # DISP — DISP 1.1A Complaints handling requirements for MiFID complaints ## DISP 1.1A.1 (Rules) This section: (1) applies to the MiFID complaints of a MiFID investment firm and does not apply to complaints that are not MiFID complaints; (2) also applies to the MiFID complaints of a third country investment firm received from a retail client or an elective professional client but does not apply to complaints that are not MiFID complaints; and (3) applies certain other provisions in DISP 1 to such complaints. ## DISP 1.1A.3 (Guidance) A MiFID complaint is, amongst other things, a complaint about:(1) the provision of investment services or ancillary services to a client by an investment firm;(2) the provision of one or more investment services to a client by a CRD credit institution;(3) selling structured deposits to clients, or advising clients on them, where the sale or advice is provided by an investment firm or a CRD credit institution;(4) the activities permitted by the UK provisions which implemented article 6(3) of the UCITS Directive when carried on by a collective portfolio management investment firm; and(5) the activities permitted by the UK provisions which implemented article 6(4) of the AIFMD when carried on by a collective portfolio management investment firm.[Note: see article 1(1), 1(3) and 1(4) of MiFID] ## DISP 1.1A.4 (Guidance) A MiFID complaint is also a complaint about the equivalent business of a third country investment firm. [Note: see articles 39 and 41 of MiFID] ## DISP 1.1A.5 (Guidance) In contrast to the other provisions in DISP 1 which generally apply to complaints from eligible complainants, subject to DISP 1.1A.6R: (1) the obligations in this section that apply to the MiFID complaints of MiFID investment firms, apply to complaints from “clients” as defined in the UK provisions which implemented MiFID (which includes retail clients, professional clients and (in relation to eligible counterparty business) eligible counterparties; and (2) the obligations in this section that apply to the MiFID complaints of third country investment firms, apply to complaints from retail clients and elective professional clients.

retrieved_chunks.content: # DISP — DISP App 1.1 Introduction ## DISP App 1.1.1 (Guidance) This appendix sets out the approach and standards which firms should use when investigating complaints relating to the sale of endowment policies for the purposes of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage. It is not intended to be comprehensive. It is primarily concerned with the assessment of whether the complainant may have suffered financial loss, and if so, how much that loss is, and therefore what amount a firm should consider offering by way of fair and appropriate compensation in circumstances where the firm's investigation of a complaint reveals: (1) the complainant has received negligent advice on investments; and (2) if this advice had not been negligent, either: (a) the complainant would be unlikely to have acquired the endowment policy but instead would have taken out the same amount of loan on a repayment basis; or (b) the complainant would have acquired an endowment mortgage for a shorter term. ## DISP App 1.1.2 (Guidance) There will also be cases where a firm will conclude after investigation that, notwithstanding its own failure to give compliant and proper advice, the complainant would nevertheless have proceeded with the endowment policy as sold, in which case no compensation will be due. ## DISP App 1.1.3 (Guidance) This appendix only addresses how firms should approach the assessment of loss and compensation where negligence on the part of the firm is established. ## DISP App 1.1.4 (Guidance) This appendix is relevant both to the obligations arising under the complaints handling rules contained in DISP 1 and to the FCA's approach to the supervision of firms. ## DISP App 1.1.5 (Guidance) This appendix is also relevant to complaints which the Ombudsman may investigate under the Compulsory Jurisdiction or Voluntary Jurisdiction of the Financial Ombudsman Service established under Part XVI of the Act (The Ombudsman Scheme). ## DISP App 1.1.6 (Guidance) Before proceeding to assess the extent of a complainant's financial loss, a firm will usually have completed the following stages: (1) gathering all relevant facts and information; (2) making a fair and objective assessment whether it has failed to comply with a relevant duty owed to the complainant; and (3) assessing whether any failure of duty by it was in the circumstances a material failure in the sense that if it had not occurred the complainant would

or (2) the subject matter of the complaint has been dealt with, or is being dealt with, by a comparable ADR entity; or (3) the subject matter of the complaint has been the subject of court proceedings where there has been a decision on the merits; or (5) dealing with such a type of complaint would otherwise seriously impair the effective operation of the Financial Ombudsman Service. ## DISP 3.3.4B (Guidance) Examples of a type of complaint that would otherwise seriously impair the effective operation of the Financial Ombudsman Service may include: (1) where it would be more suitable for the complaint to be dealt with by a court or a comparable ADR entity; or (2) where the subject matter of the complaint has already been dealt with by a comparable dispute resolution scheme; or (3) where the subject matter of the complaint has previously been considered or excluded under the Financial Ombudsman Service (unless material new evidence which the Ombudsman considers likely to affect the outcome has subsequently become available to the complainant); or (4) it is a complaint which: (a) involves (or might involve) more than one eligible complainant; and (b) has been referred without the consent of the other eligible complainant or complainants, and the Ombudsman considers that it would be inappropriate to deal with the complaint without that consent.

retrieved_chunks.content: # MCOB — MCOB 4.8A Execution-only sales ## MCOB 4.8A.1 (Guidance) This section sets out the conditions which must be satisfied for a firm to enter into or vary a regulated mortgage contract with a customer, or arrange such a transaction for a customer, without giving advice, or where the advice given by the firm has been rejected. If the interaction with the customer constitutes or includes advice or a recommendation (see PERG 4.6), then, unless the customer has rejected advice, the sale cannot be an execution-only sale, and the firm would need to comply with MCOB 4.7A (Advised sales). If a firm intends (where permitted under this section) to operate a business model under which it will not give advice to particular customers, it may wish to refer to PERG (particularly PERG 4.6) for guidance on the regulatory perimeter in relation to the regulated activities which constitute advising on home finance transactions. ## MCOB 4.8A.4 (Guidance) Firms are reminded that MCOB 2.5A.1 R (The customer’s best interests) applies in all cases, including in relation to execution-only sales. ## MCOB 4.8A.4A (Rules) A firm must consider what procedures it is appropriate to establish to identify execution-onlycustomers for whom advice on suitability, or other customer support, may be necessary to avoid causing foreseeable harm in connection with entering into or varying a regulated mortgage contract. ## MCOB 4.8A.5 (Rules) A firm must not encourage a customer to opt out of receiving advice on regulated mortgage contracts from, or reject advice given by, it or any associate. ## MCOB 4.8A.6 (Guidance) MCOB 2.5A.1R and MCOB 4.8A.5R (The customer’s best interests) mean that a firm should not steer the customer to elect to enter into an execution-only sale. ## MCOB 4.8A.6A (Guidance) Firms will not be treated as having breached MCOB 2.5A.1R or MCOB 4.8A.5R merely because they market execution-only sales or apply different pricing to execution-only sales from that applied to advised sales, provided that they act in a manner consistent with their obligations under the

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us. We are sorry to hear about your experience and we take your concerns seriously. We are writing to acknowledge your complaint and to explain what happens next. Your complaint raises two main concerns: first, that you were not given information about our complaints procedure or your right to refer matters to the Financial Ombudsman Service when you opened your account; and second, that we did not assess whether the structured product you purchased was appropriate for you, and did not warn you that no such assessment was being made.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately reflects both limbs of the complaint — the failure to provide complaints procedure information and the failure to assess appropriateness for the structured product.

The customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). It says 'We are investigating' and 'We will set out our findings on this point in our final response as well.' This is a holding/investigation posture, not a final decision. Despite being labelled as a final response stage, no decision or reasons are communicated to the customer.

The customer draft does not mention the complainant's right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. There is no signposting of FOS contact details, leaflet, or website in the customer-facing text.
The customer draft contains no mention of a six-month deadline from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry to hear about your experience and we take your concerns seriously... We are investigating whether this was done in your case... We are also investigating your concerns about the purchase of the structured product, including whether the correct steps were taken before that transaction was completed.
verdicts.reasoning: The response is clear, non-dismissive, and engages with both limbs of the complaint in a respectful tone. However, for a final response it does not substantively address the merits of the complaint — it merely promises future investigation. It treats the complaint superficially by deferring all substantive engagement to a future response, which is inappropriate at the final response stage.

The customer draft does not address redress at all. The handler answer notes that financial loss requires human review, but no position on redress — whether an offer or an explanation that none is due — is communicated to the customer. The complaint plainly raises significant financial loss.

verdicts.evidence:
We are writing to acknowledge your complaint and to explain what happens next.
Your complaint raises two main concerns: first, that you were not given information about our complaints procedure...
verdicts.reasoning: The draft explicitly acknowledges the complaint and accurately restates the two issues raised by the customer.
The response does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor provide reasons for a decision; it only says the matters are being investigated.

verdicts.evidence: We are required to provide customers with information about how to complain to us and about the Financial Ombudsman Service at the point we first make contact with them.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft mentions the Financial Ombudsman Service but does not clearly inform the complainant of their right to refer this specific complaint to the FOS nor give contact details, meeting only the partial criteria.
There is no statement of the six‑month deadline for referring the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

verdicts.evidence:
We are sorry to hear about your experience and we take your concerns seriously.
We are investigating whether this was done in your case, and we will address this fully in our final response.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is courteous, acknowledges the concerns, and promises a thorough investigation without dismissing the complaint, satisfying the fair‑treatment requirement.
The draft does not address whether any redress or remedial action will be offered, nor does it explain why none might be due.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I have a regulated mortgage with your bank and I'm writing to complain about two issues. First, when I fell into arrears last year, you applied a £250 'arrears management fee' each month for three consecutive months, plus compound interest on those fees. I've checked with other lenders and nobody charges anything close to that — most charge £25-50 at most. I believe these charges are grossly excessive and the compound interest on a second charge mortgage is unlawful. Second, when I tried to call your complaints line to raise this issue, the only number provided was an 0871 number. I was on hold for 45 minutes and the call cost me over £18. I shouldn't have to pay a premium rate number just to make a complaint. I'm currently on long-term sick leave following a cancer diagnosis and am struggling financially as a result of reduced income. I'd like a full refund of the excessive charges, the interest applied to them, and reimbursement of my phone costs.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises three distinct regulatory issues, all of which appear well-founded on the retrieved provisions. The customer is also showing clear signs of vulnerability (cancer diagnosis, financial hardship from reduced income), which means human review is mandatory before anything is sent.

**Issue 1 – Arrears management charges (£250/month)**
MCOB 12.4.1 (not directly retrieved but referenced in the retrieved chunks) and the retrieved MCOB 12.4.4R are relevant: a firm must not take into account certain cost categories (funding/capital, general bank charges not incurred as a result of the payment shortfall, unrecovered fees, advertising costs, regulatory fines, and certain executive staff costs) when calculating the cost of additional administration arising from a payment shortfall. The charges must reflect actual additional administration costs. The customer's comparison evidence (£25–50 at comparable lenders vs £250) is also directly relevant to MCOB 12.5.3G, which says when assessing whether a charge is excessive, the firm should consider the amount compared with charges for similar products or services on the market. MCOB 12.5.1R and 12.5.2R require that charges are not excessive. You will need to review the firm's cost-of-administration calculation for these arrears management fees to determine whether £250/month can be justified. On the face of it, the comparison evidence is a strong indicator of excessiveness under MCOB 12.5.3G.

**Issue 2 – Compound interest on arrears charges (second charge mortgage)**
MCOB 12.5.5R is directly on point: a second charge lender may only charge interest on charges applied to a customer for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract if the interest is simple interest. If this is a second charge regulated mortgage contract, compound interest on the arrears management fees is unlawful on its face. You must confirm whether this is a first or second charge mortgage. If it is a second charge, the compound interest element should be reversed. If it is a first charge, MCOB 12.5.5R does not apply, but the excessive charges rules (MCOB 12.5.1R and 12.5.2R) may still be relevant to the interest element.

**Issue 3 – 0871 complaints line**
GEN 7.2.1R requires a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract to not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate. GEN 7.2.5R explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant with the call charges rule. This appears to be a clear breach. The customer's £18 call cost is a direct financial loss arising from this breach and should be considered for reimbursement. You should also check whether the complaints line number has since been changed.

**Vulnerability**
The customer has disclosed a cancer diagnosis and financial hardship. This is a clear vulnerability indicator. The firm's handling must reflect appropriate care and consideration throughout.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending:**
1. Is this a first or second charge regulated mortgage contract? (This determines whether MCOB 12.5.5R applies to the compound interest issue.)
2. Can the firm produce a cost-of-administration breakdown justifying the £250/month arrears management fee?
3. Has the 0871 complaints line number been changed? If not, this is an ongoing breach.
4. What is the exact amount of compound interest charged on the arrears fees?
5. The customer is requesting financial redress (refund of charges, interest, and phone costs). Any decision on quantum must be made by a human reviewer with appropriate authority.

Do not send the customer draft until all of the above are confirmed and a human reviewer has approved it.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear about your health and the financial pressures you are facing, and I want to assure you that we are taking your complaint seriously.

You have raised three concerns, which I will address in turn.

**Arrears management charges**

Our rules require that any charges we apply to customers in payment difficulties must not be excessive and must reflect the actual additional administration costs we incur as a result of the payment shortfall. They must not include items such as general overhead costs, funding costs, or regulatory fines. We are also required to consider how our charges compare with those of other lenders for similar services.

You have told us that comparable lenders charge significantly less than the £250 per month we applied. We take this seriously. We are reviewing the basis on which these charges were calculated to determine whether they can be justified under our regulatory obligations. We will write to you with our findings.

**Interest applied to arrears charges**

We are also reviewing the interest that was applied to the arrears management fees. The rules that apply to this depend on the type of mortgage you hold, and we are confirming the relevant details. We will address this point fully in our final response.

**Complaints line telephone number**

We are required by our regulator not to charge you more than the basic rate for calling us in connection with your mortgage. Numbers beginning with 0871 do not meet this requirement. We are sorry that you were directed to such a number and that you incurred a cost of over £18 as a result. We are looking into this and will address your request for reimbursement of that cost in our final response.

**Next steps**

We are investigating all three aspects of your complaint and will send you our final response as soon as possible. If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

If, once you receive our final response, you remain dissatisfied, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further details about this when we write to you with our final response.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires that any regulated mortgage contract does not impose, and cannot be used to impose, excessive charges upon a customer.
Requires that a firm's charges to a customer in connection with administering a regulated mortgage contract are not excessive.

Sets out factors for determining whether a charge is excessive, including comparison with charges for similar products or services on the market — directly relevant to the customer's comparison evidence.
Provides that a second charge lender may only charge simple interest (not compound interest) on charges applied to a customer for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract.

Specifies cost categories that must not be included when calculating the cost of additional administration arising from a customer's payment shortfall, limiting what can be recovered through arrears charges.
Confirms that payment shortfall and excessive charges requirements continue to apply after a regulated mortgage contract ends, and that references to 'customer' include former customers.
Requires a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract not to bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate — the complaints line obligation.
Defines the basic rate as the simple cost of connection, which must not provide the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues.
Explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant with the call charges rule, directly applicable to the complaints line used by the customer.

The excessive charges requirements will continue to apply to a firm after a home reversion plan has ended. References in this chapter to 'customer' will include references to a former customer as appropriate. ## MCOB 12.1.5 (Guidance) The FCA will expect a firm to ensure that charges made to a customer arising from the sale of a repossessed property and charges arising in relation to a sale shortfall are not excessive and are subject to the same considerations as apply with respect to payment shortfall charges under this chapter. ## MCOB 12.1.5A (Rules) The rules in MCOB 12.4 (Payment shortfall charges: regulated mortgage contracts) and MCOB 12.5 (Excessive charges: regulated mortgage contracts, home reversion plans and regulated sale and rent back agreements) apply to: (1) second charge regulated mortgage contracts entered into before 21 March 2016, in relation to charges imposed on a customer for events occurring on or after 21 March 2016; and (2) regulated mortgage contracts which are legacy CCA mortgage contracts secured by a first charge legal mortgage, in relation to charges imposed on a customer for events occurring on or after the earliest of: (a) the date on which the lender first acts in compliance or purported compliance with rules in the FCA Handbook which apply to regulated mortgage contracts in respect of the contract; (b) the date from which the lender notifies the borrower in writing that it will act in compliance with such rules in respect of the contract; and (c) 21 March 2017. ## MCOB 12.1.6 (Rules) This chapter does not apply to a firm carrying on reversion activities or regulated sale and rent back activities in respect of a customer acting in his capacity as an unauthorised reversion provider or as an unauthorised SRB agreement provider.

retrieved_chunks.content: # MCOB — MCOB 12.5 Excessive charges: regulated mortgage contracts, home reversion plans and regulated sale and rent back agreements ## MCOB 12.5.1 (Rules) A firm must ensure that any regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement that it enters into does not impose, and cannot be used to impose, excessive charges upon a customer. ## MCOB 12.5.2 (Rules) A firm must ensure that its charges to a customer in connection with the firm entering into, making a further advance or further release on, administering, arranging or advising on a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement , or arranging or advising on a variation to the terms of a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement are not excessive. ## MCOB 12.5.3 (Guidance) When determining whether a charge is excessive, a firm should consider: (1) the amount of its charges for the services or products in question compared with charges for similar products or services on the market; (2) the degree to which the charges are an abuse of the trust that the customer has placed in the firm; and (3) the nature and extent of the disclosure of the charges to the customer. ## MCOB 12.5.4 (Guidance) Mortgage lenders are also subject to requirements relating to responsible lending (see MCOB 11). ## MCOB 12.5.5 (Rules) A second charge lender may only charge interest on charges applied to a customer for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract if the interest is simple interest.

retrieved_chunks.content: # MCOB — MCOB 12.2 Purpose ## MCOB 12.2.1 (Guidance) (1) Principle 6 requires a firm to pay due regard to the interests of its customers and treat them fairly. A firm is also under an obligation, as a consequence of this sourcebook's disclosure requirements, to make charges transparent to customers. This chapter reinforces these requirements by preventing a firm from imposing unfair and excessive charges. (2) The level of charges under a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement is not typically a matter for regulation. However, in certain limited circumstances, the FCA believes that customers should be protected from unfair and excessive charging practices. This chapter considers four specific circumstances, where: (a) the charges imposed upon a customer seeking to terminate a regulated mortgage contract before the end of the term of the contract do not reflect the cost of termination to the firm; (b) the charges imposed on a customer in payment difficulties are not based upon the costs incurred by the firm; (c) the charges (including rates of interest) imposed on a customer under a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement are excessive and contrary to the customer's interests; and (d) the charges made to a customer in connection with a firm entering into, making a further advance on, administering, arranging or advising on a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement , or arranging or advising on a variation to the terms of a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement are excessive.

retrieved_chunks.content: # GEN — GEN 7.2 Call charges ## GEN 7.2.1 (Rules) A firm which operates a telephone line for the purpose of enabling a consumer to contact the firm in relation to a contract that has been entered into with the firm, must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate for the telephone call. ## GEN 7.2.2 (Guidance) The contract entered into with the firm may be in writing or otherwise. ## GEN 7.2.3 (Rules) For the purposes of the call charges rule, the basic rate is the simple cost of connection and must not provide the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues. ## GEN 7.2.4 (Rules) The following numbers, if used by firms, would comply with the call charges rule: (1) geographic numbers or numbers which are always set at the same rate, which usually begin with the prefix 01, 02 or 03; (2) calls which can be free of charge to call, for example 0800 and 0808 numbers; and (3) standard mobile numbers, which usually begin with the prefix 07, provided that the firm ordinarily uses a mobile number to receive telephone calls. ## GEN 7.2.5 (Rules) The following numbers, if used by firms, would not comply with the call charges rule: (1) premium rate numbers that begin with the prefix 09; (2) other revenue sharing numbers in which a portion of the call charge can be used to either provide a service or make a small payment to the firm, such as telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 084 or 0871, 0872 or 0873; and (3) telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 0870 as the cost of making a telephone call on such numbers can be higher than a geographic cost and will vary depending on the consumer’s telephone tariff.

retrieved_chunks.content: A charge of this sort is often referred to as a “facility fee” and payable periodically, for example annually. (d) The definitions of an arranged overdraft charge and an unarranged overdraft charge are not limited to charges that are described as financial consideration for the provision of credit. They could include, for example, a charge that is expressed as being referable to the execution of the payment transaction, if the charge is payable only where the transaction results in the account being in an overdrawn position or remaining in such a position. A charge for a payment transaction that is payable irrespective of whether or not the current account has a credit balance or a debit balance is not, however, caught by these definitions. (e) The definitions also do not include charges for operating or maintaining a personal current account (as distinct from charges for granting or continuing to make available an arranged overdraft facility in connection with the account), provided that the incidence and amount of the charges are not affected by whether or how much the customer uses an overdraft. A monthly account charge could be an example of such a charge. (9) CONC 5C.2 requires firms to use only a rate of interest expressed as a percentage applied on an annual basis to the relevant balance of arranged overdraft or unarranged overdraft. If interest is compounded, firms are free to choose the intervals at which they add arranged overdraft charges and unarranged overdraft charges to the principal balance, provided that the same compounding frequency is used in relation to the customer’s arranged overdraft and unarranged overdraft in respect of the same personal current account. (10) Firms are reminded of the obligation in CONC 3.5.3R(1) to include a representative example (including the representative APR) in a financial promotion that indicates a rate of interest or an amount relating to the cost of credit. Firms are also reminded of the obligation in CONC 3.5.7R(1) to include in a financial promotion a representative APR if the financial promotion states or includes certain matters.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear about your health and the financial pressures you are facing, and I want to assure you that we are taking your complaint seriously. You have raised three concerns, which I will address in turn.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and then accurately identifies and addresses all three issues raised: arrears management charges, interest on those charges, and the 0871 complaints line cost.

Despite being labelled as a 'final' response stage, the customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). Instead, it repeatedly says 'We are reviewing' and 'We will address this point fully in our final response.' No decision is communicated to the customer on any of the three issues.

verdicts.evidence: If, once you receive our final response, you remain dissatisfied, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further details about this when we write to you with our final response.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft mentions the right to refer to the FOS and names it, but does not provide contact details, a website, or a leaflet. It defers providing those details to a future final response. For a final response (which this is supposed to be), this is insufficient — it mentions the FOS but omits how to contact them.
The customer draft does not state the six-month deadline from the date of the final response within which the complainant must refer the complaint to the FOS. No time limit is mentioned at all.

verdicts.evidence: Our rules require that any charges we apply to customers in payment difficulties must not be excessive... You have told us that comparable lenders charge significantly less than the £250 per month we applied. We take this seriously.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the substance of each complaint point in clear, non-dismissive language and acknowledges the customer's concerns. However, for a final response it fails to reach any conclusions or decisions, which undermines fair treatment — the customer is left without resolution despite this being the final response stage.

verdicts.evidence: We are looking into this and will address your request for reimbursement of that cost in our final response.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft acknowledges the customer's request for redress (refund of charges, interest, and phone costs) but does not reach any position on whether redress is warranted or offer any. It defers all redress decisions to a future communication, which is inadequate for a final response.

verdicts.evidence: I am sorry to hear about your health and the financial pressures you are facing (customer draft); The customer has disclosed a cancer diagnosis and financial hardship. This is a clear vulnerability indicator. The firm's handling must reflect appropriate care and consideration throughout (handler answer); Do not send the customer draft until all of the above are confirmed and a human reviewer has approved it.
verdicts.reasoning: Both the handler answer and the customer draft recognise the vulnerability. The handler answer explicitly identifies the cancer diagnosis and financial hardship as vulnerability indicators, mandates human review, and the customer draft uses supportive, empathetic language acknowledging the customer's health and financial situation.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us... You have raised three concerns, which I will address in turn.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft acknowledges receipt and that three concerns were raised, but it does not restate the specific issues (arrears fees, interest, 0871 number), so the understanding is only generic.
The customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor provide reasons for a decision; it only says the firm is still reviewing.

verdicts.evidence: If, once you receive our final response, you remain dissatisfied, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
verdicts.reasoning: The right to refer to the FOS is mentioned, but no details on how to do so (contact information or website) are provided, so the element is only partially satisfied.
The draft does not mention the six‑month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: We are investigating all three aspects of your complaint and will send you our final response as soon as possible.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with each point, uses courteous language, and does not dismiss or minimise the complainant's concerns, demonstrating fair treatment.

verdicts.evidence: We are looking into this and will address your request for reimbursement of that cost in our final response.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft acknowledges the request for redress but does not provide a decision or offer; it merely promises to consider it later, so the element is only partially met.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: "The customer has disclosed a cancer diagnosis and financial hardship... The firm's handling must reflect appropriate care and consideration throughout." Customer draft: "I am sorry to hear about your health and the financial pressures you are facing..."
verdicts.reasoning: Both the internal note and the customer‑facing text recognise the complainant's vulnerability and indicate that the handling will be adapted accordingly.

claims.claim: MCOB 12.4.4R requires that a firm must not take into account certain cost categories (funding/capital, general bank charges not incurred as a result of the payment shortfall, unrecovered fees, advertising costs, regulatory fines, and certain executive staff costs) when calculating the cost of additional administration arising from a payment shortfall.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.4.4R as retrieved explicitly lists these excluded cost categories: funding or capital, general bank charges not incurred as a result of a customer having a payment shortfall, unrecovered fees, advertising costs, regulatory fines, and executive staff costs (with limited exceptions).

claims.claim: The charges must reflect actual additional administration costs.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.4.4R frames the calculation as 'the cost of the additional administration required as a result of a customer having a payment shortfall,' and MCOB 12.4.2G confirms charges are based on additional administration costs.

claims.claim: MCOB 12.5.3G says when assessing whether a charge is excessive, the firm should consider the amount compared with charges for similar products or services on the market.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.5.3(1) states: 'the amount of its charges for the services or products in question compared with charges for similar products or services on the market.'

claims.claim: MCOB 12.5.1R and 12.5.2R require that charges are not excessive.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.5.1R states a firm must ensure the contract does not impose excessive charges, and MCOB 12.5.2R states charges in connection with the contract must not be excessive.

claims.claim: MCOB 12.5.5R states that a second charge lender may only charge interest on charges applied to a customer for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract if the interest is simple interest.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision states exactly: 'A second charge lender may only charge interest on charges applied to a customer for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract if the interest is simple interest.'

claims.claim: If this is a second charge regulated mortgage contract, compound interest on the arrears management fees is unlawful on its face.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.5.5R permits only simple interest on charges for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract, so compound interest would breach this rule.

claims.claim: If it is a first charge mortgage, MCOB 12.5.5R does not apply.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.5.5R explicitly applies only to 'a second charge lender' in relation to 'a second charge regulated mortgage contract,' so it does not apply to first charge mortgages.

claims.claim: GEN 7.2.1R requires a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract to not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate.
claims.reasoning: GEN 7.2.1R as retrieved states: 'A firm which operates a telephone line for the purpose of enabling a consumer to contact the firm in relation to a contract that has been entered into with the firm, must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate for the telephone call.'

claims.claim: GEN 7.2.5R explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant with the call charges rule.
claims.reasoning: GEN 7.2.5R(2) explicitly lists 'telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 084 or 0871, 0872 or 0873' as numbers that would not comply with the call charges rule.

claims.claim: Our rules require that any charges we apply to customers in payment difficulties must not be excessive and must reflect the actual additional administration costs we incur as a result of the payment shortfall.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.5.1R and 12.5.2R require charges not to be excessive, and MCOB 12.4.4R (in the mcob12s4 chunk) frames charges as based on the cost of additional administration required as a result of a payment shortfall.

claims.claim: Charges must not include items such as general overhead costs, funding costs, or regulatory fines.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.4.4R explicitly excludes funding or capital, general bank charges not incurred as a result of a payment shortfall, and regulatory fines from the calculation.

claims.claim: We are also required to consider how our charges compare with those of other lenders for similar services.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.5.3(1) states a firm should consider 'the amount of its charges for the services or products in question compared with charges for similar products or services on the market.'

claims.claim: The rules that apply to interest on arrears charges depend on the type of mortgage the customer holds.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.5.5R applies specifically to second charge regulated mortgage contracts, meaning the applicable rules differ depending on whether the mortgage is first or second charge.

claims.claim: We are required by our regulator not to charge you more than the basic rate for calling us in connection with your mortgage.
claims.reasoning: GEN 7.2.1R states a firm must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate for a telephone call to contact the firm in relation to a contract entered into with the firm.

claims.claim: Numbers beginning with 0871 do not meet this requirement.
claims.reasoning: GEN 7.2.5R(2) explicitly states that numbers beginning with 0871 would not comply with the call charges rule.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I took out a home insurance policy through my bank's branch last year. I've since discovered that the bank received a significantly higher commission from the insurer they recommended compared to two other insurers whose products were arguably better suited to my needs and cheaper. When I asked the branch advisor at the time whether there were other options, they simply said 'this is the best one for you' without explaining why or mentioning any alternatives. I feel I was steered into a more expensive, less suitable policy because the bank was incentivised by the commission arrangement. I raised this with the bank's complaints team three weeks ago and received an acknowledgement, but I'm now writing to request a full resolution. I'm a retired pensioner on a fixed income and the additional premium cost has been a real stretch for my household budget.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises serious concerns that require careful handling. The customer alleges they were steered into a more expensive, less suitable home insurance policy because the bank received higher commission from the recommended insurer — and that the advisor failed to disclose alternatives or explain the recommendation. There are also clear vulnerability indicators: the customer is a retired pensioner on a fixed income who has experienced financial stretch as a result.

**Regulatory position based on retrieved provisions:**

1. **ICOBS 6A (customer's best interests rule):** Provision 2 (icobs6as5) indicates that where a firm receives greater remuneration — such as a higher commission rate — compared to other arrangements available to it, it must demonstrate how that selection was consistent with the customer's best interests rule. Where remuneration conflicts with that duty, the firm must take appropriate action. This is directly relevant: if the bank selected this insurer because of higher commission rather than the customer's best interests, that would be a potential breach of the customer's best interests rule.

2. **ICOBS 6A (retail premium finance / remuneration conflicts):** The same provision notes that where remuneration conflicts with the customer's best interests rule, the firm must take appropriate actions to address the situation, including changing providers where necessary. This reinforces the obligation to act in the customer's interests over the firm's financial gain.

**Important caveats and assumptions you must confirm before sending any response:**

- The retrieved provisions relate to retail premium finance and insurance distribution remuneration. The core 'customer's best interests' principle appears applicable, but the specific ICOBS rule reference (e.g. ICOBS 6.1A.4R or similar) is not fully cited in the retrieved chunks — the chunk references the rule by description rather than full provision number. You should verify the exact rule reference before finalising.
- The complaint involves a potential financial remedy (the difference in premium cost) and an allegation of mis-selling. This must go to human review before any response is sent.
- The customer is a retired pensioner on a fixed income — this is a vulnerability indicator under FCA expectations and must be factored into how the complaint is handled and the tone of any response.
- The complaint was acknowledged three weeks ago. You should check whether any regulatory response deadline is approaching or has been missed.
- The customer is requesting 'full resolution' — this implies they may be seeking redress. Do not commit to any specific remedy in the customer letter at this stage.
- The CONC provisions retrieved (Provisions 1 and 3) relate to consumer credit and discretionary commission arrangements in motor finance — they are not directly applicable to a home insurance commission complaint and should not be relied upon here.

**Recommended action:** Route to a senior complaints handler or compliance team given the vulnerability indicators, potential mis-selling allegation, and financial redress implications. Do not send the draft below without human review. The draft below acknowledges the complaint substantively but does not commit to any outcome or redress.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us to follow up on your complaint. I am sorry that you have had cause to raise this concern, and I want to assure you that we are taking it seriously.

You have told us that when you took out your home insurance policy through our branch, you were not made aware of alternative options, and that the policy you were recommended may have been more expensive and less well suited to your needs than other products that were available. You have also told us that you have since learned that we received a higher level of commission from the insurer whose product was recommended to you.

We take our responsibility to act in your best interests very seriously. When we recommend an insurance product, we are required to ensure that our recommendation is consistent with your best interests — and where the remuneration we receive from an insurer could conflict with that duty, we must be able to demonstrate that your interests came first. We are reviewing whether that standard was met in your case.

We understand that the additional cost has placed a real strain on your household budget, and we are sorry if our service fell short of what you should have received.

We are currently investigating your complaint fully and will write to you again with our findings and our final response. We will aim to do so as promptly as possible.

If you remain dissatisfied once you have received our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further details about how to do this when we send our final response.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,
[Complaints Handler Name]
[Bank Name] Complaints Team

Where remuneration received by the firm conflicts with the customer's best interests rule, the firm must take appropriate actions to address the situation, including changing providers where necessary.
