[Note: paragraph 3.7i (box) of CBG] ## CONC 4.5.5 (Guidance) The purpose of CONC 4.5.6R to CONC 4.5.8G is to prohibit credit brokers and lenders to whom they introduce customers wishing to enter into regulated credit agreements to finance the acquisition of motor vehicles from making or relying on arrangements under which credit brokers are given authority to decide or negotiate the prices of those regulated credit agreements on behalf of lenders and the amount of commission the credit brokers earn is affected by those prices. ## CONC 4.5.6 (Rules) A lender or credit broker must not: (1) enter into or have rights or obligations under a discretionary commission arrangement; or (2) seek to exercise, enforce or rely on rights or obligations under a discretionary commission arrangement, including any rights or obligations to receive or tender payment of commission, fee or other financial consideration. ## CONC 4.5.7 (Guidance) The following are examples of discretionary commission arrangements: (1) An agreement under which the lender sets a minimum rate of interest and the commission payable by the lender to the credit broker in respect of a regulated credit agreement entered into by the lender is calculated by reference to the difference between the rate of interest negotiated by the credit broker and payable by the customer under the regulated credit agreement and the minimum rate of interest. These types of arrangements are often referred to as “increasing difference in charges” or “interest rate upward adjustment” arrangements. (2) An agreement under which the lender sets a maximum rate of interest and the commission payable by the lender to the credit broker in respect of a regulated credit agreement entered into by the lender is calculated by reference to the difference between the rate of interest negotiated by the credit broker and payable by the customer under the regulated credit agreement and the maximum rate of interest. These types of arrangements are often referred to as “decreasing difference in charges” or “interest rate downward adjustment” arrangements. (3) An arrangement or agreement under which the commission payable by the lender to the credit broker in respect of a regulated credit agreement entered into by the lender varies (within set parameters) according to the rate of interest negotiated by the credit broker and payable by the customer under the regulated credit agreement.

verdicts.evidence: You have told us that when you took out your home insurance policy through our branch, you were not made aware of alternative options, and that the policy you were recommended may have been more expensive and less well suited to your needs than other products that were available. You have also told us that you have since learned that we received a higher level of commission from the insurer whose product was recommended to you.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately reflects all key elements: lack of alternatives disclosed, more expensive/less suitable policy, and higher commission received by the bank.

The response is labelled as a 'final' response stage, but the customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). Instead it says 'We are currently investigating your complaint fully and will write to you again with our findings and our final response.' No decision or reasons are provided.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied once you have received our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further details about how to do this when we send our final response.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft mentions the right to refer to the FOS and identifies it by name, but defers providing contact details/how to do so to a future final response. Since this is supposed to be the final response stage, the customer is not given the means to contact the FOS (no address, phone, website, or leaflet). This is partial compliance.
The customer draft does not mention the six-month deadline from the date of the final response within which the complainant must refer the complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: We take our responsibility to act in your best interests very seriously. When we recommend an insurance product, we are required to ensure that our recommendation is consistent with your best interests — and where the remuneration we receive from an insurer could conflict with that duty, we must be able to demonstrate that your interests came first. We are reviewing whether that standard was met in your case.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the substance of the complaint in clear, non-dismissive language and acknowledges the customer's financial strain. However, for a final response stage, it fails to actually resolve the complaint — it merely promises further investigation. This treats the complaint superficially at the resolution stage, though the tone and engagement are appropriate.

The customer draft does not address whether redress or remedial action is appropriate. The handler answer explicitly states 'Do not commit to any specific remedy in the customer letter at this stage,' and the customer draft offers no position on redress whatsoever, despite the complaint plainly raising financial loss (higher premium costs).

verdicts.evidence: The customer is a retired pensioner on a fixed income — this is a vulnerability indicator under FCA expectations and must be factored into how the complaint is handled and the tone of any response. [Handler] / We understand that the additional cost has placed a real strain on your household budget, and we are sorry if our service fell short of what you should have received. [Customer draft]
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer explicitly identifies the vulnerability (retired pensioner, fixed income) and instructs it be factored into handling and tone. The customer draft acknowledges the financial strain sympathetically. The handler also recommends routing to a senior handler given vulnerability indicators. Both recognition and action are present.

verdicts.evidence: “Thank you for writing to us to follow up on your complaint… You have told us that when you took out your home insurance policy… you were not made aware of alternative options… we received a higher level of commission…"
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and restates the key facts the complainant raised, satisfying the acknowledgement requirement.
The draft only says the complaint is being investigated and that a final response will follow; no decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) or reasons for a decision are provided.

verdicts.evidence: “If you remain dissatisfied once you have received our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.”
verdicts.reasoning: The right to refer to the FOS is mentioned, but the draft does not give the means (contact details, website, etc.) for doing so, so the element is only partially satisfied.
The draft does not state the six‑month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: “I am sorry that you have had cause to raise this concern… We are currently investigating your complaint fully… We understand that the additional cost has placed a real strain on your household budget…”
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the substance of the complaint in a respectful, non‑dismissive tone, demonstrating fair treatment.
The draft does not address whether any redress or remedial action will be offered, nor does it explain why none would be due.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: “The customer is a retired pensioner on a fixed income — this is a vulnerability indicator … must be factored into how the complaint is handled and the tone of any response.”
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer recognises the complainant’s vulnerability and recommends appropriate handling; the element allows satisfaction by either the handler answer or the customer draft.

claims.reasoning: Provision 2 (icobs6as5) states exactly this: where the firm receives a greater level of remuneration compared to other arrangements available to it, it will need to demonstrate how this selection was consistent with the customer's best interests rule.

claims.reasoning: Provision 2 (icobs6as5) explicitly states that where remuneration conflicts with the duty to comply with the customer's best interests rule, firms will need to take appropriate actions including, where necessary, changing retail premium finance providers.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am 78 years old and have limited mobility due to a hip replacement. In February, my bank switched my longstanding savings account to a new 'Digital Flex Saver' product. This account requires customers to manage everything through a smartphone app and offers no branch or telephone access for transactions. I complained to the bank by phone on Monday morning, and by Wednesday afternoon the adviser called me back saying they'd move me back to my old account type and waive the fees I'd been charged for the failed app transactions. I said that sounded fine and thanked them. However, I never received anything in writing afterwards — no letter, no email, nothing confirming what happened or telling me about the Ombudsman. I'm now worried because I've since realised the new product was completely unsuitable for someone like me who cannot use a smartphone, and I want to understand my rights. I feel the bank shouldn't have moved me onto this product in the first place given my age and circumstances, and I also want to know why I didn't get the written confirmation I believe I'm entitled to after my complaint was resolved.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have come in directly from a customer rather than from a handler — please confirm whether you are the complaints handler or the customer before sending any response. I will treat this as a handler query for now and flag accordingly.

Regulatory position:

1. WRITTEN CONFIRMATION AFTER RESOLUTION: The customer's complaint was resolved verbally by close of business the following business day (Monday complaint, Wednesday resolution call). Under the internal policy (referencing DISP 1.5), a complaint resolved by close of business on the next business day may be handled under the 'next business day rule'. However, the resolution here appears to have taken until Wednesday — two business days after Monday — which means it likely falls outside the next-business-day rule and should have been treated as a standard complaint requiring a written final response. Even if it were within the next-business-day rule, the bank's own procedure requires the outcome to be logged in the CMS.

2. FINAL RESPONSE REQUIREMENTS: Under DISP 1.6.2R (as referenced in the retrieved provisions and the internal policy), a final response must: (a) accept or reject the complaint with reasons, or offer remedial action; (b) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (c) provide the FOS website address; (d) inform the customer they may refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and (e) indicate whether the bank consents to waive the relevant FOS time limits. None of this was provided. The verbal resolution call does not satisfy these requirements.

3. FOS TIME LIMITS: The six-month period for the customer to refer to the FOS is only triggered by a valid final response (DISP 2.8.3G). Because no compliant final response was issued, the six-month clock has not started. The customer therefore retains the right to refer to the FOS, subject to the six-year/three-year longstop limits in DISP 2.8.2R.

4. SUITABILITY / PRODUCT SWITCH CONCERN: The customer raises a substantive concern that the Digital Flex Saver was unsuitable given their age, limited mobility, and inability to use a smartphone. The retrieved provisions include BCOBS 4.1 material on notice requirements for account changes and fixed-term savings, which is partially relevant to whether adequate notice and information was given before the switch. However, the retrieved context does not fully cover the suitability assessment obligations for product switches or the bank's obligations when migrating vulnerable customers to digital-only products. This gap means the substantive suitability complaint cannot be fully addressed from the retrieved provisions alone.

5. VULNERABILITY: The customer is 78 years old with limited mobility following a hip replacement and is unable to use a smartphone. This is a clear indicator of potential vulnerability. Human review is required before any response is sent.

RECOMMENDED ACTION:
- Confirm whether the complaint was logged in the CMS on Monday and whether a complaint reference was issued.
- Confirm the exact dates of receipt and resolution to determine whether the next-business-day rule applies.
- Issue a written final response (or, if the matter is still open, a written holding response) that meets all DISP 1.6.2R requirements, including the FOS leaflet, website address, and time-limit wording.
- Escalate the substantive suitability concern (product switch without regard to the customer's circumstances) to the Head of Customer Operations and consider whether this indicates a systemic issue affecting other vulnerable customers.
- Do not send the draft below without senior/compliance review given the vulnerability indicators and the potential systemic product-switch issue.

ASSUMPTIONS TO CONFIRM: (a) That the resolution call on Wednesday constitutes the bank's intended final position (i.e., move back to old account and waive fees); (b) that no written communication of any kind was sent after the call; (c) the customer's preferred communication channel for the written response given their mobility limitations.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. We are sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced following the switch of your savings account to the Digital Flex Saver, and we want to make sure we put things right properly.

We understand that after you called us on Monday, one of our advisers spoke with you on Wednesday and agreed to move you back to your previous account type and to waive the fees you had been charged for failed app transactions. We are glad that a resolution was reached, and we are sorry that you did not receive anything in writing to confirm this.

We should have sent you a written response setting out the outcome of your complaint, your options, and your rights. We recognise that did not happen, and we apologise for that.

To confirm in writing: we have agreed to [re-open / reinstate] your previous savings account and to refund the fees charged in connection with the Digital Flex Saver app transactions. [Please confirm the specific account and fee details before sending.]

You have also raised a concern that the Digital Flex Saver was not a suitable product for you given your age and circumstances, and that the bank should not have moved you onto it in the first place. We take this concern seriously. We are reviewing this aspect of your complaint and will write to you again with our findings as soon as possible. We will aim to provide you with a full response within [insert timescale], and if we are not able to do so within that time, we will write to explain why and let you know when you can expect to hear from us.

Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service

If you are not satisfied with our response, or if you do not wish to wait for our further response on the suitability concern, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at [FOS website address]. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, which explains how to do this and the time limits that apply.

[Insert time-limit waiver wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R as applicable — this must be confirmed by compliance before sending.]

We are committed to resolving this for you and will be in touch shortly. In the meantime, if you have any questions or would prefer to speak with us by telephone, please do not hesitate to call us on [telephone number].

Yours sincerely,
[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]

cited_provisions.relevance: Sets out the mandatory requirements for a final response, including enclosing the FOS explanatory leaflet, providing the FOS website address, informing the customer of their right to refer to the FOS, and indicating the respondent's position on time-limit waiver — none of which were provided after the verbal resolution call.

Establishes the time limits within which a complainant must refer a complaint to the FOS; because no valid final response was issued, the six-month period has not been triggered and the customer retains their referral rights subject to the longstop limits.

Confirms that the six-month FOS referral time limit is only triggered by a response that qualifies as a final response and that tells the complainant about the time limit — the verbal call does not satisfy this.

Relevant to whether the bank provided adequate notice and information to the customer before switching the account to the Digital Flex Saver, including explaining the consequences of the change and the options available.

retrieved_chunks.content: --- ## 7. Procedures ### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination. All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted. ### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must: 1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected. 2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer. 3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale. ### 7.3 Investigation Process The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include: 1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems. 2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made. 3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure, and referring to the DPO, MLRO, or CTO respectively where applicable. 4. Documenting all investigative steps and evidence reviewed in the CMS. Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target. ### 7.4 Final Response Letters Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must: 1.

: (1) shall apply at, or immediately after, the point when contact is first made with an eligible complainant; and (2) where the respondent is a not-for-profit debt advice body: (a) may be met at, or immediately after, the point when contact is first made with an eligible complainant, by making an oral reference to the availability of the information if the respondent does not communicate with the eligible complainant in writing then; and (b) must be met in writing on the first occasion on which the respondent communicates with the eligible complainant in writing. ## DISP 1.2.2A (Rules) If an MCD credit intermediary has, before or at the point of sale, provided an eligible complainant with appropriate information in a durable medium about their internal procedures for the reasonable and prompt handling of complaints pursuant to another rule, the MCD credit intermediary need not refer to the availability of that information again under DISP 1.2.1R(2)(b). ## DISP 1.2.3 (Guidance) The summary details concerning internal complaints handling procedures should cover at least: (1) how the respondent fulfils its obligation to handle and seek to resolve relevant complaints; and (2) (where the complaint falls within the jurisdiction of the Financial Ombudsman Service) that, if the complaint is not resolved, the complainant may be entitled to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service. ## DISP 1.2.4 (Guidance) Those summary details may be set out in a leaflet, and their availability may be referred to in contractual documentation. ## DISP 1.2.5 (Guidance) Respondents may also display or reproduce the Financial Ombudsman Service logo (under licence) in: (1) branches and sales offices to which eligible complainants have access; or (2) marketing literature or correspondence directed at eligible complainants; provided it is done in a way which is not misleading. ## DISP 1.2.6 (Guidance) Where respondents are required to provide information in relation to the Financial Ombudsman Service, they may also, where relevant, do so in relation to the Pensions Ombudsman on the same basis as set out in DISP 1.2.1(4)R and DISP 1.2.3G. ## DISP 1.2.7 (Guidance) Where respondents are permitted to display or reproduce the Financial Ombudsman Service

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I opened a cash deposit ISA with your bank in March after seeing your online promotion advertising a 4.5% fixed rate for 12 months. The promotion clearly stated '4.5% AER fixed for 12 months' with no mention of any conditions or restrictions. When I transferred £20,000 from my previous ISA provider, I was placed on a 3.8% variable rate instead. I queried this immediately and was told the 4.5% rate was only available for new money, not ISA transfers — but this was never stated in the promotion I responded to. I wrote a formal complaint letter six weeks ago and received an acknowledgement email, but it didn't tell me anything about timescales for a response or what would happen if I remained unhappy. I've heard nothing since. I'm 78 years old and this ISA represents a significant portion of my retirement savings. The difference in interest rate is costing me approximately £140 over the year, and I'm worried about my finances as I rely on interest income to supplement my state pension.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious concerns that require careful handling and mandatory human review before any response is sent.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Misleading financial promotion (COBS 4.7 / BCOBS 2.4.2):** The customer opened a cash deposit ISA in response to an online promotion. BCOBS 2.4.2G indicates that COBS 4.7.1R applies to financial promotions relating to cash deposit ISAs. If the promotion stated '4.5% AER fixed for 12 months' without disclosing that this rate was unavailable for ISA transfers, this is a potentially significant compliance issue with the financial promotion rules. You must urgently retrieve and review the actual promotion the customer saw to assess whether it was fair, clear and not misleading.

2. **Complaints handling failures:** The acknowledgement email sent six weeks ago did not inform the customer of timescales or their right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service. DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in the bank's own procedure at section 7.5) requires that where a final response has not been issued by eight weeks, a written response must explain why and inform the complainant of their FOS referral right. The eight-week deadline falls in approximately two weeks from now (assuming the complaint was received when the letter was sent). You must issue a substantive response — or at minimum a holding response meeting DISP 1.6.2R requirements — before that deadline.

3. **Customer vulnerability:** The customer is 78 years old, relies on interest income to supplement their state pension, and has £20,000 of retirement savings at stake. This is a clear indicator of potential vulnerability. The bank's obligations to deliver good outcomes for customers (PRIN 12, referenced in section 7.6 of the complaints procedure) are engaged. This complaint must be handled with heightened care.

4. **Financial redress:** The customer estimates a loss of approximately £140 in interest over the year. Under the bank's own procedure (section 7.5/7.6), any complaint involving financial redress must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. This applies here.

5. **Root cause analysis:** If the promotion was indeed misleading, this may be a systemic issue affecting other ISA customers who transferred funds in response to the same promotion. A thematic RCA should be flagged to the Head of Customer Operations.

**Recommended immediate actions:**
- Retrieve and review the online promotion the customer responded to.
- Escalate to the Head of Customer Operations given the vulnerability indicators and potential financial redress.
- Issue a holding response within the next two weeks (before the eight-week deadline) if a full final response cannot be completed in time — this holding response must comply with DISP 1.6.2R by explaining the delay and informing the customer of their FOS referral right.
- Do not admit liability or confirm redress in the customer letter at this stage — those decisions must follow the investigation and senior sign-off.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the draft below:**
- The eight-week deadline has not already passed (the customer says the complaint was sent six weeks ago — confirm the exact receipt date in your CMS).
- The draft below is a holding response only, on the assumption the full investigation cannot be completed immediately. If a final response can be issued now, a different letter will be needed.
- The FOS referral right wording below is included because DISP 1.6.2R requires it in an eight-week holding response. Confirm the complaint receipt date so the timing is accurate.
- The customer's vulnerability must be flagged in the CMS and considered in how you communicate with them (e.g. offer a phone call).

The customer draft below is a holding response only. It does not admit liability, does not confirm redress, and does not make findings on the promotion. All of those decisions must follow the investigation and Head of Customer Operations sign-off.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for your formal complaint letter, and I am sorry that you have not heard from us since our initial acknowledgement. I understand how important this matter is to you, and I want to assure you that we are taking it seriously.

Your complaint concerns the interest rate applied to your cash deposit ISA following your transfer of £20,000, and whether the online promotion you responded to accurately described the terms of the rate on offer. We are investigating this carefully.

We have not yet been able to complete our investigation and issue you with a full response. We are sorry for this delay. We aim to provide you with our final response as soon as possible, and we will write to you again once our investigation is complete.

In the meantime, I want to make sure you are aware of your rights. If you remain dissatisfied, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service — a free, independent service for customers. You do not need to wait for our final response before doing so. The Financial Ombudsman Service can be contacted at:

Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
Telephone: 0800 023 4567

I have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet for your information.

If you would find it helpful to discuss your complaint by telephone in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us at [contact details].

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Date]

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I used a price comparison website called QuickLoans Compare to find a short-term loan of £300 for 30 days. The website showed me results but the cheapest option wasn't listed first — instead, a more expensive lender called FastCash was displayed at the top with a prominent 'Recommended' badge. I later discovered FastCash pays the highest commission to QuickLoans Compare. I clicked through and took out the FastCash loan, paying £90 in interest over 30 days, when I've since found that another lender on the same site would have charged me only £60 for the identical loan amount and duration. I complained to QuickLoans Compare three weeks ago about being misled by their biased ranking. They wrote back saying they weren't responsible for my lending decision and forwarded my complaint to FastCash, the lender, without explaining why or giving me FastCash's contact details. I've now heard nothing from either party. I want the £30 difference refunded and a proper explanation of why my complaint was passed on without my knowledge of where it went.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues, both concerning QuickLoans Compare (the price comparison website operator), not FastCash (the lender). The handler should note the following:

**Issue 1 – Biased ranking influenced by commission (CONC 2.5A.6R area)**
The retrieved provisions include CONC 2.5A.8R (a firm must not display a financial promotion other than a search result in or between search results) and CONC 2.5A.9R (results must be clearly distinguishable from other financial promotions). Critically, the retrieved context at conc2s13 contains guidance (CONC 2.5A guidance) stating that commission payments to the firm should not affect the ranking or prominence of display of search results. The 'Recommended' badge placed on FastCash — the highest-commission lender — and its top placement appear to be directly contrary to this principle. However, I must flag that the specific rule reference for the ranking/commission prohibition (likely CONC 2.5A.6R) is referenced in the retrieved chunk only in the context of guidance notes, not as a directly quoted rule text. The principle is clearly stated in the retrieved context but the exact rule number for the ranking obligation is not fully reproduced. I am therefore flagging insufficient_context as true and routing to human review so a compliance officer can confirm the precise rule reference before any regulatory assertion is made to the customer.

**Issue 2 – Complaint handling: forwarding without explanation or contact details**
The retrieved provisions do not include DISP 1 complaint handling rules covering the obligation to acknowledge complaints, explain referrals, or provide contact details when redirecting a complaint. The DISP 1 material retrieved (disp1s23) relates only to complaints data reporting, not to complaint handling obligations. This is a significant gap. The customer's complaint about the handling process (no explanation, no contact details, silence from both parties) cannot be addressed with regulatory precision from the retrieved context alone.

**Recommended action:**
1. Do not send the customer draft without human review — the regulatory basis for the ranking complaint needs confirmation of the exact rule reference, and the complaint handling obligations cannot be cited from the retrieved context.
2. A compliance officer or senior handler should confirm: (a) the exact CONC 2.5A rule that prohibits commission-influenced ranking; (b) the applicable DISP 1 rules on complaint acknowledgement, referral obligations, and time limits; (c) whether the £30 redress claim is within scope for this firm to address or whether it should be directed back to QuickLoans Compare as the regulated firm responsible.
3. The customer draft below is conservative — it acknowledges the complaint, confirms the issues raised, and sets out next steps without making specific regulatory assertions that cannot be fully grounded. It does not promise the £30 refund (which would require a financial decision by a human reviewer) and does not cite rule numbers to the customer.

**Assumption flagged:** The draft assumes this bank/firm is handling the complaint on behalf of or in relation to QuickLoans Compare. If this firm is FastCash or an unrelated party, the draft and regulatory analysis will need to be reconsidered entirely. Please confirm the firm's role before sending.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about the experience you have had, and I want to assure you that we are taking your concerns seriously.

You have raised two concerns, which I will address in turn.

**How your search results were displayed**

You have told us that when you used QuickLoans Compare, the results were not displayed in order of cost, and that a lender paying the highest commission to the website was given a prominent 'Recommended' position at the top of the results. Price comparison websites that display credit products are subject to FCA rules that require search results to be presented fairly and that prohibit commission arrangements from influencing the ranking or prominence given to any lender's products. The concerns you have raised about the way results were displayed and the role of commission payments are therefore serious ones that we are investigating.

**How your complaint was handled**

You have also told us that your complaint was passed to another firm without explanation and without you being given that firm's contact details, and that you have since heard nothing from either party. We recognise this is frustrating and falls short of what you should expect. We are looking into this aspect of your complaint as well.

**What happens next**

We are currently reviewing your complaint in full. We will write to you again with our findings and, where we are able to, set out what we propose to do to put things right. We aim to do this as promptly as possible.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us using the details below.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Contact details]

States that commission payments to the firm should not affect the ranking or prominence of display of search results, directly relevant to the allegation that FastCash's top ranking was driven by its higher commission payments to QuickLoans Compare.

and (2) determine the appropriate redress (if any) to offer to a complainant. ## DISP App 3.1.5 (Guidance) In this appendix: (1) (a) at step 1, “historic interest” means the interest the complainant paid to the firm because a payment protection contract was added to a loan or credit product; (b) at step 2, “historic interest” means in relation to any sum, the interest the complainant paid as a result of that sum being included in the loan or credit product; (2) 'simple interest' means a non-compound rate of 8% per annum; (3) 'claim' means a claim by a complainant seeking to rely upon the policy under the payment protection contract that is the subject of the complaint; (4) “actual profit share” means a reasonable estimate of the profit share that was paid under profit share arrangements and that is notionally attributable to the payment protection contract; (5) “anticipated profit share” means a reasonable estimate of the profit share which it was reasonably foreseeable at the time of sale would be paid over the relevant period or periods under profit share arrangements, and that would be notionally attributable to the payment protection contract; (6) “commission” means the part of the total amount paid in relation to a payment protection contract that was not due to be passed to and retained by the insurer, excluding any sums which may be payable under profit share arrangements; (7) “failure to disclose commission’ means failure to make the disclosure at DISP App 3.3A.2E; (8) “profit share arrangements” means arrangements (including contractual) that firms have to potentially receive back some of the total amount paid in relation to a payment protection contract which had initially gone to the insurer. For example, these arrangements might include amounts paid to cover potential claims on policies, but which remain unspent after a fixed period, for example because actual claims did not exceed certain levels.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with the final response letter I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about being mis-sold a packaged bank account. In your letter, you stated that you did not uphold my complaint because you believe I was given adequate information about the account benefits at the time of sale. However, I was never told about the eligibility criteria for the travel insurance included in the package — I am 78 years old and have a pre-existing heart condition, which means I have never been able to claim on the policy despite paying £15 per month for it since 2016. Your response did not address this point at all, and I do not believe you investigated my complaint properly. You also failed to mention anything about my right to take this further if I remain unhappy. I want to know what my options are now, and I believe I am owed a full refund of all the fees I have paid.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to be the customer's own complaint/escalation letter, not a handler query — please confirm whether you want me to draft a response to send to this customer, or whether you are asking a question about the regulatory position. I am treating this as a request to draft a response to the customer.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **FOS referral rights omission:** The original final response letter dated 15 November appears to have failed to inform the customer of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, failed to enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, and failed to provide the FOS website address. These are binding requirements under DISP 1.6.2R. This is a significant compliance failure and must be remedied immediately.

2. **Packaged bank account / travel insurance eligibility:** ICOBS 5.1.3CR requires firms to provide customers with an annual statement setting out any qualifying requirements to claim benefits under policies in a packaged bank account, and — critically — where a customer has reached an age limit on claiming benefits under a travel insurance policy, the firm must state this clearly and prominently in the statement (ICOBS 5.1.3CR(2)). The customer states they are 78 with a pre-existing heart condition and have never been able to claim. The original response did not address this point at all. This is a substantive gap in the investigation.

3. **Adequacy of investigation:** The customer's core allegation — that they were never told about eligibility criteria, including age limits — was not addressed in the final response. The bank's internal procedure requires the final response to state clearly whether the complaint is upheld, partially upheld, or rejected, with reasons. A response that ignores a material point of complaint does not meet this standard.

4. **Redress/compensation:** The customer is claiming a full refund of fees paid since 2016 (approximately 9 years at £15/month = ~£1,620). This is a financial redress question. I cannot assess or quantify redress from the retrieved provisions alone — this requires a full reinvestigation and a senior review.

**Recommended action:**
- Treat this as a request to reopen/reinvestigate the complaint, given the material point was not addressed.
- The new response must include the FOS referral rights notice, the FOS leaflet, and the FOS website address.
- The complaint should be escalated for reinvestigation, specifically examining: (a) what eligibility information was provided at point of sale; (b) whether annual statements were issued in compliance with ICOBS 5.1.3CR; (c) whether the customer's age limit was flagged as required by ICOBS 5.1.3CR(2).
- **Human review is required** before any response is sent: (i) the customer is 78 with a heart condition — potential vulnerability indicators; (ii) financial redress of potentially ~£1,620 is in scope; (iii) the original final response may have been non-compliant; (iv) the bank's internal policy requires Head of Customer Operations countersignature for complaints with financial redress of £500 or more.

**Assumption flagged:** The draft below treats the new letter as a remedying response that acknowledges the gap and commits to reinvestigation. It does NOT admit liability or promise a refund. If the reinvestigation concludes redress is owed, a separate final response with redress calculation will be needed. The six-month FOS referral clock runs from the date of a compliant final response — the 15 November letter may not have started that clock if it omitted the required FOS notice; confirm with your compliance team.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry that our response dated 15 November did not meet the standard you deserved, and I want to address the concerns you have raised.

**We are reopening your complaint**

Having reviewed your letter, we recognise that our previous response did not address a central part of your complaint: that you were not told about the eligibility criteria for the travel insurance included in your packaged bank account, including any age-related restrictions that may have affected your ability to claim. We apologise for this omission. We are treating your letter as a request to reinvestigate your complaint and will look at this point fully.

As part of our reinvestigation, we will examine:
- What information was provided to you about the eligibility requirements for the travel insurance at the time the account was sold to you;
- Whether we met our obligation to provide you with annual statements setting out the qualifying requirements for each benefit under the policy, and whether any age limit affecting your travel insurance cover was clearly communicated to you; and
- Whether, in light of your circumstances, the account was suitable for you.

We will write to you again with our findings as soon as possible, and in any event within eight weeks of the date we received your original complaint.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

We should have included this information in our letter of 15 November, and we apologise for the oversight. You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), free of charge, if you remain dissatisfied with our response. You can contact the FOS at:

- **Website:** www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- **Telephone:** 0800 023 4567

We have enclosed a copy of the FOS's explanatory leaflet, which sets out further details about the service and the time limits for referring a complaint.

We take your concerns seriously and are committed to investigating them properly. If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the final response to inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, and provide the FOS website address — all of which were omitted from the 15 November letter.

Requires firms to provide packaged bank account customers with an annual statement setting out the qualifying requirements to claim each benefit under the policy, which is directly relevant to the customer's allegation that eligibility criteria were never communicated.

Requires firms to state clearly and prominently in the annual statement where a customer has reached an age limit on claiming benefits under a travel insurance policy included in a packaged bank account — directly relevant given the customer is 78 and alleges they were never informed of age-related restrictions.

The FCA indicates that firms should ensure customers are asked clear questions and are informed of their responsibilities regarding disclosure, including the consequences of misrepresentation — relevant context for assessing what information should have been provided at point of sale.

(1A) Where any policy (except for private health or medical insurance, and pet insurance) included in a packaged bank account renews automatically, the statement must include the information the firm is required to provide under ICOBS 6.2.6R on the right to cancel the automatic renewal element of the policy at any time. (2) Where a customer has reached an age limit on claiming benefits under a travel insurance policy included in a packaged bank account (or will reach an age limit before the next annual statement is due), a firm must state this clearly and prominently in the statement and on an annual basis thereafter. (3) The statement (provided under ICOBS 5.1.3C R (1)) must not: (a) include any information other than that provided in accordance with this rule, ICOBS 6.1.7-AG(2), ICOBS 6A.4.5R(1) and ICOBS 6A.4.7G ; or (b) form part of another document provided to the customer by the firm; or (c) be included in the same mailing as any other document provided to the customer by the firm. ## ICOBS 5.1.4 (Guidance) A firm should bear in mind the restriction on rejecting claims (ICOBS 8.1.1R (3)). Ways of ensuring a customer knows what he must disclose include: (1) explaining to a commercial customer the duty to disclose all circumstances material to a policy, what needs to be disclosed, and the consequences of any failure to make such a disclosure; (2) ensuring that the commercial customer is asked clear questions about any matter material to the insurance undertaking; (3) explaining to the customer the responsibility of consumers to take reasonable care not to make a misrepresentation and the possible consequences if a consumer is careless in answering the insurer's questions, or if a consumer recklessly or deliberately makes a misrepresentation; and (4) asking the customer clear and specific questions about the information relevant to the policy being arranged or varied.

retrieved_chunks.content: The final response must: 1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received. 2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons. 3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated. 4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5). 5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer. All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. ### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS: - **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected. - **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right. All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. Crest Bank will cooperate fully with the FOS in respect of any referred complaints, providing all requested documentation within the timeframes set by the FOS. ### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows: 1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure. 2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes. 3.

The reprojection letter should set out the range of options facing the complainant to deal with the projected shortfall, if any. ## DISP App 1.4.15 (Guidance) Example 9 Example 9 Term extends beyond retirement age: example of failure to explain investment risks Background 45 year old male non-smoker, having taken out a £50,000 loan in 1998 for a term of 25 years. Unsuitable sale identified on the grounds of affordability and complaint raised on 12th anniversary. It has always been the intention of the complainant to retire at state retirement age 65. Term from date of sale to retirement is 20 years and the maturity date of the mortgage is five years after retirement. In addition, an endowment does not meet the complainant's attitude to investment risk and a repayment mortgage would have been taken out if properly advised. Established facts Surrender value (on the 25 year policy) at time complaint assessed: £12,500 Capital repaid under repayment mortgage of term to retirement date (20 years): £21,000 Surrender value less capital repaid: (£8.500) Difference in outgoings (repayment - endowment): £5,400 Cost of converting from endowment mortgage to repayment mortgage: £200 Basis of compensation: The surrender value of the (25 year term) endowment policy is compared to the capital that would have been repaid to date under a repayment mortgage arranged to repay the loan at retirement age, in this example, a repayment mortgage for a term of 20 years. The complainant has gained from lower outgoings of the endowment mortgage to date. In calculating the redress, the gain may be offset against the loss unless the complainant's particular circumstances are such that it would be unreasonable to take account of the gain. The conversion costs are also taken into account in calculating the redress.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry that our response dated 15 November did not meet the standard you deserved, and I want to address the concerns you have raised. ... we recognise that our previous response did not address a central part of your complaint: that you were not told about the eligibility criteria for the travel insurance included in your packaged bank account, including any age-related restrictions that may have affected your ability to claim.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately reflects the subject matter — the mis-sold packaged bank account, the failure to disclose travel insurance eligibility criteria, and the age-related restrictions.

This is labelled as a final response stage, but the customer draft does not state a decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). Instead, it commits to reopening and reinvestigating the complaint. No substantive decision on the complaint's merits is communicated to the customer.

verdicts.evidence: You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), free of charge, if you remain dissatisfied with our response. You can contact the FOS at: Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk Telephone: 0800 023 4567 We have enclosed a copy of the FOS's explanatory leaflet...
verdicts.reasoning: The customer-facing text clearly states the complainant's right to refer to the FOS, provides contact details (website and telephone), and mentions the enclosed explanatory leaflet.

The customer draft mentions the FOS referral right and that the leaflet 'sets out further details about the service and the time limits for referring a complaint,' but it does not explicitly state the six-month deadline from the date of the final response. A vague reference to 'time limits' in the leaflet does not satisfy the requirement to tell the complainant they have six months.

verdicts.evidence: Having reviewed your letter, we recognise that our previous response did not address a central part of your complaint... We apologise for this omission. We are treating your letter as a request to reinvestigate your complaint and will look at this point fully.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the substance of the complaint, acknowledges the firm's own failings in the original response, uses clear and non-dismissive language, and sets out specific steps for reinvestigation. It treats the complainant's concerns seriously and respectfully.

verdicts.evidence: The handler answer discusses: 'The customer is claiming a full refund of fees paid since 2016 (approximately 9 years at £15/month = ~£1,620). This is a financial redress question. I cannot assess or quantify redress from the retrieved provisions alone — this requires a full reinvestigation and a senior review.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer acknowledges the redress question but the customer-facing draft does not communicate any position on redress — it neither offers redress nor explains why none is due at this stage. The complaint plainly raises financial loss, and the customer draft only commits to reinvestigation without addressing the redress claim directly.

verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer explicitly identifies the vulnerability indicators (age 78, heart condition) and flags them for adapted handling (human review required, escalation). The customer draft uses empathetic, clear language appropriate to the complainant. Both elements together demonstrate recognition and action on vulnerability.

claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R requires the final response to enclose the FOS leaflet, provide the website address, and inform the complainant of their referral right.

claims.claim: The complaint should be escalated for reinvestigation, specifically examining: (a) what eligibility information was provided at point of sale; (b) whether annual statements were issued in compliance with ICOBS 5.1.3CR; (c) whether the customer's age limit was flagged as required by ICOBS 5.1.3CR(2).
claims.reasoning: Points (b) and (c) are directly supported by the ICOBS 5.1.3CR provision regarding annual statements and age limit disclosure. Point (a) regarding point of sale information is not directly addressed by the retrieved provisions but is a reasonable investigative step.

claims.claim: The bank's internal policy requires Head of Customer Operations countersignature for complaints with financial redress of £500 or more.
claims.reasoning: The internal policy states: 'All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.'

claims.claim: The six-month FOS referral clock runs from the date of a compliant final response — the 15 November letter may not have started that clock if it omitted the required FOS notice.
claims.reasoning: The internal policy mentions that the customer has six months from the date of the final response to refer to the FOS. However, the specific claim that the clock does not start if the FOS notice was omitted is not explicitly stated in any retrieved provision, though it is a reasonable inference from DISP 1.6.2R's requirements for what constitutes a valid final response.

claims.claim: We will write to you again with our findings as soon as possible, and in any event within eight weeks of the date we received your original complaint.
claims.reasoning: The internal policy states the final response must be issued within eight weeks of the date the complaint was received, and DISP 1.6.2R provides for a written response at eight weeks if a final response cannot yet be issued.

claims.claim: You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), free of charge, if you remain dissatisfied with our response.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R requires the firm to inform the complainant that if they remain dissatisfied they may refer the complaint to the FOS. The 'free of charge' element is standard FOS information though not explicitly stated in the retrieved provision.

claims.claim: The FOS website is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk and telephone is 0800 023 4567.
claims.reasoning: The internal policy explicitly states: 'FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567)'.

claims.claim: We should have included FOS referral information in our letter of 15 November.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R requires the final response to include FOS referral information, the leaflet, and the website address. If the 15 November letter was a final response, this information was required.

claims.claim: We have enclosed a copy of the FOS's explanatory leaflet.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R requires the firm to enclose a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet.

claims.claim: We met our obligation to provide you with annual statements setting out the qualifying requirements for each benefit under the policy, and whether any age limit affecting your travel insurance cover was clearly communicated to you.
claims.reasoning: The customer draft references this as a point of investigation. ICOBS 5.1.3CR explicitly requires annual statements setting out qualifying requirements and clear/prominent disclosure of age limits on travel insurance.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with the final response letter I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about being mis-sold a payment protection insurance policy alongside my personal loan in 2012. Your letter states that you do not uphold my complaint because you believe adequate information was provided at the point of sale. However, I note that your response fails to address the key issue I raised — that I was self-employed at the time and was never told the policy would not cover me for unemployment. You have not explained what evidence you considered or how you reached your decision. I was told verbally by the adviser that the PPI would cover all eventualities. I have now been left £3,200 out of pocket in premiums for a policy that could never have paid out on the unemployment element. I do not accept your decision and wish to know what my options are. I also want to understand whether I can take this further, as your final response letter did not mention anything about the Financial Ombudsman Service or any time limits for escalation.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This is a PPI mis-sale complaint where the customer is challenging the bank's final response letter dated 15 November. There are two immediate regulatory concerns you must address:

1. **Missing FOS referral information (DISP 1.6.2R):** The customer explicitly states the final response letter did not mention the Financial Ombudsman Service or any time limits for escalation. Under DISP 1.6.2R, a final response must: (a) inform the complainant they may refer the complaint to the FOS if dissatisfied; (b) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (c) provide the FOS website address; and (d) include the appropriate wording regarding whether the bank consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R. Your internal policy (section 7.5) also requires the letter to state that the customer has six months from the date of the final response to refer to the FOS. If the 15 November letter genuinely omitted all of this, the final response was non-compliant. You will need to issue a new letter that remedies this omission. **Assumption to confirm:** Please verify the original letter before sending any new response — it is possible the customer overlooked the FOS section, but if it was genuinely absent, a corrective letter is required.

2. **Substance of the mis-sale complaint:** The customer raises a substantive point that the original response did not address: they were self-employed and were never told the unemployment element would not cover them. Under DISP App 3.6.2E (evidential provisions), relevant mis-sale indicators include recommending a policy without taking reasonable steps to establish whether the complainant was eligible to claim, and providing misleading or inaccurate information about the policy. The customer also alleges a verbal misrepresentation by the adviser. The original decision letter apparently did not address this specific ground. This is a substantive complaint that requires proper re-examination of the evidence, including the customer's demands, needs and circumstances at the time of sale (DISP App 3.6.3E).

**Recommended action:**
- Do not simply re-send the original rejection. The complaint needs to be re-examined on its merits, specifically addressing the self-employment/unemployment exclusion point.
- A new response letter must include the full FOS referral wording and leaflet.
- Given the potential for financial redress (the customer cites £3,200 in premiums) and the regulatory compliance failure in the original letter, this must be reviewed by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch, per your internal policy for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more.
- **Do not commit to any specific redress figure in the customer letter** — redress calculation under DISP App 3.7 is complex and must be assessed separately.
- Flag: the PPI deadline of 29 August 2019 (DISP 2.8.9R) is relevant to FOS jurisdiction for sale-related PPI complaints. The customer's complaint was apparently made before that date (the bank issued a final response on 15 November, implying the complaint was received earlier), but you should confirm the exact complaint receipt date and whether the 29 August 2019 deadline was met. The customer's six-month window to refer to the FOS runs from the date of the final response — if the 15 November letter is treated as the final response, that window is running now.

**Human review is required** before this letter is sent: it involves potential financial redress exceeding £500, a possible regulatory compliance failure in the original final response, and a substantive mis-sale allegation that has not been properly addressed.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for your letter responding to our final response dated 15 November. We are sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied, and we take your concerns seriously.

We note that you have raised two matters: first, that our response did not address the specific issue of your self-employment status and the unemployment element of the policy; and second, that our letter did not inform you of your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. We apologise for any distress or confusion this has caused.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

We want to make sure you are aware of your options. If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), free of charge. The FOS is an independent service that resolves disputes between consumers and financial businesses.

You can contact the Financial Ombudsman Service at:
- Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- Telephone: 0800 023 4567

We have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, which sets out further details about the service and the time limit within which you must refer your complaint to them if you wish to do so.

[HANDLER NOTE — INSERT APPROPRIATE DISP 1 ANNEX 3R WORDING HERE REGARDING TIME LIMIT WAIVER CONSENT BEFORE SENDING]

**The substance of your complaint**

You have told us that at the time the policy was sold in 2012, you were self-employed, and that you were not informed that the unemployment element of the policy would not cover you in those circumstances. You have also told us that the adviser verbally represented that the policy would cover all eventualities. We acknowledge that our previous response did not address this specific concern, and we are sorry for that.

We are reviewing your complaint again in light of the points you have raised. We will write to you with our findings as soon as we are able to do so.

We recognise that you have been waiting for a resolution and we are sorry for the inconvenience this has caused.

Yours sincerely,

[Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the final response to inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, and include wording on whether the respondent consents to waive relevant time limits — all of which the customer states were absent from the 15 November letter.

Sets out evidential indicators of mis-sale, including recommending a policy without taking reasonable steps to establish eligibility and providing misleading or inaccurate information — directly relevant to the customer's allegation that they were not told the unemployment element would not cover a self-employed person.

Provides that relevant evidence in assessing a PPI mis-sale complaint includes the complainant's demands, needs and intentions at the time of sale — relevant to the need to re-examine the customer's circumstances, including their self-employment status.

Confirms that DISP Appendix 3 applies to complaints about the sale of a payment protection contract expressing dissatisfaction about the sale, including where claims have been rejected on grounds of ineligibility or exclusion — applicable here as the customer's complaint concerns the unemployment exclusion.

Sets the 29 August 2019 deadline for PPI sale-related complaints to be referred to the FOS; relevant to confirming whether the customer's complaint was received in time and to the FOS referral wording required in the response.

retrieved_chunks.content: The final response must: 1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received. 2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons. 3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated. 4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5). 5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer. All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. ### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS: - **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected. - **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right. All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. Crest Bank will cooperate fully with the FOS in respect of any referred complaints, providing all requested documentation within the timeframes set by the FOS. ### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows: 1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure. 2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes. 3.

For example, if the monthly repayment amount in relation to the loan only is to be £200, the price of the alternative regular premium payment protection contract will be £18. ## DISP App 3.7.14 (Evidential Provisions) Where the firm presumes that the complainant would have purchased a regular premium payment protection contract and if the complainant expressly wishes it, the existing cover should continue until the end of the existing policy term. The complainant should pay the price of the alternative regular premium payment protection contract (at DISP App 3.7.13 E) and should be able to cancel at any time.

metadata.section_name: DISP App 3.7 Approach to redress at step 1

retrieved_chunks.content: # DISP — DISP App 3.1 Introduction ## DISP App 3.1.1 (Guidance) (1) This appendix sets out how: (a) a firm should handle complaints relating to the sale of a payment protection contract by the firm which express dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling); and (b) a firm that is a CCA lender and which has received such a complaint should consider whether there was a failure to disclose commission in relation to the sale of a payment protection contract which covers or covered or purported to cover a credit agreement (this includes partial coverage). (2) It relates to the sale of any payment protection contract whenever the sale took place and irrespective of whether it was on an advised or non-advised basis; conducted through any sales channel; in connection with any type of loan or credit product, or none; whether the insurer was in the same group as the firm or not; whether the premium was financed by the credit product or not; and for a regular premium or single premium payment. It applies whether the policy is currently in force, was cancelled during the policy term or ran its full term. (3) It does not set out how a firm which has received a complaint referred to in (1)(a) should assess: (a) whether the firm’s conduct of the sale was in breach of a fiduciary duty where there has been a failure to disclose either the existence of, or the level of, any commission and/or profit share paid; (b) whether any omission (other than the omission referred to in DISP App 3.3A.2E) to disclose either the existence of, or level of, commission and/or profit share made the relationship unfair under section 140A of the CCA; (c) any other issue not dealt with in step 1 or step 2 set out in this appendix.
