verdicts.evidence: You have told us that you were charged a total of £340 in unarranged overdraft charges between June and September this year, and that you were not notified that your account had gone into an unarranged overdraft before those charges were applied. You are asking for a full refund.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and then accurately reflects both the substance (£340 in charges, lack of notification) and the remedy sought (full refund). It also acknowledges the procedural shortcoming of the previous letter.

The customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) on the substantive complaint about the overdraft charges. It says 'We are reviewing the charges' and 'We will write to you again with our findings.' For a final response, a decision with reasons is required. No decision is communicated.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact the FOS at: Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk Telephone: 0800 023 4567 We have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet
verdicts.reasoning: The customer-facing text clearly states the complainant's right to refer to the FOS, provides the website and telephone number, and mentions the enclosed leaflet. This satisfies the element.

The customer draft does not state the six-month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS. It contains a placeholder '[HANDLER TO INSERT: DISP 1 Annex 3R prescribed wording on time limits and waiver consent, once confirmed by compliance.]' but the actual six-month time limit is not communicated to the customer. A placeholder is not sufficient to satisfy this requirement.

verdicts.evidence: We take these concerns seriously. We are reviewing the charges applied to your account and the notifications, if any, that were sent to you during the relevant period.
verdicts.reasoning: The response is respectful, acknowledges the procedural failure, and engages with the customer's concerns in a non-dismissive tone. However, it does not actually address the substance of the complaint on its merits — it defers the decision entirely. For a final response stage, this represents only partial engagement with the complaint's substance, though the tone and acknowledgement of the procedural shortcoming are appropriate.

The customer draft does not address whether redress is appropriate. It neither offers redress nor explains why none is due. The handler answer explicitly states that the draft 'does not uphold or reject the substantive complaint or offer redress,' and the customer-facing text only says the matter is under review. No position on redress is communicated to the customer.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied and that our letter of 15 November did not give you the information you needed about your options.
verdicts.reasoning: The opening paragraph directly acknowledges the complainant's dissatisfaction and references the specific issue (missing information in the 15 November letter), satisfying the acknowledgement requirement.
The customer‑facing draft does not state whether the complaint is upheld, partially upheld, or rejected, nor does it provide any substantive reasons for a decision.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact the FOS at: – Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk – Telephone: 0800 023 4567
verdicts.reasoning: The draft clearly informs the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS and provides the necessary contact details and mention of the explanatory leaflet.

verdicts.evidence: We have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet, which sets out ... the time limits that apply to referring a complaint.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft references that time limits are set out in the leaflet but does not explicitly state the six‑month period or anchor it to the date of this final response.

verdicts.evidence: We take these concerns seriously. We are reviewing the charges applied to your account and the notifications, if any, that were sent to you during the relevant period. We will write to you again with our findings.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is courteous, acknowledges the complainant's concerns, and promises a further review, demonstrating a fair and non‑dismissive approach.

verdicts.evidence: You are asking for a full refund. We take these concerns seriously. We are reviewing the charges applied to your account ... We will write to you again with our findings.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft acknowledges the request for a refund and indicates a review will take place, but it does not provide a decision or explanation regarding whether redress will be offered.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with the final response I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about unauthorised transactions on my current account. Your letter rejected my complaint, stating that I had failed to keep my PIN secure. However, I believe this conclusion is unfair as I have never shared my PIN with anyone and I reported the transactions within hours of them occurring. Your final response letter did not include a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, nor did it provide their website address. It also failed to tell me whether you consent to waive any time limits if I refer my complaint to the Ombudsman. I feel I have been treated unfairly and I want to know what my options are now. I am a pensioner on a fixed income and the loss of £1,200 from these transactions has left me unable to pay my heating bills this winter.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have been submitted by the customer directly, but I am treating it as the handler presenting the customer's complaint for a response to be drafted.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Deficient final response letter:** The final response dated 15 November was materially deficient. Under DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in the retrieved provisions), a final response must: (a) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (b) provide the FOS website address; (c) inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied; and (d) indicate whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R, using the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R. None of these requirements were met. A remedying letter must be sent as a matter of urgency.

2. **Six-month FOS referral time limit:** Under DISP 2.8.2R, the six-month period for the customer to refer to the FOS runs from the date of the final response. However, per DISP 2.8.3G, the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that is a valid final response — and a response that omits the required FOS information may not have triggered the clock. You should seek legal/compliance input on whether the 15 November letter constitutes a valid final response for time-limit purposes. In the meantime, the remedying letter should include the waiver position clearly.

3. **Substance of the complaint — unauthorised transactions:** The customer denies sharing their PIN and reported promptly. Under BCOBS 5.1.12R, a firm may only hold a customer liable for losses on unauthorised payments in limited circumstances (fraud, or intentional/gross negligence failure to keep security features safe). The firm must not seek to make the customer liable beyond those circumstances. The retrieved provisions do not give me enough to assess whether the bank's rejection on the PIN-security ground was substantively correct — that requires a review of the evidence. This must be reviewed by the handler and, given the financial amount (£1,200), by the Head of Customer Operations before any final position is confirmed.

4. **Vulnerability:** The customer has identified themselves as a pensioner on a fixed income who cannot pay heating bills as a result of this loss. This is a clear indicator of financial vulnerability and potential hardship. This must be flagged and factored into how the complaint is handled and prioritised.

5. **Redress threshold:** Crest Bank's internal policy requires all final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more to be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. At £1,200, this applies here.

**Recommended action:**
- Do not send the customer draft below without Head of Customer Operations sign-off.
- Compliance/legal should confirm whether the 15 November letter triggered the six-month FOS clock.
- The substantive rejection on PIN-security grounds must be reviewed against the evidence before the position is confirmed or maintained in a new final response.
- The customer draft below acknowledges the procedural failures, informs the customer of their FOS rights, and explains the bank will review the matter — it does NOT confirm or reverse the rejection, as that requires the substantive review above.
- **Assumption:** The draft treats the new letter as a remedying communication that also serves as a fresh final response only if the handler confirms the substantive position is being maintained after review. If the position changes (i.e., the complaint is upheld), the letter will need to be rewritten to reflect that outcome and include redress details. Flag this clearly before sending.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced, and I want to address both the concerns you have raised about our letter of 15 November and your underlying complaint about the unauthorised transactions on your account.

**Our letter of 15 November**

Having reviewed our final response letter, I must apologise unreservedly. That letter did not include a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, did not provide their website address, and did not tell you whether we consent to waive any relevant time limits should you wish to refer your complaint to the Ombudsman. These are things we are required to include, and we fell short of that standard. I am sorry.

We are enclosing the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet with this letter. Their website address is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. [HANDLER TO INSERT: confirm here whether Crest Bank consents or does not consent to waive the time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R, using the prescribed wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R.]

**Your complaint about the unauthorised transactions**

We take your complaint very seriously. You have told us that you did not share your PIN with anyone and that you reported the transactions within hours of them occurring. We understand that the loss of £1,200 has caused you real hardship, and we are sorry for the distress this has caused you.

We are reviewing the substance of our decision in light of the concerns you have raised. We will write to you again with our findings. [HANDLER TO INSERT: if the substantive position is being maintained, set out the reasons clearly and in plain English. If the complaint is being upheld or partially upheld, set out the remedy offered and how it has been calculated, and remove this placeholder.]

**Next steps**

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us at [contact details]. We are committed to resolving this matter as quickly as possible given the circumstances you have described.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Sets out the mandatory contents of a final response, including enclosing the FOS explanatory leaflet, providing the FOS website address, informing the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and indicating whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits — all of which were absent from the 15 November letter.

Establishes the six-month time limit within which a complainant must refer a complaint to the FOS after receiving a final response, and which the final response must address in terms of the respondent's waiver position.

Indicates that the six-month time limit is only triggered by a valid final response that tells the complainant about the time limit — relevant to whether the deficient 15 November letter started the clock running.

Sets out the limited circumstances in which a firm may hold a banking customer liable for losses on unauthorised payments (fraud or intentional/gross negligent failure to keep security features safe), which is directly relevant to the bank's rejection on PIN-security grounds.

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

retrieved_chunks.content: The final response must: 1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received. 2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons. 3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated. 4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5). 5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer. All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. ### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS: - **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected. - **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right. All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. Crest Bank will cooperate fully with the FOS in respect of any referred complaints, providing all requested documentation within the timeframes set by the FOS. ### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows: 1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure. 2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes. 3.

put the complainant back into the position the complainant should have been in had the act or omission not occurred. (4) If a respondent reports on the amount of redress paid under DISP 1.10B.7D(2)(b) the redress should not include the amount of a non-executed, defective or unauthorised payment transaction but should include any redress paid as a result of losses incurred by the complainant as a result of the non-executed, defective or unauthorised payment transaction. ## DISP 1.10B.9 (Directions) The relevant reporting period is the year immediately following: (1) where the respondent has an accounting reference date, its accounting reference date; and (2) where the respondent does not have an accounting reference date, 31 December each year. ## DISP 1.10B.10 (Directions) Reports are to be submitted to the FCA within 30 business days of the end of the relevant reporting periods through, and in the electronic format specified in, the FCA complaints reporting system or the appropriate section of the FCA website. ## DISP 1.10B.11 (Directions) If a respondent is unable to submit a report in electronic format because of a systems failure of any kind, the respondent must notify the FCA, in writing and without delay, of that systems failure. ## DISP 1.10B.12 (Rules) (1) If a respondent does not submit a complete report by the date on which it is due, in accordance with DISP 1.10B.10D, the respondent must pay the administrative fee specified in SUP 16.3.14R.(2) The administrative fee in (1) does not apply if the respondent has notified the FCA of a systems failure in accordance with DISP 1.10B.11R. ## DISP 1.10B.13 (Directions) A closed complaint is a complaint where: (1) the respondent has sent a final response; or (2) the complainant has indicated in writing acceptance of the respondent’s earlier response under DISP 1.6.4R (where applicable). ## DISP 1.10B.14 (Guidance) (1) To improve consumer awareness and to help respondents compare their performance against their peers, the FCA may publish: (a) complaints data about the payment services and electronic money sector as a whole; and (b) respondent level complaints data and information giving context to the complaints

(1A) Paragraph (1) does not apply where: (a) the loss, theft or misappropriation of the payment instrument was not detectable by the banking customer prior to the payment; or (b) the loss was caused by acts or omissions of an employee or branch of the firm or of an entity which carried out activities on behalf of the firm. (2) A firm may, in an agreement for a retail banking service, provide for a banking customer to be liable for all losses in respect of unauthorised payments: (a) where a banking customer has acted fraudulently; or (b) (subject to (3)) where a banking customer has intentionally, or with gross negligence, failed to comply with his or her obligations under the agreement for the retail banking service in relation to the issue or use of the payment instrument or to take all reasonable steps to keep its personalised security features safe. (3) Except where a banking customer has acted fraudulently, a firm must not, in an agreement for a retail banking service, seek to make a banking customer liable for any losses in respect of unauthorised payments where: (a) the unauthorised payment arises after the banking customer has notified the firm of the loss, theft, misappropriation or unauthorised use of the payment instrument; (b) the firm has failed to ensure that appropriate means are available at all times to enable the banking customer to notify it of the loss, theft, misappropriation or unauthorised use of a payment instrument; or (c) the payment instrument has been used in connection with (i) a distance contract; or (ii) a distance selling contract other than an excepted contract. (4) Except as provided in (1) to (3), a firm must not, in an agreement for a retail banking service, seek to make a banking customer liable for any consequential loss in respect of an unauthorised payment. ## BCOBS 5.1.13 (Rules) (1) The reference date used by a firm for the purpose of calculating interest on funds credited to an account of a banking customer held with it must be

or (2) the subject matter of the complaint has been dealt with, or is being dealt with, by a comparable ADR entity; or (3) the subject matter of the complaint has been the subject of court proceedings where there has been a decision on the merits; or (4) the subject matter of the complaint is the subject of current court proceedings, unless proceedings are stayed or sisted (by agreement of all parties, or order of the court) so that the matter may be considered by the Financial Ombudsman Service; or (5) dealing with such a type of complaint would otherwise seriously impair the effective operation of the Financial Ombudsman Service. ## DISP 3.3.4B (Guidance) Examples of a type of complaint that would otherwise seriously impair the effective operation of the Financial Ombudsman Service may include: (1) where it would be more suitable for the complaint to be dealt with by a court or a comparable ADR entity; or (2) where the subject matter of the complaint has already been dealt with by a comparable dispute resolution scheme; or (3) where the subject matter of the complaint has previously been considered or excluded under the Financial Ombudsman Service (unless material new evidence which the Ombudsman considers likely to affect the outcome has subsequently become available to the complainant); or (4) it is a complaint which: (a) involves (or might involve) more than one eligible complainant; and (b) has been referred without the consent of the other eligible complainant or complainants, and the Ombudsman considers that it would be inappropriate to deal with the complaint without that consent.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced, and I want to address both the concerns you have raised about our letter of 15 November and your underlying complaint about the unauthorised transactions on your account.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately identifies both aspects: the deficient final response letter and the underlying complaint about unauthorised transactions.

The customer draft does not state a decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). It says 'We are reviewing the substance of our decision' and includes a placeholder for the handler to insert the position later. No decision is communicated to the customer, and no reasons engaging with the substantive points are provided. For a final response, this is required.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. ... We are enclosing the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet with this letter. Their website address is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly states the complainant's right to refer to the FOS, provides the website address, and mentions enclosing the explanatory leaflet. This satisfies the requirement.

The customer draft does not state that the complainant has six months from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. The placeholder about waiving time limits in DISP 2.8.2R/2.8.7R is directed at the handler and does not communicate the six-month deadline to the customer.

verdicts.evidence: We take your complaint very seriously. You have told us that you did not share your PIN with anyone and that you reported the transactions within hours of them occurring. We understand that the loss of £1,200 has caused you real hardship, and we are sorry for the distress this has caused you.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the customer's concerns in a sympathetic and non-dismissive manner, acknowledges the procedural failures, and reflects the customer's specific points. However, it does not actually resolve the complaint on its merits — it defers the substantive decision. While the tone is fair and empathetic, the lack of a substantive engagement with the decision means it only partially demonstrates fair treatment of the complaint.

The customer draft does not communicate any position on redress to the customer. It states the bank is 'reviewing the substance of our decision' but does not offer redress, explain why none is due, or reach any conclusion. The handler answer discusses the need for review but this is not reflected in a customer-facing outcome. The complaint plainly raises a £1,200 financial loss.

verdicts.evidence: Handler: 'The customer has identified themselves as a pensioner on a fixed income who cannot pay heating bills as a result of this loss. This is a clear indicator of financial vulnerability and potential hardship. This must be flagged and factored into how the complaint is handled and prioritised.' Customer draft: 'We understand that the loss of £1,200 has caused you real hardship, and we are sorry for the distress this has caused you... We are committed to resolving this matter as quickly as possible given the circumstances you have described.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer explicitly identifies the vulnerability indicators (pensioner, fixed income, inability to pay heating bills) and directs that it be factored into handling and prioritisation. The customer draft acknowledges the hardship and commits to resolving quickly given the circumstances. Both recognition and action are present.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us... address both the concerns you have raised about our letter of 15 November and your underlying complaint about the unauthorised transactions on your account.
verdicts.reasoning: The opening paragraph of the customer draft explicitly acknowledges the complaint and correctly identifies the unauthorised transaction issue.
The draft states the firm is reviewing the decision and will write again, but it does not provide a clear decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor reasons for that decision.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing text clearly informs the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS and provides the website address.
The draft mentions the right to refer but does not state the six‑month deadline from the date of this final response.

verdicts.evidence: I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced... We are reviewing the substance of our decision... We understand that the loss of £1,200 has caused you real hardship.
verdicts.reasoning: The tone is apologetic, acknowledges hardship, and promises a review, showing an even‑handed and non‑dismissive approach.
The draft does not address whether any redress will be offered or explain why none is due, despite the complainant citing a £1,200 loss.

verdicts.evidence: Handler answer: "Vulnerability: ... pensioner on a fixed income ... This is a clear indicator of financial vulnerability and potential hardship. This must be flagged and factored into how the complaint is handled and prioritised."
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer recognises the complainant's vulnerability and instructs that it be flagged and considered, satisfying the 'either' audience requirement.

claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision from DISP 1.6 explicitly states the final response must enclose 'a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet'.

claims.claim: Under DISP 1.6.2R, a final response must provide the FOS website address.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision states the final response must 'provides the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service'.

claims.claim: Under DISP 1.6.2R, a final response must inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if dissatisfied.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision states the final response must 'informs the complainant that if he remains dissatisfied with the respondent's response, he may now refer his complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service'.

claims.claim: Under DISP 1.6.2R, a final response must indicate whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R, using the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision explicitly states the final response must 'indicates whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R (Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?) by including the appropriate wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3R'.

claims.claim: Under DISP 2.8.2R, the six-month period for the customer to refer to the FOS runs from the date of the final response.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision states the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint referred 'more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response'.

claims.claim: Per DISP 2.8.3G, the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that is a valid final response.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision at DISP 2.8.3 states: 'The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication.'

claims.claim: A response that omits the required FOS information may not have triggered the six-month clock.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.3G states the six-month time limit is only triggered by a final response and that 'The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit'. This supports the inference that a deficient response may not trigger the clock, but the provision does not explicitly state that omitting FOS information renders the response invalid for time-limit purposes.

claims.claim: Under BCOBS 5.1.12R, a firm may only hold a customer liable for losses on unauthorised payments in limited circumstances (fraud, or intentional/gross negligence failure to keep security features safe).
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision from BCOBS 5.1 sets out that a firm may provide for a banking customer to be liable for all losses in respect of unauthorised payments only where the customer has acted fraudulently or has intentionally or with gross negligence failed to keep personalised security features safe. The claim accurately summarises this, though it references BCOBS 5.1.12R rather than the exact paragraph numbering visible in the chunk — the substance is directly supported.

claims.claim: The firm must not seek to make the customer liable beyond those circumstances (fraud or intentional/gross negligence).
claims.reasoning: The provision states 'Except as provided in (1) to (3), a firm must not, in an agreement for a retail banking service, seek to make a banking customer liable for any consequential loss in respect of an unauthorised payment' and subsection (3) further restricts liability in certain scenarios.

claims.claim: Crest Bank's internal policy requires all final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more to be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved internal policy provision states: 'All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.'

claims.claim: The final response letter of 15 November did not include a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, did not provide their website address, and did not tell the customer whether the firm consents to waive any relevant time limits.
claims.reasoning: The claim describes factual deficiencies in the firm's own letter. The regulatory requirements that these items must be included are grounded in DISP 1.6.2R (retrieved), but the factual assertion that the letter omitted them is based on the complaint facts, not the provisions themselves. The regulatory standard against which the deficiency is measured is grounded.

claims.claim: These are things we are required to include (the FOS leaflet, website address, and time limit waiver position).
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R as retrieved explicitly requires all three elements in a final response.

claims.claim: The customer has the right to refer their complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge, if they remain dissatisfied.
claims.reasoning: The right to refer is supported by DISP 1.6.2R which requires the firm to inform the complainant they 'may now refer his complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service'. The 'free of charge' element is not explicitly stated in the retrieved provisions, though it is a well-known feature of the FOS. Based strictly on retrieved text, this is partially grounded.

claims.claim: The FOS website address is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.
claims.reasoning: The internal policy provision states: 'must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk)'.

claims.claim: The customer has six months from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.2R states the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint referred 'more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response'.

fixed-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with your bank's handling of my complaint about unauthorised overdraft charges totalling £487 applied to my current account in March 2024. I originally submitted my complaint on 15 April 2024. I received your final response letter dated 28 May 2024 which rejected my complaint, stating the charges were correctly applied under the account terms and conditions. However, I believe your response was inadequate and unfair. You failed to properly consider that I was in hospital at the time the charges accrued and had no ability to manage my account. Furthermore, I note that your final response letter did not include a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet, nor did it provide their website address. It simply said I could 'contact the ombudsman if unhappy' without any further detail about the time limits for doing so. I want to know what my rights are and whether I can still escalate this matter, as it has now been approximately five months since I received your response.
fixed-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have been submitted by the customer directly, not by a handler. I am treating it as a complaint escalation for the handler's attention and drafting accordingly.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Defective final response:** The customer's allegation that the final response letter of 28 May 2024 did not include the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, the FOS website address, or adequate information about time limits is a serious concern. DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in the retrieved provisions) requires a final response to enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant they may refer to the FOS if dissatisfied, and indicate whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R. If the 28 May 2024 letter did not include these elements, it was non-compliant with DISP 1.6.2R.

2. **Time limit for FOS referral:** Under DISP 2.8.2R, the FOS cannot consider a complaint referred more than six months after the date of the final response. The final response was dated 28 May 2024 and the customer states approximately five months have elapsed. The six-month deadline is therefore imminent — likely around 28 November 2024. **This is urgent and must be confirmed immediately.** If the deadline has already passed or is about to pass, this requires escalation.

3. **Defective final response and time limit waiver:** Because the original final response appears to have been non-compliant (missing the FOS leaflet, website, and time limit information), the handler should consider whether the bank should consent to waive the relevant time limits under DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R, as required to be indicated in the final response under DISP 1.6.2R. A remedying response should be issued promptly.

4. **Substance of the complaint — unarranged overdraft charges:** The charges of £487 relate to unarranged overdraft charges. CONC 5C.3.1R and CONC 5C.3.2R are relevant: if the unarranged overdraft charges exceeded what would have been payable under the arranged overdraft facility (the price cap rule), the obligation to pay is unenforceable and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid. The handler must check whether the charges comply with CONC 5C.3.1R. Additionally, BCOBS 8.4.13R requires the bank to have sent an alert when the account entered unarranged overdraft, informing the customer of the reason, potential charges, and the time available to take action. The handler must verify whether such alerts were sent in March 2024.

5. **Vulnerability:** The customer states they were hospitalised when the charges accrued. This is a potential vulnerability indicator and must be considered in the review of the complaint. This alone warrants human review.

6. **Crest Bank internal policy:** The internal policy requires final responses to be issued within eight weeks and to include FOS referral rights per DISP 1.6.2R. The policy also requires financial redress of £500 or more (this is £487, just below) to be countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations — however, given the regulatory significance of the defective final response and vulnerability indicators, senior review is strongly recommended regardless.

**Recommended actions:**
1. Urgently confirm the exact date the final response was sent and calculate whether the six-month FOS referral window has expired or is imminent.
2. Retrieve the original final response letter and confirm whether it included the FOS leaflet, website address, and time limit wording as required by DISP 1.6.2R.
3. If the final response was defective, issue a remedying letter immediately that includes all required DISP 1.6.2R elements and consider consenting to waive the time limits.
4. Review whether the unarranged overdraft charges comply with CONC 5C.3.1R (price cap) and whether BCOBS 8.4.13R alerts were sent.
5. Consider the customer's hospitalisation as a vulnerability factor in reassessing the complaint.
6. This complaint must be reviewed by a senior handler before any response is sent.

**Assumptions to confirm:** (a) The exact date the final response was sent (to calculate the FOS deadline precisely). (b) The content of the original final response letter. (c) Whether BCOBS 8.4.13R alerts were sent in March 2024. (d) Whether the charges comply with CONC 5C.3.1R. (e) Evidence of the customer's hospitalisation.
fixed-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied with our handling of your complaint about the unarranged overdraft charges applied to your account in March 2024, and we are sorry to learn of the difficult personal circumstances you experienced at that time.

We take your concerns seriously and we want to address them properly.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

We have reviewed the final response letter we sent you on 28 May 2024. You have raised a concern that it did not include full details of your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS). We apologise for any omission in that letter. To ensure you have the information you need:

If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at:
- **Website:** www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- **Telephone:** 0800 023 4567

We have enclosed a copy of the FOS's standard explanatory leaflet with this letter.

Please be aware that there is a time limit for referring complaints to the FOS. Ordinarily, you have six months from the date of our final response to make a referral. As our final response was dated 28 May 2024, we strongly encourage you to contact the FOS promptly if you wish to pursue this route, as that period may be close to expiring. [HANDLER NOTE: Please confirm the precise deadline and insert confirmed wording here before sending.]

**Our review of your complaint**

We are also taking note of the additional information you have provided, including that you were in hospital when the charges accrued and were unable to manage your account at that time. We want to ensure your complaint has been considered fully and fairly in light of all relevant circumstances, and we are reviewing the matter again.

We will write to you again with the outcome of that review. If we are not in a position to provide a full response within eight weeks of your original complaint date, we will write to explain the reason for any delay.

We are sorry for the distress this situation has caused you and we are committed to resolving it as quickly as possible.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires a final response to enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and indicate whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits — elements the customer alleges were absent from the 28 May 2024 letter.
Sets the six-month time limit from the date of the final response within which the complainant must refer their complaint to the FOS, making the imminent deadline a matter of urgency in this case.

Establishes that the FOS can only consider a complaint once a final response has been issued or eight weeks have elapsed, confirming the final response of 28 May 2024 is the relevant trigger for the FOS referral window.

Prohibits firms from charging a customer using an unarranged overdraft more than they would have paid under an equivalent arranged overdraft facility, which must be verified in respect of the £487 charges.
Provides that if an unarranged overdraft charge is imposed in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R, the obligation to pay is unenforceable and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid.

Requires the firm to send an alert when a personal current account enters unarranged overdraft, informing the customer of the reason, potential charges, and the time available to take action — relevant to whether the bank met its obligations in March 2024.

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

retrieved_chunks.content: The final response must: 1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received. 2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons. 3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated. 4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5). 5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer. All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. ### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS: - **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected. - **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right. All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. Crest Bank will cooperate fully with the FOS in respect of any referred complaints, providing all requested documentation within the timeframes set by the FOS. ### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows: 1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure. 2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes. 3.

than those the firm is aware of. (2) The alert must communicate to the banking customer in plain simple language: (a) the reason why the alert has been sent; (b) that the banking customer will or may incur charges if they become or remain overdrawn, if this is the case. ## BCOBS 8.4.13 (Rules) (1) A firm must send an alert to a banking customer if the firm: (a) knows based on information available to it that the banking customer’s personal current account has entered unarranged overdraft; (b) is reasonably able to determine that, taking into account information it has access to on transactions due to be settled, the banking customer’s personal current account will enter unarranged overdraft in the absence of: (i) action by the banking customer; or (ii) a transaction other than those the firm is aware of. (2) The alert must communicate to the banking customer in plain simple language: (a) the reason why the alert has been sent; (b) that the banking customer will or may incur charges if they enter or remain in unarranged overdraft, if this is the case; and (c) that the banking customer has a period of time during which they have an opportunity to take action to avoid or reduce charges, and specify: (i) the actions which may be taken; and (ii) the time by which the customer must take such action to reduce or avoid the charge or charges. ## BCOBS 8.4.14 (Guidance) (1) The period communicated under BCOBS 8.2.13R(2)(c) should give the banking customer as good an opportunity to take action to avoid or reduce charges as possible, having regard to: (a) the time when the alert is required to be sent; (b) the terms and conditions applicable to the personal current account; and (c) the firm’s obligations under: (i) the Payment Services Regulations; and (ii) BCOBS 5.1.1R. (2) Firms should keep in mind regulation 89 of the Payment Services Regulations (Value date and availability of funds).

retrieved_chunks.content: (d) The relevant rate of interest for the purposes of (c) is: (i) where there is only one rate of interest that applies to arranged overdrafts connected to the comparable account, that rate; or (ii) where there are two or more rates of interest that apply to arranged overdrafts connected to the comparable account, the highest of those rates that is imposed on a not insignificant number of the customers to whom the account has been made available. ## CONC 5C.3.2 (Rules) If a firm imposes an unarranged overdraft charge in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b), the obligation to pay the charge is unenforceable against the customer and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid by, or on behalf of, the customer under the obligation imposed. ## CONC 5C.3.3 (Guidance) (1) The purpose of CONC 5C.3.1R is to forbid firms from charging a customer who borrows a particular amount using an unarranged overdraft facility more than they would have had to pay (disregarding any fee-free amount) if they had borrowed an equivalent amount using their arranged overdraft facility (or, if they do not have an arranged overdraft facility, the highest amount that would have been payable (disregarding any fee-free amount) by a not insignificant number of other customers if they had borrowed an equivalent amount under an arranged overdraft facility connected with a comparable personal current account). (2) In CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b), CONC 5C.3.1R(3)(d)(ii) and CONC 5C.3.2R, “impose” an unarranged overdraft charge includes creating the contractual right to receive it, and relying on, or enforcing, the contractual right or purporting to do so (“imposes” and “imposed” should be read accordingly). (3) CONC 5C.3.1R does not affect an unarranged overdraft charge, liability for which accrued before the date on which CONC 5C.3.1R came into force.

retrieved_chunks.content: # DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time? ## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules) The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if: (1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or (2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or (2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint: (a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or (b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or (3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme: (a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or (b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme; unless: (4) the respondent consents and: (a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and (b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint. ## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules) Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint. ## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules) The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service: (1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or (2) more than: (a) six years

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied with our handling of your complaint about the unarranged overdraft charges applied to your account in March 2024, and we are sorry to learn of the difficult personal circumstances you experienced at that time.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately reflects its subject matter — the unarranged overdraft charges in March 2024 and the customer's dissatisfaction with the handling, including their personal circumstances.

The customer draft does not state a decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). Instead, it says 'we are reviewing the matter again' and 'We will write to you again with the outcome of that review.' No decision is communicated, and no reasons engaging with the substantive points are provided. Despite being labelled as a 'final' response stage, the draft does not deliver a final decision.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at: Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk Telephone: 0800 023 4567 We have enclosed a copy of the FOS's standard explanatory leaflet with this letter.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly states the complainant's right to refer to the FOS, provides contact details (website and telephone), and mentions enclosing the standard explanatory leaflet.

verdicts.evidence: Please be aware that there is a time limit for referring complaints to the FOS. Ordinarily, you have six months from the date of our final response to make a referral. As our final response was dated 28 May 2024, we strongly encourage you to contact the FOS promptly if you wish to pursue this route, as that period may be close to expiring.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft mentions the six-month time limit and anchors it to the date of the final response. However, it references the previous final response date (28 May 2024) rather than stating the six months runs from the date of THIS final response (the current letter). The element requires the final response to tell the complainant they have six months from the date of 'this final response.' The draft is somewhat ambiguous — it references the original final response date rather than clearly starting a new six-month clock from the current letter. This is partial because it communicates the concept but does not clearly anchor it to the current final response date.

verdicts.evidence: We want to ensure your complaint has been considered fully and fairly in light of all relevant circumstances, and we are reviewing the matter again.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the customer's concerns in a sympathetic and non-dismissive tone, acknowledges the deficiency in the original final response, and notes the hospitalisation. However, it does not actually address the substance of the complaint on its merits — it defers everything to a future review. While the tone is appropriate and respectful, the lack of substantive engagement with the complaint points (whether charges were fair, whether alerts were sent, whether hospitalisation should have been considered) means it only partially demonstrates fair treatment.

The customer draft does not address whether redress or remedial action is appropriate. It does not offer any redress, nor does it explain why none is due. It merely states the matter is being reviewed again. The handler answer discusses the possibility of redress considerations but this is not reflected in any position communicated to the customer.

verdicts.evidence: Handler: 'The customer states they were hospitalised when the charges accrued. This is a potential vulnerability indicator and must be considered in the review of the complaint. This alone warrants human review.' Customer draft: 'we are sorry to learn of the difficult personal circumstances you experienced at that time' and 'We are also taking note of the additional information you have provided, including that you were in hospital when the charges accrued and were unable to manage your account at that time.'
verdicts.reasoning: The vulnerability is identified in the handler answer as requiring specific consideration and action (human review, reassessment). The customer draft acknowledges the hospitalisation with supportive language and commits to reviewing the complaint in light of those circumstances. Both identification and action are present.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry to hear that you remain dissatisfied with our handling of your complaint about the unarranged overdraft charges applied to your account in March 2024...

The customer draft does not state a firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) nor provide reasons for a decision; it only says the matter will be reviewed further.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at: Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk Telephone: 0800 023 4567. We have enclosed a copy of the FOS's standard explanatory leaflet with this letter.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I took out a personal loan with your bank in March 2024. At no point during the application process or at the point of sale was I given any information about your complaints procedure or told how I could complain if something went wrong. I've since had a problem with an overcharge on my account and when I tried to find out how to complain, there was nothing on my loan agreement, nothing in the paperwork I was given, and I had to spend over an hour on the phone just to find out where to send my complaint. I also notice your website has no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service anywhere. I believe you have failed in your duty to make me aware of my rights and I want this addressed.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
The customer's complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues under DISP 1.2.1R (Consumer awareness rules):

1. **Failure to provide complaints procedure information at point of sale**: DISP 1.2.1R(2)(b) requires respondents to refer eligible complainants to the availability of complaints procedure information in writing at, or immediately after, the point of sale (for non-payment service products). A personal loan taken out in March 2024 would fall under this obligation. The customer states they received nothing in their loan agreement or accompanying paperwork — if accurate, this is a breach of DISP 1.2.1R(2)(b).

2. **Failure to publish FOS information on the website**: DISP 1.2.1R(4)(a) requires respondents to provide information about the Financial Ombudsman Service (including its website address) on the respondent's website where one exists. The customer states there is no mention of the FOS on your website — if accurate, this is a further breach of DISP 1.2.1R(4)(a).

3. **Failure to provide complaints procedure information on request / when acknowledging the complaint**: DISP 1.2.1R(3) requires this information to be provided in writing and free of charge on request and when acknowledging a complaint. The customer's experience of spending over an hour on the phone to find out how to complain is relevant context here.

**Recommended action**: You should investigate whether the point-of-sale documentation for this customer's March 2024 loan included the required reference to complaints procedure information, and whether your website currently carries FOS information. If either is confirmed absent, you should acknowledge the failing, confirm the information is now being provided, and consider whether the customer suffered any material detriment (e.g. delay in raising their underlying overcharge complaint) that warrants any further remedy. The underlying overcharge complaint itself is a separate matter and should be handled accordingly.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft**:
- This is a personal loan (not a payment service), so DISP 1.2.1R(2)(b) applies rather than the payment services route under (2)(a). Please confirm the product type.
- The customer draft below does not address the underlying overcharge complaint — that will need a separate response once investigated.
- Human review is required given the potential regulatory breach and the possibility that the delay in complaining about the overcharge caused financial detriment to the customer.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch and for raising these concerns. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you experienced when trying to find out how to make a complaint, and I want to assure you that we take this seriously.

You have raised two specific issues, and I want to address each of them.

**Information about our complaints process**

We are required to make information about our internal complaints procedure available to you, and to refer you to that information in writing at or around the time you take out a product with us. We are also required to provide that information to you in writing, free of charge, when you request it and when we acknowledge your complaint. I understand that you did not receive this information in your loan documentation and that you had significant difficulty finding out how to complain. I am sorry that this was your experience, and we are looking into how this occurred.

**Information about the Financial Ombudsman Service on our website**

We are also required to publish information about the Financial Ombudsman Service — an independent, free service that can consider unresolved complaints — on our website. You have told us that you could not find this information. We are reviewing this as a matter of urgency.

**What happens next**

We will investigate both of these concerns and write to you with our findings. In the meantime, I want to make sure you have the information you need:

- Our complaints process: [insert details or enclose leaflet]
- The Financial Ombudsman Service can be contacted at [insert FOS contact details] and their website is www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.

We are also aware that you have a separate concern about an overcharge on your account. We will address that complaint in a further communication once we have completed our review.

If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Bank Name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires respondents to publish complaints procedure information, refer eligible complainants to it at point of sale, provide it on request and when acknowledging a complaint, and publish FOS information on their website — all of which the customer alleges were not done.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.8 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of a payment protection contract, knowledge by the complainant that there was a problem with the sale of the payment protection contract generally (for example where there has been a rejection of a claim on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion, or the complainant has received a customer contact letter explaining that they may have been mis-sold) would not in itself ordinarily be sufficient to establish for the purposes of the three-year time period in DISP 2.8.2R(2) that the complainant had become aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he or she had cause for complaint in respect of a failure to make the disclosure set out at DISP App 3.3A.2E (relating to failure to disclose commission).

# DISP — DISP 1.2 Consumer awareness rules

## DISP 1.2.1 (Rules)

To aid consumer awareness of the protections offered by the provisions in this chapter, respondents must:

(1) publish appropriate information regarding their internal procedures for the reasonable and prompt handling of complaints;

(2) refer eligible complainants to the availability of this information:

(a) in relation to a payment service, in the information on out-of-court complaint and redress procedures required to be provided or made available under regulations 43(2)(e) (Information required prior to the conclusion of a single payment service contract) or 48 (Prior general information for framework contracts) of the Payment Services Regulations; or

(aa) in relation to CBTL arrangers, in the information on registering complaints internally and out-of-court complaint and redress procedures provided under article 7(1)(h) of Schedule 2 to the MCD Order; or

(b) otherwise, in writing at, or immediately after, the point of sale ; and

(c) in relation to a payment service, at the branch where the service is provided;

(3) provide such information in writing and free of charge to eligible complainants:

(a) on request; and

(b) when acknowledging a complaint; and

(4) provide information to eligible complainants, in a clear, comprehensible and easily accessible way, about the Financial Ombudsman Service including the Financial Ombudsman Service's website address:

(a) on the respondent's website, where one exists; and

(b) if applicable, in the general conditions of the respondent's contract with the eligible complainant.

[Note: article 15 of the UCITS Directive, article 13(2) of the ADR Directive, article 14(1) of the ODR Regulation, regulation 19 of the ADR Regulations and article 101 of the Payment Services Directive ]

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# DISP — DISP INTRO 1 Introduction

## DISP INTRO 1.4

Chapters 2 - 4: The Financial Ombudsman ServiceChapters 2, 3 and 4 set out how the Financial Ombudsman Service (operated by FOS Ltd) considers unresolved complaints.Chapter 2 sets out the scope of the Financial Ombudsman Service's two jurisdictions:• the Compulsory Jurisdiction; and• the Voluntary Jurisdiction.The scope of the two jurisdictions is defined by: the type of activity to which the complaint relates; the place where the activity took place; the eligibility of the complainant; and the time limits for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.Chapter 3 sets out the procedures of the Financial Ombudsman Service, including consideration and determination of complaints and how the Financial Ombudsman Service deals with information received.Chapter 4 sets out the terms under which VJ participants participate in the Voluntary Jurisdiction.

## DISP INTRO 1.5

Appendix 1: FCA’s guidance on handling mortgage-endowment complaintsThis appendix contains the FCA's guidance to firms on handling complaints relating to mortgage endowments.

## DISP INTRO 1.6

Appendix 3: FCA’s rules and guidance on handling payment protection insurance complaintsThis appendix sets out the approach which firms should use when handling complaints relating to the sale of payment protection contracts.

## DISP INTRO 1.7

Financial Ombudsman Service feesThe rules on fees charged in respect of the Financial Ombudsman Service are in Chapter 5 of the Fees manual.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules)

The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if:

(1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or

(2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint:

(a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or

(b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme:

(a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or

(b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme;

unless:

(4) the respondent consents and:

(a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and

(b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint.

## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules)

Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint.
