# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules)

The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if:

(1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or

(2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint:

(a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or

(b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme:

(a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or

(b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme;

unless:

(4) the respondent consents and:

(a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and

(b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint.

## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules)

Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.8 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of a payment protection contract, knowledge by the complainant that there was a problem with the sale of the payment protection contract generally (for example where there has been a rejection of a claim on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion, or the complainant has received a customer contact letter explaining that they may have been mis-sold) would not in itself ordinarily be sufficient to establish for the purposes of the three-year time period in DISP 2.8.2R(2) that the complainant had become aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he or she had cause for complaint in respect of a failure to make the disclosure set out at DISP App 3.3A.2E (relating to failure to disclose commission).

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 7. Procedures

### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints

A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination.

All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.

### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement

Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must:

1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected.
2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer.
3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.

### 7.3 Investigation Process

The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include:

1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems.
2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made.
3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# DISP — DISP 1.10B Payment services and electronic money complaints reporting

## DISP 1.10B.9 (Directions)

The relevant reporting period is the year immediately following:

(1) where the respondent has an accounting reference date, its accounting reference date; and

(2) where the respondent does not have an accounting reference date, 31 December each year.

## DISP 1.10B.10 (Directions)

Reports are to be submitted to the FCA within 30 business days of the end of the relevant reporting periods through, and in the electronic format specified in, the FCA complaints reporting system or the appropriate section of the FCA website.

## DISP 1.10B.11 (Directions)

If a respondent is unable to submit a report in electronic format because of a systems failure of any kind, the respondent must notify the FCA, in writing and without delay, of that systems failure.

## DISP 1.10B.12 (Rules)

(1) If a respondent does not submit a complete report by the date on which it is due, in accordance with DISP 1.10B.10D, the respondent must pay the administrative fee specified in SUP 16.3.14R.(2) The administrative fee in (1) does not apply if the respondent has notified the FCA of a systems failure in accordance with DISP 1.10B.11R.

## DISP 1.10B.13 (Directions)

A closed complaint is a complaint where:

(1) the respondent has sent a final response; or

(2) the complainant has indicated in writing acceptance of the respondent’s earlier response under DISP 1.6.4R (where applicable).

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I made a complaint to my bank, Westfield Banking PLC, six weeks ago about a payment protection insurance policy that was sold to me through their branch but was actually underwritten by a separate insurer. Three weeks after I complained, the bank wrote to me saying they were forwarding my complaint to the insurer because the insurer was solely responsible. However, the bank didn't tell me the insurer's contact details in that letter — just said 'the relevant insurer' without a name, address, or phone number. I also feel that three weeks was not 'prompt' — I was left waiting with no update for all that time. I've now heard nothing from the insurer either, and I don't even know who to chase. I want to know why the bank took so long to forward my complaint and why they failed to give me the insurer's details so I could follow up myself.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct regulatory issues under DISP 1.7.1R, both of which appear to have merit on the facts as described.

**Issue 1 — Failure to provide the other respondent's contact details**
DISP 1.7.1R(2) requires that when forwarding a complaint, the respondent must inform the complainant in a final response of the other respondent's contact details. The bank's letter apparently named only 'the relevant insurer' without providing a name, address, or telephone number. This appears to be a breach of DISP 1.7.1R(2). The customer was left unable to identify or contact the insurer, which is precisely the harm this rule is designed to prevent.

**Issue 2 — Whether three weeks was 'prompt'**
DISP 1.7.1R(1) requires the forwarding to be done 'promptly'. Three weeks without any communication is likely to fall short of this standard, though 'promptly' is not defined with a specific number of days in the retrieved provisions. You should consider whether there was any legitimate reason for the delay (e.g., complexity in identifying the correct insurer). If there was no good reason, this is a further breach of DISP 1.7.1R(1).

**Issue 3 — The insurer's obligations going forward**
Under DISP 1.7.2R, once the insurer received the forwarded complaint, it is treated as if the complaint had been made directly to it from the date it received the forwarded complaint. Under DISP 1.7.3G, the standard eight-week time limit (DISP 1.6.2R) runs from that date. You should establish: (a) when exactly the bank forwarded the complaint to the insurer; and (b) whether the insurer has yet issued any response. If the insurer is approaching or has passed eight weeks from receipt of the forwarded complaint, it will be required under DISP 1.6.2R to issue either a final response or an explanation of delay with FOS referral rights.

**Recommended action**
1. Acknowledge the bank's apparent failures under DISP 1.7.1R(1) and (2) in the customer response.
2. Provide the insurer's full contact details in your response to the customer — you will need to identify these before sending.
3. Confirm to the customer that the insurer is now handling the complaint and the applicable time limits.
4. Do not admit liability on behalf of the bank without further review, but the factual position on the two DISP 1.7.1R points appears clear.

**Assumptions requiring confirmation before sending:**
- You must confirm the insurer's full name and contact details to include in the customer letter.
- You must confirm the exact date the bank forwarded the complaint to the insurer, so the insurer's eight-week deadline can be calculated.
- The complaint involves a PPI policy — check whether any PPI-specific time limit rules (DISP 2.8.9R) are relevant to the underlying complaint, though they do not affect the forwarding issue itself.

Human review is required before sending given the regulatory breach findings and the need to confirm factual details.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting in touch and for raising these concerns with us. I am sorry for the frustration and uncertainty you have experienced since making your complaint six weeks ago.

You have raised two specific concerns about how your complaint was handled, and I want to address each of them.

**Forwarding your complaint to the insurer**

When a bank has reasonable grounds to believe that another firm may be solely responsible for the matter you have complained about, it is permitted to forward your complaint to that firm in writing. When it does so, it is required to act promptly and to provide you with that firm's contact details so that you can follow up directly.

Having reviewed the letter sent to you three weeks after you complained, I can see that it did not include the insurer's name or contact details. I apologise for this — you should have been given that information so that you could contact the insurer yourself if needed. I am providing those details below.

I also acknowledge your concern that three weeks passed before you received any communication. We are reviewing whether the time taken to forward your complaint met the standard required of us, and I am sorry that you were left without an update during that period.

**The insurer's handling of your complaint**

Now that your complaint has been forwarded, the insurer is responsible for investigating it and responding to you. The complaint is treated as having been received by the insurer on the date they received the forwarded complaint from us. The insurer is required to send you a final response within eight weeks of that date, or to write to you explaining why they are not yet in a position to do so and confirming your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

**Insurer contact details**

[Insurer name]
[Address]
[Telephone number]

If you remain dissatisfied with how either we or the insurer have handled your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567. I have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet for your reference.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires a respondent forwarding a complaint to do so promptly and to inform the complainant in a final response of the other respondent's contact details; both requirements appear to have been breached here.

Provides that once the insurer received the forwarded complaint, it is treated as if the complaint had been made directly to it from the date of receipt, establishing the insurer's obligations and the start of its time limits.
Indicates that the standard time limits run from the date the forwarded complaint is received by the insurer, informing the customer of when the insurer's eight-week deadline begins.

Requires the insurer, as the respondent now handling the complaint, to issue a final response or an explanation of delay within eight weeks of receiving the forwarded complaint, and to inform the complainant of FOS referral rights.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.8 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of a payment protection contract, knowledge by the complainant that there was a problem with the sale of the payment protection contract generally (for example where there has been a rejection of a claim on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion, or the complainant has received a customer contact letter explaining that they may have been mis-sold) would not in itself ordinarily be sufficient to establish for the purposes of the three-year time period in DISP 2.8.2R(2) that the complainant had become aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he or she had cause for complaint in respect of a failure to make the disclosure set out at DISP App 3.3A.2E (relating to failure to disclose commission).

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# DISP — DISP 1.7 Complaints forwarding rules

## DISP 1.7.1 (Rules)

A respondent that has reasonable grounds to be satisfied that another respondent may be solely or jointly responsible for the matter alleged in a complaint may forward the complaint, or the relevant part of it, in writing to that other respondent, provided it:

(1) does so promptly;

(2) informs the complainant promptly in a final response of why the complaint has been forwarded by it to the other respondent, and of the other respondent's contact details; and

(3) where jointly responsible for the fault alleged in the complaint, it complies with its own obligations under this chapter in respect of that part of the complaint it has not forwarded.

## DISP 1.7.2 (Rules)

When a respondent receives a complaint that has been forwarded to it under DISP 1.7.1 R, the complaint is treated for the purposes of DISP as if made directly to that respondent, and as if received by it when the forwarded complaint was received.

## DISP 1.7.3 (Guidance)

On receiving a forwarded complaint, the standard time limits will apply from the date on which the respondent receives the forwarded complaint.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 7. Procedures

### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints

A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination.

All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.

### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement

Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must:

1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected.
2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer.
3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.

### 7.3 Investigation Process

The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include:

1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems.
2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made.
3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules)

The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if:

(1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or

(2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint:

(a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or

(b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme:

(a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or

(b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme;

unless:

(4) the respondent consents and:

(a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and

(b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint.

## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules)

Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch and for raising these concerns with us. I am sorry for the frustration and uncertainty you have experienced since making your complaint six weeks ago. You have raised two specific concerns about how your complaint was handled, and I want to address each of them.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately identifies the two specific concerns: the failure to provide insurer contact details and the delay in forwarding the complaint. This reflects an accurate understanding of what is being complained about.

verdicts.evidence: I can see that it did not include the insurer's name or contact details. I apologise for this — you should have been given that information so that you could contact the insurer yourself if needed... We are reviewing whether the time taken to forward your complaint met the standard required of us, and I am sorry that you were left without an update during that period.
verdicts.reasoning: On the first issue (missing contact details), the response effectively acknowledges fault and apologises, which implies an uphold. However, on the second issue (promptness), the response says 'we are reviewing' rather than reaching a decision. There is no explicit statement of 'we uphold/partially uphold/reject your complaint' as a clear decision. The response partially addresses the points but does not state a definitive overall decision.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with how either we or the insurer have handled your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567. I have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet for your reference.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly states the right to refer to the FOS, provides contact details (website and phone number), and mentions the enclosed leaflet. This fully satisfies the requirement.

The customer draft does not mention the six-month time limit from the date of the final response within which the complainant must refer the complaint to the FOS. While FOS referral rights are mentioned, the critical six-month deadline is entirely absent.

verdicts.evidence: I apologise for this — you should have been given that information so that you could contact the insurer yourself if needed... I also acknowledge your concern that three weeks passed before you received any communication... I am sorry that you were left without an update during that period.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages substantively with both points raised, uses clear and non-dismissive language, acknowledges failings, apologises, and provides practical information (insurer details, explanation of the insurer's obligations). It treats the complainant's concerns seriously and even-handedly.

The customer draft does not address whether any redress or remedial action is appropriate for the bank's acknowledged failures (the breach of providing contact details and the delay). While the complaint may not involve direct financial loss, the bank acknowledges regulatory failings that caused detriment (inability to chase the insurer, frustration from delay), yet no position on redress is communicated to the customer — no offer of compensation and no explanation of why none is due.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch and for raising these concerns with us. I am sorry for the frustration and uncertainty you have experienced since making your complaint six weeks ago.
verdicts.reasoning: The opening paragraph recognises the complainant’s submission, cites the six‑week timeframe and the specific frustration, accurately reflecting the substance of the complaint.
The draft never states a firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) on the complaint; it only says the firm is reviewing the issue and offers no conclusive outcome.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with how either we or the insurer have handled your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing text clearly informs the complainant of the right to refer to the FOS and provides the necessary contact details.
The response mentions the right to refer to the FOS but does not specify the six‑month deadline from the date of this final response.

verdicts.evidence: I apologise for this — you should have been given that information... I am providing those details below. I also acknowledge your concern that three weeks passed before you received any communication.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft engages with each point raised, uses apologetic and non‑dismissive language, and offers the missing insurer details, demonstrating fair and balanced treatment.
The letter does not address whether any financial redress or remedial action will be offered, nor does it explain why none is due.

claims.claim: DISP 1.7.1R(2) requires that when forwarding a complaint, the respondent must inform the complainant in a final response of the other respondent's contact details.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.7.1R(2) states the respondent must inform the complainant promptly in a final response of why the complaint has been forwarded and of the other respondent's contact details.

claims.claim: The bank's failure to provide the insurer's name, address, or telephone number appears to be a breach of DISP 1.7.1R(2).
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.7.1R(2) requires the respondent to inform the complainant of the other respondent's contact details. Failing to provide identifiable contact details would be a breach of this requirement.

claims.claim: DISP 1.7.1R(1) requires the forwarding to be done 'promptly'.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.7.1R(1) explicitly states the respondent must forward the complaint 'promptly'.

claims.claim: 'Promptly' is not defined with a specific number of days in the retrieved provisions.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision DISP 1.7.1R uses the word 'promptly' without specifying a number of days.

claims.claim: Under DISP 1.7.2R, once the insurer received the forwarded complaint, it is treated as if the complaint had been made directly to it from the date it received the forwarded complaint.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.7.2R states: 'the complaint is treated for the purposes of DISP as if made directly to that respondent, and as if received by it when the forwarded complaint was received.'

claims.claim: Under DISP 1.7.3G, the standard eight-week time limit (DISP 1.6.2R) runs from the date the insurer received the forwarded complaint.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.7.3G states: 'On receiving a forwarded complaint, the standard time limits will apply from the date on which the respondent receives the forwarded complaint.'

claims.claim: If the insurer has passed eight weeks from receipt of the forwarded complaint, it will be required under DISP 1.6.2R to issue either a final response or an explanation of delay with FOS referral rights.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R requires that by the end of eight weeks the respondent must send either a final response or a written response explaining why it cannot yet provide one and informing the complainant of FOS referral rights.

claims.claim: PPI-specific time limit rules (DISP 2.8.9R) may be relevant to the underlying complaint but do not affect the forwarding issue itself.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.9R is retrieved and relates to PPI complaint time limits for FOS referral. The claim that it doesn't affect the forwarding issue is a reasonable inference but not explicitly stated in the provisions.

claims.claim: When a bank has reasonable grounds to believe that another firm may be solely responsible for the matter complained about, it is permitted to forward the complaint to that firm in writing.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.7.1R states: 'A respondent that has reasonable grounds to be satisfied that another respondent may be solely or jointly responsible for the matter alleged in a complaint may forward the complaint... in writing to that other respondent.'

claims.claim: When forwarding a complaint, the bank is required to act promptly and to provide the complainant with that firm's contact details so that the complainant can follow up directly.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.7.1R(1) requires promptness and (2) requires informing the complainant of the other respondent's contact details.

claims.claim: Now that the complaint has been forwarded, the insurer is responsible for investigating it and responding to the customer.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.7.2R states the forwarded complaint is treated as if made directly to the receiving respondent, meaning it takes on the obligations under DISP.

claims.claim: The complaint is treated as having been received by the insurer on the date they received the forwarded complaint from the bank.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.7.2R explicitly states the complaint is treated 'as if received by it when the forwarded complaint was received.'

claims.claim: The insurer is required to send a final response within eight weeks of the date it received the forwarded complaint, or to write explaining why it is not yet in a position to do so and confirming the customer's right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R requires a final response by the end of eight weeks or a written response explaining the delay and informing the complainant of FOS referral rights. Combined with DISP 1.7.3G confirming time limits run from receipt of forwarded complaint.

claims.claim: If the customer remains dissatisfied with how either the bank or the insurer have handled the complaint, the customer has the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R(1)(e) requires informing the complainant they may refer to FOS if dissatisfied. The 'free of charge' aspect is not explicitly stated in the retrieved provisions, though it is generally true.

claims.claim: The Financial Ombudsman Service can be contacted at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567.
claims.reasoning: The internal policy document states FOS contact details must include 'website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567'.

claims.claim: A copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet is enclosed.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R(1)(d) requires the final response to enclose 'a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet'.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I took out a payment protection insurance policy with your bank in 2002 and cancelled it in 2005. I recently heard from a friend that I might have been mis-sold PPI and decided to complain. Your bank has sent me a letter saying my complaint is 'time-barred' and that you won't look into whether I was mis-sold the policy. However, the letter simply states my complaint is rejected due to time limits — it doesn't explain my right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service or give me the six-month deadline for doing so. I feel this is unfair as I believe I should at least have been told about my options, even if you consider the complaint too old.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
The customer's complaint raises a valid procedural point. Under DISP 1.8.1R, a respondent may reject a time-barred complaint without considering its merits, but must do so by way of a final response that complies with DISP 1.6.2R. DISP 1.6.2R requires that a final response: encloses a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; provides the FOS website address; informs the complainant they may refer the complaint to the FOS if dissatisfied; and indicates whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R, using the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R.

On the substance of time-barring: the PPI sale took place in 2002 and the policy was cancelled in 2005. Under DISP 2.8.9R, the Ombudsman cannot consider a PPI mis-sale complaint relating to a sale on or before 29 August 2017 unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or FOS on or before 29 August 2019 (with a written acknowledgement), or one of the other conditions in DISP 2.8.9R(2) is met (exceptional circumstances, respondent consent, or a post-29 August 2017 claim rejection on grounds of ineligibility/exclusion/limitation). You should check whether any of those conditions apply before confirming the time-bar position.

The procedural deficiency is clear: the letter sent to the customer did not include the FOS referral rights, the six-month deadline, or the Annex 3R wording on time limits. This is a breach of DISP 1.6.2R (as required by DISP 1.8.1R). A remedying final response must be issued that corrects this.

Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft below:
1. Confirm whether the bank consents or does not consent to waive the time limits in DISP 2.8.2R — this determines which Annex 3R wording to use. The draft below uses the 'does not consent to waive' wording (Annex 3R option 3), but you must select the correct option.
2. Confirm that none of the DISP 2.8.9R(2) conditions apply (i.e. no complaint was made before 29 August 2019, no exceptional circumstances, and no post-2017 claim rejection on ineligibility/exclusion grounds).
3. The six-month referral window to the FOS runs from the date of the remedying final response — confirm this letter will serve as that final response.
4. Human review is required before sending given the potential procedural breach and the need to confirm the consent/waiver position.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for your complaint and for bringing this matter to our attention. We are sorry that our earlier letter did not set out your options clearly. We want to put that right now.

We have reviewed your complaint about the sale of your payment protection insurance policy, which was taken out in 2002 and cancelled in 2005.

Our position on your complaint

Having considered your complaint, we are not able to investigate the merits of your mis-sale concern. This is because the rules that govern when a complaint can be referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service set a deadline for PPI mis-sale complaints relating to sales that took place on or before 29 August 2017. Under those rules, a complaint needed to have been referred to us or to the Ombudsman on or before 29 August 2019. As your complaint was received after that date, and we are not aware of any earlier complaint having been made, we are not in a position to consider it on its merits.

We recognise this may be disappointing, and we are sorry we did not explain this clearly in our first letter.

Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service

You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge — but you must do so within six months of the date of this letter.

The Ombudsman might not be able to consider your complaint if:
• what you are complaining about happened more than six years ago, and
• you are complaining more than three years after you realised (or should have realised) that there was a problem.

We think that your complaint was made outside of these time limits, but this is a matter for the Ombudsman to decide. If the Ombudsman agrees with us, they will not have our permission to consider your complaint and so will only be able to do so in very limited circumstances — for example, if the Ombudsman believes that the delay was as a result of exceptional circumstances.

I have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, which explains how to contact them and what they can do. You can also find further information at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.

If you have any questions about this letter, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Name]
[Job title]
[Bank name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Permits the respondent to reject a time-barred complaint without considering its merits, but requires it to do so by way of a final response complying with DISP 1.6.2R or DISP 1.6.2AR.

Sets out the mandatory content of a final response, including enclosing the FOS leaflet, providing the FOS website address, informing the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and indicating whether the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits using Annex 3R wording.

Provides the specific time-bar rule for PPI mis-sale complaints, requiring that complaints relating to sales on or before 29 August 2017 must have been referred on or before 29 August 2019 unless one of the specified conditions is met.
Sets out the general six-year and three-year time limits for referral to the FOS, and the six-month window from the date of the final response, which applies to this complaint.

Prescribes the wording to be used in a final response where the respondent considers the complaint outside the DISP 2.8.2R(2) time limits but does not consent to waive those limits, while consenting to waive the six-month limit — used as the basis for the time-limit notice in the customer draft (subject to handler confirmation of the correct option).

# DISP — DISP 1.8 Complaints time barring rule

## DISP 1.8.1 (Rules)

If a respondent receives a complaint which is outside the time limits for referral to the Financial Ombudsman Service (see DISP 2.8 ) it may reject the complaint without considering the merits, but must explain this to the complainant in a final response in accordance with DISP 1.6.2 R or DISP 1.6.2AR .

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

## DISP 2.8.5 (Rules)

The six-year and the three-year time limits do not apply where:

(1) [deleted]

(2) the complaint concerns a contract or policy which is the subject of a review directly or indirectly under:

(a) the terms of the Statement of Policy on 'Pension transfers and Opt-outs' issued by the FSA on 25 October 1994; or

(b) the terms of the policy statement for the review of specific categories of FSAVC business issued by the FSA on 28 February 2000.

## DISP 2.8.6 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage, the receipt by the complainant of a letter which states that there is a risk (rather than a high risk) that the policy would not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount is not, itself, sufficient to cause the three year time period in DISP 2.8.2R (2) to start to run.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.8 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of a payment protection contract, knowledge by the complainant that there was a problem with the sale of the payment protection contract generally (for example where there has been a rejection of a claim on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion, or the complainant has received a customer contact letter explaining that they may have been mis-sold) would not in itself ordinarily be sufficient to establish for the purposes of the three-year time period in DISP 2.8.2R(2) that the complainant had become aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he or she had cause for complaint in respect of a failure to make the disclosure set out at DISP App 3.3A.2E (relating to failure to disclose commission).

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for your complaint and for bringing this matter to our attention. We are sorry that our earlier letter did not set out your options clearly. We want to put that right now. We have reviewed your complaint about the sale of your payment protection insurance policy, which was taken out in 2002 and cancelled in 2005.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint, references the PPI policy sale and cancellation dates, and also acknowledges the procedural issue the customer raised about the earlier letter not explaining their options.

verdicts.evidence: Having considered your complaint, we are not able to investigate the merits of your mis-sale concern. This is because the rules that govern when a complaint can be referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service set a deadline for PPI mis-sale complaints relating to sales that took place on or before 29 August 2017. Under those rules, a complaint needed to have been referred to us or to the Ombudsman on or before 29 August 2019. As your complaint was received after that date, and we are not aware of any earlier complaint having been made, we are not in a position to consider it on its merits.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft clearly states the decision (complaint rejected/not investigated on merits) and provides specific reasons tied to the PPI time-bar rules, explaining why the complaint falls outside the deadline. It also acknowledges the procedural failing of the earlier letter, which was the customer's specific concern.

You can also find further information at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk.

The language is clear, non-dismissive, and empathetic. It engages with both the substantive time-bar issue and the procedural complaint about the earlier letter.

claims.claim: Under DISP 1.8.1R, a respondent may reject a time-barred complaint without considering its merits, but must do so by way of a final response that complies with DISP 1.6.2R.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.8.1R states that if a respondent receives a complaint outside the time limits for referral to the FOS, it may reject the complaint without considering the merits, but must explain this in a final response in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R.

claims.claim: DISP 1.6.2R requires that a final response encloses a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R(1)(d) explicitly requires the final response to enclose a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet.

claims.claim: DISP 1.6.2R requires that a final response provides the FOS website address.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R(1)(da) requires the final response to provide the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service.

claims.claim: DISP 1.6.2R requires that a final response informs the complainant they may refer the complaint to the FOS if dissatisfied.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R(1)(e) requires the response to inform the complainant that if he remains dissatisfied, he may now refer his complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

claims.claim: DISP 1.6.2R requires that a final response indicates whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R, using the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R(1)(f) requires the response to indicate whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits by including the appropriate wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3R.

claims.claim: Under DISP 2.8.9R, the Ombudsman cannot consider a PPI mis-sale complaint relating to a sale on or before 29 August 2017 unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or FOS on or before 29 August 2019 (with a written acknowledgement).
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.9R(1) states the Ombudsman cannot consider such a complaint unless one of the conditions in (2) is met, and (2)(a) specifies referral on or before 29 August 2019 with a written acknowledgement or other record.

claims.claim: One of the other conditions in DISP 2.8.9R(2) is exceptional circumstances.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.9R(2)(b) states a condition is that the failure to comply with the time limit was as a result of exceptional circumstances.

claims.claim: One of the other conditions in DISP 2.8.9R(2) is respondent consent.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.9R(2)(c) states a condition is that the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint.

claims.claim: One of the other conditions in DISP 2.8.9R(2) is a post-29 August 2017 claim rejection on grounds of ineligibility/exclusion/limitation.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.9R(2)(d) specifies a condition involving a complaint made on or after 29 August 2019 relating to a live policy with a rejection on grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation on or after 29 August 2017.

claims.claim: The earlier letter's failure to include FOS referral rights, the six-month deadline, or the Annex 3R wording on time limits constitutes a breach of DISP 1.6.2R (as required by DISP 1.8.1R).
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R requires these elements in a final response, and DISP 1.8.1R requires the rejection to be communicated in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. Omitting these elements would be non-compliant.

claims.claim: The draft uses the 'does not consent to waive' wording (Annex 3R option 3).
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision shows Annex 3R option (3) wording for when the respondent does not consent to waive the DISP 2.8.2R(2) time limits but does consent to waive the six-month time limit. The customer draft closely mirrors this wording.

claims.claim: The six-month referral window to the FOS runs from the date of the remedying final response.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.2R(1) states the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if referred more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response. DISP 2.8.3G confirms the six-month time limit is only triggered by a final response.

claims.claim: The rules that govern when a complaint can be referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service set a deadline for PPI mis-sale complaints relating to sales that took place on or before 29 August 2017, requiring a complaint to have been referred to the firm or to the Ombudsman on or before 29 August 2019.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.9R(1) and (2)(a) establish that the Ombudsman cannot consider a PPI mis-sale complaint relating to a sale on or before 29 August 2017 unless the complaint was referred on or before 29 August 2019.

claims.claim: The customer has the right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge, but must do so within six months of the date of this letter.
claims.reasoning: The Annex 3R option (3) wording actually states 'The time limit for referring complaints to the Ombudsman is usually six months but we will consent to the Ombudsman considering your complaint even if you refer the complaint later than this.' This means under option (3), the respondent consents to waive the six-month limit. The customer draft instead imposes a strict six-month deadline, which contradicts the Annex 3R option (3) wording. If the handler intended option (3), the six-month hard deadline is incorrect. If a different option was intended (no consent to waive any time limits), there is no exact matching provision retrieved.

claims.claim: The Ombudsman might not be able to consider the complaint if what the customer is complaining about happened more than six years ago, and the customer is complaining more than three years after they realised (or should have realised) that there was a problem.
claims.reasoning: This is the standard Annex 3R option (3) wording which states: 'The Ombudsman might not be able to consider your complaint if: what you're complaining about happened more than six years ago, and you're complaining more than three years after you realised (or should have realised) that there was a problem.'

claims.claim: The firm thinks the complaint was made outside of these time limits but this is a matter for the Ombudsman to decide.
claims.reasoning: This mirrors the Annex 3R option (3) wording: 'We think that your complaint was made outside of these time limits but this is a matter for the Ombudsman to decide.'

For example, if the Ombudsman believes that the delay was as a result of exceptional circumstances.'

structure-titan.scenario_text: I opened a current account with your bank online in January 2024. During the application process, I noticed that a 'Card Protection Plan' at £7.99/month was already pre-selected as part of the account setup — there was a tick already in the box. I didn't untick it because I assumed it was a required part of the account, and the page layout made it look like it was bundled in. I've now been charged £7.99 per month for the past 14 months — that's over £110 total — for something I never actively chose. When I look back at the confirmation email and all the paperwork I received when I opened the account, I also notice that at no point were you clear about how to make a complaint or that I could go to the Financial Ombudsman Service if I was unhappy. I only found out about the Ombudsman from a friend. I want a full refund of all charges for this Card Protection Plan and I want to know why I was never told about my rights to complain.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct issues: (1) a pre-ticked box used to enrol the customer in a Card Protection Plan (CPP) without active consent, and (2) failure to provide information about complaint rights and the Financial Ombudsman Service at the point of sale.

**Issue 1 – Pre-ticked box / eligibility and suitability**
The retrieved provisions most directly applicable to payment protection contracts include ICOBS 5.1.2R, which requires a firm arranging a payment protection contract to take reasonable steps to ensure the customer only buys a policy under which they are eligible to claim benefits, and ICOBS 6.4.5R, which requires the firm to draw the consumer's attention to the importance of reading documentation before the end of the cancellation period to check suitability. However, the core complaint here is about a pre-ticked opt-in mechanism — this is a mis-selling/consent issue. The retrieved provisions do not include a specific rule expressly prohibiting pre-ticked boxes (e.g. from ICOBS or COBS on unsolicited services or active consent requirements). The provisions retrieved are focused on PPI-style payment protection contracts and their redress methodology (DISP App 3.7), eligibility checks (ICOBS 5.1.2R), and pre/post-contract information (ICOBS 6.4). A Card Protection Plan may or may not fall within the definition of a 'payment protection contract' under ICOBS — this needs to be confirmed. If it does, ICOBS 5.1.2R and ICOBS 6.4.5R are relevant. If it does not, the retrieved provisions may not adequately cover the consent/pre-ticked box issue, and `insufficient_context` is flagged accordingly.

**Issue 2 – Failure to provide complaint/FOS information**
ICOBS 6.4.4AR is directly on point: in relation to a payment protection contract, the firm must provide the consumer with information about how to complain and that complaints may be referred to the FOS, in good time before conclusion of the policy. The customer states this information was absent from all documentation. If the CPP qualifies as a payment protection contract, this is a clear breach of ICOBS 6.4.4AR. If it does not qualify, the retrieved provisions do not supply an equivalent rule for other insurance types.

**Redress**
DISP App 3.7 evidential provisions set out a methodology for PPI redress — including paying back total amounts paid plus simple interest (DISP App 3.7.3E) — but these are evidential provisions, not standalone binding rules, and they apply specifically where the firm concludes the complainant would not have bought the contract (following the DISP App 3.6 assessment). The customer is claiming £111.86 (14 × £7.99). Any redress decision requires a proper assessment under DISP App 3.6 (not retrieved) before DISP App 3.7 applies.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending:**
1. Does the Card Protection Plan qualify as a 'payment protection contract' under ICOBS? This determines which provisions apply.
2. Was the pre-ticked box mechanism used? (Customer asserts yes — check the January 2024 online journey records.)
3. Was complaint/FOS information absent from all pre-contractual documentation? (Check the January 2024 application flow and confirmation email.)
4. Has the customer cancelled the CPP, or is it still active?
5. The DISP App 3.7 redress methodology is evidential and requires a prior causation assessment — do not commit to a full refund in the customer letter without completing that assessment.
6. The customer letter below does NOT include the standard FOS referral time-limit notice because the complaint has just been received and no final response is being issued yet — confirm whether this is an acknowledgement or a final response.

**Human review is required** before sending: this involves potential financial redress of over £110, possible regulatory breach (pre-ticked box, missing FOS information), and the retrieved context does not fully cover the consent/pre-ticked box issue. A senior handler or compliance should review.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about your experience when opening your current account in January 2024, and I want to assure you that we are taking your concerns seriously.

You have raised two concerns, which I will address in turn.

**The Card Protection Plan charges**

You have told us that the Card Protection Plan was pre-selected during your online account application, and that the page layout led you to believe it was a required part of the account rather than an optional add-on. You have asked for a refund of the charges you have paid — a total of approximately £111.86 over 14 months.

We are required to take reasonable steps to ensure that customers only purchase insurance policies under which they are eligible to claim benefits, and to draw customers' attention to the importance of reading policy documentation before the end of the cancellation period to check that the policy is suitable for them. We are investigating whether the way the Card Protection Plan was presented during your application met those obligations.

We are now reviewing the records of your account application, including the online journey you completed in January 2024, to understand exactly what happened. We will write to you again with our findings and our decision on your refund request as soon as our investigation is complete.

**Information about your right to complain**

You have also told us that your confirmation email and account documentation did not explain how to make a complaint or that you could refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. We are sorry if that was the case. Where we arrange insurance policies for customers, we are required to provide clear information — before the policy is concluded — about how to complain and about the availability of the Financial Ombudsman Service. We are reviewing whether this information was provided to you as it should have been, and we will address this as part of our response.

**Next steps**

We will complete our investigation and send you a full written response. If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us using the details below.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires a firm arranging a payment protection contract to take reasonable steps to ensure the customer only buys a policy under which they are eligible to claim benefits, directly relevant to whether the CPP was appropriately sold.

Requires the firm to draw the consumer's attention to the importance of reading payment protection contract documentation before the end of the cancellation period to check suitability, relevant to whether the customer was given adequate opportunity to assess the CPP.

Requires the firm to provide the consumer with information about how to complain and that complaints may be referred to the FOS in good time before conclusion of a payment protection contract, directly relevant to the customer's complaint that this information was absent.

Sets out the evidential approach to redress where the firm concludes the complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract — relevant to the potential refund calculation if the CPP qualifies and causation is established.

Indicates that where redress is due, the firm should pay the total amount paid by the complainant in respect of the payment protection contract including historic interest — relevant to the quantum of the potential refund claimed.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# ICOBS — ICOBS 6.4 Pre- and post-contract information: protection policies

## ICOBS 6.4.4A (Rules)

In relation to a payment protection contract, a firm must provide a consumer with information about:

(1) how the consumer can complain to the insurance undertaking and that complaints may subsequently be referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service (or other applicable named complaints scheme); and

(2) the consumer’s entitlement to compensation from the compensation scheme (or other applicable compensation scheme), or that there is no compensation scheme, in the event where the insurance undertaking is unable to meet its liabilities;

in good time before the conclusion of the policy.

## ICOBS 6.4.5 (Rules)

(1) A firm must draw a consumer's attention to the importance of reading payment protection contract documentation before the end of the cancellation period to check that the policy is suitable for the consumer.

(2) This must be done orally if a firm provides information orally on any main characteristic of a policy.

## ICOBS 6.4.6 (Rules)

A firm must provide price information in a way calculated to enable the customer to relate it to a regular budget.

## ICOBS 6.4.7 (Guidance)

Price information is likely also to include at least the total premium (or the basis for calculating it so that the customer can verify it) and, where relevant:

(1) for policies of over one year with reviewable premiums, the period for which the quoted premium is valid, and the timing of reviews;

(2) other fees, administrative charges and taxes payable by the customer through the firm; and

(3) a statement identifying separately the possibility of any taxes not payable through the firm.

metadata.section_name: ICOBS 6.4 Pre- and post-contract information: protection policies

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP App 3.7 Approach to redress at step 1

## DISP App 3.7.10 (Evidential Provisions)

The firm should pay to the complainant a sum equal to the amount in DISP App 3.7.3 E less the amount the complainant would have paid for the alternative regular premium payment protection contract.

## DISP App 3.7.11 (Evidential Provisions)

The firm should consider whether it is appropriate to deduct the value of any paid claims from the redress.

## DISP App 3.7.12 (Evidential Provisions)

Additionally, where a single premium was added to a loan, DISP App 3.7.4 E applies except that in respect of DISP App 3.7.4 E (1)(a) the cancellation value should only be used if the complainant expressly wishes to cancel the policy.

## DISP App 3.7.13 (Evidential Provisions)

The firm should, for the purposes of redressing the complaint, use the value of £9 per £100 of benefits payable as the monthly price of the alternative regular premium payment protection contract. For example, if the monthly repayment amount in relation to the loan only is to be £200, the price of the alternative regular premium payment protection contract will be £18.

## DISP App 3.7.14 (Evidential Provisions)

Where the firm presumes that the complainant would have purchased a regular premium payment protection contract and if the complainant expressly wishes it, the existing cover should continue until the end of the existing policy term. The complainant should pay the price of the alternative regular premium payment protection contract (at DISP App 3.7.13 E) and should be able to cancel at any time. This pricing does not apply where DISP App 3.7.4 E (1)(b) applies.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP App 3.7 Approach to redress at step 1

## DISP App 3.7.-1 (Evidential Provisions)

This section applies to step 1.

## DISP App 3.7.1 (Evidential Provisions)

Where the firm concludes in accordance with DISP App 3.6 that the complainant would still have bought the payment protection contract he bought, no redress will be due to the complainant in respect of the identified breach or failing, subject to DISP App 3.7.6 E.

## DISP App 3.7.2 (Evidential Provisions)

Where the firm concludes that the complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract he bought, and the firm is not using the alternative approach to redress (set out in DISP App 3.7.7 E to 3.7.15 E) or other appropriate redress (see DISP App 3.8), the firm should, as far as practicable, put the complainant in the position he would have been if he had not bought any payment protection contract.

## DISP App 3.7.3 (Evidential Provisions)

In such cases the firm should pay to the complainant a sum equal to the total amount paid by the complainant in respect of the payment protection contract including historic interest where relevant (plus simple interest on that amount). If the complainant has received any rebate, for example if the customer cancelled a single premium payment protection contract before it ran full term and received a refund, the firm may deduct the value of this rebate from the amount otherwise payable to the complainant.

metadata.section_name: DISP App 3.7 Approach to redress at step 1
