# ICOBS — ICOBS 5.1 General

## ICOBS 5.1.1 (Guidance)

(1) In line with Principle 6, a firm should take reasonable steps to ensure that a customer only buys a policy under which he is eligible to claim benefits.

(2) If, at any time while arranging a policy, a firm finds that parts of the cover apply, but others do not, it should inform the customer so he can take an informed decision on whether to buy the policy.

(3) This guidance does not apply to policies arranged as part of a packaged bank account.

## ICOBS 5.1.2 (Rules)

(1) A firm arranging a payment protection contract must:

(a) take reasonable steps to ensure that the customer only buys a policy under which he is eligible to claim benefits; and

(b) if, at any time while arranging the policy, it finds that parts of the cover do not apply, inform the customer so he can take an informed decision on whether to buy the policy.

(2) This rule does not apply to payment protection contract arranged as part of a packaged bank account.

## ICOBS 5.1.3 (Guidance)

(1) For a typical payment protection contract the reasonable steps required in the first part of the eligibility rule are likely to include checking that the customer meets any qualifying requirements for different parts of the policy.

(2) This guidance does not apply to payment protection contracts arranged as part of a packaged bank account.

## ICOBS 5.1.3A (Rules)

A firm arranging policies as part of a packaged bank account must:

(1) take reasonable steps to establish whether the customer is eligible to claim each of the benefits under each policy included in the packaged bank account which must include checking that the customer meets any qualifying requirements to claim each of the benefits under each policy; and

(2) inform the customer whether or not he would be eligible to claim each of the benefits under each policy included in the packaged bank account so that the customer can take an informed decision about the arrangements proposed.

metadata.section_name: ICOBS 5.1 General

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about your experience when opening your current account in January 2024, and I want to assure you that we are taking your concerns seriously. You have raised two concerns, which I will address in turn.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and then accurately identifies both issues: the pre-ticked Card Protection Plan charges and the failure to provide complaint/FOS information. This reflects an accurate understanding of what is being complained about.

The response stage is marked as 'final', yet the customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). Instead, it says 'We are investigating' and 'We will complete our investigation and send you a full written response.' No decision is communicated, and no reasons for a decision are given.

The customer draft does not inform the complainant of their right to refer this complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. While the draft discusses the FOS in the context of the firm's obligation to have told the customer about it at point of sale, it never tells the customer they can now refer this complaint to the FOS, nor provides FOS contact details.
The customer draft contains no mention of a six-month deadline from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: We are required to take reasonable steps to ensure that customers only purchase insurance policies under which they are eligible to claim benefits... We are investigating whether the way the Card Protection Plan was presented during your application met those obligations.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages substantively with both issues raised, uses clear and non-dismissive language, acknowledges the customer's perspective, and explains what the firm is doing. It does not minimise or dismiss the concerns. The tone is even-handed and respectful.

verdicts.evidence: We will write to you again with our findings and our decision on your refund request as soon as our investigation is complete.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft acknowledges the refund request and states it will be addressed after investigation, but does not reach or communicate a position on redress. For a final response, this is insufficient — no offer is made and no reasoned explanation for why none is due is provided. However, it does at least acknowledge the question of redress rather than ignoring it entirely.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am sorry to hear about your experience when opening your current account in January 2024
verdicts.reasoning: The opening paragraph acknowledges the complaint and accurately restates the key facts (account opening date and Card Protection Plan issue).
The draft states the firm will investigate and will write a decision later; no firm decision (uphold/partially uphold/reject) or reasons are provided.

The letter only mentions that the firm is required to provide information about the Financial Ombudsman Service, but does not tell the complainant they may refer this complaint to the FOS nor give contact details.
There is no statement of the six‑month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS from the date of the final response.

verdicts.evidence: You have raised two concerns, which I will address in turn.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with each issue raised, uses courteous language, and does not dismiss or minimise the complainant's concerns.
The draft acknowledges the request for a refund but only says the firm will investigate; it does not offer redress or explain why none would be offered.

claims.claim: ICOBS 5.1.2R requires a firm arranging a payment protection contract to take reasonable steps to ensure the customer only buys a policy under which they are eligible to claim benefits.
claims.reasoning: ICOBS 5.1.2(1)(a) states exactly this: 'A firm arranging a payment protection contract must take reasonable steps to ensure that the customer only buys a policy under which he is eligible to claim benefits.'

claims.claim: ICOBS 6.4.5R requires the firm to draw the consumer's attention to the importance of reading documentation before the end of the cancellation period to check suitability.
claims.reasoning: ICOBS 6.4.5(1) states: 'A firm must draw a consumer's attention to the importance of reading payment protection contract documentation before the end of the cancellation period to check that the policy is suitable for the consumer.'

claims.claim: The retrieved provisions do not include a specific rule expressly prohibiting pre-ticked boxes.
claims.reasoning: This is a factual observation about the retrieved provisions. None of the retrieved provisions contain a rule expressly prohibiting pre-ticked boxes.

claims.claim: ICOBS 6.4.4AR requires that in relation to a payment protection contract, the firm must provide the consumer with information about how to complain and that complaints may be referred to the FOS, in good time before conclusion of the policy.
claims.reasoning: ICOBS 6.4.4A(1) states the firm must provide information about 'how the consumer can complain to the insurance undertaking and that complaints may subsequently be referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service... in good time before the conclusion of the policy.'

claims.claim: DISP App 3.7 evidential provisions set out a methodology for PPI redress including paying back total amounts paid plus simple interest (DISP App 3.7.3E).
claims.reasoning: DISP App 3.7.3E states: 'the firm should pay to the complainant a sum equal to the total amount paid by the complainant in respect of the payment protection contract including historic interest where relevant (plus simple interest on that amount).'

claims.claim: The DISP App 3.7 provisions are evidential provisions, not standalone binding rules.
claims.reasoning: The provisions are explicitly labelled as 'Evidential Provisions' in the retrieved text.

claims.claim: The DISP App 3.7 redress provisions apply specifically where the firm concludes the complainant would not have bought the contract (following the DISP App 3.6 assessment).
claims.reasoning: DISP App 3.7.2E states: 'Where the firm concludes that the complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract he bought... the firm should, as far as practicable, put the complainant in the position he would have been if he had not bought any payment protection contract.' And 3.7.1E references conclusions 'in accordance with DISP App 3.6.'

claims.claim: Any redress decision requires a proper assessment under DISP App 3.6 before DISP App 3.7 applies.
claims.reasoning: DISP App 3.7.1E explicitly states it applies 'Where the firm concludes in accordance with DISP App 3.6', confirming the 3.6 assessment is a prerequisite.

claims.claim: We are required to take reasonable steps to ensure that customers only purchase insurance policies under which they are eligible to claim benefits.
claims.reasoning: ICOBS 5.1.2(1)(a) requires a firm arranging a payment protection contract to 'take reasonable steps to ensure that the customer only buys a policy under which he is eligible to claim benefits.' The customer-facing paraphrase is faithful to this rule.

claims.claim: We are required to draw customers' attention to the importance of reading policy documentation before the end of the cancellation period to check that the policy is suitable for them.
claims.reasoning: ICOBS 6.4.5(1) states: 'A firm must draw a consumer's attention to the importance of reading payment protection contract documentation before the end of the cancellation period to check that the policy is suitable for the consumer.'

claims.claim: Where we arrange insurance policies for customers, we are required to provide clear information — before the policy is concluded — about how to complain and about the availability of the Financial Ombudsman Service.
claims.reasoning: ICOBS 6.4.4A applies specifically to 'a payment protection contract', not to all insurance policies. The customer letter broadens the scope to 'insurance policies' generally, which goes beyond what the retrieved provision states.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I took out a personal loan of £8,000 over the phone with your bank on 15 March. It was all done remotely — I never visited a branch. On 22 March, within the 14-day window, I called your cancellation line to exercise my right to cancel the distance contract. The adviser I spoke to said I couldn't cancel because the funds had already been disbursed and I'd started making use of the credit. She refused to process my cancellation and told me I'd need to repay the full loan with all interest over the term. I asked to speak to a manager but was told nobody was available and that I'd get a callback, which never came. I'm now being charged interest on a loan I tried to cancel within the legal timeframe. I'm 74 years old and on a fixed pension income — this additional debt is causing me significant financial distress. I also want to note that when I tried calling your complaints line to raise this issue, the number listed was an 0844 number which cost me over £3 in call charges before I got through. I want the cancellation honoured, all interest charges refunded, and compensation for the distress caused.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious regulatory issues that require urgent human review and careful handling. Here is the regulatory position based on the retrieved provisions:

**1. Right to cancel the personal loan**
CONC 11.1.1R establishes a 14-calendar-day right to cancel a distance credit agreement without penalty and without giving any reason. The customer contacted the cancellation line on 22 March, which is 7 days after the contract date of 15 March — well within the 14-day window. The adviser's refusal to process the cancellation on the basis that funds had been disbursed and the customer had started using the credit does not appear to be a valid ground for refusing cancellation under CONC 11.1.1R. You should check whether CONC 11.1.2R applies to exclude this agreement from the right to cancel: the key exclusions are (a) agreements to which s.66A CCA applies (right to withdraw — which itself covers regulated consumer credit agreements up to £60,260), (b) agreements secured by legal mortgage on land, and (c) restricted-use credit agreements to finance land purchase or bridging loans. A £8,000 unsecured personal loan taken over the phone does not appear to fall within any of these exclusions, but you must verify this against the specific agreement terms. Note: if s.66A CCA applies instead, the customer may have a right to withdraw under that section rather than under CONC 11.1.1R — CONC 11.1.3G notes these are alternative routes. Either way, the customer appears to have a right to exit the agreement within 14 days.

**2. What the customer can be required to pay on cancellation**
CONC 11.1.11R provides that on cancellation, the customer may only be required to pay for the service actually provided in proportion to the full contract, and the amount must not be a penalty. Critically, under CONC 11.1.11R(2), the firm cannot require any payment if it commenced performance before expiry of the cancellation period without the consumer's prior request. You will need to establish whether the customer requested early disbursement or whether the bank disbursed the funds without such a request. If the bank disbursed without the customer's prior request to begin performance, no charge at all may be levied. The adviser's instruction that the customer must repay the full loan with all interest over the term is inconsistent with CONC 11.1.11R on its face.

**3. Refund obligation**
CONC 11.1.12R requires the firm to return any sums received from the consumer (less any permitted charge under CONC 11.1.11R) without undue delay and within 30 calendar days of receiving the cancellation notification. The customer's call on 22 March may constitute notification — you should check whether that call was logged and whether the 30-day clock has started. If the cancellation was not processed, the clock may not have been treated as running, but the customer's attempt to cancel on 22 March is the relevant date. CONC 11.1.13R also entitles the firm to receive back from the consumer any sums or property received, within 30 days of the consumer dispatching cancellation notice — so the loan principal would need to be repaid by the customer, but without the interest charges over the term.

**4. Call charges complaint**
CONC 8.7.7R notes that firms should be aware of the call charges rule in GEN 7. The customer reports being charged over £3 on an 0844 number to reach the complaints line. You should check GEN 7 separately (it is not fully reproduced in the retrieved provisions) but this is a potential additional regulatory breach that should be investigated.

**5. Vulnerability**
The customer is 74 years old, on a fixed pension income, and reports significant financial distress. This is a clear indicator of potential vulnerability. The firm's obligations under its vulnerability policies and the Consumer Duty apply. This must be factored into how the complaint is handled and the urgency of resolution.

**Recommended action:**
- Treat the 22 March call as the cancellation notification and investigate whether it was logged.
- Confirm whether the loan agreement is excluded from CONC 11.1.1R by CONC 11.1.2R (check agreement type and whether s.66A CCA applies as an alternative route).
- Establish whether the customer requested early disbursement before the cancellation period expired — this determines whether any charge can be levied under CONC 11.1.11R(2).
- Do not instruct the customer to repay interest over the full term; this is inconsistent with CONC 11.1.11R.
- Investigate the 0844 call charge issue under GEN 7.
- Consider the customer's vulnerability and the urgency of resolution given their fixed income and distress.
- This complaint must go to human review before any response is sent, given the financial redress implications, vulnerability indicators, and potential regulatory breaches.

**Assumptions to confirm:** (a) The loan is an unsecured personal credit agreement not excluded by CONC 11.1.2R; (b) the 22 March call was or can be treated as a valid cancellation notification; (c) whether the customer requested early disbursement of funds before the cancellation period expired; (d) the current date relative to the 30-day refund window under CONC 11.1.12R.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for contacting us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced and the distress this situation has caused you. I want to assure you that we are taking your complaint very seriously.

You have raised a number of important concerns: that your attempt to cancel your personal loan on 22 March was refused; that you have since been charged interest on a loan you sought to cancel within the permitted timeframe; and that you incurred call charges when contacting our complaints line. I am writing to acknowledge your complaint and to let you know what happens next.

**Your right to cancel**

When a personal loan is arranged remotely — for example, by telephone — you have the right to cancel the agreement within 14 calendar days, without giving any reason and without penalty. This cancellation period begins from the date the contract is made, or from the date you receive the full contractual terms and conditions, whichever is later. Your contact with us on 22 March falls within that window, and we are investigating why your cancellation was not processed at that time.

**What you would be required to pay on cancellation**

If your cancellation is confirmed as valid, you would be required to repay the loan principal. However, you should not be required to pay interest charges over the full term of the loan as a condition of cancellation — the rules on what a firm may charge on cancellation are clear that any amount must reflect only the service actually provided and must not operate as a penalty. We are reviewing the specific circumstances, including whether any charge is permissible at all in your case.

**Call charges**

We are also investigating your concern about the cost of calling our complaints line. We take our obligations regarding telephone charges seriously and will address this as part of our review.

**Next steps**

We are treating your complaint as a priority. A member of our complaints team will be in contact with you within [X working days] to provide a full response. Given the circumstances you have described, we want to resolve this as quickly as possible.

If you remain dissatisfied with our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with full details of how to do this when we send our final response.

Once again, I am sorry for the distress and inconvenience this has caused you.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Establishes the 14-calendar-day right to cancel a distance credit agreement without penalty and without giving any reason, which the customer sought to exercise on 22 March.
Sets out the exclusions from the right to cancel under CONC 11.1.1R for certain credit agreements; must be checked to confirm the customer's loan is not excluded.
Explains the relationship between the CONC 11 cancellation right and the s.66A CCA right to withdraw, noting a customer may choose between them where both apply.
Defines when the 14-day cancellation period begins, relevant to confirming the customer's 22 March call was within the window.

Limits what the firm may charge on cancellation to a proportionate amount for services actually provided, and prohibits any charge if the firm commenced performance before expiry of the cancellation period without the consumer's prior request.
Requires the firm to return any sums received from the consumer (less permitted charges) without undue delay and within 30 calendar days of receiving the cancellation notification.

Entitles the firm to receive back from the consumer any sums received under the contract within 30 days of the consumer dispatching cancellation notice, relevant to the repayment of the loan principal.
Requires firms to note the effect of the call charges rule in GEN 7, relevant to the customer's complaint about the cost of the 0844 complaints line number.

# CONC — CONC 11.1 The right to cancel

## CONC 11.1.1 (Rules)

Except as provided for in CONC 11.1.2 R or where PROF 5.4.1 R (1) or PROF 5.4.1 R (2) applies, a consumer has a right to cancel a distance contract without penalty and without giving any reason, within 14 calendar days where that contract is:

(1) a credit agreement;

(2) an agreement between a consumer and a firm the subject matter of which comprises or relates to credit broking, debt counselling, debt adjusting, providing credit information services or providing credit references, other than an agreement that relates to any of those activities in relation to a consumer hire agreement.

[Note: article 6(1) of the Distance Marketing Directive in relation to distance contracts that are consumer credit agreements]

metadata.section_name: CONC 11.1 The right to cancel

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 8.7 Charging for debt counselling, debt advice and related services

## CONC 8.7.1 (Guidance)

(1) The distance marketing rules in CONC 2.6, including the right to cancel in CONC 11, apply to firms with respect to distance contracts which are credit agreements, consumer hire agreements and agreements the subject matter of which comprises, or relates to, debt counselling, debt adjusting, providing credit information services and providing credit references. CONC 11 excludes various credit agreements from the right to cancel.

(2) Where a consumer uses the right to cancel under CONC 11 or under the Financial Services (Distance Marketing) Regulations 2004 to cancel an agreement with a firm to set up or administer a debt solution, the firm should refund any sum paid, less a charge that the firm is entitled to make under CONC 11.1.11 R or regulation 13(6) to (9) of those Regulations.[Note: paragraphs 3.29 and 3.31 of DMG]

(3) The firm may be entitled to impose a charge in (2) if the customer requested the firm to begin to carry out its service within the cancellation period (see CONC 11.1.1 R or regulation 10 of the Financial Services (Distance Marketing) Regulations 2004).

metadata.section_name: CONC 8.7 Charging for debt counselling, debt advice and related services

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 11.1 The right to cancel

## CONC 11.1.3 (Guidance)

Section 66A of the CCA (right to withdraw) does not apply to an agreement for credit exceeding £60,260 (unless the agreement is a residential renovation agreement) , an agreement secured on land, a restricted-use credit agreement to finance the purchase of land or an agreement for a bridging loan in connection with the purchase of land. Section 67 of the CCA (cancellable agreements) applies to regulated credit agreements (apart from agreements secured on land, restricted-use credit agreements to finance the purchase of land or agreements for a bridging loan in connection with the purchase of land and agreements covered by section 66A) and consumer hire agreements (to which this section does not apply) in the circumstances specified in the section. A customer with a right to cancel under section 67 of the CCA may choose to cancel the agreement under that section or under CONC 11.1.1 R.

## CONC 11.1.4 (Guidance)

A firm may provide longer or additional cancellation rights voluntarily but, if it does, these should be on terms at least as favourable to the customer as those in this chapter, unless the differences are clearly explained.

## CONC 11.1.5 (Rules)

The cancellation period begins:

(1) either from the day the distance contract is made; or

(2) from the day on which the consumer receives the contractual terms and conditions of the service and any other pre-contractual information required, as the case may be, under CONC 2.7.6 R or under CONC 2.7.12 R, if that is later than the date referred to in (1) above.[Note: article 6(1) of the Distance Marketing Directive in relation to distance contracts]

metadata.section_name: CONC 11.1 The right to cancel

# CONC — CONC 11.1 The right to cancel

## CONC 11.1.2 (Rules)

(1) For a credit agreement there is no right to cancel under CONC 11.1.1 R, unless (2) or (3) applies, in respect of:

(a) a regulated consumer credit agreement (within the meaning of that section) to which section 66A (right to withdraw) of the CCA applies;

(b) a credit agreement under which a lender provides credit to a consumer and where the consumer's obligation to repay is secured by a legal mortgage on land;

(c) a credit agreement cancelled under regulation 15(1) of the Consumer Protection (Distance Selling) Regulations 2000 (automatic cancellation of a related credit agreement) or under regulation 38 of the Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013 (effects of withdrawal or cancellation on ancillary contracts) ;

(d) a credit agreement cancelled under regulation 23 of the Timeshare, Holiday Products, Resale and Exchange Contracts Regulations 2010 (automatic termination of credit agreement); and

(e) a restricted-use credit agreement to finance the purchase of land or an existing building, or an agreement for a bridging loan in connection with the purchase of land or an existing building.

(2) There is a right to cancel under CONC 11.1.1 R where the lender has not complied with CONC 2.7.6 R (requirement to communicate terms and conditions etc), unless the distance contract falls with the exception in CONC 2.7.12 R and the firm has complied with the requirements of that rule.

(3) There is a right to cancel under CONC 11.1.1 R where the circumstances in CONC 2.7.12 R apply but the lender has not supplied all the contractual terms and conditions and information as required in CONC 2.7.12 R.

(4) In the case of a distance contract comprising an initial service agreement followed by successive operations or a series of separate operations of the same nature performed over time, the right to cancel under CONC 11.1.1R applies only to the initial service agr

retrieved_chunks.content:
# COBS — COBS 15.5 Special situations

## COBS 15.5.4 (Guidance)

When a consumer cancels a distance contract under this chapter, his notice may also operate to cancel any attached contract which is also a distance financial services contract unless the consumer gives notice that cancellation of the main contract is not to operate to cancel the attached contract (see regulation 12 of the Distance Marketing Regulations). Where relevant, this should be disclosed to the consumer along with other information on cancellation.

## COBS 15.5.5 (Guidance)

This chapter does not act to cancel distance contracts entered into by an appointed representative or where applicable, by a tied agent, as principal such as a distance contract to provide advisory services, but the Distance Marketing Regulations (regulations 9 to 13, see regulation 4(3)) may have this effect.

## COBS 15.5.6 (Guidance)

Where a life policy or unit bought on opening or transferring an ISA is cancellable, the right to cancel, or substitute right to withdraw, applies to the entire arrangement. For example, a maxi-ISA comprising a life policy in the stocks and shares component and a cash component would be cancellable as a whole with a cancellation period of 30 calendar days. However, a firm is free to give the consumer the option of cancelling individual components separately with the same cancellation period if it wishes.

metadata.section_name: COBS 15.5 Special situations

# CONC — CONC 11.1 The right to cancel

## CONC 11.1.11 (Rules)

(1) When a consumer exercises the right to cancel the consumer may only be required to pay, without any undue delay, for the service actually provided by the firm in accordance with the contract. The amount payable must not:

(a) exceed an amount which is in proportion to the extent of the service already provided in comparison with the full coverage of the contract;

(b) in any case be such that it could be construed as a penalty.[Note: article 7(1), (2) and (3) of the Distance Marketing Directive in relation to distance contracts]

(2) The firm may not require a consumer to pay any amount on the basis of this rule unless it can prove that the consumer was duly informed about the amount payable and, in conformity with the distance marketing disclosure rules (CONC 2.7.2 R to CONC 2.7.5 R). However, in no case may the firm require such payment if it has commenced the performance of the contract before expiry of the cancellation period without the consumer's prior request.[Note: article 7(1), (2) and (3) of the Distance Marketing Directive in relation to distance contracts]

## CONC 11.1.12 (Rules)

The firm must, without undue delay and within 30 calendar days, return to the consumer any sums it has received from the consumer except for any amount that the consumer may be required to pay under CONC 11.1.11 R. This period begins from the day on which the firm receives the notification of cancellation.

[Note: article 7(1), (2) and (3) of the Distance Marketing Directive in relation to distance contracts]

## CONC 11.1.13 (Rules)

The firm is entitled to receive from the consumer any sums or property the consumer has received from the firm without any undue delay and no later than within 30 calendar days. This period begins from the day on which the consumer dispatches the notification of cancellation.

[Note: article 7(5) of the Distance Marketing Directive in relation to distance contracts]

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 4.6 Cancellation of distance mortgage mediation contracts, distance home purchase mediation contracts and distance regulated sale and rent back mediation contracts

## MCOB 4.6.10 (Rules)

When a consumer exercises a right to cancel under MCOB 4.6.4 R:

(1) the firm must:

(a) pay to the consumer without delay, and no later than 30 days after the date on which the firm received notice of cancellation from him, any sums which he has paid to or for the benefit of the firm in connection with the contract (including sums paid by the consumer to agents of the firm) except for the amount referred to in (b);

(b) subject to (c), the firm is permitted to require the consumer to pay for the services it has actually provided in connection with the contract; the amount payable, however, must be in accordance with the sums which the consumer agreed to pay and must not:

(i) exceed an amount which is in proportion to the extent of the service already provided to the consumer by the firm; and

(ii) be such that it could be construed as a penalty;

(c) sub-paragraph (b) applies only if:

(i) where performance of the contract has commenced before expiry of the cancellation period, this was requested by the consumer ; and

(ii) the firm can demonstrate that the consumer was provided with details of the amount which he may be required to pay if exercising his right to cancel in accordance with MCOB 4 Annex 3(5).

(2) The firm is entitled to receive without delay, and no later than 30 days after the date on which the consumer posted or otherwise sent notice of cancellation to the firm any property that became the consumer's under the contract and any sums payable to the firm under (1)(b).

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 4.6 Cancellation of distance mortgage mediation contracts, distance home purchase mediation contracts and distance regulated sale and rent back mediation contracts

## MCOB 4.6.4 (Rules)

(1) A consumer has a right to cancel a distance mortgage mediation contract, a distance home purchase mediation contract or a distance regulated sale and rent back mediation contract in accordance with this section.

(2) The right to cancel must be exercised within 14 days beginning on the later of:

(a) the day of the conclusion of the contract; or

(b) the day on which the consumer receives the contractual terms and conditions and other information required by MCOB 4.4 and MCOB 4.5.

## MCOB 4.6.5 (Rules)

A consumer who has a right to cancel a distance mortgage mediation contract, a distance home purchase mediation contract or a distance regulated sale and rent back mediation contract may, without giving any reason, cancel the contract by serving notice on the firm, before the expiry of the cancellation period in MCOB 4.6.4 R either:

(1) by serving on, or otherwise sending by post, notice to the firm's last known address, addressed to the firm, its appointed representative or on any agent of the firm with authority to accept notice on the firm's behalf; or

(2) in accordance with any other practical instructions for exercising that right provided to the consumer in accordance with MCOB 4 Annex 3(5).

## MCOB 4.6.6 (Rules)

Where the notice of cancellation is in a durable medium and is served in accordance with MCOB 4.6.5 R, it must be treated as being served on the firm on the date it is despatched by the consumer .

## MCOB 4.6.7 (Guidance)

In the event of any dispute, unless there is clear written evidence to the contrary, the firm should treat the date cited by the consumer as being the date when notice was given, posted or otherwise sent.

# MCOB — MCOB 4.6 Cancellation of distance mortgage mediation contracts, distance home purchase mediation contracts and distance regulated sale and rent back mediation contracts

## MCOB 4.6.8 (Rules)

By exercising a right to cancel under MCOB 4.6.4 R the consumer withdraws from the contract and the entire contract is terminated.

## MCOB 4.6.9 (Guidance)

Regulation 11 (Automatic cancellation of an attached distance contract) of the Distance Marketing Regulations, has the effect that when notice of cancellation is given in relation to a contract, that notice also operates to cancel any attached contract, which is also a distance financial services contract. An example of such an attached contract might be a distance non-investment insurance contract.

verdicts.evidence: You have raised a number of important concerns: that your attempt to cancel your personal loan on 22 March was refused; that you have since been charged interest on a loan you sought to cancel within the permitted timeframe; and that you incurred call charges when contacting our complaints line.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately reflects all three substantive issues raised by the complainant: the refused cancellation, the ongoing interest charges, and the call charges.

This is labelled as a final response stage, but the customer draft does not state a decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). Instead, it says 'we are investigating' and promises a future 'full response.' No clear decision is communicated, and no final outcome is reached on any of the complaint points.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with full details of how to do this when we send our final response.
verdicts.reasoning: The draft mentions the right to refer to the FOS, but defers providing contact details/how to do so to a future final response. Since this is supposed to be the final response stage, the customer is not given the means to contact the FOS (no address, phone number, website, or leaflet reference). The mention is present but incomplete.
The customer draft does not state that the complainant has six months from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. No time limit for referral is mentioned at all.

Your contact with us on 22 March falls within that window, and we are investigating why your cancellation was not processed at that time.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages substantively with the complaint points, explains the customer's rights clearly, does not dismiss or minimise concerns, uses clear and supportive language, and addresses each issue raised. The tone is empathetic and even-handed.

verdicts.evidence: If your cancellation is confirmed as valid, you would be required to repay the loan principal. However, you should not be required to pay interest charges over the full term of the loan as a condition of cancellation... We are reviewing the specific circumstances, including whether any charge is permissible at all in your case.

It defers the final determination to a future response. The handler answer discusses redress in detail but the customer-facing text does not communicate a definitive position.

Customer draft: 'We are treating your complaint as a priority... Given the circumstances you have described, we want to resolve this as quickly as possible.' and 'I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced and the distress this situation has caused you.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer explicitly identifies the vulnerability indicators (age, fixed pension income, financial distress) and recommends urgency and appropriate handling. The customer draft adapts its tone to be supportive and empathetic, prioritises the complaint, and acknowledges the distress. Both elements together satisfy this requirement.

verdicts.evidence: You have raised a number of important concerns: that your attempt to cancel your personal loan on 22 March was refused; that you have since been charged interest on a loan you sought to cancel within the permitted timeframe; and that you incurred call charges when contacting our complaints line.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately restates the key issues raised.
The draft does not state a clear decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) on the complaint; it only says the matter is being investigated.

claims.claim: CONC 11.1.1R establishes a 14-calendar-day right to cancel a distance credit agreement without penalty and without giving any reason.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.1R as retrieved states a consumer has a right to cancel a distance contract without penalty and without giving any reason, within 14 calendar days, where that contract is a credit agreement.

claims.claim: The adviser's refusal to process the cancellation on the basis that funds had been disbursed and the customer had started using the credit does not appear to be a valid ground for refusing cancellation under CONC 11.1.1R.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.1R provides the right to cancel without penalty and without giving any reason within 14 days. The rule does not include disbursement of funds or use of credit as grounds for refusing cancellation.

claims.claim: CONC 11.1.2R may exclude the agreement from the right to cancel under CONC 11.1.1R, with key exclusions being (a) agreements to which s.66A CCA applies, (b) agreements secured by legal mortgage on land, and (c) restricted-use credit agreements to finance land purchase or bridging loans.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.2R(1) lists exclusions including (a) regulated consumer credit agreements to which s.66A CCA applies, (b) credit agreements secured by legal mortgage on land, and (e) restricted-use credit agreements to finance purchase of land or bridging loans. The handler's summary captures the main exclusions correctly.

claims.claim: Section 66A CCA (right to withdraw) does not apply to agreements for credit exceeding £60,260 (unless a residential renovation agreement), agreements secured on land, restricted-use credit agreements to finance the purchase of land, or bridging loans in connection with the purchase of land.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.3G states exactly this about the scope of s.66A CCA.

claims.claim: CONC 11.1.3G notes that s.66A CCA and CONC 11.1.1R are alternative routes for cancellation.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.3G discusses s.67 CCA as an alternative to CONC 11.1.1R (a customer may choose to cancel under s.67 or under CONC 11.1.1R). However, for s.66A, CONC 11.1.2R(1)(a) excludes agreements from CONC 11.1.1R where s.66A applies — they are not presented as alternative routes the customer can choose between, but rather s.66A displaces CONC 11.1.1R.

claims.claim: CONC 11.1.11R provides that on cancellation, the customer may only be required to pay for the service actually provided in proportion to the full contract, and the amount must not be a penalty.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.11R(1) states the consumer may only be required to pay for the service actually provided, the amount must not exceed a proportionate amount, and must not be construed as a penalty.

claims.claim: Under CONC 11.1.11R(2), the firm cannot require any payment if it commenced performance before expiry of the cancellation period without the consumer's prior request.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.11R(2) explicitly states: 'in no case may the firm require such payment if it has commenced the performance of the contract before expiry of the cancellation period without the consumer's prior request.'

claims.claim: The adviser's instruction that the customer must repay the full loan with all interest over the term is inconsistent with CONC 11.1.11R.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.11R limits what can be charged on cancellation to proportionate service actually provided and prohibits penalties. Requiring full interest over the term would exceed proportionate service and could be construed as a penalty.

claims.claim: CONC 11.1.12R requires the firm to return any sums received from the consumer (less any permitted charge under CONC 11.1.11R) without undue delay and within 30 calendar days of receiving the cancellation notification.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.12R states: 'The firm must, without undue delay and within 30 calendar days, return to the consumer any sums it has received from the consumer except for any amount that the consumer may be required to pay under CONC 11.1.11 R. This period begins from the day on which the firm receives the notification of cancellation.'

claims.claim: CONC 11.1.13R entitles the firm to receive back from the consumer any sums or property received, within 30 days of the consumer dispatching cancellation notice.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.13R states: 'The firm is entitled to receive from the consumer any sums or property the consumer has received from the firm without any undue delay and no later than within 30 calendar days. This period begins from the day on which the consumer dispatches the notification of cancellation.'

claims.claim: The loan principal would need to be repaid by the customer, but without the interest charges over the term.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.13R entitles the firm to receive back sums the consumer received (i.e., the loan principal). CONC 11.1.11R limits charges to proportionate service actually provided, which would not include full-term interest.

claims.claim: CONC 8.7.7R notes that firms should be aware of the call charges rule in GEN 7.
claims.reasoning: CONC 8.7.7 states: 'Firms should note the effect of the call charges rule in GEN 7.'

claims.claim: When a personal loan is arranged remotely — for example, by telephone — you have the right to cancel the agreement within 14 calendar days, without giving any reason and without penalty.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.1R provides a right to cancel a distance contract (which includes one arranged by telephone) that is a credit agreement within 14 calendar days without penalty and without giving any reason.

claims.claim: The cancellation period begins from the date the contract is made, or from the date you receive the full contractual terms and conditions, whichever is later.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.5R states the cancellation period begins from the day the distance contract is made, or from the day the consumer receives the contractual terms and conditions and other pre-contractual information, if that is later.

claims.claim: If your cancellation is confirmed as valid, you would be required to repay the loan principal.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.13R entitles the firm to receive back from the consumer any sums or property the consumer has received from the firm, which would include the loan principal.

claims.claim: You should not be required to pay interest charges over the full term of the loan as a condition of cancellation — the rules on what a firm may charge on cancellation are clear that any amount must reflect only the service actually provided and must not operate as a penalty.
claims.reasoning: CONC 11.1.11R(1) states the amount payable must not exceed a proportionate amount relative to service already provided and must not be construed as a penalty. Full-term interest would violate both conditions.

claims.reasoning: None of the retrieved provisions address the right to refer complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service. While this is a well-known regulatory right under DISP rules, it is not supported by any of the retrieved chunks.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am a 72-year-old retired schoolteacher with no prior investment experience beyond a basic savings account. In January 2024, I visited my bank branch to discuss options for my £45,000 in savings. The adviser suggested I purchase a structured product linked to equity indices, describing it as 'like a savings account but with better returns.' I was not asked any questions about my investment knowledge, experience with financial instruments, or understanding of risks. The adviser simply told me to sign the application form. I have since discovered the product carries significant capital-at-risk and I do not understand how it works. I have already lost approximately £3,200 in value. I believe the bank failed to check whether this product was appropriate for someone like me, and I want my money back. I am also finding this situation very stressful as I rely on my savings for day-to-day living in retirement and am struggling financially as a result of this loss.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises serious concerns about appropriateness assessment, suitability, and potential mis-selling of a structured product to a 72-year-old retired customer with no investment experience. However, the retrieved provisions do not adequately cover the core regulatory requirements that would govern this complaint — specifically, the COBS rules on appropriateness assessment (COBS 10A), suitability (COBS 9), and the DISP complaints handling rules. The retrieved chunks relate primarily to risk summaries for high-risk investments (COBS 4 Annex 1), basic advice on stakeholder products (COBS 9.6), and pension drawdown warnings — none of which directly address the appropriateness/suitability obligations that are central to this complaint.

The DISP App 1.4 chunk retrieved (dispapp1s4-006) does touch on redress calculation for unsuitable sales, but only in the context of endowment mortgage mis-selling, not structured products.

Given the insufficient retrieved context, I am flagging this for mandatory human review. Do not send any customer-facing response until the following has been confirmed:

1. COBS 10A appropriateness rules: Was an appropriateness assessment conducted before the sale? The customer explicitly states no questions were asked about knowledge or experience. This is a potential breach of the appropriateness assessment obligation for non-advised sales of complex instruments, or a suitability breach if the sale was advised.
2. Advised vs non-advised: Was this sale advised (triggering COBS 9 suitability rules) or execution-only/non-advised (triggering COBS 10A appropriateness rules)? The description ('adviser suggested') strongly suggests this was an advised sale, which would engage full suitability obligations.
3. Vulnerability: The customer is 72, retired, relying on savings for day-to-day living, has no investment experience, and is reporting financial stress and distress. This is a strong vulnerability indicator requiring heightened care under the Consumer Duty (Principle 12 / PRIN 2A) — though those provisions are not in the retrieved context.
4. Redress: The customer is seeking return of their money. Any redress decision must be made by a human reviewer with access to the full file, the product terms, and the applicable suitability/appropriateness rules.
5. Regulatory deadlines: Confirm when the complaint was received and whether the 8-week final response deadline under DISP 1 is approaching or has been missed.

A conservative acknowledgement letter is drafted below, but it must not be sent without human review. It does not admit liability, quantify redress, or make commitments beyond what the retrieved provisions support.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for contacting us about your concerns regarding the structured product you purchased in January 2024. I am sorry to hear that this situation has caused you significant stress, particularly given that you rely on your savings for day-to-day living.

We take your concerns very seriously. You have told us that when you visited the branch, you were not asked about your investment knowledge, your experience with financial products, or your understanding of the risks involved, and that the product was described to you in a way that did not reflect the risks it carries. You have also told us that you have experienced a loss in value and that you do not understand how the product works. We understand how distressing this must be.

We are now investigating your complaint carefully. As part of that investigation, we will look at the circumstances of the sale, including what information was provided to you and what steps were taken to understand your situation before the product was recommended or sold to you.

We will write to you again with our findings. We aim to provide you with a full response as quickly as possible.

If you have any questions in the meantime, or if there is anything further you would like to tell us about your circumstances, please do not hesitate to contact us at [contact details].

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Bank name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Provides guidance on redress calculation methodology for unsuitable sales where investment risk was not properly explained, which is relevant context for the redress aspect of this complaint, though it relates to endowment mortgages rather than structured products.

# COBS — COBS 9 Annex 2 Sales processes for stakeholder products

If a retail client is willing to accept the risk of capital reduction in some circumstances but not others then, before any recommendation to acquire a CIS stakeholder product or linked life stakeholder product is made, the retail client should be reminded of the other circumstances in which he or she is unwilling to accept risk to capital.

Stakeholder pensions

16.

A stakeholder pension scheme should not be recommended, and the retail client should be advised to seek alternative or further advice, if it appears that the retail client:

(a)

has or will have access to an occupational pension scheme; or

(b)

is likely to view income in retirement from state benefits as sufficient; or

(c)

already has a pension to which he or she could make further contributions; or

(d)

wishes to retire within five years.

17.

It may also be appropriate to advise the retail client that other courses of action may be more beneficial than buying a stakeholder pension scheme (for example joining an occupational pension scheme).

18.

A firm designing a sales process for use in the workplace may take account of the benefits offered by the employer. If a firm recommends a stakeholder pension scheme on the basis of benefits provided by an employer, then it should explain the basis of the recommendation to the retail client and suggest that the retail client seek advice if he or she has any concerns.

19.

A firm should design its processes with a view to addressing the risk that retail clients will fail to appreciate the significance of questions about their pension provision and should accordingly incorporate a range of questions and information designed to foster the retail client's understanding of the issues and to elicit appropriate information.

20.

Retail client should be told that a stakeholder pension scheme is life-styled and what this means.

21.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# COBS — COBS 9.6 Special rules for giving basic advice on a stakeholder product

## COBS 9.6.10 (Rules)

When a firm gives basic advice it must not:

(1) describe or recommend a stakeholder product outside the firm's range; or

(2) describe or recommend a smoothed linked long term stakeholder product; or

(3) describe fund choice, or recommend a particular fund, if a stakeholder product offers a choice of funds; or

(4) recommend the level of contributions required to be made to a stakeholder pension scheme to achieve a specific income in retirement; or

(5) recommend or agree that a client makes a contribution to an ISA which exceeds the HM Revenue & Customs ISA limits.

## COBS 9.6.11 (Rules)

(1) If a firm starts the sales process for a stakeholder product that is not a deposit-based stakeholder product, it must not depart from that process unless it has advised the retail client that it will not provide basic advice on stakeholder products during the period of departure. A firm that does that must not provide basic advice during the departure period.

(2) Before a firm returns to the sales process for stakeholder products, it must tell the retail client that that process is about to recommence.

## COBS 9.6.12 (Rules)

A firm must only recommend a stakeholder product to a retail client if:

(1) it has taken reasonable steps to assess the client's answers to the scripted questions and any other facts, circumstances or information disclosed by the client during the sales process;

(2) (unless the relevant product is a deposit-based stakeholder product) having done so, it has reasonable grounds for believing that the stakeholder product is suitable for the client; and

(3) the firm reasonably believes that the client understands the firm's advice and the basis on which it was provided.

## COBS 9.6.13 (Guidance)

COBS 9 Annex 2 gives guidance on the steps a firm could take to help it meet these suitability obligations.

# COBS — COBS 19.10 Drawdown, investment pathways and cash warnings

## COBS 19.10.39 (Guidance)

The firm should also:

(1) (if appropriate) inform the retail client that:

(a) this warning is not advice or a substitute for it;

(b) the value of any investment can fall as well as rise;

(2) explain to and/or illustrate for the retail client that different types of investment have a different balance of risk to potential gain;

(3) provide the retail client with a statement to the effect that (to the extent applicable) the firm offers pathway investments and other investments; and

(4) remind the retail client (in line with the requirements in COBS 19.4) that the retail client can:

(a) shop around (with an explanation of how to do that);

(b) seek advice from a FCA-regulated financial adviser; and

(c) review information on the MoneyHelper website, available on https://www.moneyhelper.org.uk .

## COBS 19.10.40 (Guidance)

In the FCA’s view, the Consumer Prices Index is a national index of retail prices and so may be used as a measure of the current inflation rate for the purposes of COBS 19.10.38R(3).

## COBS 19.10.41 (Guidance)

This section sets out when and how warnings must be given to retail clients who have purchased certain fixed-term products, and what the warnings should include.

## COBS 19.10.42 (Rules)

COBS 19.10.43R applies where:

(1) a non-advised retail client has purchased a fixed-term product within a personal pension scheme or stakeholder pension scheme;

(2) at the end of the fixed term, that product has a fixed cash value payable to the retail client or the retail client’s beneficiary; and

(3) the retail client has not given the firm instructions to transfer the full value out of the product.

# DISP — DISP App 1.4 Policy reconstruction

## DISP App 1.4.15 (Guidance)

Example 9

Example 9

Term extends beyond retirement age: example of failure to explain investment risks

Background

45 year old male non-smoker, having taken out a £50,000 loan in 1998 for a term of 25 years. Unsuitable sale identified on the grounds of affordability and complaint raised on 12th anniversary.

It has always been the intention of the complainant to retire at state retirement age 65.

Term from date of sale to retirement is 20 years and the maturity date of the mortgage is five years after retirement.

In addition, an endowment does not meet the complainant's attitude to investment risk and a repayment mortgage would have been taken out if properly advised.

Established facts

Surrender value (on the 25 year policy) at time complaint assessed:

£12,500

Capital repaid under repayment mortgage of term to retirement date (20 years):

£21,000

Surrender value less capital repaid:

(£8.500)

Difference in outgoings (repayment - endowment):

£5,400

Cost of converting from endowment mortgage to repayment mortgage:

£200

Basis of compensation:

The surrender value of the (25 year term) endowment policy is compared to the capital that would have been repaid to date under a repayment mortgage arranged to repay the loan at retirement age, in this example, a repayment mortgage for a term of 20 years. The complainant has gained from lower outgoings of the endowment mortgage to date. In calculating the redress, the gain may be offset against the loss unless the complainant's particular circumstances are such that it would be unreasonable to take account of the gain. The conversion costs are also taken into account in calculating the redress.

Redress generally

Loss from surrender value less capital repaid:

(£8,500)

Gain from total lower outgoings under endowment mortgage:

£5,400

Cost of converting to a repayment mortgage:

(£200)

Net loss:

(£3,300)

Therefore total redress is:

£

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for contacting us about your concerns regarding the structured product you purchased in January 2024. I am sorry to hear that this situation has caused you significant stress... You have told us that when you visited the branch, you were not asked about your investment knowledge, your experience with financial products, or your understanding of the risks involved, and that the product was described to you in a way that did not reflect the risks it carries.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately reflects the subject matter — the structured product sale, the lack of appropriateness assessment, the misleading description, the financial loss, and the stress caused.

This is labelled as a final response stage, but the customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). It says 'We are now investigating your complaint carefully' and 'We will write to you again with our findings,' which is the language of a holding response, not a final response. No decision or reasons are communicated.

The customer-facing draft makes no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service or the complainant's right to refer the complaint to the FOS. For a final response, this is a mandatory inclusion under DISP 1.6.2R.
There is no mention of the six-month time limit for referring the complaint to the FOS from the date of the final response anywhere in the customer draft.

verdicts.evidence: We take your concerns very seriously... We are now investigating your complaint carefully. As part of that investigation, we will look at the circumstances of the sale, including what information was provided to you and what steps were taken to understand your situation before the product was recommended or sold to you.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is empathetic, non-dismissive, and engages with the specifics of the complaint. However, for a final response, fair treatment requires actually reaching a conclusion on the merits. The response only promises further investigation without resolving the complaint, which is inadequate for a final response and therefore only partially meets the fair treatment standard.

The customer explicitly requested their money back and reported a £3,200 loss. The customer draft does not address redress at all — no offer, no explanation of why none is due, and no position communicated. The handler answer flags redress for human review but this is not reflected in any customer-facing outcome.

verdicts.evidence: Handler: 'The customer is 72, retired, relying on savings for day-to-day living, has no investment experience, and is reporting financial stress and distress. This is a strong vulnerability indicator requiring heightened care under the Consumer Duty.' Customer draft: 'I am sorry to hear that this situation has caused you significant stress, particularly given that you rely on your savings for day-to-day living... We understand how distressing this must be.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer explicitly identifies the vulnerability indicators and flags the need for heightened care. The customer draft adapts its language to be supportive and empathetic, acknowledging the financial reliance and stress. Both recognition and action are present.

verdicts.evidence: Thank you for contacting us about your concerns regarding the structured product you purchased in January 2024.
verdicts.reasoning: The opening sentence acknowledges the complaint and accurately reflects the product and date the customer is complaining about.
The customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor provide reasons for a decision.
There is no reference to the Financial Ombudsman Service or how the complainant can refer the complaint.
The draft does not mention the six‑month period within which the complainant may refer the matter to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: We take your concerns very seriously... We understand how distressing this must be. We are now investigating your complaint carefully.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the specific points raised, uses clear and empathetic language, and does not dismiss or minimise the complainant’s concerns.
The draft does not address whether any redress or remedial action will be offered, nor does it explain why none would be offered.

verdicts.evidence: The customer is 72, retired, relying on savings for day‑to‑day living, has no investment experience, and is reporting financial stress and distress. This is a strong vulnerability indicator requiring heightened care...
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer recognises the complainant’s vulnerability indicators and notes the need for heightened care, satisfying the ‘either’ audience requirement.

claims.claim: The complaint raises concerns about appropriateness assessment under COBS 10A.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision (cobs10as4-003) relates to COBS 10A.4 (when appropriateness assessment need not be done), which is part of the COBS 10A framework. However, it only covers exemptions from the appropriateness assessment, not the core obligation to conduct one.

claims.claim: The complaint raises concerns about suitability under COBS 9.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provisions cover COBS 9.6 (basic advice on stakeholder products) and COBS 9 Annex 2 (sales processes for stakeholder products), which are part of the COBS 9 suitability framework but do not cover the general suitability obligations for advised sales of structured products.

claims.claim: The retrieved chunks relate primarily to risk summaries for high-risk investments (COBS 4 Annex 1), basic advice on stakeholder products (COBS 9.6), and pension drawdown warnings.
claims.reasoning: This is an accurate characterization of the retrieved provisions: multiple chunks are from COBS 4 Annex 1 (risk summaries), COBS 9.6/9 Annex 2 (stakeholder products/basic advice), and COBS 19.10 (drawdown/cash warnings).

claims.claim: DISP App 1.4 chunk touches on redress calculation for unsuitable sales, but only in the context of endowment mortgage mis-selling, not structured products.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved DISP App 1.4.15 provision explicitly provides an example of redress calculation for an endowment mortgage unsuitable sale, not structured products.

claims.claim: COBS 10A contains appropriateness assessment obligations requiring firms to assess knowledge and experience before non-advised sales of complex instruments.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision (COBS 10A.4) addresses when appropriateness assessment need not be done, implying that such an obligation exists for complex instruments. However, the core obligation itself (COBS 10A.2) requiring the assessment is not directly retrieved.

claims.claim: If the sale was advised, full suitability obligations under COBS 9 are engaged.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved COBS 9.6.12 provision shows that suitability obligations apply when recommending products (at least for stakeholder products under basic advice). The general COBS 9 suitability obligation for advised sales is not directly retrieved.

claims.claim: The Consumer Duty (Principle 12 / PRIN 2A) requires heightened care for vulnerable customers.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved provision covers the Consumer Duty, Principle 12, or PRIN 2A, nor vulnerability obligations.

claims.claim: There is an 8-week final response deadline under DISP 1 for complaints handling.
claims.reasoning: No retrieved provision covers the DISP 1 complaints handling time limits or the 8-week deadline.

claims.claim: The firm is investigating the complaint including what information was provided and what steps were taken to understand the customer's situation before the product was recommended or sold.
claims.reasoning: The concept of assessing a client's situation before recommending a product is reflected in COBS 9.6.12 (requiring reasonable steps to assess client answers and reasonable grounds for believing suitability). However, this provision is specific to stakeholder products and basic advice, not structured products generally.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I remortgaged my property last month and was charged an early repayment charge (ERC) of £8,400 by my previous lender. When I queried this, I was told it was calculated using a method they couldn't clearly explain to me — they just said it was 'standard practice' and couldn't give me a breakdown showing it as a specific cash value or explain what costs it was meant to cover. I also never recall seeing this charge disclosed in my original mortgage illustration or offer document when I took the product out three years ago. I called to complain on Monday morning and spoke to someone who said they'd 'look into it and get back to me' but I've heard nothing since. It's now been over three weeks. I'm 74 years old and on a fixed pension income, and this unexpected charge has put me into significant financial difficulty — I had to use my savings set aside for care costs to cover it. I want the charge refunded or at minimum a clear justification showing it meets the regulatory requirements.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several distinct regulatory concerns under MCOB that you must address carefully before responding to the customer. Here is the position on each:

**1. Validity of the ERC (MCOB 12.3.1R)**
MCOB 12.3.1R requires that any ERC on a regulated mortgage contract must (a) be expressible as a cash value, and (b) be a reasonable pre-estimate of the costs to the lender from early repayment. The customer's account — that the lender could not express the charge as a cash value or explain what costs it was meant to cover — if accurate, would indicate a potential breach of this binding rule. You must investigate whether the £8,400 charge meets both limbs of MCOB 12.3.1R. Obtain the lender's calculation methodology and assess whether it can be expressed as a cash value and whether it represents a reasonable pre-estimate of costs. Note also that MCOB 12.3.2G states that the 'Rule of 78' method should not be used as it overstates costs — check whether that or a similarly inappropriate method was applied.

**2. Disclosure in the original illustration and offer document (MCOB 12.3.4R and MCOB 5.6.84R)**
MCOB 12.3.4R requires that before entering into a regulated mortgage contract, the firm must disclose the maximum ERC as a cash amount in both the pre-contract illustration (under MCOB 5) and the offer document illustration (under MCOB 6.4.1R). MCOB 5.6.84R(1)(g) requires the illustration to show 'the maximum charge you could pay' as a cash amount. If the customer genuinely did not receive this disclosure — either in the original illustration or the offer document — this would be a further potential breach of binding rules. You must retrieve the original illustration and offer document to verify whether the ERC was disclosed in the required form.

**3. Complaint handling delay**
The customer says they complained on Monday three weeks ago and have heard nothing since. You must check your internal records to confirm the exact date of receipt. The retrieved provisions do not include DISP timescale rules, so I cannot confirm the precise deadlines from the retrieved context — this is a gap in the retrieved provisions. However, given the three-week silence, there is a real risk that acknowledgement and/or response deadlines under DISP have been or are about to be missed. Flag this urgently for your complaints team to check against your DISP obligations.

**4. Customer vulnerability**
The customer is 74 years old, on a fixed pension income, and has stated that the unexpected charge has caused significant financial difficulty — specifically that they had to use savings set aside for care costs. This is a clear indicator of potential vulnerability (financial difficulty, age, reliance on fixed income, impact on care provision). This must be factored into how you handle and prioritise this complaint. The complaint should be escalated and handled with appropriate care.

**5. Redress**
The customer is seeking a refund of the £8,400 or a clear justification. Whether a refund is appropriate depends on the outcome of your investigation into MCOB 12.3.1R compliance and the disclosure investigation. Do not commit to a refund or any specific remedy in the customer letter at this stage — the draft below acknowledges the complaint and confirms investigation without making financial commitments.

**Assumptions you must confirm before sending the customer draft:**
- Confirm the exact date the complaint was received to assess DISP deadline compliance.
- Confirm whether this is a regulated mortgage contract (the MCOB rules apply to regulated mortgage contracts; if it is an MCD regulated mortgage contract, MCOB 2A.4 and related provisions also apply).
- Confirm whether the original illustration and offer document are retrievable.
- Consider whether a vulnerability flag should be raised on the customer's file and whether any additional support should be offered.
- Human review is required before sending given the financial impact, potential vulnerability, possible regulatory breaches, and potential redress implications.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for contacting us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced following your remortgage, and I want to assure you that we are taking your concerns seriously.

I am writing to confirm that we have received your complaint, which covers the following concerns:

- The early repayment charge of £8,400 applied to your previous mortgage, including whether it was calculated correctly and whether a clear explanation and breakdown was provided to you;
- Whether this charge was properly disclosed to you in your original mortgage illustration and offer document when you took out the product; and
- The fact that you have not received a substantive response since you raised your concerns.

I also want to acknowledge that you have told us this charge has caused you significant financial difficulty. We take that seriously and will ensure your complaint is handled with the care and priority it deserves.

**What we are doing**

We are now investigating your complaint fully. As part of this, we will:

- Review how the early repayment charge was calculated, including whether it can be expressed as a specific cash amount and whether it represents a reasonable pre-estimate of the costs arising from your early repayment — as our regulator's rules require;
- Check the original mortgage illustration and offer document provided to you at the time you took out the mortgage, to confirm whether the maximum early repayment charge was disclosed to you in the required form and as a cash amount; and
- Review the handling of your complaint since you first raised it.

**Next steps**

We will write to you again with the outcome of our investigation. If you have any questions in the meantime, or if there is anything further you would like to tell us about your circumstances, please do not hesitate to contact us at [contact details].

If you remain dissatisfied with our response once we have completed our investigation, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further information about this when we send you our final response.

Once again, I am sorry for the distress and inconvenience this matter has caused you.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Firm Name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires that any ERC on a regulated mortgage contract must be expressible as a cash value and be a reasonable pre-estimate of the lender's costs — the customer's account that neither condition was met raises a potential breach of this binding rule.
The FCA indicates that the Rule of 78 method should not be used for ERC calculation as it overstates costs — relevant to assessing whether the lender's unexplained methodology was appropriate.

Requires the firm to disclose the maximum ERC as a cash amount in both the pre-contract illustration and the offer document illustration before entering into the regulated mortgage contract — the customer states this disclosure was not made.

Requires the illustration for a regulated mortgage contract to include, under the heading on early repayment charges, the maximum ERC shown as a cash amount described as 'the maximum charge you could pay' — relevant to assessing whether the original illustration complied.

Requires that a consumer under an MCD regulated mortgage contract has the right to discharge obligations early, with a corresponding reduction in total cost of credit — relevant if the mortgage is an MCD regulated mortgage contract.

Confirms that the right to early repayment does not prevent an ERC being imposed, provided it complies with MCOB 12.3.1R — relevant to the interaction between the early repayment right and the ERC validity question.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 12.3 Early repayment charges: regulated mortgage contracts

## MCOB 12.3.1 (Rules)

A firm must ensure that any regulated mortgage contract that it enters into does not impose, and cannot be used to impose, an early repayment charge other than one that is:

(1) able to be expressed as a cash value; and

(2) a reasonable pre-estimate of the costs as a result of the customer repaying the amount due under the regulated mortgage contract before the contract has terminated.

## MCOB 12.3.2 (Guidance)

A firm can choose the method it employs for calculating early repayment charges in accordance with MCOB 12.3.1 R. A firm should not use the 'Rule of 78' , which is not appropriate as it effectively overstates the cost to the mortgage lender.

## MCOB 12.3.3 (Guidance)

A firm may calculate the same level of early repayment charge for all regulated mortgage contracts of a similar type (for example a tranche of regulated mortgage contracts offering a particular fixed rate of interest), rather than on the basis of the individual regulated mortgage contract with the particular customer.

## MCOB 12.3.4 (Rules)

Before:

(1) entering into a regulated mortgage contract with a customer; or

(2) making a further advance on an existing regulated mortgage contract; or

(3) changing all or part of a regulated mortgage contract from one interest rate to another; a firm must disclose to the customer:

(a) in the illustration provided in accordance with MCOB 5, MCOB 7.6.7 R, MCOB 7.6.18 R, MCOB 7.6.22 R, MCOB 7.6.31 R, or MCOB 9; and

(b) in the illustration provided as part of the offer document in accordance with MCOB 6.4.1 R(1) and MCOB 9.5;

the maximum amount payable as an early repayment charge in respect of that regulated mortgage contract, if an early repayment charge applies.

# MCOB — MCOB 2A.4 Early repayment

## MCOB 2A.4.1 (Rules)

(1) An MCD mortgage lender must give a consumer who enters into an MCD regulated mortgage contract the right to discharge fully or partially his obligations under that MCD regulated mortgage contract prior to its expiry.

(2) If the consumer exercises the right in (1), the MCD mortgage lender must reduce the total cost of the credit to the consumer by an amount equal to the interest and costs for the remaining duration of the MCD regulated mortgage contract.

[Note: article 25(1) of the MCD]

## MCOB 2A.4.2 (Rules)

MCOB 2A.4.1R (1) does not prevent an MCD mortgage lender from imposing an early repayment charge in accordance with MCOB 12.3.1 R.

## MCOB 2A.4.3 (Guidance)

MCOB 7A.3 sets out the MCOB disclosure rules in relation to early repayment.

# MCOB — MCOB 5.6 Content of illustrations

## MCOB 5.6.88 (Rules)

(1) In calculating example cash amounts in accordance with MCOB 5.6.84 R(1)(f), it must be assumed that:

(a) the regulated mortgage contract is repaid in full;

(b) unless the original amount borrowed is used, that all payments due on the regulated mortgage contract are actually paid;

(c) additional fees and charges such as insurance premiums have been paid; and

(d) no underpayments or overpayments have been made.

(2) If:

(a) cashbacks or other incentives need to be repaid; or

(b) fees need to be paid;

the amounts that would need to be repaid or paid must be included in the example cash amounts.

(3) Where the calculation of the early repayment charge is based on the interest rate charged on the regulated mortgage contract or on interest rates generally, the interest rates used for the calculation of the example cash amounts must be those in force at the date that the illustration is issued to the customer.

(4) The example cash amounts must reflect the maximum charge in a particular year. Where it is possible to state exact early repayment charges (that is, where all such charges are based on the original amount borrowed), the illustration must do so.

## MCOB 5.6.89 (Rules)

Where the cash examples from MCOB 5.6.88 R included in the illustration would vary either if the interest rate charged on the regulated mortgage contract changed or with changes in interest rates generally, an appropriate warning that the early repayment charges may vary from the cash examples must be included in the illustration.
