MCD mortgage lender has different ERR calculation methods that apply over time (eg, 0.5% over Bank of England rate for the first two years and then 2% over Bank of England rate for the rest of the mortgage lifetime).

Calculate using the method which produces the highest additional APRC.

MCD mortgage lender has different methods that apply to different proportions of the principal (eg, ERR + x% applies to 50% principal and SVR applies to the other 50%)

Calculate using the ERR where applicable and the FCA's benchmark rate, where applicable, and use both to calculate the additional APRC.

MCD mortgage lender uses an ERR where its basis has changed in the past 20 years

Consider whether there was an equivalent predecessor ERR and use the ERR (and its equivalent predecessor(s), if any) provided that it (or they) have existed at least 20 years, otherwise use the FCA's benchmark rate.

MCD mortgage lender has an ERR calculation method that applies for a fixed period of time after which the lender’s standard variable rate applies (eg, 0.5% over Bank of England rate for the first two years and then the lender’s standard variable rate applies for the rest of the mortgage lifetime).

Calculate using the method which produces the highest additional APRC.

# MCOB — MCOB 9.4 Content of illustrations

## MCOB 9.4.88 (Rules)

(1) In calculating example cash amounts in accordance with MCOB 9.4.83 R(1)(e), it must be assumed that:

(a) the lifetime mortgage is repaid in full;

(b) all payments due (if applicable) on the lifetime mortgage are actually paid;

(c) additional fees and charges such as insurance premiums have been paid; and

(d) no underpayments or overpayments (if applicable) have been made.

(2) If:

(a) cashbacks or other incentives need to be repaid; or

(b) fees need to be paid;

the amounts that would need to be repaid or paid must be included in the example cash amounts.

(3) Where the calculation of the early repayment charge is based on the interest rate charged on thelifetime mortgage, or on interest rates generally, the interest rate(s) used for the calculation of the example cash amounts must be those in force at the date that the illustration is issued to the customer.

(4) The example cash amounts must reflect the maximum charge in a particular year. Where it is possible to state exact early repayment charges (that is, where all such charges are based on the original amount borrowed), the illustration must do so.

## MCOB 9.4.89 (Rules)

Where the cash examples from MCOB 9.4.88 R included in the illustration would vary either if the interest rate charged on the lifetime mortgage changed or with changes in interest rates generally, an appropriate warning that the early repayment charges may vary from the cash examples must be included in the illustration.

## MCOB 9.4.90 (Rules)

Where the early repayment charge could be higher than those stated in the illustration if the lifetime mortgage continued after the end of the term estimated in accordance with MCOB 9.4.10 R or MCOB 9.4.12 R, Section 13 of the illustration must include a clear statement to that effect.

verdicts.evidence: I am writing to confirm that we have received your complaint, which covers the following concerns: - The early repayment charge of £8,400 applied to your previous mortgage, including whether it was calculated correctly and whether a clear explanation and breakdown was provided to you; - Whether this charge was properly disclosed to you in your original mortgage illustration and offer document when you took out the product; and - The fact that you have not received a substantive response since you raised your concerns.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately reflects all three substantive elements of the complaint: the ERC calculation/explanation, the disclosure issue, and the delay in response.

This is labelled as a 'final' response stage, yet the customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). It merely confirms the complaint is being investigated and promises a future outcome. No decision or reasons engaging with the substantive points are provided.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with our response once we have completed our investigation, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further information about this when we send you our final response.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft mentions the right to refer to the FOS, but does not provide contact details, a website, or a leaflet. It defers this information to a future final response. For a response that is supposed to be the final response, this is insufficient — it acknowledges the FOS but does not give the means to contact them.
The customer draft does not state the six-month deadline from the date of the final response within which the complainant must refer the complaint to the FOS. No time limit is mentioned at all.

The customer draft does not mention the eight-week timescale for issuing a final response or the complainant's right to go to the FOS if eight weeks pass without resolution. While this is marked as a 'final' response stage, the content reads as a holding response but still fails to include the eight-week timeline information that a holding response would require.

verdicts.evidence: We are now investigating your complaint fully. As part of this, we will: - Review how the early repayment charge was calculated, including whether it can be expressed as a specific cash amount and whether it represents a reasonable pre-estimate of the costs arising from your early repayment — as our regulator's rules require; - Check the original mortgage illustration and offer document provided to you at the time you took out the mortgage...
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages substantively with each point raised, uses clear and non-dismissive language, acknowledges the customer's financial difficulty, and explains what steps will be taken. It is even-handed and specific to the complaint.

The customer draft does not address whether redress is appropriate, does not make an offer, and does not explain why none is due. The handler answer explicitly states 'Do not commit to a refund or any specific remedy in the customer letter at this stage,' and the customer draft follows this by not addressing redress at all. For a final response where the complaint plainly raises financial loss, this is missing.

verdicts.evidence: Handler: 'The customer is 74 years old, on a fixed pension income, and has stated that the unexpected charge has caused significant financial difficulty... This is a clear indicator of potential vulnerability... The complaint should be escalated and handled with appropriate care.' Customer draft: 'I also want to acknowledge that you have told us this charge has caused you significant financial difficulty. We take that seriously and will ensure your complaint is handled with the care and priority it deserves.'
verdicts.reasoning: Both the handler answer and the customer draft identify the vulnerability indicators (age, fixed income, financial hardship, impact on care costs) and act on them — the handler recommends escalation, vulnerability flagging, and additional support, while the customer draft acknowledges the difficulty and promises prioritised handling.

verdicts.evidence:
I am writing to confirm that we have received your complaint, which covers the following concerns:
- The early repayment charge of £8,400 applied to your previous mortgage, including whether it was calculated correctly and whether a clear explanation and breakdown was provided to you;
- Whether this charge was properly disclosed to you in your original mortgage illustration and offer document when you took out the product; and
- The fact that you have not received a substantive response since you raised your concerns.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing draft opens by acknowledging receipt and accurately restates the three main issues raised by the complainant.

The draft states the complaint is being investigated and that a future outcome will be provided, but it does not give a firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor reasons for such a decision.

verdicts.evidence: If you remain dissatisfied with our response once we have completed our investigation, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
verdicts.reasoning: The right to refer to the FOS is mentioned, but the draft does not provide details on how to do so (contact details, website, etc.).
There is no statement of the six‑month deadline from the date of the final response for referring the complaint to the FOS.
The customer draft does not set out the firm’s eight‑week timescale for a final response nor link it to the option of referring to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced... We are now investigating your complaint fully.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is courteous, acknowledges the complainant’s hardship, and explains the investigation without dismissing any points, demonstrating fair and balanced treatment.
The draft does not address whether any redress will be offered or explain why none might be due; it only promises an investigation.

verdicts.evidence:
Handler answer: "This is a clear indicator of potential vulnerability (financial difficulty, age, reliance on fixed income, impact on care provision)."
Customer draft: "I also want to acknowledge that you have told us this charge has caused you significant financial difficulty. We take that seriously and will ensure your complaint is handled with the care and priority it deserves."
verdicts.reasoning: Both the internal note and the customer‑facing text recognise the complainant’s vulnerability and indicate that the handling will be adapted accordingly.

claims.claim: MCOB 12.3.1R requires that any ERC on a regulated mortgage contract must (a) be expressible as a cash value, and (b) be a reasonable pre-estimate of the costs to the lender from early repayment.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.3.1R as retrieved states the ERC must be (1) able to be expressed as a cash value and (2) a reasonable pre-estimate of the costs as a result of early repayment. This matches the claim.

claims.claim: MCOB 12.3.2G states that the 'Rule of 78' method should not be used as it overstates costs.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.3.2G in the retrieved provision states: 'A firm should not use the Rule of 78, which is not appropriate as it effectively overstates the cost to the mortgage lender.'

claims.claim: MCOB 12.3.4R requires that before entering into a regulated mortgage contract, the firm must disclose the maximum ERC as a cash amount in both the pre-contract illustration (under MCOB 5) and the offer document illustration (under MCOB 6.4.1R).
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.3.4R as retrieved requires disclosure of the maximum amount payable as an ERC in the illustration provided in accordance with MCOB 5 and in the illustration provided as part of the offer document in accordance with MCOB 6.4.1R(1). The claim that it must be disclosed 'as a cash amount' is supported by the rule requiring disclosure of 'the maximum amount payable' which is inherently a cash amount, and further supported by MCOB 5.6.84R(1)(g) which specifies 'shown as cash amounts'.

claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision MCOB 5.6.84R(1)(g) states the maximum ERC 'must be shown as cash amounts and described in the illustration as "the maximum charge you could pay"'. This directly supports the claim.

claims.claim: The retrieved provisions do not include DISP timescale rules, so the precise deadlines for complaint acknowledgement and response cannot be confirmed from the retrieved context.

Reviewing the retrieved provisions confirms that no DISP timescale rules (e.g., DISP 1.6) are included. The only DISP provision retrieved relates to policy reconstruction (DISP App 1.4.15). The claim is accurate.

claims.claim: If the mortgage is an MCD regulated mortgage contract, MCOB 2A.4 and related provisions also apply.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provision MCOB 2A.4 sets out rules specifically for MCD regulated mortgage contracts regarding early repayment, including MCOB 2A.4.1R and 2A.4.2R which confirms that MCOB 2A.4 applies to MCD regulated mortgage contracts.

claims.claim: The MCOB rules on ERCs apply to regulated mortgage contracts.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.3.1R explicitly refers to 'any regulated mortgage contract' and the section is titled 'Early repayment charges: regulated mortgage contracts'.

claims.claim: The firm will review how the early repayment charge was calculated, including whether it can be expressed as a specific cash amount and whether it represents a reasonable pre-estimate of the costs arising from early repayment — as the regulator's rules require.
claims.reasoning: This is a plain-language restatement of MCOB 12.3.1R which requires the ERC to be (1) able to be expressed as a cash value and (2) a reasonable pre-estimate of costs from early repayment. The claim accurately reflects the regulatory requirement.

claims.claim: The firm will check the original mortgage illustration and offer document to confirm whether the maximum early repayment charge was disclosed in the required form and as a cash amount.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.3.4R requires disclosure of the maximum ERC in the illustration under MCOB 5 and in the offer document illustration under MCOB 6.4.1R. MCOB 5.6.84R(1)(g) requires it to be shown as a cash amount. The customer-facing claim accurately reflects these requirements.

claims.claim: If the customer remains dissatisfied after the firm's final response, they have the right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: While this is a well-known right under DISP rules, none of the retrieved provisions contain the DISP rules regarding referral to the Financial Ombudsman Service. The only DISP provision retrieved (DISP App 1.4.15) relates to policy reconstruction and does not address FOS referral rights.

claims.claim: The firm will provide further information about the Financial Ombudsman Service when it sends its final response.
claims.reasoning: This relates to DISP requirements about including FOS information in final response letters, but no such DISP provision is included in the retrieved provisions.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to complain about my home insurance policy. I am a retired 78-year-old and I took out a home contents and buildings policy through your branch last year. When I purchased the policy, I specifically told your adviser that I also rent out a small annexe attached to my property on a buy-to-let basis, and asked whether this would be covered. I was assured it would be fine under my standard home policy. However, when I recently made a claim for storm damage to the annexe roof, you rejected it saying the annexe is a commercial let property and isn't covered under my consumer home insurance policy. I feel I was mis-sold this policy — I was never told I needed a separate commercial policy for the annexe, and your adviser should have categorised my needs properly. I'm now facing a £4,200 repair bill I cannot afford on my pension. To make matters worse, I first raised this complaint by telephone over five weeks ago and have received no acknowledgement whatsoever — no letter, no email, nothing. I had to call again today and was told there was no record of my earlier call. This is completely unacceptable treatment of a loyal customer.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious concerns that require careful handling and human review before any response is sent.

**1. Potential mis-sale / unsuitable advice**
The customer alleges that a branch adviser assured them their standard home contents and buildings policy would cover a buy-to-let annexe, and that no separate commercial policy was recommended. This is a potential mis-sale / unsuitable advice allegation. The retrieved provisions do not include the specific FCA rules on suitability of advice or product mis-sale for general insurance (e.g. ICOBS 5 or ICOBS 4), so I cannot ground a regulatory position on the mis-sale element from the retrieved context alone. I have flagged `insufficient_context` accordingly. The ICOBS 8.1.2A guidance retrieved does touch on unreasonable claim rejection (including misrepresentation), but the customer's complaint is primarily about advice suitability and product categorisation, not a misrepresentation by the customer. You should obtain the full ICOBS provisions on advised sales and suitability before finalising the response.

**2. Claim rejection**
The claim was rejected on the basis that the annexe is a commercial let and not covered under the consumer policy. ICOBS 8.1.2A(G) indicates that rejection of a consumer's claim may be unreasonable in certain circumstances, including where the policy is drafted or operated in a way that does not allow the insurer to reject. You will need to review the policy wording carefully and consider whether the adviser's assurance at point of sale has any bearing on the rejection. This requires legal and compliance input.

**3. Failure to acknowledge — potential DISP breach**
The customer says they raised the complaint by telephone over five weeks ago and received no acknowledgement. Under Crest Bank's own procedure (reflecting DISP 1.6.2R), a written acknowledgement must be sent within five business days of receipt. If the complaint was received five weeks ago and no acknowledgement was sent, this is a likely breach of DISP 1.6.2R. You must investigate whether the original call was logged in the CMS. If it was not logged, this is also a breach of the same-day logging requirement. The complaint reference date for DISP timescale purposes (including the eight-week final response deadline) may run from the original call five weeks ago — you must establish the exact date of that call urgently, as the eight-week clock may already be running and could be close to expiry.

**4. Vulnerability indicators**
The customer is 78 years old, retired, and states they are facing a £4,200 repair bill they cannot afford on their pension. These are clear vulnerability indicators. Under Crest Bank's Vulnerable Customer Policy (referenced in the Complaints Handling Procedure section 7.7), the complaint must be flagged as involving a potentially vulnerable customer in the CMS, prioritised, and aimed to be resolved within four weeks. Given the complaint may already be five weeks old, this is urgent.

**5. Redress**
The customer is facing a £4,200 repair bill. Any decision on redress or compensation must not be made in this letter — it requires investigation and, per the escalation procedure (section 8.3 of the Complaints Handling Procedure), must be escalated to the CCO and Head of Customer Operations given the financial redress element.

**Recommended immediate actions:**
- Establish the date of the original telephone complaint and log it in the CMS immediately if not already done.
- Flag as a vulnerable customer complaint in the CMS.
- Escalate to CCO and Head of Customer Operations within one business day (financial redress potential exceeds £10,000 threshold is not met at £4,200, but the regulatory breach and vulnerability indicators warrant escalation — check your internal threshold).
- Retrieve the full ICOBS suitability and advised sales provisions before finalising the substantive response.
- The draft below acknowledges the complaint and the handling failure, but does NOT make any commitment on the mis-sale or claim outcome — those require investigation. It also does NOT include the FOS referral notice with a specific time limit, as I cannot confirm from the retrieved context alone whether the eight-week period has already expired (which would trigger the prescribed FOS notice). **You must confirm whether the eight-week deadline has been reached before sending.** If it has, a different letter (a final response with FOS referral rights) is required. The draft below is appropriate only if the eight-week period has not yet expired.

**Assumption flagged:** The customer draft below treats this as an interim acknowledgement/update letter, not a final response. If the eight-week deadline has already passed (running from the original call five weeks ago), you must issue a final response with the FOS referral notice instead. Do not send this draft without confirming the complaint receipt date and the current position on the eight-week clock.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for contacting us again today, and I am sorry that you have had to do so. I want to begin by sincerely apologising for the way your complaint has been handled so far. You first raised this matter with us by telephone approximately five weeks ago, and you should have received a written acknowledgement from us within five business days of that call. I understand that you received nothing, and that when you called again today you were told there was no record of your earlier contact. This is not the standard of service we expect to provide, and I am sorry for the distress and inconvenience this has caused you.

I have now logged your complaint in our system and assigned it the reference number [COMPLAINT REF]. Your complaint concerns two matters:

1. The rejection of your claim for storm damage to the annexe roof of your property; and
2. Your concern that you were not given appropriate advice when you took out your home contents and buildings policy, having told our adviser at the time that you rent out an annexe on a buy-to-let basis.

I want to assure you that we take both of these concerns very seriously. We will conduct a thorough and impartial investigation, which will include reviewing the records of your original policy sale, any notes from your conversation with our adviser, and the basis on which your claim was rejected.

Given your circumstances, I want to make sure we handle this as quickly as possible and keep you informed at every stage. If at any point you would prefer to receive correspondence in a different format, or would find it easier to speak with us by telephone, please do not hesitate to let us know.

We will write to you again with the outcome of our investigation as soon as we are able. If you have any questions in the meantime, please contact me directly using the details below.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]
[Date]

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 1. Document Control

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Policy Title** | Customer Complaints Handling Procedure |
| **Version** | 2.1 |
| **Effective Date** | 1 February 2025 |
| **Next Review Date** | 31 January 2026 |
| **Document Owner** | Priya Sharma, Head of Customer Operations |
| **Approving Body** | Compliance Oversight Committee |

---

## 2. Purpose

This policy establishes Crest Bank's framework for receiving, investigating, and resolving customer complaints in a manner that is fair, consistent, and compliant with FCA requirements. It ensures that customers who are dissatisfied with Crest Bank's products or services receive a timely and transparent response, and that complaint data is used systematically to drive service improvement. The policy applies to all regulated and unregulated complaints received across Crest Bank's digital channels.

---

## 3. Scope

This policy applies to:

- **Staff:** All customer-facing staff in Customer Operations, as well as staff in Product, Risk, Compliance, and Technology who may be involved in complaint investigation or root cause analysis.
- **Products:** Personal current accounts, instant-access and fixed-term savings accounts, personal loans, and SME business current accounts.
- **Customer Segments:** All retail customers (approximately 850,000) and SME customers (approximately 12,000).
- **Channels:** In-app messaging, email, web contact form, and any written correspondence received at Crest Bank's registered address.
- **Systems:** Crest Bank's complaint management system (CMS), core banking platform, and customer identity and verification (CIV) system.

---

# ICOBS — ICOBS 8.1 Insurers: general

## ICOBS 8.1.2A (Guidance)

(1) Cases in which rejection of a consumer’s claim would be unreasonable (in the FCA’s view) include, but are not limited to rejection:

(a) for misrepresentation, unless it is a qualifying misrepresentation (see ICOBS 8.1.3R);

(b) where the claim is subject to the Insurance Act 2015, for breach of warranty or term, or for fraud, unless the insurer is able to rely on the relevant provisions of the Insurance Act 2015; and

(c) where the policy is drafted or operated in a way that does not allow the insurer to reject.

(2) The Insurance Act 2015 sets out a number of situations in which an insurer may have no liability or obligation to pay. For example:

(a) section 10 provides situations in which an insurer has no liability under a policy due to a breach of warranty;

(b) section 11 places restrictions on an insurer’s ability to reject a claim for breach of a term where compliance is aimed at reducing certain types of risk; and

(c) sections 12 and 13 provide for the extent to which a firm is entitled to reject fraudulent claims.

## ICOBS 8.1.2B (Rules)

For contracts entered into or variations agreed on or after 1 August 2017, a rejection of a consumer policyholder's claim for breach of a condition or warranty (that is not subject to and within section 10 or 11 of the Insurance Act 2015) is unreasonable unless the circumstances of the claim are connected to the breach.

# DISP — DISP App 1.4 Policy reconstruction

## DISP App 1.4.14 (Guidance)

Example 8

Example 8

Term extends beyond retirement age and policy reconstruction

Background

45 year old male non-smoker, having taken out a £50,000 loan in 1998 for a term of 25 years. Unsuitable sale identified on the grounds of affordability and complaint raised on 12th policy anniversary.

It has always been the intention of the complainant to retire at State retirement age 65.

Term from date of sale to retirement is 20 years and the maturity date of the mortgage is 5 years after retirement.

Established facts

Established premium paid by investor on policy of original term (25 years):

£81.20

Premium that would have been payable on policy with term from sale to retirement (20 years):

£111.20

Actual policy value at time complaint assessed:

£12,500

Value of an equivalent 20-year policy at time complaint assessed:

£21,300

Difference in policy values at time complaint assessed:

£8,800

Difference in outgoings (20 year policy - 25 year policy):

£4,320

Basis of compensation

The policy is reconstructed as if it had been set up originally on a term to mature at retirement age, in this example, a term of 20 years. The difference in the current value of the policy actually sold to the complainant and the current value of the reconstructed policy, as if the premium on the reconstructed policy had been paid from outset, is calculated. The complainant has gained from lower outgoings (lower premiums) of the actual endowment policy to date. In calculating the redress, the gain may be offset against the loss unless the complainant's particular circumstances are such that it would be unreasonable to take account of the gain.

Redress generally if it is not unreasonable to take account of the whole of the gain from lower outgoings

Loss from current value of reconstructed policy less current value of actual policy:

(£8,800)

Gain from total lower outgoings under actual policy:

£4,320

retrieved_chunks.content:
# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 7. Procedures

### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints

A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination.

All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.

### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement

Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must:

1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected.
2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer.
3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.

### 7.3 Investigation Process

The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include:

1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems.
2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made.
3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

### 7.6 Root Cause Analysis

In accordance with Crest Bank's obligations under PRIN 12 to deliver good outcomes for customers, the Head of Customer Operations will ensure that root cause analysis (RCA) is conducted as follows:

1. **Individual complaints:** Each upheld complaint must include a brief RCA note in the CMS, identifying the immediate cause of the failure.
2. **Thematic analysis:** On a monthly basis, the complaints team will analyse complaint data to identify recurring themes, products, or processes generating disproportionate complaint volumes.
3. **Formal RCA:** Where a theme generates five or more upheld complaints within a rolling 30-day period, or where a single complaint indicates a systemic product or process failure, the Head of Customer Operations will commission a formal RCA report within **ten business days**.
4. The formal RCA report will be shared with the relevant business area owner and the CCO, who will agree a remediation plan with defined owners and completion dates.
5. Remediation plans will be tracked in the CMS and reported to the Executive Risk Committee monthly until closure.

### 7.7 Vulnerable Customers

Where a complaint is received from a customer who has been identified as potentially vulnerable, as defined in Crest Bank's Vulnerable Customer Policy, the complaints handler must:

1. Flag the complaint as involving a vulnerable customer in the CMS.
2. Prioritise the investigation and aim to issue a final response within **four weeks** rather than the standard eight weeks.
3. Offer alternative communication formats or channels if requested by the customer.
4. Consider whether the circumstances giving rise to the complaint indicate that additional support or referral to specialist services is appropriate.

---

# Treating Customers Fairly Policy

---

## 1. Document Control

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Policy Title** | Treating Customers Fairly Policy |
| **Version** | 2.1 |
| **Effective Date** | 1 February 2025 |
| **Next Review Date** | 31 January 2026 |
| **Document Owner** | Rachel Whitfield, Chief Compliance Officer |
| **Approving Body** | Compliance Oversight Committee |

---

## 2. Purpose

This policy sets out Crest Bank's commitment to ensuring that fair treatment of customers is embedded throughout the organisation, from product design through to post-sale service and complaint resolution. It gives effect to the FCA's Consumer Duty requirements and the longstanding Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) framework, translating regulatory obligations into actionable standards for all staff. The policy provides a consistent basis against which Crest Bank's conduct can be assessed, monitored, and improved.

---

## 3. Scope

This policy applies to:

- **Staff:** All Crest Bank employees, contractors, and third-party agents who interact with customers, design products, develop communications, or make decisions affecting customer outcomes.
- **Products and services:** Personal current accounts, instant-access and fixed-term savings accounts, personal loans, and SME business current accounts.
- **Customer segments:** All retail customers (approximately 850,000) and SME customers (approximately 12,000), including prospective customers at the pre-sale stage.
- **Systems and channels:** All digital channels through which Crest Bank delivers services, including the mobile application, web platform, in-app messaging, and automated decisioning systems.

This policy does not apply to Crest Bank's relationships with wholesale counterparties or institutional clients.

---

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 8. Monitoring, Reporting and Escalation

### 8.1 Complaint MI

The Head of Customer Operations will produce a monthly Complaint MI Report covering:

- Total complaints received, broken down by product, channel, and complaint category.
- Complaints resolved within timescale vs. those breaching the eight-week deadline.
- Upheld rate, partial upheld rate, and rejection rate.
- Total financial redress paid.
- Complaints referred to the FOS and outcomes of FOS decisions.
- Open RCA actions and their status.

### 8.2 Reporting to Governance Bodies

| Report | Recipient | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Complaint MI Report | Executive Risk Committee | Monthly |
| Complaint MI Summary | Compliance Oversight Committee | Monthly |
| Complaint Trends Summary | Board Risk Committee | Quarterly |
| FCA Complaints Return (DISP 1 Annex 1) | FCA (via RegData) | Half-yearly |

### 8.3 Escalation

- Any complaint involving potential regulatory breach, media risk, or financial redress exceeding £10,000 must be escalated to the CCO and Head of Customer Operations within **one business day** of identification.
- Any complaint indicating potential financial crime must be referred to the MLRO on the same business day.
- Any complaint indicating a material data breach must be referred to the DPO on the same business day, who will assess notification obligations under UK GDPR.
- Systemic issues identified through RCA that cannot be remediated within 30 days must be escalated to the Executive Risk Committee for direction.

---

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 6. Roles and Responsibilities

| Role | Responsibilities Under This Policy |
|---|---|
| **Priya Sharma, Head of Customer Operations** | Policy owner; oversees day-to-day complaint handling operations; approves final response letters for complex or high-value complaints; ensures adequate resourcing of the complaints team. |
| **Rachel Whitfield, Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)** | Ensures the policy remains compliant with DISP and other applicable rules; reviews complaint MI; chairs the Compliance Oversight Committee agenda item on complaints. |
| **James Patel, MLRO** | Reviews complaints that indicate potential financial crime, fraud, or suspicious activity and determines whether a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) is required. |
| **Fatima Al-Rashid, Data Protection Officer (DPO)** | Reviews complaints that raise data protection or privacy concerns; liaises with the ICO where required. |
| **Tom Hargreaves, Chief Technology Officer (CTO)** | Responsible for complaints arising from platform or technology failures; supports root cause analysis for technology-related complaints. |
| **Complaints Handlers (Customer Operations)** | Receive, log, acknowledge, investigate, and resolve complaints within prescribed timescales; draft final response letters; escalate where required. |
| **Executive Risk Committee** | Receives monthly complaint MI; directs remedial action where systemic issues are identified. |
| **Board Risk Committee** | Receives quarterly complaint MI summary; provides board-level oversight of complaint trends and outcomes. |

---

verdicts.evidence: I have now logged your complaint in our system and assigned it the reference number [COMPLAINT REF]. Your complaint concerns two matters: 1. The rejection of your claim for storm damage to the annexe roof of your property; and 2. Your concern that you were not given appropriate advice when you took out your home contents and buildings policy, having told our adviser at the time that you rent out an annexe on a buy-to-let basis.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint, apologising for the handling failure, and then clearly and accurately identifies both substantive issues raised by the complainant.

The customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) on the complaint. It explicitly states that an investigation will be conducted and the outcome will follow later. Despite this being labelled as a 'final' response stage, no decision or reasons are provided.

The customer-facing draft contains no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service or the complainant's right to refer the complaint to the FOS. The handler answer discusses the FOS referral notice but explicitly states it was not included in the customer draft.
There is no mention of the six-month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS in the customer-facing draft.

The customer draft does not mention the eight-week timescale for issuing a final response, nor does it inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS if the complaint is not resolved within eight weeks. The handler answer discusses the eight-week clock internally but this information does not appear in the customer-facing text.

verdicts.evidence: I want to assure you that we take both of these concerns very seriously. We will conduct a thorough and impartial investigation, which will include reviewing the records of your original policy sale, any notes from your conversation with our adviser, and the basis on which your claim was rejected.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the complaint in clear, non-dismissive language, acknowledges the handling failure, and promises investigation. However, since this is supposed to be a final response and no substantive decision is reached on the merits of the complaint, it only partially demonstrates fair treatment — it does not actually resolve or engage with the substance on its merits, merely promises to do so later.

The customer-facing draft makes no mention of redress, compensation, or remedial action. The handler answer explicitly states that no commitment on redress should be made in this letter and that it requires further investigation. The complaint plainly raises a £4,200 financial loss but no position on redress is communicated to the customer.

verdicts.evidence: Handler: 'The customer is 78 years old, retired, and states they are facing a £4,200 repair bill they cannot afford on their pension. These are clear vulnerability indicators. Under Crest Bank's Vulnerable Customer Policy... the complaint must be flagged as involving a potentially vulnerable customer in the CMS, prioritised, and aimed to be resolved within four weeks.' Customer draft: 'Given your circumstances, I want to make sure we handle this as quickly as possible and keep you informed at every stage. If at any point you would prefer to receive correspondence in a different format, or would find it easier to speak with us by telephone, please do not hesitate to let us know.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer clearly identifies the vulnerability indicators (age, retirement, financial hardship) and recommends specific actions (flagging in CMS, prioritisation). The customer draft adapts the communication by offering alternative formats, prioritising speed, and using supportive language. Both elements together satisfy this requirement.

The draft only promises an investigation and does not state any firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor provide reasons for a decision.
There is no mention of the complainant’s right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.
The draft does not set out the six‑month period for referring a final response to the FOS.
The response does not communicate the eight‑week deadline for the firm’s final response or the option to go to the FOS after eight weeks.

verdicts.evidence: I want to assure you that we take both of these concerns very seriously. We will conduct a thorough and impartial investigation... If at any point you would prefer to receive correspondence in a different format...
verdicts.reasoning: The language is apologetic, non‑dismissive, and commits to an impartial investigation, showing the complaint is being treated fairly.
The draft does not address whether any redress will be offered or explain why none may be due.

verdicts.evidence: These are clear vulnerability indicators. Under Crest Bank's Vulnerable Customer Policy ... the complaint must be flagged as involving a potentially vulnerable customer...

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am a 72-year-old retired customer and I invested £45,000 in a structured product through my bank two years ago. I was told it was low-risk and suitable for my retirement income needs. In reality, it was a high-risk product and I have lost approximately £12,000. When I complained to my bank three weeks ago, they wrote back saying they were not responsible because the product was manufactured by another investment firm, and they forwarded my complaint to that other firm. However, my bank was the one that advised me to buy it and assessed my risk profile. They have not addressed their own role in the advice at all, and I have not received any substantive response from either firm. I am on a fixed pension income and this loss has caused me significant financial hardship — I am struggling to pay my utility bills. I want my bank to properly investigate their part in this and provide appropriate redress for the unsuitable advice they gave me.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious concerns that require careful handling and mandatory human review before any response is sent.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Suitability of advice:** The customer alleges the bank recommended a high-risk structured product as low-risk and suitable for their retirement income needs. Under COBS 9A.2.20AR, a firm must not make a personal recommendation on an insurance-based investment product where none of the products are suitable for the client. COBS 9A.2.21G further indicates that a transaction may be unsuitable due to the risks of the associated financial instruments or the type of transaction. If the bank provided a personal recommendation, it cannot deflect responsibility to the product manufacturer — the advice obligation sits with the advising firm.

2. **Deflection to the manufacturer is likely inadequate:** The bank's response that it is 'not responsible because the product was manufactured by another firm' does not address its own advisory role. The bank assessed the customer's risk profile and gave the recommendation. Crest Bank's own Complaints Handling Procedure (section 7.4) requires the final response to address the substance of the complaint, including the bank's own conduct. Forwarding the complaint without addressing the bank's advisory role does not constitute a proper investigation.

3. **Complaint handling timescales:** The complaint was received three weeks ago. Under DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in Crest Bank's procedure, section 7.4 and 7.5), a final response must be issued within eight weeks of receipt. The bank has approximately five weeks remaining. If no substantive response is issued within eight weeks, the bank must write to the customer explaining the delay and confirming their right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

4. **Vulnerability:** The customer is 72 years old, retired, on a fixed pension income, and reports significant financial hardship — struggling to pay utility bills. These are clear vulnerability indicators. Under Crest Bank's Vulnerable Customers Policy (sections 7.3 and 7.3.2, referencing PRIN 2A and BCOBS 5), communications must be reviewed to ensure they are clear, appropriately toned, and do not cause additional distress. Reasonable adjustments should be considered. A vulnerability flag should be considered for this customer's account.

5. **Redress:** The customer has suffered an approximate £12,000 loss and is in financial hardship. Any assessment of redress is a financial decision requiring specialist review. Under Crest Bank's procedure, complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

**Recommended action:**
- Do not send any response without human review and sign-off by the Head of Customer Operations.
- Escalate immediately given the vulnerability indicators and the financial hardship disclosed.
- Assign a lead investigator to examine the bank's own advisory conduct — specifically whether the suitability assessment was adequate and whether the recommendation was appropriate for this customer's age, risk profile, and retirement income needs.
- Consider whether a vulnerability flag should be applied to the customer's account and what reasonable adjustments are appropriate.
- Do not accept the previous response's framing that the bank bears no responsibility. The bank's advisory role must be investigated on its own merits.
- Note: the retrieved provisions do not include the full text of DISP 1.6.2R directly from the FCA Handbook — the reference is drawn from Crest Bank's internal procedure. If there is any doubt about the precise regulatory requirements, verify against the FCA Handbook directly.
- The customer draft below is an acknowledgement only — it does not make any admission, quantify redress, or commit to an outcome. It is intended to confirm receipt, explain next steps, and signal that the bank is taking the complaint seriously. It must still be reviewed before sending.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced, and I want to assure you that we take your concerns very seriously.

We have received your complaint and are treating it as a priority. I understand that you are concerned about the advice you received when you invested in this product, the suitability of that advice given your circumstances and retirement income needs, and the response you have received from us so far. I also recognise that the financial loss you have described is causing you real hardship, and I am sorry that you are in this position.

We are now investigating your complaint fully, including our own role in the advice and suitability assessment that took place at the time of your investment. We will not simply refer you to the product manufacturer — it is right that we examine our own conduct and respond to you directly on that.

We aim to provide you with a full written response within eight weeks of the date we received your complaint. If for any reason we are unable to do so within that timeframe, we will write to you to explain why and to confirm your options at that stage.

If you have any questions in the meantime, or if there is anything we can do to make this process easier for you, please do not hesitate to contact us. If you would find it helpful to speak with someone directly, please let us know and we will arrange this.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires that a firm must not make a personal recommendation on an insurance-based investment product where none of the products are suitable for the client, directly relevant to the allegation of unsuitable advice.

Indicates that a transaction may be unsuitable due to the risks of the associated financial instruments or the type of transaction, supporting the assessment of whether the structured product recommendation was appropriate.

Requires a final response to be issued within eight weeks of receipt of the complaint, and requires the customer to be informed of their right to refer to the FOS — the bank has approximately five weeks remaining.

Referenced in Crest Bank's Vulnerable Customers Policy as requiring communications with vulnerable customers to be clear, appropriately toned, and not cause additional distress — relevant given the customer's age, fixed income, and financial hardship.
Referenced alongside PRIN 2A in Crest Bank's Vulnerable Customers Policy as applying to communications with customers who have an active vulnerability flag.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# DISP — DISP App 1.4 Policy reconstruction

## DISP App 1.4.15 (Guidance)

Example 9

Example 9

Term extends beyond retirement age: example of failure to explain investment risks

Background

45 year old male non-smoker, having taken out a £50,000 loan in 1998 for a term of 25 years. Unsuitable sale identified on the grounds of affordability and complaint raised on 12th anniversary.

It has always been the intention of the complainant to retire at state retirement age 65.

Term from date of sale to retirement is 20 years and the maturity date of the mortgage is five years after retirement.

In addition, an endowment does not meet the complainant's attitude to investment risk and a repayment mortgage would have been taken out if properly advised.

Established facts

Surrender value (on the 25 year policy) at time complaint assessed:

£12,500

Capital repaid under repayment mortgage of term to retirement date (20 years):

£21,000

Surrender value less capital repaid:

(£8.500)

Difference in outgoings (repayment - endowment):

£5,400

Cost of converting from endowment mortgage to repayment mortgage:

£200

Basis of compensation:

The surrender value of the (25 year term) endowment policy is compared to the capital that would have been repaid to date under a repayment mortgage arranged to repay the loan at retirement age, in this example, a repayment mortgage for a term of 20 years. The complainant has gained from lower outgoings of the endowment mortgage to date. In calculating the redress, the gain may be offset against the loss unless the complainant's particular circumstances are such that it would be unreasonable to take account of the gain. The conversion costs are also taken into account in calculating the redress.

Redress generally

Loss from surrender value less capital repaid:

(£8,500)

Gain from total lower outgoings under endowment mortgage:

£5,400

Cost of converting to a repayment mortgage:

(£200)

Net loss:

(£3,300)

Therefore total redress is:

£

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 1. Document Control

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Policy Title** | Customer Complaints Handling Procedure |
| **Version** | 2.1 |
| **Effective Date** | 1 February 2025 |
| **Next Review Date** | 31 January 2026 |
| **Document Owner** | Priya Sharma, Head of Customer Operations |
| **Approving Body** | Compliance Oversight Committee |

---

## 2. Purpose

This policy establishes Crest Bank's framework for receiving, investigating, and resolving customer complaints in a manner that is fair, consistent, and compliant with FCA requirements. It ensures that customers who are dissatisfied with Crest Bank's products or services receive a timely and transparent response, and that complaint data is used systematically to drive service improvement. The policy applies to all regulated and unregulated complaints received across Crest Bank's digital channels.

---

## 3. Scope

This policy applies to:

- **Staff:** All customer-facing staff in Customer Operations, as well as staff in Product, Risk, Compliance, and Technology who may be involved in complaint investigation or root cause analysis.
- **Products:** Personal current accounts, instant-access and fixed-term savings accounts, personal loans, and SME business current accounts.
- **Customer Segments:** All retail customers (approximately 850,000) and SME customers (approximately 12,000).
- **Channels:** In-app messaging, email, web contact form, and any written correspondence received at Crest Bank's registered address.
- **Systems:** Crest Bank's complaint management system (CMS), core banking platform, and customer identity and verification (CIV) system.

---

# COBS — COBS 9A.2 Assessing suitability: the obligations

## COBS 9A.2.20A (Rules)

When providing a personal recommendation on an insurance-based investment product in accordance with COBS 9A.2.1R and COBS 9A.2.16R, a firm must not make a recommendation where none of the insurance-based investment products are suitable for the client.

[Note: article 9(6) of the IDD Regulation]

## COBS 9A.2.21 (Guidance)

(1) A transaction may be unsuitable for a client due to the risks of the associated financial instruments, the type of transaction, the characteristics of the order or the frequency of the trading.(1A) An insurance-based investment product may be unsuitable for a client due to the risks of the underlying investment assets, the type or characteristics of the product or the frequency of switching of underlying investment assets.(2) A series of transactions, each of which are suitable when viewed in isolation may be unsuitable if the recommendation or the decisions to trade are made with a frequency that is not in the best interests of the client.(3) In the case of portfolio management, a transaction might be unsuitable if it would result in an unsuitable portfolio.[Note: recital 9 to the IDD Regulation]

metadata.section_name: COBS 9A.2 Assessing suitability: the obligations

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# COBS — COBS 9.6 Special rules for giving basic advice on a stakeholder product

## COBS 9.6.10 (Rules)

When a firm gives basic advice it must not:

(1) describe or recommend a stakeholder product outside the firm's range; or

(2) describe or recommend a smoothed linked long term stakeholder product; or

(3) describe fund choice, or recommend a particular fund, if a stakeholder product offers a choice of funds; or

(4) recommend the level of contributions required to be made to a stakeholder pension scheme to achieve a specific income in retirement; or

(5) recommend or agree that a client makes a contribution to an ISA which exceeds the HM Revenue & Customs ISA limits.

## COBS 9.6.11 (Rules)

(1) If a firm starts the sales process for a stakeholder product that is not a deposit-based stakeholder product, it must not depart from that process unless it has advised the retail client that it will not provide basic advice on stakeholder products during the period of departure. A firm that does that must not provide basic advice during the departure period.

(2) Before a firm returns to the sales process for stakeholder products, it must tell the retail client that that process is about to recommence.

## COBS 9.6.12 (Rules)

A firm must only recommend a stakeholder product to a retail client if:

(1) it has taken reasonable steps to assess the client's answers to the scripted questions and any other facts, circumstances or information disclosed by the client during the sales process;

(2) (unless the relevant product is a deposit-based stakeholder product) having done so, it has reasonable grounds for believing that the stakeholder product is suitable for the client; and

(3) the firm reasonably believes that the client understands the firm's advice and the basis on which it was provided.

## COBS 9.6.13 (Guidance)

COBS 9 Annex 2 gives guidance on the steps a firm could take to help it meet these suitability obligations.

# Vulnerable Customers Policy

7.2.5 Customers will be informed that a note has been made on their account to help Crest Bank provide appropriate support, and will be advised of their right to request its removal.

### 7.3 Making Reasonable Adjustments

7.3.1 Upon identifying a vulnerability indicator, staff will consider and offer adjustments appropriate to the customer's circumstances. Adjustments may include, but are not limited to:

- Providing communications in alternative formats (e.g. larger text, simplified language, or audio).
- Allowing additional time during telephone interactions.
- Directing the customer to Crest Bank's dedicated vulnerability support line.
- Facilitating third-party account access under Crest Bank's Third Party Mandate procedure.
- Temporarily pausing collection activity or fee application where a customer discloses acute financial difficulty.
- Signposting to external support services (e.g. StepChange, Mind, Citizens Advice).

7.3.2 In accordance with PRIN 2A and BCOBS 5, all communications with customers who have an active vulnerability flag will be reviewed to ensure they are clear, appropriately toned, and do not create additional distress or confusion.

7.3.3 Staff will not pressure a vulnerable customer to complete a transaction or application where the customer has expressed uncertainty or distress.

### 7.4 Product Suitability and Design

7.4.1 In accordance with PRIN 12 and PRIN 2A, Product Managers will conduct a vulnerability impact assessment as part of the standard product approval and review process. This assessment will consider whether the product's features, terms, eligibility criteria, and associated communications are appropriate for customers who may be in vulnerable circumstances.

7.4.2 Where a product assessment identifies that a product or feature carries elevated risk of harm for vulnerable customers, the Product Governance Committee will determine whether design changes, additional safeguards, or targeted elig

# COBS — COBS 19.10 Drawdown, investment pathways and cash warnings

## COBS 19.10.10 (Rules)

The requirements in this section do not apply to a firm:(1) in relation to sums or assets in a retail client’s pension scheme that the retail client requests to use to purchase a fixed-term product that:(a) provides a guaranteed income, a guaranteed capital return or both, to the retail client or the retail client’s beneficiary; and(b) does not involve any investment risk to the retail client if the retail client remains in the product for the fixed term;(2) when the firm carries out the retail client’s previous instructions to designate their funds on a regular basis into a drawdown fund;(3) when the retail client has been taken through the investment pathways by another firm (F1) and has been referred to the firm’s (F2’s) drawdown fund to invest in one of the pathway investments that the firm (F2) offers (see COBS 19.10.1R(7));(4) when the firm has determined, on reasonable grounds, that the retail client has received a personal recommendation in relation to the action referred to in COBS 19.10.8R(1) or (2); or(5) when the firm has provided a targeted support service to the retail client, or the firm arranged for another firm to provide that targeted support service, where:(a) the targeted support service includes a recommendation to the client on how they should invest their drawdown funds;(b) the retail client has accepted and is taking steps to action the recommendation referred to in (a); and(c) as part of the design and delivery of that targeted support service, the firm providing the targeted support service considered the factors that a firm would need to take into account in following the requirements in this section.
