# DISP — DISP 1.1 Purpose and application

## DISP 1.1.9A (Guidance)

The scope of this sourcebook does not include:

(1) a complaint about pre-commencement investment business which was regulated by a recognised professional body (those complaints will be handled under the arrangements of that professional body); or

(2) a complaint about the administration of an occupational pension scheme, because this is not a regulated activity (firms should refer complainants to the Pensions Ombudsman rather than to the Financial Ombudsman Service and should refer consumers’ general requests for information or guidance to MoneyHelper ).

## DISP 1.1.10 (Rules)

In relation to a firm's obligations under this chapter, references to a complaint also include an expression of dissatisfaction which is capable of becoming a:

(1) relevant new complaint;

(2) relevant transitional complaint;

(3) relevant new credit-related complaint;

(4) relevant new claims management complaint; or

(5) relevant transitional funeral plan complaint.

## DISP 1.1.10-A (Rules)

Where insurance distribution activities are carried on from an establishment maintained by it or its appointed representative in the United Kingdom (or in an EEA State, in the case of a TP firm with respect to services provided into the United Kingdom) , a firm must have in place and operate appropriate and effective procedures for registering and responding to complaints from a person who is not an eligible complainant.

[Note: article 14 of the IDD]

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.1 Purpose and application

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 1.1A Complaints handling requirements for MiFID complaints

## DISP 1.1A.29 (Rules)

When handling a MiFID complaint, MiFID investment firms must communicate with clients or potential clients clearly, in plain language that is easy to understand and must reply to the complaint without undue delay.

## DISP 1.1A.30 (Rules)

MiFID investment firms must communicate their position on the MiFID complaint to clients or potential clients and inform the clients or potential clients about their options, including that they may be able to refer the complaint to an alternative dispute resolution entity, as defined in regulation 4 of the ADR Regulations, or that the client may be able to take civil action.[Note: See the ADR Directive.]

## DISP 1.1A.31 (Rules)

The explanation given by MiFID investment firms to clients or potential clients in accordance with DISP 1.1A.30R must also:(1) enclose a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet;(2) provide the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service;(3) inform the complainant that if, still dissatisfied with the respondent's response, the complaint may now be referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service; and(4) indicate whether or not the respondent consents to waiving the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R (Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?) by including the appropriate wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3R.[Note: article 13 of the ADR Directive]

## DISP 1.1A.32 (Guidance)

The information regarding the Financial Ombudsman Service required to be provided in a final response sent under DISP 1.1A.30R and referred to in DISP 1.1A.31R should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently within the text of those responses.[Note: article 13 of the ADR Directive]

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.1A Complaints handling requirements for MiFID complaints

# DISP — DISP App 3.11 Obligation to write letters to certain rejected complainants

## DISP App 3.11.-1 (Rules)

In this section:

(1) “purported complaint” means an expression of dissatisfaction which would have been a complaint, had it related to an activity which comes under the jurisdiction of the Financial Ombudsman Service;

(2) “recurring non-disclosure of commission” means any omission of the kind described at DISP App 3.1.1G(3)(b); and

(3) “non-disclosure of commission” means “failure to disclose commission” as defined at DISP App 3.1.5G(7) or recurring non-disclosure of commission.

## DISP App 3.11.1 (Rules)

DISP App 3.11.2R and DISP App 3.11.3R apply where:

(1) a complainant has made a complaint to a firm in relation to its sale of a payment protection contract which covered or purported to cover a credit agreement (this includes partial coverage);

(2) the complaint was rejected by the firm before 29 August 2017 in that the firm did not offer the complainant the redress they would have been offered had the firm concluded that the complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract they bought; and

(3) any referral of the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service has been concluded and did not result in the firm offering (or being required to pay) the complainant redress on the basis that the complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract they bought.

metadata.section_name: DISP App 3.11 Obligation to write letters to certain rejected complainants

verdicts.evidence: We understand that you are raising two concerns: first, that when you opened your account you were not provided with information about how to make a complaint or about the Financial Ombudsman Service; and second, that before you purchased a structured product through your account, no one at our firm asked you about your knowledge or experience of complex investments, and you were not warned that we were not assessing whether the product was appropriate for you. You have also told us that you have suffered a significant financial loss as a result.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by thanking the customer, acknowledging the complaint, and accurately reflecting both limbs of the complaint (failure to provide complaints/FOS information and failure to assess appropriateness for a complex product), as well as the financial loss.

The customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject) nor provide reasons. It explicitly states it is still investigating and will write again with a 'full response.' Despite being labelled as a final response stage, the actual customer-facing text is an acknowledgement/holding letter with no decision.

The customer draft does not mention the Financial Ombudsman Service referral right at all. While the handler answer notes that FOS referral information should be included in the final response, this information does not appear in the customer-facing text. The audience rule requires it to be in the customer draft.

The customer draft contains no mention of a six-month deadline to refer the complaint to the FOS. The handler answer mentions DISP 1 Annex 3R wording should be inserted but it is not present in the customer-facing text.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry that your experience has fallen short of what you should expect. We are now looking into both of these concerns carefully and will write to you again with our full response as soon as we are able.
verdicts.reasoning: The response is empathetic, non-dismissive, and acknowledges both concerns clearly. However, for a final response stage, it fails to engage with the substance of the complaint on its merits — it does not address the regulatory obligations, does not reach any conclusion, and merely promises further investigation. While the tone is fair, the lack of substantive engagement means it only partially demonstrates fair treatment of the complaint.

The customer draft does not address redress at all. The handler answer explicitly states 'Do NOT quantify or promise any redress' and the customer-facing text makes no mention of whether redress is appropriate or not, despite the complaint plainly raising significant financial loss.

verdicts.evidence: We understand that you are raising two concerns: first, that when you opened your account you were not provided with information about how to make a complaint or about the Financial Ombudsman Service; and second, that before you purchased a structured product through your account, no one at our firm asked you about your knowledge or experience of complex investments, and you were not warned that we were not assessing whether the product was appropriate for you.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing text explicitly acknowledges the complaint and accurately restates the two issues raised by the complainant.
The draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) nor provide reasons for a decision; it only says the firm is investigating and will respond later.
There is no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service as a route for the complainant to refer the complaint, nor any guidance on how to do so.
The draft does not state the six‑month deadline for referring the complaint to the FOS from the date of the final response.

verdicts.evidence: We are sorry to hear about your experience and we take your concerns very seriously... We are now looking into both of these concerns carefully and will write to you again with our full response as soon as we are able.
verdicts.reasoning: The language is courteous, acknowledges the complainant’s concerns, and promises a thorough investigation without dismissing or minimizing the issues, demonstrating fair and impartial treatment.
The response does not address whether any redress or remedial action will be offered, nor does it explain why none would be offered.

claims.claim: Under DISP 1.1A.10R, MiFID investment firms must publish details of their complaints handling process (including the complaints management policy and contact details of the complaints management function) and provide this to clients on request or when acknowledging a MiFID complaint.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.1A.10R as retrieved states exactly this: MiFID investment firms must publish details of the process to be followed when handling a MiFID complaint, including information about the complaints management policy and contact details of the complaints management function, and provide this to clients or potential clients on request or when acknowledging a MiFID complaint.

claims.claim: Under DISP 1.1A.11R, firms must provide information about the FOS (including its website address) on their website and in the general conditions of contracts with eligible complainants.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.1A.11R as retrieved states that a MiFID investment firm must provide information to eligible complainants about the Financial Ombudsman Service (including the FOS's website address) on its website and, if applicable, in the general conditions of its contracts with eligible complainants.

claims.claim: DISP 1.1A.10R and DISP 1.1A.11R are binding rules.
claims.reasoning: Both provisions are labelled as '(Rules)' in the retrieved text, confirming they are binding rules.

claims.claim: If the firm is a MiFID investment firm (which a share dealing firm almost certainly is), these obligations applied at account opening and on an ongoing basis.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provisions confirm the obligations apply to MiFID investment firms, but do not specifically state they apply 'at account opening' — they apply on the website, in general conditions, on request, or when acknowledging a complaint. The claim that a share dealing firm is 'almost certainly' a MiFID investment firm is not directly supported by the retrieved provisions.

The claim of a potential breach is partially supported but overstates the trigger for the obligation.

DISP 1.1A.31R specifically requires enclosing the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, providing the FOS website address, informing the complainant they may refer to FOS, and including the appropriate DISP 1 Annex 3R wording on time limit waiver.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I have a regulated mortgage with your bank and I'm writing to complain about two issues. First, when I fell into arrears last year, you applied a £250 'arrears management fee' each month for three consecutive months, plus compound interest on those fees. I've checked with other lenders and nobody charges anything close to that — most charge £25-50 at most. I believe these charges are grossly excessive and the compound interest on a second charge mortgage is unlawful. Second, when I tried to call your complaints line to raise this issue, the only number provided was an 0871 number. I was on hold for 45 minutes and the call cost me over £18. I shouldn't have to pay a premium rate number just to make a complaint. I'm currently on long-term sick leave following a cancer diagnosis and am struggling financially as a result of reduced income. I'd like a full refund of the excessive charges, the interest applied to them, and reimbursement of my phone costs.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises three distinct regulatory issues, all of which have clear grounding in the retrieved provisions. There is also a strong vulnerability indicator (cancer diagnosis, financial hardship from reduced income) which requires careful handling and mandates human review before any response is sent.

**Issue 1 — Arrears management charges (£250/month)**
MCOB 12.4.1R requires that payment shortfall charges must be objectively justifiable as equal to or lower than a reasonable calculation of the cost of the additional administration required. A charge of £250/month, compared to market rates of £25–50, raises a serious prima facie case that this charge is not objectively justifiable. MCOB 12.5.2R also requires that charges in connection with administering a regulated mortgage contract are not excessive, and MCOB 12.5.3G sets out factors to consider including comparison with market charges and the degree to which charges abuse the customer's trust. The handler must investigate whether the firm can objectively justify £250 as reflecting actual additional administration costs. If it cannot, the charges are likely in breach of MCOB 12.4.1R and/or MCOB 12.5.2R.

**Issue 2 — Compound interest on arrears charges (second charge mortgage)**
MCOB 12.5.5R is directly on point: a second charge lender may only charge simple interest on charges applied to a customer for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract. If this is a second charge mortgage and compound interest has been applied to the arrears management charges, that is a breach of MCOB 12.5.5R. The handler must confirm: (a) whether this is a second charge regulated mortgage contract, and (b) whether the interest applied to the arrears charges was compound rather than simple. If both are confirmed, the compound element of the interest must be reversed. Note: MCOB 12.5.5R applies to second charge regulated mortgage contracts entered into before 21 March 2016 only in relation to charges imposed for events occurring on or after 21 March 2016 (MCOB 12.1.5AR).

**Issue 3 — 0871 complaints line**
GEN 7.2.1R requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate. GEN 7.2.3R defines basic rate as the simple cost of connection, not providing the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues. GEN 7.2.5R explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant. The use of an 0871 number for the complaints line is a clear breach of GEN 7.2.1R. The customer's call costs (£18+) arose directly from this breach and reimbursement is appropriate — however, the quantum of any refund must be confirmed by the handler and approved before committing to it in writing.

**Vulnerability**
The customer has disclosed a cancer diagnosis and financial hardship. This is a clear vulnerability indicator. The handler must ensure the firm's vulnerability policies are applied throughout this complaint, and the response must be handled with appropriate care and sensitivity.

**Assumptions requiring confirmation before sending the customer draft:**
1. Confirm whether this is a second charge regulated mortgage contract (for MCOB 12.5.5R to apply on compound interest).
2. Confirm whether the interest applied to the arrears charges was compound (not simple).
3. Confirm the exact call cost the customer incurred (the customer states 'over £18' — obtain evidence if possible).
4. Confirm whether the firm can objectively justify the £250 arrears management charge under MCOB 12.4.1R.
5. Confirm the firm's vulnerability policy has been flagged on this account.
6. Any financial redress (refund of charges, interest, call costs) must be approved by the appropriate authority before the customer draft is finalised — the draft below acknowledges the complaint and commits to investigation but does not confirm specific refund amounts.

Human review is required before sending given: potential redress across multiple heads, confirmed customer vulnerability, possible regulatory breaches on two separate rules, and the need to confirm the second charge status before the compound interest position can be stated definitively to the customer.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear about your health and the financial pressures you are facing, and I want to assure you that we are taking your complaint very seriously.

Your complaint raises three concerns, which I will address in turn.

**Arrears management charges**

You have told us that we applied a £250 arrears management fee each month for three consecutive months while your account was in arrears, and that you believe this is significantly higher than charges applied by other lenders for the same type of administration. We are required by our regulator to ensure that charges applied when a customer has a payment shortfall are no higher than a reasonable calculation of the actual additional administration costs we incur. We are also required to ensure that our charges are not excessive, taking into account — among other things — how our charges compare with those of other lenders for similar services.

We are investigating whether the charges applied to your account meet these requirements. We will write to you again with our findings.

**Interest applied to arrears charges**

You have also raised a concern about the interest applied to those arrears charges. We are reviewing the basis on which that interest was calculated and whether it was applied correctly in accordance with our regulatory obligations. We will include our findings on this point in our further response to you.

**Cost of calling our complaints line**

You have told us that the only telephone number available to contact us was an 0871 number, and that you incurred a call cost of over £18 as a result. Our regulator requires that any telephone line we operate for customers to contact us about their contract must not cost more than the basic rate of connection — and 0871 numbers do not meet that requirement. We take this concern seriously and are reviewing the matter. We will address the question of your call costs as part of our response.

**Next steps**

We aim to provide you with a full response to all three points as quickly as possible. We will keep you updated on our progress.

If you have not received a final response from us within eight weeks of the date you first raised your complaint, or if you are dissatisfied with our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further details about this when we send our final response.

In the meantime, if you would like to discuss your complaint or if there is anything we can do to support you given your current circumstances, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires that payment shortfall charges must be objectively justifiable as equal to or lower than a reasonable calculation of the cost of the additional administration required, directly applicable to the £250 monthly arrears management fee.
Requires that charges in connection with administering a regulated mortgage contract are not excessive, applicable to the arrears management fees charged.

Sets out factors for determining whether a charge is excessive, including comparison with market charges and the degree to which charges abuse the customer's trust — relevant to assessing the £250 fee against market rates of £25–50.

Provides that a second charge lender may only charge simple interest (not compound interest) on charges applied to a customer for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract — directly applicable to the compound interest allegation if this is a second charge mortgage.

Confirms that MCOB 12.4 and MCOB 12.5 rules apply to second charge regulated mortgage contracts entered into before 21 March 2016 in relation to charges imposed for events occurring on or after 21 March 2016, relevant to confirming the temporal scope of MCOB 12.5.5R.

Requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate — the complaints line obligation falls squarely within this rule.
Defines basic rate as the simple cost of connection, not providing the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues — relevant to establishing that 0871 numbers exceed the basic rate.
Explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant with the call charges rule, directly confirming the breach alleged by the customer.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 12.5 Excessive charges: regulated mortgage contracts, home reversion plans and regulated sale and rent back agreements

## MCOB 12.5.1 (Rules)

A firm must ensure that any regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement that it enters into does not impose, and cannot be used to impose, excessive charges upon a customer.

## MCOB 12.5.2 (Rules)

A firm must ensure that its charges to a customer in connection with the firm entering into, making a further advance or further release on, administering, arranging or advising on a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement , or arranging or advising on a variation to the terms of a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement are not excessive.

## MCOB 12.5.3 (Guidance)

When determining whether a charge is excessive, a firm should consider:

(1) the amount of its charges for the services or products in question compared with charges for similar products or services on the market;

(2) the degree to which the charges are an abuse of the trust that the customer has placed in the firm; and

(3) the nature and extent of the disclosure of the charges to the customer.

## MCOB 12.5.4 (Guidance)

Mortgage lenders are also subject to requirements relating to responsible lending (see MCOB 11).

## MCOB 12.5.5 (Rules)

A second charge lender may only charge interest on charges applied to a customer for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract if the interest is simple interest.

metadata.section_name: MCOB 12.5 Excessive charges: regulated mortgage contracts, home reversion plans and regulated sale and rent back agreements

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 12.1 Application

## MCOB 12.1.4 (Rules)

The payment shortfall charges and excessive charges requirements in this chapter will continue to apply to a firm after a regulated mortgage contract has come to an end following the sale of a repossessed property. The excessive charges requirements will continue to apply to a firm after a home reversion plan has ended. References in this chapter to 'customer' will include references to a former customer as appropriate.

## MCOB 12.1.5 (Guidance)

The FCA will expect a firm to ensure that charges made to a customer arising from the sale of a repossessed property and charges arising in relation to a sale shortfall are not excessive and are subject to the same considerations as apply with respect to payment shortfall charges under this chapter.

## MCOB 12.1.5A (Rules)

The rules in MCOB 12.4 (Payment shortfall charges: regulated mortgage contracts) and MCOB 12.5 (Excessive charges: regulated mortgage contracts, home reversion plans and regulated sale and rent back agreements) apply to:

(1) second charge regulated mortgage contracts entered into before 21 March 2016, in relation to charges imposed on a customer for events occurring on or after 21 March 2016; and

(2) regulated mortgage contracts which are legacy CCA mortgage contracts secured by a first charge legal mortgage, in relation to charges imposed on a customer for events occurring on or after the earliest of:

(a) the date on which the lender first acts in compliance or purported compliance with rules in the FCA Handbook which apply to regulated mortgage contracts in respect of the contract;

(b) the date from which the lender notifies the borrower in writing that it will act in compliance with such rules in respect of the contract; and

(c) 21 March 2017.

metadata.section_name: MCOB 12.1 Application

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 12.2 Purpose

## MCOB 12.2.1 (Guidance)

(1) Principle 6 requires a firm to pay due regard to the interests of its customers and treat them fairly. A firm is also under an obligation, as a consequence of this sourcebook's disclosure requirements, to make charges transparent to customers. This chapter reinforces these requirements by preventing a firm from imposing unfair and excessive charges.

(2) The level of charges under a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement is not typically a matter for regulation. However, in certain limited circumstances, the FCA believes that customers should be protected from unfair and excessive charging practices. This chapter considers four specific circumstances, where:

(a) the charges imposed upon a customer seeking to terminate a regulated mortgage contract before the end of the term of the contract do not reflect the cost of termination to the firm;

(b) the charges imposed on a customer in payment difficulties are not based upon the costs incurred by the firm;

(c) the charges (including rates of interest) imposed on a customer under a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement are excessive and contrary to the customer's interests; and

(d) the charges made to a customer in connection with a firm entering into, making a further advance on, administering, arranging or advising on a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement , or arranging or advising on a variation to the terms of a regulated mortgage contract, home reversion plan or regulated sale and rent back agreement are excessive.

metadata.section_name: MCOB 12.2 Purpose

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 12.4 Payment shortfall charges: regulated mortgage contracts

## MCOB 12.4.1D (Guidance)

A firm may recalculate the periodic instalment of capital or interest (or both), provided that any such recalculation is consistent with the firm’s obligations under the Handbook.

If a firm exercises a power under the terms of a mortgage contract to recalculate periodic instalments of capital or interest (or both) using a mortgage balance that includes charges (such as arrears management charges) or interest arising because one or more monthly instalments were missed, the firm does not have to keep a record of the amount of each periodic instalment of capital or interest (or both) omitting any element of such charges or interest to comply with MCOB 12.4.1BR.

## MCOB 12.4.2 (Guidance)

For each type of payment shortfall charge (for example, a monthly arrears management charge), a firm may calculate the same level of additional administration costs and payment shortfall charges for all regulated mortgage contracts where the customer is in payment shortfall , rather than performing a calculation on the basis of the individual regulated mortgage contract with the particular customer.

## MCOB 12.4.3 (Guidance)

Firms are also subject to requirements on information provision and standards relating to payment shortfalls and repossessions (see MCOB 13 (Payment difficulties and repossessions)).

metadata.section_name: MCOB 12.4 Payment shortfall charges: regulated mortgage contracts

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 4.6A Rolling-up of fees or charges into loan

## MCOB 4.6A.1 (Rules)

A mortgage lender may not offer a regulated mortgage contract to a customer on the basis that fees or charges of any kind (receivable either by the mortgage lender or another party) are automatically added to the sum advanced.

## MCOB 4.6A.2 (Rules)

A firm must not undertake any action that commits a customer to an application for a regulated mortgage contract where a fee or charge of any kind (receivable either by the firm or another party) is to be added to the sum advanced under the regulated mortgage contract, unless the customer has made a positive choice to add the fee or charge to the sum advanced.

metadata.section_name: MCOB 4.6A Rolling-up of fees or charges into loan

retrieved_chunks.content:
# GEN — GEN 7.2 Call charges

## GEN 7.2.1 (Rules)

A firm which operates a telephone line for the purpose of enabling a consumer to contact the firm in relation to a contract that has been entered into with the firm, must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate for the telephone call.

## GEN 7.2.2 (Guidance)

The contract entered into with the firm may be in writing or otherwise.

## GEN 7.2.3 (Rules)

For the purposes of the call charges rule, the basic rate is the simple cost of connection and must not provide the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues.

## GEN 7.2.4 (Rules)

The following numbers, if used by firms, would comply with the call charges rule:

(1) geographic numbers or numbers which are always set at the same rate, which usually begin with the prefix 01, 02 or 03;

(2) calls which can be free of charge to call, for example 0800 and 0808 numbers; and

(3) standard mobile numbers, which usually begin with the prefix 07, provided that the firm ordinarily uses a mobile number to receive telephone calls.

## GEN 7.2.5 (Rules)

The following numbers, if used by firms, would not comply with the call charges rule:

(1) premium rate numbers that begin with the prefix 09;

(2) other revenue sharing numbers in which a portion of the call charge can be used to either provide a service or make a small payment to the firm, such as telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 084 or 0871, 0872 or 0873; and

(3) telephone numbers that begin with the prefix 0870 as the cost of making a telephone call on such numbers can be higher than a geographic cost and will vary depending on the consumer’s telephone tariff.

metadata.section_name: GEN 7.2 Call charges

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 12.4 Payment shortfall charges: regulated mortgage contracts

## MCOB 12.4.1 (Rules)

(1) A firm must ensure that any regulated mortgage contract that it enters into does not impose, and cannot be used to impose, a charge or charges for a payment shortfall on a customer unless the firm is able objectively to justify that the charge is equal to or lower than a reasonable calculation of the cost of the additional administration required as a result of the customer having a payment shortfall .

(2) [deleted]

## MCOB 12.4.1A (Evidential Provisions)

The imposition of a charge for a payment shortfall on a customer who is adhering to an arrangement under which the customer and the firm agree that the customer will make payments of a set amount per month (or other agreed period) on agreed dates may be relied upon as tending to show contravention of MCOB 12.4.1R (1)

## MCOB 12.4.1B (Rules)

When:

(1) a customer has a payment shortfall in respect of a regulated mortgage contract;

(2) a payment is made which is not sufficient to cover all of the amounts that are currently due under that contract and the firm must therefore decide how to allocate the payment; and

(3) as part of that decision, the firm is considering the respective priority to be given to:

(a) the current month’s periodic instalment of capital or interest (or both);

(b) the payment shortfall; and

(c) interest or charges resulting from the payment shortfall,

the firm must set that order of priority in a way that will minimise the amount of the payment shortfall once the payment has been allocated.

## MCOB 12.4.1C (Guidance)

MCOB 12.4.1BR does not preclude a firm applying part or all of a payment received to fees and charges not resulting from the payment shortfall (such as ground rent settled on behalf of the customer).

metadata.section_name: MCOB 12.4 Payment shortfall charges: regulated mortgage contracts
MCOB 12.4 Payment shortfall charges: regulated mortgage contracts

retrieved_chunks.content:
# MCOB — MCOB 12.3 Early repayment charges: regulated mortgage contracts

## MCOB 12.3.1 (Rules)

A firm must ensure that any regulated mortgage contract that it enters into does not impose, and cannot be used to impose, an early repayment charge other than one that is:

(1) able to be expressed as a cash value; and

(2) a reasonable pre-estimate of the costs as a result of the customer repaying the amount due under the regulated mortgage contract before the contract has terminated.

## MCOB 12.3.2 (Guidance)

A firm can choose the method it employs for calculating early repayment charges in accordance with MCOB 12.3.1 R. A firm should not use the 'Rule of 78' , which is not appropriate as it effectively overstates the cost to the mortgage lender.

## MCOB 12.3.3 (Guidance)

A firm may calculate the same level of early repayment charge for all regulated mortgage contracts of a similar type (for example a tranche of regulated mortgage contracts offering a particular fixed rate of interest), rather than on the basis of the individual regulated mortgage contract with the particular customer.

## MCOB 12.3.4 (Rules)

Before:

(1) entering into a regulated mortgage contract with a customer; or

(2) making a further advance on an existing regulated mortgage contract; or

(3) changing all or part of a regulated mortgage contract from one interest rate to another; a firm must disclose to the customer:

(a) in the illustration provided in accordance with MCOB 5, MCOB 7.6.7 R, MCOB 7.6.18 R, MCOB 7.6.22 R, MCOB 7.6.31 R, or MCOB 9; and

(b) in the illustration provided as part of the offer document in accordance with MCOB 6.4.1 R(1) and MCOB 9.5;

the maximum amount payable as an early repayment charge in respect of that regulated mortgage contract, if an early repayment charge applies.

metadata.section_name: MCOB 12.3 Early repayment charges: regulated mortgage contracts

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

(c) If the agreement provides that a charge is payable by a customer in exchange for the creation or continuation of an arranged overdraft facility, whether or not the customer in fact uses the facility, this charge is also caught by the definition of an arranged overdraft charge unless the facility has a pre-agreed limit in excess of £10,000. A charge of this sort is often referred to as a “facility fee” and payable periodically, for example annually.

(d) The definitions of an arranged overdraft charge and an unarranged overdraft charge are not limited to charges that are described as financial consideration for the provision of credit. They could include, for example, a charge that is expressed as being referable to the execution of the payment transaction, if the charge is payable only where the transaction results in the account being in an overdrawn position or remaining in such a position. A charge for a payment transaction that is payable irrespective of whether or not the current account has a credit balance or a debit balance is not, however, caught by these definitions.

(e) The definitions also do not include charges for operating or maintaining a personal current account (as distinct from charges for granting or continuing to make available an arranged overdraft facility in connection with the account), provided that the incidence and amount of the charges are not affected by whether or how much the customer uses an overdraft. A monthly account charge could be an example of such a charge.

(9) CONC 5C.2 requires firms to use only a rate of interest expressed as a percentage applied on an annual basis to the relevant balance of arranged overdraft or unarranged overdraft. If interest is compounded, firms are free to choose the intervals at which they add arranged overdraft charges and unarranged overdraft charges to the principal balance, provided that the same compounding frequency is used i

metadata.section_name: CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

Additionally, the phrasing implies this is not the final response, despite the scenario stating it is the final response stage. The right is mentioned but not fully signposted with how to contact the FOS.

The customer draft does not state that the complainant has six months from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. It only mentions the eight-week timeline and defers FOS details to a later response.

Our regulator requires that any telephone line we operate for customers to contact us about their contract must not cost more than the basic rate of connection — and 0871 numbers do not meet that requirement.

However, for a final response, it fails to reach any conclusion or decision, which undermines fair treatment — the customer is left without resolution despite this being the final response stage. The engagement is substantive but incomplete.

verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft acknowledges the question of redress implicitly (call costs, charges) but does not reach any clear position on whether redress is offered or explain why none is due. It defers all redress decisions to a future communication. The handler answer discusses redress in detail but explicitly states it should not be committed to in the customer draft.

verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer explicitly identifies the cancer diagnosis and financial hardship as vulnerability indicators and mandates application of vulnerability policies. The customer draft acknowledges the health situation and financial pressures with empathetic language and offers support. Both the handler answer and customer draft recognise and act on the vulnerability.

verdicts.reasoning: The customer‑facing draft opens by thanking the complainant, expresses empathy, and explicitly restates the three issues raised, showing an accurate understanding of the complaint.
The draft does not state any firm decision (uphold, partially uphold, reject) on the issues; it only says the matters are being investigated.

verdicts.evidence: you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly informs the complainant of the right to refer the complaint to the FOS.
The draft mentions an eight‑week response deadline but does not state the six‑month period within which the complainant may refer the matter to the FOS.

verdicts.evidence: We are investigating whether the charges applied to your account meet these requirements... We will keep you updated on our progress.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with each point raised, uses respectful, non‑dismissive language and promises to keep the complainant informed, demonstrating fair treatment.
The draft only says the firm is investigating; it does not reach a position on whether any refund or other redress will be provided, nor does it explain why none might be due.

verdicts.reasoning: Both the handler note and the customer draft recognise the complainant’s cancer diagnosis and financial hardship and indicate that the firm will handle the matter sensitively and offer support.

claims.claim: MCOB 12.4.1R requires that payment shortfall charges must be objectively justifiable as equal to or lower than a reasonable calculation of the cost of the additional administration required.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.4.1R(1) states that a firm must ensure that a charge for a payment shortfall is not imposed unless the firm is able objectively to justify that the charge is equal to or lower than a reasonable calculation of the cost of the additional administration required.

claims.claim: MCOB 12.5.2R requires that charges in connection with administering a regulated mortgage contract are not excessive.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.5.2R explicitly states that a firm must ensure that its charges to a customer in connection with administering a regulated mortgage contract are not excessive.

claims.claim: MCOB 12.5.3G sets out factors to consider including comparison with market charges and the degree to which charges abuse the customer's trust.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.5.3G lists (1) comparison with charges for similar products or services on the market and (2) the degree to which the charges are an abuse of the trust that the customer has placed in the firm.

claims.claim: MCOB 12.5.5R states that a second charge lender may only charge simple interest on charges applied to a customer for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.5.5R directly states: 'A second charge lender may only charge interest on charges applied to a customer for breach of a second charge regulated mortgage contract if the interest is simple interest.'

claims.claim: MCOB 12.5.5R applies to second charge regulated mortgage contracts entered into before 21 March 2016 only in relation to charges imposed for events occurring on or after 21 March 2016 (MCOB 12.1.5AR).
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.1.5AR(1) states that the rules in MCOB 12.4 and MCOB 12.5 apply to second charge regulated mortgage contracts entered into before 21 March 2016, in relation to charges imposed on a customer for events occurring on or after 21 March 2016.

claims.claim: GEN 7.2.1R requires that a firm operating a telephone line for consumers to contact it in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate.
claims.reasoning: GEN 7.2.1R states exactly this: a firm which operates a telephone line for the purpose of enabling a consumer to contact the firm in relation to a contract must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate.

claims.claim: GEN 7.2.3R defines basic rate as the simple cost of connection, not providing the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues.
claims.reasoning: GEN 7.2.3R states: 'the basic rate is the simple cost of connection and must not provide the firm with a contribution to its costs or revenues.'

claims.claim: GEN 7.2.5R explicitly lists 0871 numbers as non-compliant.
claims.reasoning: GEN 7.2.5R(2) explicitly lists telephone numbers beginning with 0871 as numbers that would not comply with the call charges rule.

claims.claim: The use of an 0871 number for the complaints line is a clear breach of GEN 7.2.1R.
claims.reasoning: GEN 7.2.1R prohibits binding consumers to pay more than basic rate, and GEN 7.2.5R(2) explicitly states 0871 numbers would not comply with the call charges rule.

claims.claim: We are required by our regulator to ensure that charges applied when a customer has a payment shortfall are no higher than a reasonable calculation of the actual additional administration costs we incur.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.4.1R(1) requires that a payment shortfall charge must be objectively justifiable as equal to or lower than a reasonable calculation of the cost of the additional administration required. The customer-facing paraphrase is faithful to this.

claims.claim: We are also required to ensure that our charges are not excessive, taking into account — among other things — how our charges compare with those of other lenders for similar services.
claims.reasoning: MCOB 12.5.2R requires charges not to be excessive, and MCOB 12.5.3G(1) lists comparison with charges for similar products or services on the market as a factor.

claims.claim: Our regulator requires that any telephone line we operate for customers to contact us about their contract must not cost more than the basic rate of connection.
claims.reasoning: GEN 7.2.1R requires that a firm must not bind the consumer to pay more than the basic rate, and GEN 7.2.3R defines basic rate as the simple cost of connection.

claims.claim: 0871 numbers do not meet that requirement.
claims.reasoning: GEN 7.2.5R(2) explicitly states that 0871 numbers would not comply with the call charges rule.

claims.claim: If you have not received a final response from us within eight weeks of the date you first raised your complaint, or if you are dissatisfied with our final response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: While this is a standard regulatory requirement under DISP rules, none of the retrieved provisions contain the eight-week time limit or the right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service. There is no supporting chunk in the retrieved provisions.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I took out a home insurance policy through my bank's branch last year. I've since discovered that the bank received a significantly higher commission from the insurer they recommended compared to two other insurers whose products were arguably better suited to my needs and cheaper. When I asked the branch advisor at the time whether there were other options, they simply said 'this is the best one for you' without explaining why or mentioning any alternatives. I feel I was steered into a more expensive, less suitable policy because the bank was incentivised by the commission arrangement. I raised this with the bank's complaints team three weeks ago and received an acknowledgement, but I'm now writing to request a full resolution. I'm a retired pensioner on a fixed income and the additional premium cost has been a real stretch for my household budget.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious regulatory concerns that require careful handling and human review before any response is sent to the customer.

**Regulatory position based on retrieved provisions:**

1. **Commission disclosure (CONC 4.5.3R):** A credit broker must prominently disclose the existence and nature of any commission where it could affect the broker's impartiality or have a material impact on the customer's transactional decision. If the bank acted as a credit broker in connection with any premium finance arrangement, this rule applies. However, this complaint is about home insurance intermediation, not a credit agreement — so CONC 4.5.3R applies only if there was a linked credit/premium finance element.

2. **Insurance commission disclosure (ICOBS 4.4.1R):** This rule requires an insurance intermediary to disclose commission on a commercial customer's request. Importantly, the retrieved provisions only provide a commission disclosure rule for *commercial* customers under ICOBS 4.4. There is no retrieved provision in the context that imposes an equivalent mandatory commission disclosure obligation for *retail/personal* insurance customers. You should flag this gap — the retrieved context may be insufficient to fully address the retail insurance commission disclosure position, and you should seek further regulatory guidance or escalate.

3. **Conduct in credit broking — preference for personal gain (CONC 2.5 chunk):** The retrieved provisions indicate that a firm must not give preference to the credit products of a particular lender where the object is for the personal gain of the firm rather than the best interests of the customer, and must not secure credit at a higher rate than requested for personal gain. These provisions relate to credit broking, not insurance intermediation directly, but they reflect the broader principle that commission-driven recommendations contrary to customer interests are prohibited.

4. **Suitability (COBS 9A.2.20AR and COBS 9A.2.21G):** These provisions relate to insurance-based investment products, not standard home insurance policies. They are unlikely to apply directly here unless the policy had an investment element.

5. **Renewal pricing (ICOBS 6B.2):** The retrieved ICOBS 6B.2 provisions relate to renewal pricing fairness. These may be relevant if the customer has since renewed, but do not directly address the original sale complaint.

**Key concerns requiring human review:**
- The customer is a retired pensioner on a fixed income — this is a potential vulnerability indicator requiring heightened care under Treating Customers Fairly/Consumer Duty principles (though no specific Consumer Duty provisions were retrieved).
- The complaint involves potential financial detriment (higher premium, less suitable product) and possible redress — a financial decision is in scope.
- The retrieved provisions do not include a clear retail insurance commission disclosure rule (ICOBS 4.5 or equivalent) that would directly govern the bank's obligation to disclose commission to a retail insurance customer at point of sale. This is a significant gap in the retrieved context.
- The complaint was acknowledged three weeks ago. You should check whether any regulatory response deadline is approaching or has been missed.
- The allegation that the advisor said 'this is the best one for you' without explanation or disclosure of alternatives or commission could engage conduct of business obligations not fully covered by the retrieved provisions.

**Recommended action:**
- Do not send a final response yet. Flag for senior review and compliance input.
- Confirm whether a premium finance/credit arrangement was part of the policy sale (which would engage CONC provisions more directly).
- Obtain the full commission disclosure records for the sale and check what, if anything, was disclosed to the customer at point of sale.
- Check the complaint timeline against your regulatory deadlines for issuing a final response.
- Consider whether the customer's vulnerability (fixed income, pensioner) requires any additional support or adjustments to how you communicate.
- The draft below is an interim acknowledgement/update letter only — it does not constitute a final response and does not admit liability or quantify redress. A final response will require further investigation and compliance sign-off.

**Assumption flagged:** The customer draft below treats this as an interim update rather than a final response, given the outstanding investigation. If a final response is now due, the letter will need to be substantially revised with findings and outcome — do not send this as a final response without human review and compliance input.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us and for your patience while we look into your complaint. I am sorry to hear about the concerns you have raised regarding the home insurance policy you took out through our branch, and I understand that the additional cost has placed a real strain on your household budget. We take this matter seriously.

You have told us that you were not made aware of other insurance options at the time of the sale, that no explanation was given for the recommendation, and that you were not informed of the commission arrangement between the bank and the insurer. You have also told us that other products may have been better suited to your needs and available at a lower cost. These are important concerns and we are investigating them carefully.

As part of our investigation, we are reviewing the records of the advice given to you at the branch, including what information was provided to you about the product recommended and any commission arrangements in place at the time.

We will write to you again with our findings and our response to your complaint as soon as our investigation is complete. If you have any questions in the meantime, or if there is anything further you would like to tell us that may assist our investigation, please do not hesitate to contact us using the details below.

Once we have completed our investigation, we will set out our conclusions and explain any next steps available to you, including your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service if you remain dissatisfied with our response.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires a credit broker to prominently disclose the existence and nature of any commission where it could affect the broker's impartiality or have a material impact on the customer's transactional decision — relevant if the bank acted as a credit broker in connection with any premium finance element of the policy.

Where commission disclosure is required under CONC 4.5.3R, the credit broker must also disclose how the commission may affect the amounts payable by the customer — relevant to whether the customer was told how the commission arrangement affected the cost of the product.

Provides guidance on when disclosure of variable commission arrangements is expected, including where the firm may earn different commission depending on which product the customer enters into — relevant to the allegation that the bank was incentivised to recommend a higher-commission product.

Requires an insurance intermediary to disclose commission to a commercial customer on request — noted here as the only retrieved commission disclosure rule for insurance intermediation, but flagged as applying to commercial customers only, not retail customers such as this complainant.

Indicates that commission disclosure for insurance intermediaries should include all forms of remuneration, including profit-sharing and volume-based arrangements — relevant to the nature of the commission arrangement alleged.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 4.5 Commissions

## CONC 4.5.7 (Guidance)

The following are examples of discretionary commission arrangements:

(1) An agreement under which the lender sets a minimum rate of interest and the commission payable by the lender to the credit broker in respect of a regulated credit agreement entered into by the lender is calculated by reference to the difference between the rate of interest negotiated by the credit broker and payable by the customer under the regulated credit agreement and the minimum rate of interest. These types of arrangements are often referred to as “increasing difference in charges” or “interest rate upward adjustment” arrangements.

(2) An agreement under which the lender sets a maximum rate of interest and the commission payable by the lender to the credit broker in respect of a regulated credit agreement entered into by the lender is calculated by reference to the difference between the rate of interest negotiated by the credit broker and payable by the customer under the regulated credit agreement and the maximum rate of interest. These types of arrangements are often referred to as “decreasing difference in charges” or “interest rate downward adjustment” arrangements.

(3) An arrangement or agreement under which the commission payable by the lender to the credit broker in respect of a regulated credit agreement entered into by the lender varies (within set parameters) according to the rate of interest negotiated by the credit broker and payable by the customer under the regulated credit agreement. These types of arrangement are often referred to as “scaled models”.

metadata.section_name: CONC 4.5 Commissions

retrieved_chunks.content:
# ICOBS — ICOBS 6B.2 Setting renewal prices

## ICOBS 6B.2.35 (Rules)

An insurance intermediary that carries out insurance distribution activities at renewal and which either:

(1) forgoes commission in whole or in part when selling to new business customers; or

(2) offers a cash or cash-equivalent incentive (within the meaning of ICOBS 6B.2.12R) to new business customers,

must, to the extent that a customer renewing a policy would be eligible to benefit from the commission forgone or the cash or cash-equivalent incentive if they were a new business customer, include that forgone commission or cash or cash-equivalent incentive when:

(1) determining the equivalent new business price at renewal; and

(2) applying ICOBS 6B.2.9R to ICOBS 6B.2.15G.

## ICOBS 6B.2.36 (Rules)

A firm that has responsibility for setting the price of an additional product that is available to a customer in connection with a home insurance or motor insurance policy must ensure that the price of the additional product at renewal is no higher than the price at which the additional product would be offered to the customer if they were a new business customer.

## ICOBS 6B.2.37 (Guidance)

Where the additional product is retail premium finance, the price referred to in ICOBS 6B.2.36R is the APR if the retail premium finance is a regulated credit agreement or in all other cases the amount paid by the customer for retail premium finance for the amount of premium to be financed by the retail premium finance.

metadata.section_name: ICOBS 6B.2 Setting renewal prices

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 2.5 Conduct of business: credit broking

## CONC 2.5.9 (Guidance)

(1) It is likely to be an inappropriate offer of an inducement or incentive to enter into an regulated credit agreement or a regulated consumer hire agreement to state that the offer in relation to the agreement will be withdrawn or the terms and conditions of the offer will worsen if the agreement is not signed immediately or within a stated period after the communication, unless the firm's offer on those terms and conditions will in fact be withdrawn or worsen in the period indicated to the customer.[Note: paragraph 3.9j (box) of CBG]

(2) An example of unfairly requesting, suggesting or directing a customer to a premium rate telephone number is likely to be to do so in relation to a customer wishing to complain about the firm's service or to request a refund, including, for example, under section 155 of the CCA.[Note: paragraph 6.19f of CBG]

(3) It is unlikely to be reasonable for it to be necessary for a customer to make more than one telephone call exceeding 15 minutes to a firm to apply for credit. Where a longer call is required, the firm should ensure the call is not made on a premium rate telephone number.[Note: paragraph 3.9i (box) of CBG]

(4) It is unlikely to be reasonable to request, suggest or direct a customer to call the firm repeatedly to check on the status of an application. A call to check on the status of an application should not last more than five minutes. [Note: paragraph 3.9i (box) of CBG]

(5) A firm should disclose to a customer the amount, or likely amount, of any fee payable for its services as early as practicable in the firm's dealings with the customer. CONC 4.4.2 R requires a credit broker to disclose any such fee agreed with the customer in writing or in another durable medium.[Note: paragraphs 2.2, 7th bullet, 3.7l and 4.9 of CBG]

metadata.section_name: CONC 2.5 Conduct of business: credit broking

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 4.5 Commissions

## CONC 4.5.4 (Rules)

At the request of the customer, a credit broker must disclose to the customer, in good time before a regulated credit agreement or a regulated consumer hire agreement is entered into, the amount (or if the precise amount is not known, the likely amount) of any commission or fee or other remuneration payable to the credit broker by the lender or owner or a third party.

[Note: paragraph 3.7i (box) of CBG]

## CONC 4.5.5 (Guidance)

The purpose of CONC 4.5.6R to CONC 4.5.8G is to prohibit credit brokers and lenders to whom they introduce customers wishing to enter into regulated credit agreements to finance the acquisition of motor vehicles from making or relying on arrangements under which credit brokers are given authority to decide or negotiate the prices of those regulated credit agreements on behalf of lenders and the amount of commission the credit brokers earn is affected by those prices.

## CONC 4.5.6 (Rules)

A lender or credit broker must not:

(1) enter into or have rights or obligations under a discretionary commission arrangement; or

(2) seek to exercise, enforce or rely on rights or obligations under a discretionary commission arrangement, including any rights or obligations to receive or tender payment of commission, fee or other financial consideration.

metadata.section_name: CONC 4.5 Commissions

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 4.5 Commissions

## CONC 4.5.3 (Rules)

A credit broker must prominently disclose to a customer in good time before a credit agreement or a consumer hire agreement is entered into, the existence and nature of any commission or fee or other remuneration payable to the credit broker by the lender or owner or a third party , where the existence or amount of the commission, fee or other remuneration could actually or potentially:

(1) affect the impartiality of the credit broker in recommending the credit agreement or the consumer hire agreement ; or

(2) if made known to the customer, have a material impact on the customer’s transactional decision to enter into the credit agreement or the consumer hire agreement . [Note: paragraph 3.7i (box) and 3.7j of CBG and 5.5 (box) of ILG]

## CONC 4.5.3A (Rules)

In circumstances where the credit broker is required to disclose the existence and nature of any commission, fee or other remuneration under CONC 4.5.3R, it must also disclose to the customer, at the same time and with equal prominence, how the existence and nature of this commission, fee or other remuneration may affect the amounts payable by the customer under the relevant credit agreement or consumer hire agreement.

metadata.section_name: CONC 4.5 Commissions

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 4.5 Commissions

## CONC 4.5.3B (Guidance)

(1) Where the amount of any commission, fee or other remuneration in CONC 4.5.3R varies due to a factor specified in the arrangement or agreement under which the commission, fee or other remuneration is payable, for example a specific feature of the credit agreement or consumer hire agreement or the level of work undertaken by the credit broker, the credit broker should make disclosure under CONC 4.5.3R in relation to the commission, fee or other remuneration.

(2) Where:(a) the firm has entered into arrangements (irrespective of how many other persons those arrangements are with) under which it may earn commission, fees or other remuneration in relation to two or more different credit agreements or consumer hire agreements; (b) the customer could be eligible for two or more of those agreements;(c) the credit agreement or the consumer hire agreement the firm is recommending is one of those agreements;(d) the commission, fees or other remuneration payable to the firm varies depending on which of the credit agreements or consumer hire agreements the customer enters into,the firm should make disclosure to the customer under CONC 4.5.3R in relation to the arrangements.

(3) The disclosure in (2) may be in general terms, but it should enable the customer reasonably to appreciate the effect of the arrangements.

(4) The credit broker is not, under CONC 4.5.3AR, required to provide to the customer an individually tailored illustration of how the commission, fees or other remuneration in CONC 4.5.3R may affect the amounts payable by the customer under the credit agreement or consumer hire agreement.

metadata.section_name: CONC 4.5 Commissions

retrieved_chunks.content:
# ICOBS — ICOBS 4.4 Commission disclosure for commercial customers

## ICOBS 4.4.1 (Rules)

(1) An insurance intermediary must, on a commercial customer's request, promptly disclose the commission that it and any associate receives in connection with a policy.

(2) Disclosure must be in cash terms (estimated, if necessary) and in writing or another durable medium. To the extent this is not possible, the firm must give the basis for calculation.

## ICOBS 4.4.2 (Guidance)

An insurance intermediary should include all forms of remuneration from any arrangements it may have. This includes arrangements for sharing profits, for payments relating to the volume of sales, and for payments from premium finance companies in connection with arranging finance.

## ICOBS 4.4.3 (Guidance)

(1) The commission disclosure rule is additional to the general law on the fiduciary obligations of an agent in that it applies whether or not the insurance intermediary is an agent of the commercial customer.

(2) In relation to contracts of insurance, the essence of these fiduciary obligations is generally a duty to account to the agent’s principal. But where a customer employs an insurance intermediary by way of business and does not remunerate him, and where it is usual for the firm to be remunerated by way of commission paid by the insurer out of premium payable by the customer, then there is no duty to account but if the customer asks what the firm's remuneration is, it must tell him.

metadata.section_name: ICOBS 4.4 Commission disclosure for commercial customers

retrieved_chunks.content:
# COBS — COBS 9A.2 Assessing suitability: the obligations

## COBS 9A.2.20A (Rules)

When providing a personal recommendation on an insurance-based investment product in accordance with COBS 9A.2.1R and COBS 9A.2.16R, a firm must not make a recommendation where none of the insurance-based investment products are suitable for the client.

[Note: article 9(6) of the IDD Regulation]

## COBS 9A.2.21 (Guidance)

(1) A transaction may be unsuitable for a client due to the risks of the associated financial instruments, the type of transaction, the characteristics of the order or the frequency of the trading.(1A) An insurance-based investment product may be unsuitable for a client due to the risks of the underlying investment assets, the type or characteristics of the product or the frequency of switching of underlying investment assets.(2) A series of transactions, each of which are suitable when viewed in isolation may be unsuitable if the recommendation or the decisions to trade are made with a frequency that is not in the best interests of the client.(3) In the case of portfolio management, a transaction might be unsuitable if it would result in an unsuitable portfolio.[Note: recital 9 to the IDD Regulation]

metadata.section_name: COBS 9A.2 Assessing suitability: the obligations

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am 78 years old and have limited mobility due to a hip replacement. In February, my bank switched my longstanding savings account to a new 'Digital Flex Saver' product. This account requires customers to manage everything through a smartphone app and offers no branch or telephone access for transactions. I complained to the bank by phone on Monday morning, and by Wednesday afternoon the adviser called me back saying they'd move me back to my old account type and waive the fees I'd been charged for the failed app transactions. I said that sounded fine and thanked them. However, I never received anything in writing afterwards — no letter, no email, nothing confirming what happened or telling me about the Ombudsman. I'm now worried because I've since realised the new product was completely unsuitable for someone like me who cannot use a smartphone, and I want to understand my rights. I feel the bank shouldn't have moved me onto this product in the first place given my age and circumstances, and I also want to know why I didn't get the written confirmation I believe I'm entitled to after my complaint was resolved.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several important issues that require careful handling and human review before any response is sent.

**1. Vulnerability and suitability concern**
The customer is 78 years old with limited mobility and cannot use a smartphone. The bank migrated them onto a product that requires smartphone-only management. This raises a potential suitability concern. However, the retrieved provisions do not include specific FCA rules on product suitability or vulnerability (e.g. FCA Consumer Duty / PRIN 12 / BCOBS suitability provisions are not retrieved in sufficient detail to ground a regulatory position on the migration decision itself). `insufficient_context` is therefore set to true on this point, and human review is required.

**2. The 'next business day' resolution and written confirmation**
The complaint was received on Monday morning and the adviser called back on Wednesday afternoon — that is more than one business day after receipt. The internal procedure (drawing on DISP 1.5 as referenced in the policy) permits informal resolution without a formal written response only where the complaint is resolved by close of business on the *next* business day. Here, resolution was communicated on Wednesday, which is the second business day at the earliest — and likely outside the next-business-day window depending on exact timing. This means the complaint almost certainly could not be closed under the informal 'next business day rule.' Accordingly, the bank was required to follow the full complaints procedure, including issuing a written acknowledgement within five business days (per the internal procedure referencing DISP 1.6.2R) and ultimately a written final response.

The customer received neither a written acknowledgement nor a written final response, and was not informed of their right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service. This appears to be a procedural breach.

**3. FOS referral rights**
Under DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in the internal procedure), the final response must inform the customer of their right to refer to the FOS. The customer has not received this. The six-month time limit for FOS referral runs from the date of the final response (DISP 2.8.2R). Importantly, per DISP 2.8.3G, the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that constitutes a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication — and that response must tell the complainant about the six-month limit. Since no written final response was issued, the six-month clock has arguably not started running, which is potentially beneficial to the customer but also means the bank's obligations remain open.

**4. Recommended action**
- Escalate to the Head of Customer Operations given the vulnerability indicators and potential regulatory significance.
- Issue a written final response (or, if the complaint is being upheld and the remedy already agreed, a written confirmation that serves as the final response) that: (a) confirms the outcome and remedy; (b) informs the customer of their FOS referral right with the six-month time limit, FOS contact details, and the explanatory leaflet; and (c) is written in plain English.
- Given the financial redress element (waived fees), the final response letter must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch per the internal procedure.
- The suitability/product migration concern should be investigated separately — this may indicate a systemic issue.

**5. Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft**
- Confirm the exact dates and times of complaint receipt and resolution call to determine definitively whether the next-business-day rule could apply.
- Confirm the amount of fees waived — if £500 or more, countersignature by Head of Customer Operations is mandatory.
- Confirm whether the customer has a preferred communication channel (given mobility limitations, a paper letter may be most appropriate).
- The customer draft below is written on the assumption that the bank is issuing a written final response now, upholding the complaint and confirming the remedy already verbally agreed. If the position changes, the draft must be revised.
- The suitability/migration point is not addressed in the customer draft below because the retrieved provisions do not adequately ground a regulatory position on it — a human reviewer should decide how to address this substantively.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting in touch with us. I am writing to confirm the outcome of your complaint and to apologise that you did not receive written confirmation sooner — you were entitled to this, and I am sorry for the oversight.

**Your complaint**

You complained that your savings account was moved to our new 'Digital Flex Saver' product, which requires a smartphone app to manage transactions, and that this product was unsuitable for your circumstances. You also raised concerns about failed app transactions and the fees you were charged as a result.

**Our findings and what we are doing**

Having reviewed your complaint, we uphold it. We are sorry that your account was migrated to a product that you were unable to use. As our adviser confirmed to you by telephone, we will:

- Move your account back to your previous account type; and
- Waive the fees you were charged for the failed app transactions.

If you have not already seen these changes reflected in your account, please contact us using the details below and we will ensure they are applied without delay.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If you are not satisfied with our response — including in relation to the decision to migrate your account in the first place, which we understand remains a concern for you — you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You have six months from the date of this letter to do so.

The Financial Ombudsman Service can be contacted at:
- **Website:** www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- **Telephone:** 0800 023 4567

I have enclosed their explanatory leaflet, which sets out further information about how they can help and the process for referring a complaint.

Thank you for bringing this to our attention. We take complaints of this kind seriously and will be reviewing what happened to make sure it does not occur again.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the bank to issue a written acknowledgement within five business days and a final response that informs the customer of their FOS referral right; the customer received neither, indicating a procedural breach.
The final response must state the outcome of the complaint and inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, including the six-month time limit and FOS contact details.

The six-month time limit for the customer to refer their complaint to the FOS runs from the date the bank sends its final response; since no written final response was issued, this clock has not yet started.

The FCA indicates that the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that constitutes a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication, and that response must tell the complainant about the time limit.

The next-business-day informal resolution rule applies only where the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day; resolution here occurred on Wednesday following a Monday receipt, likely outside this window.
