# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# CASS — CASS 13.5 Client bank account acknowledgement letters

## CASS 13.5.12 (Guidance)

Under CASS 13.5.10R, a firm should obtain a replacement client bank account acknowledgement letter whenever:

(1) there has been a change in any of the parties’ names or addresses or a change in any of the details of the relevant account(s) as set out in the letter; or

(2) it becomes aware of an error or misspelling in the letter.

## CASS 13.5.13 (Rules)

If a firm’s client bank account is transferred to another approved bank, the firm must promptly draw up a new client bank account acknowledgement letter under CASS 13.5.2R and ensure that the new client bank account acknowledgement letter is duly countersigned and returned by the relevant approved bank within 20 business days of the firm sending it to that person.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 7. Procedures

### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints

A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination.

All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.

### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement

Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must:

1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected.
2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer.
3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.

### 7.3 Investigation Process

The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include:

1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems.
2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made.
3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 1. Document Control

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Policy Title** | Customer Complaints Handling Procedure |
| **Version** | 2.1 |
| **Effective Date** | 1 February 2025 |
| **Next Review Date** | 31 January 2026 |
| **Document Owner** | Priya Sharma, Head of Customer Operations |
| **Approving Body** | Compliance Oversight Committee |

---

## 2. Purpose

This policy establishes Crest Bank's framework for receiving, investigating, and resolving customer complaints in a manner that is fair, consistent, and compliant with FCA requirements. It ensures that customers who are dissatisfied with Crest Bank's products or services receive a timely and transparent response, and that complaint data is used systematically to drive service improvement. The policy applies to all regulated and unregulated complaints received across Crest Bank's digital channels.

---

## 3. Scope

This policy applies to:

- **Staff:** All customer-facing staff in Customer Operations, as well as staff in Product, Risk, Compliance, and Technology who may be involved in complaint investigation or root cause analysis.
- **Products:** Personal current accounts, instant-access and fixed-term savings accounts, personal loans, and SME business current accounts.
- **Customer Segments:** All retail customers (approximately 850,000) and SME customers (approximately 12,000).
- **Channels:** In-app messaging, email, web contact form, and any written correspondence received at Crest Bank's registered address.
- **Systems:** Crest Bank's complaint management system (CMS), core banking platform, and customer identity and verification (CIV) system.

---

# BCOBS — BCOBS 4.1 Enabling banking customers to make informed decisions

banking service until a specified future date or the end of

a fixed period, a firm should provide notice of the expiry of the application of that

rate of interest to the banking customer on

paper or in another durable medium within a reasonable period before that

rate of interest ceases to apply.

(5A) Paragraph (5) does not apply where the balance of the account is less than £100 at the time when the firm would otherwise provide the notice.

(6) [deleted]

(6A) In relation to a fixed-term savings account, a firm should provide notice of the expiry of the fixed term to the banking customer on paper or in another durable medium in good time before the end of the fixed term. This notice should explain, in easily understandable language and in a clear and comprehensible form:

(a) the consequences of the expiry of the fixed term, including whether the firm proposes to transfer the balance of the account to another fixed-term savings account if the banking customer does not provide further instructions to the firm while the customer has an opportunity to do so; and

(b) the options available to the banking customer for dealing with the balance in the fixed term savings account, including when and how these options may be exercised.

(6B) Where a notice under (3)(c), (5) or (6A) is provided by the firm more than 14 days before the change to which the notice relates takes effect, a firm should also provide a reminder to the banking customer within a period beginning 14 days before the relevant change takes effect and ending on the day before it does so. The firm may choose the medium in which the reminder is provided. In doing so, the firm should take account of any preferences expressed by the banking customer about the medium of communication between the firm and the banking customer, for example, if the banking customer has indicated a preference to receive information by mobile telephone te

# DISP — DISP 1 Annex 3 Appropriate wording for inclusion in a final response or written acceptance

The Ombudsman might not be able to consider your complaint if:• you received a letter warning you that there was a high risk that your mortgage endowment policy would not produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount at maturity; and• you’re complaining more than three years after you received that letter, and• you’re complaining more than six months after the date on which we sent you a further communication notifying you when the three-year period would expire.We think that your complaint was made outside of these time limits but this is a matter for the Ombudsman to decide. If the Ombudsman agrees with us, they will not have our permission to consider your complaint and so will only be able to do so in limited circumstances.”

The respondent consents to waive all applicable time limits

(5)

“You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.

Although there are time limits for referring your complaint to the Ombudsman, we will consent to the Ombudsman considering your complaint even if you refer the complaint outside the time limits.”

Other circumstances not dealt with above

(6)

Where the respondent proposes to waive the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R and appropriate wording for the respondent circumstances is not set out in (1) to (5), the respondent must adapt the appropriate wording as necessary.

claims.claim: The internal procedure permits informal resolution without a formal written response only where the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the 'next business day rule' under DISP 1.5).
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure states: 'Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5).'

claims.claim: The bank was required to issue a written acknowledgement within five business days (per the internal procedure referencing DISP 1.6.2R).
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure states the handler must 'Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within five business days of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R.'

claims.claim: Under DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in the internal procedure), the final response must inform the customer of their right to refer to the FOS.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure states the final response must 'Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R.'

claims.claim: The six-month time limit for FOS referral runs from the date of the final response (DISP 2.8.2R).
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.2R(1) states the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if referred 'more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication.'

claims.claim: Per DISP 2.8.3G, the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that constitutes a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication — and that response must tell the complainant about the six-month limit.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.3G states: 'The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication. The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.'

claims.claim: Since no written final response was issued, the six-month clock has arguably not started running.
claims.reasoning: This follows logically from DISP 2.8.3G which states the six-month time limit is only triggered by a final response that tells the complainant about the time limit. If no such response was issued, the clock has not started.

claims.claim: The final response letter must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure states: 'All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.'

claims.claim: Complex complaints or those involving multiple business areas must be escalated to the Head of Customer Operations.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure states: 'Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations.'

claims.claim: The customer did not receive a written acknowledgement or a written final response, and was not informed of their right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service, which appears to be a procedural breach.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure requires a written acknowledgement within five business days and the final response procedure (chunk 006) requires informing the customer of FOS referral rights. Failure to do either constitutes a procedural breach per these provisions.

claims.claim: The customer was entitled to written confirmation of the complaint outcome.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure requires a final response letter to be issued to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, which supports the claim that the customer was entitled to written confirmation.

claims.claim: The customer has the right to refer their complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure requires the final response to inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS. The 'free of charge' language is also found in DISP 1 Annex 3 (chunk disp1s18-002).

claims.claim: The customer has six months from the date of this letter to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.2R(1) provides that the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint referred more than six months after the date the respondent sent the final response. The internal procedure (chunk 006) also requires stating the six-month time limit.

claims.claim: The Financial Ombudsman Service can be contacted at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk and telephone 0800 023 4567.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure states: 'All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567).'

claims.claim: An explanatory leaflet from the Financial Ombudsman Service is enclosed with the letter.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure mentions including FOS contact details and the six-month time limit but does not explicitly mention enclosing an explanatory leaflet. However, DISP rules generally require this — the retrieved provisions do not explicitly state this requirement.

claims.claim: The complaint was received on Monday morning and the adviser called back on Wednesday afternoon, which is more than one business day after receipt and therefore outside the next-business-day window.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure defines the next business day rule as resolution 'by close of business on the next business day.' The factual claim about Monday/Wednesday timing is from the complaint facts (not a retrieved provision), but the regulatory interpretation that Wednesday exceeds the next-business-day window is supported by the procedure's definition.

claims.claim: The final response must be issued within eight weeks of the date the complaint was received.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure states the final response must 'Be issued within eight weeks of the date the complaint was received.'

claims.reasoning: This is a meta-claim about the absence of relevant provisions in the retrieved set. Reviewing all retrieved provisions confirms none address product suitability for vulnerable customers or PRIN 12/Consumer Duty in sufficient detail.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I opened a cash deposit ISA with your bank in March after seeing your online promotion advertising a 4.5% fixed rate for 12 months. The promotion clearly stated '4.5% AER fixed for 12 months' with no mention of any conditions or restrictions. When I transferred £20,000 from my previous ISA provider, I was placed on a 3.8% variable rate instead. I queried this immediately and was told the 4.5% rate was only available for new money, not ISA transfers — but this was never stated in the promotion I responded to. I wrote a formal complaint letter six weeks ago and received an acknowledgement email, but it didn't tell me anything about timescales for a response or what would happen if I remained unhappy. I've heard nothing since. I'm 78 years old and this ISA represents a significant portion of my retirement savings. The difference in interest rate is costing me approximately £140 over the year, and I'm worried about my finances as I rely on interest income to supplement my state pension.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several serious concerns that require careful handling.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Complaint handling timescales and FOS referral rights (DISP 1.6.2R via internal policy section 7.5):** The complaint was received six weeks ago. The acknowledgement email did not inform the customer of timescales or FOS referral rights. Under your internal procedure (which references DISP 1.6.2R), the final response must be issued within eight weeks of receipt. You are now approaching that deadline. Critically, the acknowledgement should have included information about the process — the failure to do so is itself a procedural shortcoming you should acknowledge. You have approximately two weeks remaining to issue a final response before the eight-week deadline is breached. If you cannot issue a final response in time, you must issue a written response at eight weeks explaining the delay and confirming FOS referral rights.

2. **Financial promotion concern (BCOBS 2.4.2G / COBS 4.7 context):** The retrieved provisions include BCOBS 2.4.2G, which notes that for cash deposit ISAs, COBS 4.7.1R (Direct offer financial promotions) also applies. The customer's complaint is that the online promotion stated '4.5% AER fixed for 12 months' with no mention that this rate was unavailable for ISA transfers — only new money. If accurate, this raises a potential concern about whether the financial promotion was clear, fair and not misleading. However, the specific provisions governing the content standards for this type of promotion (e.g. COBS 4.7.1R itself) are not fully reproduced in the retrieved context, so I cannot ground a definitive regulatory finding on the promotion's compliance. This must be reviewed by a human with access to the full relevant provisions.

3. **Vulnerability:** The customer is 78 years old, relies on interest income to supplement their state pension, and has £20,000 of retirement savings affected. This is a clear indicator of potential financial vulnerability. This complaint must be handled with heightened care and sensitivity.

4. **Financial redress:** The customer estimates a loss of approximately £140 in interest over the year. This is a financial redress matter. Under your internal procedure, any complaint involving financial redress must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

5. **Recommended action:** (a) Escalate immediately to the Head of Customer Operations given the complexity, vulnerability indicators, and financial redress element. (b) Investigate the original promotion urgently — obtain a copy of the online promotion as seen by the customer and assess whether the restriction to 'new money only' was disclosed. (c) Issue a holding response now (within the eight-week window) acknowledging the delay in substantive communication, apologising for the failure to provide timescale information in the acknowledgement, and confirming FOS referral rights. (d) Issue a full final response before the eight-week deadline if at all possible, or a delay letter at eight weeks if not. (e) The customer draft below is a holding/interim letter only — it does not uphold or reject the complaint, as that determination requires the investigation above. Flag this clearly before sending.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft:** (i) Confirm the exact date the formal complaint letter was received (the eight-week clock runs from that date). (ii) Confirm whether a copy of the original promotion can be retrieved. (iii) Confirm the Head of Customer Operations is aware and has approved despatch of even this interim letter given the vulnerability and redress elements. The customer draft below does NOT include FOS prescribed referral wording as a final response has not yet been issued — that wording must appear in the final response or the eight-week delay letter.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for your patience, and I am sorry that we have not been in better contact with you since you submitted your formal complaint six weeks ago.

I want to acknowledge straightaway that when we confirmed receipt of your complaint, we should have told you clearly what to expect — including the timescale within which we aim to respond and what options are available to you if you remain unhappy. We did not do that, and I apologise for the concern this has caused you.

Your complaint concerns the interest rate applied to your cash deposit ISA following your transfer of £20,000 from a previous ISA provider. You have told us that the online promotion you responded to advertised a 4.5% AER fixed rate for 12 months, with no mention that this rate was restricted to new money and not available for ISA transfers, and that you were placed on a 3.8% variable rate instead. We take this concern seriously.

We are now investigating your complaint fully. This includes reviewing the promotion you saw and the circumstances in which your ISA was opened. We understand that this ISA represents an important part of your retirement finances and that the difference in interest rate has a real impact on your income. We are treating your complaint with the priority it deserves.

We aim to send you our full response as soon as possible, and in any event within eight weeks of the date we received your complaint. If for any reason we are unable to do so within that timeframe, we will write to you to explain why and to set out your options at that point, including your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

If you have any questions in the meantime, or if there is anything further you would like to tell us about your complaint, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the firm to issue a final response within eight weeks of receiving the complaint and to notify the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS; the internal procedure at section 7.4 and 7.5 implements this rule and sets out the specific requirements for final response letters and eight-week delay letters.

Indicates that for a financial promotion relating to a cash deposit ISA, COBS 4.7.1R (Direct offer financial promotions) also applies, making that rule potentially relevant to the customer's complaint about the content of the online promotion.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# CONC — CONC 3.5 Financial promotions about credit agreements not secured on land

## CONC 3.5.3 (Rules)

(1) Where a financial promotion indicates a rate of interest or an amount relating to the cost of credit whether expressed as a sum of money or a proportion of a specified amount, the financial promotion must also:

(a) include a representative example in accordance with CONC 3.5.5 R, and

(b) specify a postal address at which the person making the financial promotion may be contacted.

[Note: regulation 4(1) of CCAR 2010]

(2) Paragraph (1)(a) does not apply where the financial promotion:

(a) falls within CONC 3.5.7 R; and

(b) does not indicate a rate of interest or an amount relating to the cost of credit other than the representative APR.

[Note: regulation 4(2) of CCAR 2010]

(2A) Paragraph (1)(a) also does not apply where the financial promotion relates only to credit agreements in respect of which the APR is 0%.

(3) Paragraph (1)(b) does not apply to financial promotions:

(a) communicated by means of television or radio broadcast; or

(b) in any form on the premises of a dealer or lender, other than financial promotions in writing which customers are intended to take away; or

(c) which include the name and address of a dealer; or

(d) which include the name and postal address of a credit broker.

[Note: regulation 4(1)b of CCAR 2010]

metadata.section_name: CONC 3.5 Financial promotions about credit agreements not secured on land

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

# COBS — COBS 4.5 Communicating with retail clients (non-MiFID provisions)

## COBS 4.5.11 (Guidance)

Information about relevant risks (COBS 4.5.2R) that a firm should give a retail client in relation to a lifetime ISA may include:

(1) an explanation of:

(a) a retail client’s eligibility to subscribe to a lifetime ISA (including annual subscription limits) and to claim the lifetime ISA government bonus;

(b) the lifetime ISA government withdrawal charge and the circumstances in which it might arise; and

(c) the process by which a retail client can transfer a lifetime ISA; and

(2) warnings that, if the retail client:

(a) incurs a lifetime ISA government withdrawal charge, the retail client may get back less than they paid in to a lifetime ISA;

(b) saves in a lifetime ISA instead of enrolling in, or contributing to a qualifying scheme, occupational pension scheme, or personal pension scheme:

(i) the retail client may lose the benefit of contributions by an employer (if any) to that scheme; and

(ii) the retail client’s current and future entitlement to means tested benefits (if any) may be affected.

## COBS 4.5.11A (Rules)

The rules in COBS 4.5.12R to COBS 4.5.15R apply to:

(1) a financial promotion relating to an authorised fund;

(2) a communication which contains a statement referring to or concerning the past performance of an authorised fund; and

(3) any other communication about an authorised fund that refers in any way to the aims of the fund or describes the benefits or risks of investing in it.

metadata.section_name: COBS 4.5 Communicating with retail clients (non-MiFID provisions)

# COBS — COBS 4.10 Approving and confirming compliance of financial promotions

## COBS 4.10.2 (Rules)

(1) Before a firm approves a financial promotion for communication by an unauthorised person, it must confirm that the financial promotion complies with the financial promotion rules.

(1A) After a firm has complied with (1), and for as long as the financial promotion is communicated, the firm must take reasonable steps to monitor the continuing compliance of that financial promotion with the financial promotion rules.

[Note: for the FCA’s guidance on ‘Ongoing monitoring’ see: https://www.fca.org.uk/firms/financial-promotions-and-adverts/approving-financial-promotions]

(1B) A firm that has approved a financial promotion issued, and for communication by, an unauthorised person must require from that person, a written quarterly attestation that there has been no material change:

(a) to the financial promotion; or

(b) in circumstances which might affect the continuing compliance of the financial promotion with the financial promotion rules.

(1C) For the purpose of (1B), a firm must:

(a) require the first attestation no less than 3 months after it approves the financial promotion; and

(b) thereafter, require attestations at least once every 3 months for as long as the financial promotion is communicated.

(2) If, at any time after a firm has complied with (1), a firm becomes aware that a financial promotion no longer complies with the financial promotion rules, it must withdraw its approval and notify any person that it knows to be relying on its approval as soon as reasonably practicable.

(3) When approving a financial promotion, the firm must confirm compliance with the financial promotion rules that would have applied if the financial promotion had been communicated by a firm other than in relation to MiFID or equivalent third country business.

metadata.section_name: COBS 4.10 Approving and confirming compliance of financial promotions

## COBS 4.12A.15 (Rules)

(1) Unless permitted by COBS 4.12A.17R and subject to (2), (3) and (4), a firm must not:

(a) communicate a direct offer financial promotion relating to a restricted mass market investment to a retail client unless the conditions in COBS 4.12A.18R (cooling off period), COBS 4.12A.20R (personalised risk warning), COBS 4.12A.21R (categorisation) and COBS 4.12A.28R (appropriateness) are satisfied; or

(b) approve a direct offer financial promotion relating to a restricted mass market investment for communication to a retail client unless the firm is satisfied that the conditions in COBS 4.12A.18R (cooling off period), COBS 4.12A.20R (personalised risk warning), COBS 4.12A.21R (categorisation) and COBS 4.12A.28R (appropriateness) will be satisfied in relation to each communication of the direct offer financial promotion.

(2) The conditions in COBS 4.12A.18R (cooling off period) and COBS 4.12A.20R (personalised risk warning) do not need to be satisfied if the retail client has previously received a direct offer financial promotion relating to a restricted mass market investment from the same person as would otherwise need to satisfy them.

(3) The condition in COBS 4.12A.28R (appropriateness) does not need to be satisfied if the specific type of restricted mass market investment to which the direct offer financial promotion relates has previously been assessed as appropriate for the retail client by the same person as would otherwise need to undertake the assessment.

# COBS — COBS 4.7 Direct offer financial promotions

## COBS 4.7.5A (Guidance)

COBS 4.13.2 R (Marketing communications relating to UCITS schemes) and COBS 4.13.3 R (Marketing communications relating to feeder UCITS) contain additional disclosure requirements for firms in relation to marketing communications that concern particular investment strategies of a UCITS scheme

## COBS 4.7.6 (Rules)

(1) A firm must not communicate or approve a direct offer financial promotion:(a) relating to a warrant or derivative;(b) to or for communication to a retail client; and(c) where the firm will not itself be required to comply with the rules on appropriateness (see COBS 10 and 10A);unless the firm has adequate evidence that the condition in (2) is satisfied.(2) The condition is that the person who will arrange or deal in relation to the derivative or warrant will comply with the rules on appropriateness or equivalent requirements for any application or order that the person is aware, or ought reasonably to be aware, is in response to the direct offer financial promotion.(3) Paragraph (1) does not apply if: (a) the firm has provided the retail client with a personal recommendation or ready-made suggestion in relation to the warrant or derivative to be promoted; or(b) the retail client has confirmed before the promotion is made that they have received a personal recommendation or ready-made suggestion from another firm in relation to the warrant or derivative to be promoted.

## COBS 4.7.6A (Guidance)

Firms are reminded of their obligations in relation to the marketing, distribution and sale of restricted speculative investments in COBS 22.5.

## COBS 4.7.6B (Guidance)

Firms are reminded of the prohibitions in relation to the marketing, distribution and sale of cryptoasset derivatives and non-UK RIE cryptoasset exchange traded notes in COBS 22.6.

metadata.section_name: COBS 4.7 Direct offer financial promotions

verdicts.evidence: Your complaint concerns the interest rate applied to your cash deposit ISA following your transfer of £20,000 from a previous ISA provider. You have told us that the online promotion you responded to advertised a 4.5% AER fixed rate for 12 months, with no mention that this rate was restricted to new money and not available for ISA transfers, and that you were placed on a 3.8% variable rate instead.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft opens by acknowledging the complaint and accurately reflects the specific subject matter — the misleading promotion, the ISA transfer, and the rate discrepancy.

The customer draft does not state any decision (uphold, partially uphold, or reject). It explicitly states the complaint is still being investigated. The handler answer also confirms this is an interim/holding letter, not a final response with a decision. Since the response stage is marked as 'final', a decision is required but not provided.

verdicts.evidence: we will write to you to explain why and to set out your options at that point, including your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service.
verdicts.reasoning: The FOS is mentioned and the right to refer is noted, but it is framed as a future contingency ('if for any reason we are unable to [respond within eight weeks]') rather than as a present right. For a final response, the complainant should be told they can now refer the complaint. Additionally, no FOS contact details, leaflet, or website are provided. This is only a partial mention.

The customer draft does not state that the complainant has six months from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FOS. No six-month deadline is mentioned anywhere in the customer-facing text.

verdicts.evidence: We aim to send you our full response as soon as possible, and in any event within eight weeks of the date we received your complaint. If for any reason we are unable to do so within that timeframe, we will write to you to explain why and to set out your options at that point, including your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service.
verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft clearly communicates the eight-week timescale for a final response and states that if the complaint is not resolved within that timeframe, the complainant may refer to the FOS. This satisfies the holding response requirement for the eight-week timeline.

verdicts.evidence: We understand that this ISA represents an important part of your retirement finances and that the difference in interest rate has a real impact on your income. We are treating your complaint with the priority it deserves.
verdicts.reasoning: The response engages with the substance of the complaint, uses clear and non-dismissive language, acknowledges the customer's specific circumstances, apologises for the earlier procedural failing, and does not minimise the concerns raised.

The customer draft does not address whether redress or remedial action is appropriate. While the handler answer discusses the £140 financial loss and flags it as a redress matter requiring escalation, no position on redress is communicated to the customer — neither an offer nor an explanation that the matter is still being considered. The complaint plainly raises financial loss but the customer-facing text is silent on redress.

verdicts.evidence: Handler: 'The customer is 78 years old, relies on interest income to supplement their state pension, and has £20,000 of retirement savings affected. This is a clear indicator of potential financial vulnerability. This complaint must be handled with heightened care and sensitivity.' Customer draft: 'We understand that this ISA represents an important part of your retirement finances and that the difference in interest rate has a real impact on your income. We are treating your complaint with the priority it deserves.'
verdicts.reasoning: The handler answer explicitly identifies the vulnerability indicators (age, reliance on interest income, retirement savings) and recommends heightened care. structure-titan.scenario_text: I used a price comparison website called QuickLoans Compare to find a short-term loan of £300 for 30 days. The website showed me results but the cheapest option wasn't listed first — instead, a more expensive lender called FastCash was displayed at the top with a prominent 'Recommended' badge. I later discovered FastCash pays the highest commission to QuickLoans Compare. I clicked through and took out the FastCash loan, paying £90 in interest over 30 days, when I've since found that another lender on the same site would have charged me only £60 for the identical loan amount and duration. I complained to QuickLoans Compare three weeks ago about being misled by their biased ranking. They wrote back saying they weren't responsible for my lending decision and forwarded my complaint to FastCash, the lender, without explaining why or giving me FastCash's contact details. I've now heard nothing from either party. I want the £30 difference refunded and a proper explanation of why my complaint was passed on without my knowledge of where it went.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several distinct regulatory issues under CONC 2.5A, all of which appear well-supported by the retrieved provisions. Here is the position on each:

**1. Biased ranking / prominence of results (core complaint)**
CONC 2.5A.6R is directly on point. It is a binding rule requiring that neither the ranking nor the prominence of display of search results for HCSTC products is based (wholly or partly) on the firm's commercial interests or its commercial relationship with any person. If FastCash paid the highest commission and was ranked first with a 'Recommended' badge on that basis, QuickLoans Compare would be in prima facie breach of CONC 2.5A.6R. CONC 2.5A.2R also applies: where a firm lists HCSTC products to enable comparison, it must not base ranking or prominence on its commercial interests or relationships.

**2. Ranking by total amount payable**
CONC 2.5A.5R(2) requires search results to be ranked in order of total amount payable, with the lowest first. The customer's account — that a cheaper loan was available on the same site but not ranked first — is consistent with a breach of this rule.

**3. Financial promotions within results**
The 'Recommended' badge displayed on FastCash's result may constitute a financial promotion displayed in or between search results, which CONC 2.5A.8R prohibits. CONC 2.5A.9R also requires search results to be clearly distinguishable from any other financial promotion. These are binding rules.

**4. Complaint handling by QuickLoans Compare**
The retrieved provisions do not include DISP (the complaints-handling sourcebook), so I cannot cite specific complaint-handling time limits or procedural obligations from the retrieved context. This is a gap. You should verify the DISP position separately — in particular whether QuickLoans Compare has issued a final response within the required period, and whether the customer's right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) has been triggered. Flag `insufficient_context` is set to true for this reason.

**5. Redress (the £30 difference)**
The retrieved provisions establish the conduct rules but do not set out a redress or compensation framework. I cannot confirm from the retrieved context that the customer is entitled to the £30 difference as a matter of regulatory obligation. This must be assessed separately — likely under DISP and general principles — and must not be promised in the customer letter. Human review is required before any commitment on redress.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft:**
- Confirm that QuickLoans Compare is the firm you (the bank/handler) are responding on behalf of, or clarify the handler's role — the complaint appears to be directed at QuickLoans Compare, not a bank. If you are handling this on behalf of QuickLoans Compare, the draft below is appropriate. If you are a third party, the draft needs adjustment.
- Confirm whether a final response has been issued by QuickLoans Compare within the DISP time limit, as this affects whether FOS referral rights have been triggered and what prescribed wording applies.
- Do not commit to the £30 refund in the customer letter — this requires a separate redress assessment and human sign-off.
- The complaint-handling procedural failures (forwarding without explanation, no contact details provided) are noted in the draft but cannot be grounded in the retrieved provisions; they are included as factual acknowledgements only.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for your complaint dated [date], and I am sorry for the experience you have described and for the delay in receiving a proper response.

Your complaint raises two main concerns, and I want to address each in turn.

**How your search results were displayed**

When a price comparison website displays high-cost short-term credit products, it is required by FCA rules to rank results in order of the total amount you would repay — with the cheapest option listed first. The ranking and prominence of results must not be influenced, in whole or in part, by the website's commercial interests or by any commercial relationship it has with a lender, including commission arrangements.

You have told us that a more expensive lender appeared at the top of your results with a 'Recommended' badge, and that you have since discovered that lender pays the highest commission to this website. You have also told us that a cheaper loan for the same amount and duration was available on the same site but was not ranked first. We take these concerns seriously. We are investigating whether the way your results were displayed complied with the applicable rules, and we will write to you with our findings.

**How your complaint was handled**

You have told us that your complaint was forwarded to FastCash without explanation and without being given FastCash's contact details, and that you have since heard nothing from either party. We acknowledge that this was not an adequate response to your complaint, and we apologise for that. We are now treating your complaint as open and are investigating it directly.

**What happens next**

We will complete our investigation and write to you with a full response as soon as possible. If you remain dissatisfied with our response once you receive it, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We will provide you with further details about how to do this when we send our final response.

Thank you for bringing this to our attention.

Yours sincerely,
[Name]
[Title]
[Firm name]

cited_provisions.relevance: Directly prohibits ranking or prominence of HCSTC search results being based on the firm's commercial interests or commercial relationships, which is the core of the customer's complaint about commission-driven ranking.
Requires that where a firm lists HCSTC products to enable comparison, neither ranking nor prominence of display may be based on the firm's commercial interests or relationships.
Requires search results to be ranked in order of total amount payable with the lowest first, which the customer's account suggests was not done.

Prohibits displaying a financial promotion other than a search result in or between search results — relevant to whether the 'Recommended' badge on FastCash's listing was a prohibited promotion within results.
Requires search results to be clearly distinguishable from any other financial promotion, relevant to the 'Recommended' badge potentially blurring that distinction.

Indicates that commission payments to the firm should not affect the ranking or prominence of search results, supporting the concern that FastCash's commission arrangement influenced its top placement.

# CONC — CONC 2.5A Conduct of business: high-cost short-term credit (HCSTC) products on price comparison websites

## CONC 2.5A.7 (Guidance)

(1) The information displayed on the price comparison website (for example, information concerning a loan, the results of a search or claims about the market coverage of the website) will need to comply with the financial promotion rules in CONC 3. In particular, it will need to comply with the requirement for a communication or a financial promotion to be clear, fair and not misleading. The results of a search also need to comply with the detailed rules in CONC 3.5. In particular, the results will require a representative example. The relevant items of the representative example must be representative of what the firm reasonably expects, at the date on which the financial promotion is made, to be representative of credit agreements to which the representative APR applies and which are expected to be entered into as a result of the promotion.

(2) The fact that a lender or credit broker pays a commission to the firm or pays for advertising or other marketing on the price comparison website (and the amount of any such commission or payment) should not affect the ranking or prominence of display of the results of a search. Such payment should also not affect whether information about a loan from a lender or credit broker whose loans the firm arranges to compare or claims to compare appears in the results of a search.

(3) CONC 2.5A.6R does not require the firm to compare loans from a lender or credit broker where it has not arranged to do so with that lender or credit broker nor where it does not claim to compare loans from that lender or credit broker.

(4) The firm should ensure that any information concerning a loan or any result of a search which relates to another firm’s credit broking service states prominently that:

(a) the firm referred to is a credit broker and is not a lender; or

(b) if the firm referred to is both a

# CONC — CONC 2.5A Conduct of business: high-cost short-term credit (HCSTC) products on price comparison websites

## CONC 2.5A.1 (Rules)

This section applies to a firm which owns or operates a website that displays any terms on which high-cost short-term credit products are available from different lenders (referred to in this section as a “price comparison website”) and in relation to which it:

(1) holds itself out as providing a price comparison service or a price service; or

(2) describes itself in any way as a price comparison website or a price website; or

(3) gives the impression in any way that the website is a price comparison website or a price website.

## CONC 2.5A.2 (Rules)

Where a firm lists information on the website it owns or operates concerning high-cost short-term credit products in order to enable a customer to compare any terms of those products, it must display the information in a way that neither the ranking of products nor the prominence of display of products is based (wholly or partly) on the firm’s commercial interests or its commercial relationship with any person.

## CONC 2.5A.3 (Rules)

A firm must ensure that the price comparison website enables:

(1) a customer to enter the value and duration of the customer’s desired loan when specifying the criteria for a search; and

(2) a search to be made of the high-cost short-term credit products covered by the website and the results of the search to be displayed on the basis of only that information.

metadata.section_name: CONC 2.5A Conduct of business: high-cost short-term credit (HCSTC) products on price comparison websites

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 2.5A Conduct of business: high-cost short-term credit (HCSTC) products on price comparison websites

## CONC 2.5A.4 (Guidance)

(1) A firm’s obligations under CONC 2.5A.3R(1) and (2) may be satisfied by enabling a customer to select from a reasonable range of options of values of loan or of durations of loan, when specifying the criteria for a search.

(2) What is a reasonable range of options for a search will depend, for example, on the breadth of value of loans or on the duration of loans that appear on the price comparison website. For example, it may be reasonable depending on the circumstances to allow a choice of bands of values or durations.

## CONC 2.5A.5 (Rules)

In response to a request to perform a search for a high-cost-short-term credit product, the firm must ensure that the price comparison website:

(1) displays specific information relating to each loan covered by the website which corresponds to the search criteria entered by the customer as a separate result;

(2) ranks those results in order of total amount payable in accordance with CONC 3.5.5R(2), with the loan with the lowest total amount payable first and the highest last; and

(3) where two or more search results have the same total amount payable in accordance with (2), ranks the results according to another criterion permissible under CONC 2.5A.

## CONC 2.5A.6 (Rules)

A firm must ensure that neither:

(1) the ranking of the results of a search for a high-cost-short-term credit product, nor

(2) the prominence of the display of the results of such a search, nor

(3) whether a loan from a lender or credit broker, whose loans the firm arranges to compare or claims to compare, is displayed in the results of such a search,

is based (wholly or partly) on the firm’s commercial interests or its commercial relationship with any person.

metadata.section_name: CONC 2.5A Conduct of business: high-cost short-term credit (HCSTC) products on price comparison websites

# CONC — CONC 2.5A Conduct of business: high-cost short-term credit (HCSTC) products on price comparison websites

## CONC 2.5A.8 (Rules)

A firm must not display a financial promotion, other than the result of a search, in or between the results of a search.

## CONC 2.5A.9 (Rules)

A firm must ensure that the results of a search are clearly distinguishable from any other financial promotion.

## CONC 2.5A.10 (Guidance)

A result of a search may include a hyperlink to the website of the lender or credit broker in question.

## CONC 2.5A.11 (Rules)

A firm must list in one place on the price comparison website the brand names of lenders whose high-cost short-term credit products are displayed on the website.

# CONC — CONC 3.9 Financial promotions and communications: debt counsellors and debt adjusters

## CONC 3.9.6 (Guidance)

An example of unfairly directing a customer to a premium rate telephone number may be to direct a person wishing to complain to such a number.

## CONC 3.9.6A (Guidance)

Firms should note the effect of the call charges rule in GEN 7.

## CONC 3.9.7 (Rules)

A firm must not:

(1) unless it is a not-for-profit debt advice body or a person who will provide such services, operate a look alike website designed to attract customers seeking free, charitable, not-for-profit or governmental or local governmental debt advice; or [Note: paragraph 3.20a of DMG]

(2) seek to use internet search tools or search engines so as to mislead a customer into visiting its website when the customer is seeking free, charitable, not-for-profit or governmental or local governmental debt advice.[Note: paragraph 3.20b of DMG]

# CONC — CONC 3.4 Risk warning for high-cost short-term credit

## CONC 3.4.1 (Rules)

(1) A firm must not communicate or approve for communication a financial promotion in relation to high-cost short-term credit, unless it contains the following risk warning: “Warning: Late repayment can cause you serious money problems. For help, go to moneyhelper.org.uk ”.

(2) [deleted]

(3) Instead of the website address in paragraph (1), a firm may include the MoneyHelper logo registered UK trade mark number UK00003476779 .

(4) The risk warning must be included in a financial promotion in a prominent way.

## CONC 3.4.2 (Guidance)

MoneyHelper has granted a licence to use the logo referred to in CONC 3.4.1R (3) for the purposes of that rule. The terms of the licence are available from MoneyHelper .

# CONC — CONC 3.3 The clear fair and not misleading rule and general requirements

## CONC 3.3.10 (Guidance)

Examples of practices that are likely to contravene the clear, fair and not misleading rule in CONC 3.3.1 R include:

(1) stating or implying that a firm is a lender (where this is not the case);[Note: paragraph 3.7e (box) of CBG]

(2) misleading a customer as to the availability of a particular credit product; [Note: paragraph 3.9p of CBG

(3) concealing or misrepresenting the identity or name of the firm; [Note: paragraph 3.7g (box) of CBG

(4) using false testimonials, endorsements or case studies;[Note: paragraph 3.18s of DMG]

(5) using false or unsubstantiated claims as to the firm's size or experience or pre-eminence; [Note: paragraph 3.18t of DMG]

(6) in relation to debt solutions, claiming or implying that a customer will be free of debt in a specified period of time or making statements emphasising a debt-free life or that a debt solution is a stress free or immediate solution;[Note: paragraphs 3.18u and 3.18v of DMG]

(7) providing online tools, which recommend a particular debt solution as suitable for a customer, such as, budget calculators or advice websites:

(a) which do not carry out a sufficiently full assessment of a customer's financial position; or

(b) which fail to provide clear warnings to a customer that financial data entered into a tool has to be accurate;

[Note: paragraph 3.20c of DMG]

# CONC — CONC 3.3 The clear fair and not misleading rule and general requirements

## CONC 3.3.11 (Guidance)

Misleading a customer as to the availability of a particular credit product is likely to include stating or implying that the firm will introduce the customer to a provider of a standard personal loan based on repayment by instalments or of an overdraft facility on a current account (for example, a bank or building society) or of a credit card, but instead introducing the customer to a provider of high-cost short-term credit.

[Note: paragraph 3.9p (box) of CBG]

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with the final response letter I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about being mis-sold a packaged bank account. In your letter, you stated that you did not uphold my complaint because you believe I was given adequate information about the account benefits at the time of sale. However, I was never told about the eligibility criteria for the travel insurance included in the package — I am 78 years old and have a pre-existing heart condition, which means I have never been able to claim on the policy despite paying £15 per month for it since 2016. Your response did not address this point at all, and I do not believe you investigated my complaint properly. You also failed to mention anything about my right to take this further if I remain unhappy. I want to know what my options are now, and I believe I am owed a full refund of all the fees I have paid.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint concerns a packaged bank account mis-sale, specifically the failure to disclose eligibility criteria for the included travel insurance (age limit and pre-existing medical condition exclusion). The customer is 78 with a pre-existing heart condition and has been paying £15/month since 2016 without being able to claim. There are several important regulatory and procedural points to address:

1. **Failure to address FOS referral rights in the final response**: The customer explicitly states the 15 November final response did not mention their right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service. Under DISP 1.6.2R, the final response must inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, enclose the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, and provide the FOS website address. This is a binding requirement. The existing final response appears non-compliant on this point and must be remedied.

2. **Substance of the complaint — eligibility/suitability for packaged bank account insurance**: Under ICOBS 5.3.2AR, when advising on a policy included in a packaged bank account, the firm must take reasonable steps to establish whether the policy is suitable for the customer's demands and needs, including relevant exclusions, excesses, limitations and conditions, and must inform the customer of any demands and needs that are not met. Under ICOBS 5.1.3CR, the firm must provide an annual eligibility statement setting out qualifying requirements and, where a customer has reached an age limit on claiming benefits under a travel insurance policy, must state this clearly and prominently. The customer's allegation that they were never told about eligibility criteria (age limit and pre-existing condition) is directly relevant to these rules. The original investigation appears not to have addressed this at all.

3. **Inadequate investigation**: The customer alleges the complaint was not properly investigated and that the specific point about eligibility was not addressed. Crest Bank's own procedure (section 7.3) requires a fair and impartial investigation. The final response must state reasons for rejection (DISP 1.6.2R). If the eligibility point was not addressed, the response may not meet this standard.

4. **Potential redress**: The customer is seeking a full refund of fees paid since 2016 (approximately 9 years × £15/month = ~£1,620). Any financial redress decision must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations under Crest Bank's internal procedure (section 7.4 of the complaints handling procedure). Do NOT commit to or quantify redress in the customer letter.

5. **Human review required**: This complaint involves (a) potential financial redress of a significant amount; (b) a vulnerable customer (age 78, pre-existing heart condition — possible vulnerability indicators); (c) a deficient final response that omitted mandatory FOS referral rights; (d) a substantive regulatory question about ICOBS 5 compliance that was not addressed in the original investigation. The Head of Customer Operations must be involved before any new final response is issued.

**Recommended action**: (a) Treat this customer communication as a re-opened complaint or a complaint about the handling of the original complaint. (b) Escalate immediately to the Head of Customer Operations given the financial redress involved and the apparent deficiency in the original final response. (c) Re-investigate the complaint, specifically addressing whether eligibility criteria (age limit and pre-existing condition exclusion) were disclosed at sale and on an annual basis thereafter, and whether the policy was suitable for this customer. (d) Issue a new final response that complies fully with DISP 1.6.2R, including FOS referral rights, the FOS leaflet, and the FOS website address. (e) Consider the customer's age and health condition as potential vulnerability indicators when communicating.

**Assumption to confirm**: The draft below treats this letter as triggering a new investigation and a new final response process. If a new final response is issued, the eight-week clock will run from the date this new complaint/re-opened complaint was received. Please confirm the date this letter was received and whether it is being treated as a new complaint or a re-opening of the original. Also confirm whether the original complaint was received more than eight weeks before 15 November (i.e. whether the original final response was issued within the eight-week deadline).

**Note on the customer draft**: The draft below acknowledges the complaint, confirms re-investigation, and informs the customer of their FOS referral right (which was omitted from the original response). It does not commit to any specific remedy or redress, as that requires investigation and Head of Customer Operations sign-off. The FOS contact details are included as required by DISP 1.6.2R and Crest Bank's procedure.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry that our response dated 15 November did not meet the standard you should expect, and I want to address your concerns directly.

You have told us that when you took out your packaged bank account, you were not informed about the eligibility requirements for the travel insurance included in the package — in particular, that your age and a pre-existing heart condition would affect your ability to claim. You have also told us that this point was not addressed in our previous response. I am sorry that this was the case.

We take these concerns seriously. We are re-opening your complaint and will conduct a thorough investigation into whether you were given adequate information about the eligibility criteria and any exclusions that applied to the travel insurance at the time of sale, and whether we provided you with the required annual information about those criteria in the years since. We will write to you again with our findings.

We aim to provide you with a full response as soon as possible and, in any event, within eight weeks of the date we received this letter. If we are not able to do so within that time, we will write to explain why and to confirm your options.

We should also have told you in our previous letter that, if you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We apologise for the omission. The Financial Ombudsman Service can be contacted at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567. You have six months from the date of our final response to refer your complaint to them. We are enclosing a copy of their explanatory leaflet, which sets out further details.

We understand this matter has caused you frustration, and we are committed to investigating it properly and responding to you fully. If you have any questions in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact us using the details below.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Contact Details]
[Complaint Reference Number]

Requires the firm, when advising on a packaged bank account policy, to take reasonable steps to establish suitability including relevant exclusions and limitations, and to inform the customer of any demands and needs not met — directly relevant to the allegation that eligibility criteria for the travel insurance were not disclosed.

Requires the firm to provide an annual eligibility statement for packaged bank account policies and, where a customer has reached an age limit on claiming travel insurance benefits, to state this clearly and prominently — relevant to whether the customer was informed of the age-related exclusion on an ongoing basis.

Requires the firm to make and retain a record of the eligibility assessment for a minimum of three years — relevant to whether records exist to demonstrate that an eligibility assessment was conducted at sale.

Requires the firm to make and retain a record of the suitability assessment and recommendation for a minimum of three years — relevant to whether records exist to support the original finding that adequate information was given.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# ICOBS — ICOBS 5.1 General

## ICOBS 5.1.3B (Rules)

A firm must make a record of the eligibility assessment and, if the customer proceeds with the arrangements proposed, retain it for a minimum period of three years from the date on which the assessment was undertaken.

## ICOBS 5.1.3C (Rules)

(1) Throughout the term of a policy included in a packaged bank account, a firm must provide the customer with an eligibility statement, in writing, on an annual basis. This statement must set out any qualifying requirements to claim each of the benefits under the policy and recommend that the customer reviews his circumstances and whether he meets these requirements.

(1A) Where any policy (except for private health or medical insurance, and pet insurance) included in a packaged bank account renews automatically, the statement must include the information the firm is required to provide under ICOBS 6.2.6R on the right to cancel the automatic renewal element of the policy at any time.

(2) Where a customer has reached an age limit on claiming benefits under a travel insurance policy included in a packaged bank account (or will reach an age limit before the next annual statement is due), a firm must state this clearly and prominently in the statement and on an annual basis thereafter.

(3) The statement (provided under ICOBS 5.1.3C R (1)) must not:

(a) include any information other than that provided in accordance with this rule, ICOBS 6.1.7-AG(2), ICOBS 6A.4.5R(1) and ICOBS 6A.4.7G ; or

(b) form part of another document provided to the customer by the firm; or

(c) be included in the same mailing as any other document provided to the customer by the firm.

metadata.section_name: ICOBS 5.1 General

retrieved_chunks.content:
# ICOBS — ICOBS 5.3 Advised sales

## ICOBS 5.3.2A (Rules)

In taking reasonable care to ensure the suitability of advice on a policy included in a packaged bank account, a firm must:

(1) establish the customer's demands and needs by using information readily available to the firm and by obtaining further relevant information from the customer, including details of existing insurance cover; it need not consider alternatives to policies nor customer needs that are not relevant to the type of policy in which the customer is interested;

(2) take reasonable steps to establish whether each policy included in the packaged bank account is suitable for the customer's demands and needs, taking into account its level of cover and cost, and relevant exclusions, excesses, limitations, and conditions;

(3) inform the customer of any demands and needs that are not met; and

(4) explain to the customer its recommendation and the reasons for the recommendation.

## ICOBS 5.3.2B (Rules)

A firm must make a record of the suitability assessment, the recommendation given and the reasons for the recommendation and, if the customer proceeds with the recommendation, retain it for a minimum period of three years from the date on which the recommendation was made.

metadata.section_name: ICOBS 5.3 Advised sales
