# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

# DISP — DISP App 3.6 Determining the effect of a breach or failing at step 1

## DISP App 3.6.2 (Evidential Provisions)

In the absence of evidence to the contrary, the firm should presume that the complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract he bought if the sale was substantially flawed, for example where the firm:

(1) pressured the complainant into purchasing the payment protection contract; or

(2) did not disclose to the complainant, in good time before the sale was concluded, and in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading, that the policy was optional; or

(3) made the sale without the complainant's explicit agreement to purchase the policy; or

(4) did not disclose to the complainant, in good time before the sale was concluded, and in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading, the significant exclusions and limitations, i.e. those that would tend to affect the decisions of customers generally to buy the policy; or

(5) did not, for an advised sale (including where the firm gave advice in a non-advised sales process) take reasonable care to ensure that the policy was suitable for the complainant's demands and needs taking into account all relevant factors, including level of cover, cost, and relevant exclusions, excesses, limitations and conditions; or

(6) did not take reasonable steps to ensure the complainant only bought a policy for which he was eligible to claim benefits; or

(7) found, while arranging the policy, that parts of the cover did not apply but did not disclose this to the customer, in good time before the sale was concluded, and in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading; or

(8) did not disclose to the complainant, in good time before the sale was concluded, and in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading, the total (not just monthly) cost of the policy separately from any other prices (or the basis for calculating it so that the complainant could verify it); or

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# ICOBS — ICOBS 6A.4 Travel insurance and medical conditions

## ICOBS 6A.4.6 (Rules)

The circumstances for the purposes of ICOBS 6A.4.5R are where a firm:(1) declines, or otherwise does not offer, a consumer a quotation due (wholly or partly) to a medical condition;(2) cancels a consumer’spolicy due (wholly or partly) to a medical condition;(3) offers a policy with a medical condition exclusion which cannot be removed from the policy;(4) offers a policy with a medical condition premium of the amount (or more) as set out in, or calculated in accordance with, ICOBS 6A.4.6AR; and/or(5) offers a policy in respect of which the medical condition premium is not known.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 7. Procedures

### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints

A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination.

All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.

### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement

Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must:

1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected.
2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer.
3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.

### 7.3 Investigation Process

The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include:

1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems.
2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made.
3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure

# DISP — DISP App 3.6 Determining the effect of a breach or failing at step 1

(9) recommended a single premium payment protection contract without taking reasonable steps, where the policy did not have a pro-rata refund, to establish whether there was a prospect that the complainant would repay or refinance the loan before the end of the term; or

(10) provided misleading or inaccurate information about the policy to the complainant; or

(11) sold the complainant a policy where the total cost of the policy (including any interest paid on the premium) would exceed the benefits payable under the policy (other than benefits payable under life cover); or

(12) in a sale of a single premium payment protection contract, failed to disclose to the complainant, in good time before the sale was concluded, and in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading:

(a) that the premium would be added to the amount provided under the credit agreement, that interest would be payable on the premium and the amount of that interest; or

(b) (if applicable) that the term of the cover was shorter than the term of the credit agreement and the consequences of that mismatch; or

(c) (if applicable) that the complainant would not receive a pro-rata refund if the complainant were to repay or refinance the loan or otherwise cancel the single premium policy after the cooling-off period.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with the final response letter I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about being mis-sold a payment protection insurance policy alongside my personal loan in 2012. Your letter states that you do not uphold my complaint because you believe adequate information was provided at the point of sale. However, I note that your response fails to address the key issue I raised — that I was self-employed at the time and was never told the policy would not cover me for unemployment. You have not explained what evidence you considered or how you reached your decision. I was told verbally by the adviser that the PPI would cover all eventualities. I have now been left £3,200 out of pocket in premiums for a policy that could never have paid out on the unemployment element. I do not accept your decision and wish to know what my options are. I also want to understand whether I can take this further, as your final response letter did not mention anything about the Financial Ombudsman Service or any time limits for escalation.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises several significant issues that require careful attention before any response is sent.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Missing FOS referral notice — potential breach of DISP 1.6.2R.** The customer states the final response letter of 15 November did not mention the Financial Ombudsman Service or any time limits for escalation. Under DISP 1.6.2R, a final response must: enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; provide the FOS website address; inform the complainant they may refer the complaint to the FOS if they remain dissatisfied; and indicate whether the respondent consents to waive relevant time limits (with the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R). If the 15 November letter omitted all of this, it was not a compliant final response. You should check the original letter urgently. If it was indeed deficient, a corrected letter must be issued.

2. **Substantive PPI mis-sale allegation — self-employment and unemployment exclusion.** The customer alleges they were self-employed at the time of sale and were never told the policy would not cover them for unemployment. Under DISP App 3.6.2E, in the absence of evidence to the contrary, the firm should presume the complainant would not have bought the PPI if the sale was substantially flawed. Relevant evidential factors here include: failure to disclose significant exclusions and limitations in a fair, clear and not misleading way before the sale (DISP App 3.6.2E(4)); failure to take reasonable steps to ensure the complainant only bought a policy for which they were eligible to claim benefits (DISP App 3.6.2E(6)); and failure to ensure the policy was suitable for the complainant's demands and needs (DISP App 3.6.2E(5)). The original final response does not appear to have addressed these specific points. The complaint should be re-examined against these evidential provisions.

3. **PPI complaint time limits — DISP 2.8.9R.** The sale took place in 2012 (before 29 August 2017). The customer's complaint must have been referred to the bank on or before 29 August 2019 to fall within the FOS's jurisdiction under DISP 2.8.9R, unless one of the exceptions applies (e.g. exceptional circumstances, or the complaint relates to a live policy with a post-August 2017 claim rejection). You must confirm when the customer first raised this complaint with the bank, and whether it was before 29 August 2019. This is critical to whether the FOS can consider it.

4. **Financial redress of £3,200 claimed.** Under your internal procedure (section 7.4), any complaint involving financial redress of £500 or more must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch. This applies here.

**Recommended action:**
- Confirm the date the complaint was first received by the bank.
- Pull the original 15 November final response letter and check whether it included the required FOS referral wording, leaflet, and website address.
- Re-examine the substance of the complaint against DISP App 3.6.2E, specifically the self-employment/unemployment exclusion and eligibility points.
- Escalate to the Head of Customer Operations given the financial redress amount.
- Issue a corrected/updated response to the customer that addresses the substantive points and includes the required FOS referral information.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft below:**
- I have assumed the 15 November letter was intended as the final response and that eight weeks from complaint receipt had elapsed by that date. If it was not a final response (e.g. it was an interim update), the regulatory position on FOS referral rights differs slightly.
- I have assumed the complaint was first raised before 29 August 2019. If it was not, the FOS time limit issue under DISP 2.8.9R becomes critical and the customer draft below should not be sent without legal/compliance review.
- The customer draft below does not admit liability or quantify redress — those decisions must be made by the investigator after re-examination.
- Because this involves potential financial redress of £3,200 and a possible deficient final response, human review is required before sending.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for writing to us following our letter of 15 November. We are sorry that our response has left you with unanswered questions and that you remain dissatisfied. We take your concerns seriously and want to address them properly.

**What you have told us**

You have told us that at the time of the sale in 2012 you were self-employed, and that you were not informed before the sale was concluded that the policy would not cover you for unemployment in those circumstances. You have also told us that the adviser indicated verbally that the policy would cover all eventualities. You believe you have paid approximately £3,200 in premiums for a policy that could never have paid out on the unemployment element.

You have also told us that our letter of 15 November did not explain your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. We apologise for that omission and address it below.

**What happens next**

We are reviewing your complaint again in light of the specific points you have raised. When assessing a complaint about the sale of a payment protection policy, we are required to consider whether significant exclusions and limitations — including those that would affect a customer's decision to buy — were clearly disclosed before the sale, and whether reasonable steps were taken to ensure you were only sold a policy under which you would be eligible to claim benefits. We will examine the evidence we hold about the sale against these points and write to you again with our findings.

**Your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

Our letter of 15 November was intended as our final response to your complaint. If you are dissatisfied with that response, or with any further response we send you, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at:

- **Website:** www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- **Telephone:** 0800 023 4567

You have six months from the date of our final response letter to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, which sets out further details about the process and the time limits that apply.

Please note that for complaints about the sale of a payment protection policy, specific time limits may apply to what the Financial Ombudsman Service is able to consider. The leaflet and the Ombudsman's own guidance explain this in more detail.

We will be in touch again as soon as our review is complete.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the final response to enclose the FOS explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and indicate whether the respondent consents to waive relevant time limits — all of which the customer states were absent from the 15 November letter.

Sets out evidential factors — including failure to disclose significant exclusions and failure to ensure the customer was eligible to claim — which bear on whether the firm should presume the complainant would not have bought the PPI had the sale been conducted properly; directly relevant to the self-employment/unemployment exclusion allegation.

Specifically addresses failure to disclose significant exclusions and limitations in a fair, clear and not misleading way before the sale — the core of the customer's allegation about the unemployment exclusion.
Addresses failure to take reasonable steps to ensure the complainant only bought a policy for which they were eligible to claim benefits — directly relevant to the self-employment point.

Governs whether the FOS can consider a PPI complaint relating to a sale on or before 29 August 2017; the complaint must have been referred to the firm on or before 29 August 2019 unless an exception applies — the handler must confirm the date of first complaint receipt.

Confirms that the DISP Appendix 3 framework applies to complaints about the sale of a PPI policy regardless of when the sale took place, whether advised or non-advised, and whether the policy is still in force or not.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# DISP — DISP App 3.6 Determining the effect of a breach or failing at step 1

## DISP App 3.6.2 (Evidential Provisions)

In the absence of evidence to the contrary, the firm should presume that the complainant would not have bought the payment protection contract he bought if the sale was substantially flawed, for example where the firm:

(1) pressured the complainant into purchasing the payment protection contract; or

(2) did not disclose to the complainant, in good time before the sale was concluded, and in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading, that the policy was optional; or

(3) made the sale without the complainant's explicit agreement to purchase the policy; or

(4) did not disclose to the complainant, in good time before the sale was concluded, and in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading, the significant exclusions and limitations, i.e. those that would tend to affect the decisions of customers generally to buy the policy; or

(5) did not, for an advised sale (including where the firm gave advice in a non-advised sales process) take reasonable care to ensure that the policy was suitable for the complainant's demands and needs taking into account all relevant factors, including level of cover, cost, and relevant exclusions, excesses, limitations and conditions; or

(6) did not take reasonable steps to ensure the complainant only bought a policy for which he was eligible to claim benefits; or

(7) found, while arranging the policy, that parts of the cover did not apply but did not disclose this to the customer, in good time before the sale was concluded, and in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading; or

(8) did not disclose to the complainant, in good time before the sale was concluded, and in a way that was fair, clear and not misleading, the total (not just monthly) cost of the policy separately from any other prices (or the basis for calculating it so that the complainant could verify it); or

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.8 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of a payment protection contract, knowledge by the complainant that there was a problem with the sale of the payment protection contract generally (for example where there has been a rejection of a claim on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion, or the complainant has received a customer contact letter explaining that they may have been mis-sold) would not in itself ordinarily be sufficient to establish for the purposes of the three-year time period in DISP 2.8.2R(2) that the complainant had become aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he or she had cause for complaint in respect of a failure to make the disclosure set out at DISP App 3.3A.2E (relating to failure to disclose commission).

# DISP — DISP App 3.1 Introduction

## DISP App 3.1.1 (Guidance)

(1) This appendix sets out how:

(a) a firm should handle complaints relating to the sale of a payment protection contract by the firm which express dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling); and

(b) a firm that is a CCA lender and which has received such a complaint should consider whether there was a failure to disclose commission in relation to the sale of a payment protection contract which covers or covered or purported to cover a credit agreement (this includes partial coverage).

(2) It relates to the sale of any payment protection contract whenever the sale took place and irrespective of whether it was on an advised or non-advised basis; conducted through any sales channel; in connection with any type of loan or credit product, or none; whether the insurer was in the same group as the firm or not; whether the premium was financed by the credit product or not; and for a regular premium or single premium payment. It applies whether the policy is currently in force, was cancelled during the policy term or ran its full term.

(3) It does not set out how a firm which has received a complaint referred to in (1)(a) should assess:

(a) whether the firm’s conduct of the sale was in breach of a fiduciary duty where there has been a failure to disclose either the existence of, or the level of, any commission and/or profit share paid;

(b) whether any omission (other than the omission referred to in DISP App 3.3A.2E) to disclose either the existence of, or level of, commission and/or profit share made the relationship unfair under section 140A of the CCA;

(c) any other issue not dealt with in step 1 or step 2 set out in this appendix.

Complaints concerning such issues should be dealt with under DISP 1.4.1R.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# DISP — DISP App 3.11 Obligation to write letters to certain rejected complainants

## DISP App 3.11.4 (Rules)

DISP App 3.11.5R and DISP App 3.11.6R apply where, in relation to the sale of a payment protection contract which covers, covered or purported to cover a credit agreement (this includes partial coverage) a complainant has made:

(1) (in relation to a regular premium payment protection contract) a complaint to the CCA lender that was rejected before 30 January 2019 in that:

(a) it was considered under step 2 of DISP Appendix 3 but redress on the basis that an unfair relationship under section 140A of the CCA had arisen was not offered; or

(b) it was not considered under step 2 of DISP Appendix 3 because the complaint was treated as a purported complaint that did not come under the jurisdiction of the Financial Ombudsman Service; or

(2) a purported complaint to the selling firm that would otherwise have fallen to be considered under step 1 of DISP Appendix 3 but was rejected before 30 January 2019 by that firm on the basis that it did not come under the jurisdiction of the Financial Ombudsman Service.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with how my complaint about mis-sold payment protection insurance (PPI) has been handled. I submitted my complaint on 15 January and received your final response letter on 28 February, which rejected my claim. Your letter stated that you found no evidence of mis-selling. However, I believe your investigation was neither competent nor impartial. You failed to obtain the original point-of-sale documentation, you did not consider that I was self-employed at the time and therefore ineligible for the unemployment element of the policy, and you ignored the fact that I had a pre-existing medical condition that would have excluded me from claiming. I also note that your final response letter did not mention anything about a time limit for referring my complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. I want you to reconsider your decision and provide appropriate redress for the premiums I paid over seven years. If you do not resolve this satisfactorily, I intend to take this to the Ombudsman, but I need clarity on what my rights are and how long I have to do so.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This is a PPI mis-selling complaint where the customer is challenging the adequacy of your investigation and the omission of FOS referral rights from the final response letter. Here is the regulatory position and recommended action:

**1. Missing FOS referral information in the final response**
DISP 1.6.2R requires that a final response must (among other things): enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant they may refer to the FOS if dissatisfied, and indicate whether the respondent consents to waive relevant time limits. The customer is correct that this information was missing. This is a breach of DISP 1.6.2R. The internal policy (section 7.5) also confirms the six-month referral window must be stated. You should acknowledge this omission.

**2. Time limits for FOS referral**
Under DISP 2.8.2R, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint referred more than six months after the respondent sent its final response. However, DISP 2.8.3G (Guidance) makes clear that the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that is a final response AND which tells the complainant about the six-month time limit. Since your final response did not include this information, the six-month clock has arguably not been validly triggered. You should flag this to your legal/compliance team to confirm, but the practical effect is that the customer's right to refer to the FOS may not yet be time-barred. The standard six-year/three-year long-stop limits in DISP 2.8.2R(2) would still apply in the background.

**3. PPI-specific time limits (DISP 2.8.9R)**
The complaint relates to a PPI sale. If the sale took place on or before 29 August 2017, the Ombudsman cannot consider it unless one of the conditions in DISP 2.8.9R(2) is met — most relevantly, whether the complaint was referred to the respondent or FOS on or before 29 August 2019. The customer says they submitted their complaint on 15 January — you need to confirm the year. If this was January 2019 or earlier, the complaint was in time. If it was January 2020 or later, the PPI deadline may have passed and the Ombudsman may be unable to consider it (unless exceptional circumstances apply or you consent to waiver). This is a critical assumption you must verify before sending any customer response.

**4. Substance of the complaint**
The customer raises three substantive grounds: (a) failure to obtain point-of-sale documentation; (b) self-employment making them ineligible for the unemployment element; (c) a pre-existing medical condition excluding them from claiming. These are eligibility/exclusion grounds. Under DISP 2.8.9R(2)(d), if the complaint relates to a rejection of a claim on grounds of ineligibility or exclusion on or after 29 August 2017, those parts of the complaint are not subject to the PPI deadline restriction. This may be relevant if the claim was rejected on those grounds.

**5. Human review required**
This complaint involves potential financial redress (seven years of premiums), allegations of an inadequate investigation, a regulatory breach (missing FOS information), and a time-sensitive PPI deadline question. It must be reviewed by the Head of Customer Operations before any response is sent, per your internal policy (section 7.4 — complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more or regulatory significance require countersignature).

**Key assumptions to confirm before sending:**
- The year the complaint was submitted (to assess the DISP 2.8.9R PPI deadline).
- Whether the PPI sale took place on or before 29 August 2017.
- Whether the claim was rejected on grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (which may engage DISP 2.8.9R(2)(d)).
- Whether the bank wishes to consent to waive any applicable time limits.
- The customer draft below is written on the assumption that the complaint was submitted in time under DISP 2.8.9R — if that is not confirmed, the draft must be revised before sending.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for your letter setting out your concerns about our handling of your PPI complaint. I am sorry that your experience has fallen short of the standard you should expect, and I want to address each of your points directly.

**Omission from our final response letter**

You are right to raise the fact that our final response letter of 28 February did not include information about your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS). We are required to include this information in every final response, and we apologise for this omission. I want to make sure you have that information now, and I set it out clearly below.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at:

- Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- Telephone: 0800 023 4567

Because our original final response letter did not include the required information about the time limit for referring your complaint to the FOS, we want to be transparent with you: the usual six-month period for referring a complaint to the FOS runs from the date of a final response that properly notifies you of this right. We are enclosing a copy of the FOS's standard explanatory leaflet, which explains the referral process and applicable time limits in full.

**Reconsideration of your complaint**

We take seriously your concerns that our investigation was inadequate. Specifically, you have told us that:

1. We did not obtain the original point-of-sale documentation;
2. You were self-employed at the time the policy was sold, which you say made you ineligible for the unemployment element of the cover; and
3. You had a pre-existing medical condition that would have excluded you from making a claim.

These are substantive points that go to the heart of whether the policy was suitable for you. We are reopening our review of your complaint to consider these matters properly. We will write to you again with our findings.

**Next steps**

We will complete our review and write to you with a further response as soon as possible. In the meantime, if you have any documents or information that you believe are relevant — including anything relating to your employment status or medical history at the time the policy was sold — please do send these to us, as they will assist our review.

Once again, I apologise for the shortcomings in our original response. We are committed to investigating your complaint thoroughly and fairly.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
[Contact Details]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the final response to enclose the FOS explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and indicate consent or otherwise on time limit waiver — all of which were omitted from the original final response.
Sets the six-month time limit (from final response) and six-year/three-year long-stop limits within which a complainant must refer a complaint to the FOS.

Indicates that the six-month time limit is only triggered by a final response that tells the complainant about the six-month limit — relevant here because the original final response omitted this information.

Restricts the Ombudsman from considering PPI sale complaints unless the complainant referred the complaint on or before 29 August 2019 (or another condition is met) — critical to assess whether this complaint is in time.

Indicates that where a complaint meets the conditions in DISP 2.8.9R(2)(d) (rejection on grounds of ineligibility or exclusion), those parts of the complaint are not subject to the PPI deadline restriction.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.8 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of a payment protection contract, knowledge by the complainant that there was a problem with the sale of the payment protection contract generally (for example where there has been a rejection of a claim on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion, or the complainant has received a customer contact letter explaining that they may have been mis-sold) would not in itself ordinarily be sufficient to establish for the purposes of the three-year time period in DISP 2.8.2R(2) that the complainant had become aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he or she had cause for complaint in respect of a failure to make the disclosure set out at DISP App 3.3A.2E (relating to failure to disclose commission).

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules)

The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if:

(1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or

(2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint:

(a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or

(b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme:

(a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or

(b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme;

unless:

(4) the respondent consents and:

(a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and

(b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint.

## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules)

Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.5 (Rules)

The six-year and the three-year time limits do not apply where:

(1) [deleted]

(2) the complaint concerns a contract or policy which is the subject of a review directly or indirectly under:

(a) the terms of the Statement of Policy on 'Pension transfers and Opt-outs' issued by the FSA on 25 October 1994; or

(b) the terms of the policy statement for the review of specific categories of FSAVC business issued by the FSA on 28 February 2000.

## DISP 2.8.6 (Guidance)

If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage, the receipt by the complainant of a letter which states that there is a risk (rather than a high risk) that the policy would not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount is not, itself, sufficient to cause the three year time period in DISP 2.8.2R (2) to start to run.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.10 (Guidance)

Where a complaint meets the requirements of DISP 2.8.9R(2)(d), those parts of the complaint that relate to the grounds of rejection of the claim are not subject to the restriction in DISP 2.8.9R(1) on an Ombudsman considering the complaint.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I received a text message from my bank three days ago saying my complaint about a £35 overdraft fee had been resolved after I agreed on the phone to accept a refund. However, the written summary I then received by post only said 'your complaint is now closed' — it didn't mention anything about the Financial Ombudsman Service, didn't give me their website address, and didn't tell me I could refer the matter to them if I changed my mind. Well, I have changed my mind. The £35 refund doesn't address the fact that the fee was applied incorrectly in the first place due to a system error, and I believe I'm owed compensation for the stress and time spent chasing this. I want to escalate this complaint. I also want to know why the bank failed to give me proper information about my rights when it sent me that summary letter.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This message appears to have come in via the complaints channel but reads as if it is written from the customer's perspective (first person). Please confirm whether this is a customer complaint forwarded to you, or a handler query. I am treating it as a customer complaint that you, the handler, need to respond to.

**Regulatory position:**

1. **Deficient summary communication.** The written summary the customer received said only 'your complaint is now closed' and contained no mention of the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), no FOS contact details, and no six-month referral time limit. Under DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in your internal procedure at section 7.5), any written response that closes a complaint — including a summary resolution communication — must inform the customer of their right to refer to the FOS, include FOS contact details, and state the six-month time limit. The summary letter as described does not meet that standard. This is a compliance gap you should flag internally.

2. **Six-month time limit not yet triggered.** Under DISP 2.8.2R and DISP 2.8.3G, the six-month window for the customer to refer to the FOS is only triggered by a response that actually tells the customer about that six-month limit. Because the summary letter did not do so, the clock has not started running. The customer therefore retains their right to refer to the FOS, and you should not represent to them that any deadline has passed.

3. **Customer wishes to escalate / reopen.** The customer is disputing that the £35 refund is adequate and is raising a new head of loss (compensation for stress and time). You should treat this as a live complaint requiring a full final response under DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must: state whether the complaint is upheld, partially upheld, or rejected (with reasons); set out any redress offered and how it is calculated; and include the FOS referral notice with the six-month time limit and FOS contact details.

4. **Overdraft fee complaint.** The customer's underlying complaint is that a £35 overdraft fee was applied incorrectly due to a system error. CONC 5C.2.1R governs the structure of overdraft charges on personal current accounts (charges must be expressed as an annual interest rate; a firm must not impose more than one charge from the same event; and certain fee structures are prohibited). You should investigate whether the fee as applied was compliant with CONC 5C.2.1R. If the fee was a flat charge rather than an interest-rate-based charge, that is itself a potential regulatory breach separate from the system error.

5. **Compensation for distress and inconvenience.** The customer is claiming compensation beyond the £35 refund. The retrieved provisions do not set out a specific rule on distress/inconvenience compensation, so I cannot ground a commitment to pay such compensation in the retrieved context. Do not promise or quantify any additional redress in the customer letter. Flag this for human review.

6. **Internal escalation.** Your internal procedure (section 7.4) requires complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or regulatory significance, to be countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations. This complaint has potential regulatory significance (deficient summary communication, possible CONC 5C breach). Consider whether escalation is required.

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the customer draft:**
- Confirm the complaint was originally received by the bank and the date it was received (to check the eight-week deadline).
- Confirm whether the £35 charge was structured as a flat fee or an interest-rate charge, to assess CONC 5C.2.1R compliance.
- Confirm whether any additional redress beyond £35 is being offered — if so, the letter will need to be updated and countersigned per your internal procedure.
- The draft below does NOT include the FOS prescribed six-month notice as a definitive statement, because the original summary letter did not trigger the clock; the new final response letter will trigger it. Ensure the final response letter (when issued) includes the full FOS notice.
- Human review is required before sending — see flag below.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for getting back to us. I am sorry to hear that you are not satisfied with how your complaint has been handled, and I want to address both the concerns you have raised.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

We recognise that the written summary you received did not include information about your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) if you remained dissatisfied. We apologise for this omission. You do have the right to refer your complaint to the FOS, and we will ensure that this letter provides you with the information you should have received at the outset.

**Your complaint about the overdraft fee and additional compensation**

We understand that you are not satisfied with the £35 refund and that you believe you are owed further compensation for the time and stress involved in pursuing this matter. We take this seriously and are reopening our investigation into your complaint to consider all of the issues you have raised.

We will write to you with our final response once our investigation is complete. Our regulator requires us to issue a final response within eight weeks of the date your complaint was first received. If for any reason we are unable to do so within that timeframe, we will write to you to explain why and to confirm your options at that stage.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If you remain dissatisfied with our final response, or if you have not received a final response within eight weeks of the date your complaint was first received, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at:

- **Website:** www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- **Telephone:** 0800 023 4567

Once we have issued our final response, you will have six months from the date of that letter to refer your complaint to the FOS if you remain dissatisfied.

Thank you for your patience. We will be in touch as soon as our review is complete.

Yours sincerely,
[Handler name]
[Job title]
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the bank to issue a final response that informs the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS; the summary letter's failure to include this information means the six-month referral clock has not been triggered and the complaint must now be treated as live.

Sets out that the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint referred more than six months after the respondent sent its final response; because the summary letter did not meet the requirements of a valid final response, this time limit has not yet started to run.
Confirms that the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that actually tells the complainant about that limit; the deficient summary letter therefore did not start the clock.

Confirms that the Ombudsman can consider a complaint once eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received it, providing the basis for informing the customer of their right to escalate if no final response is issued within eight weeks.

Governs the structure of overdraft charges on personal current accounts, requiring charges to be expressed as an annual interest rate; relevant to assessing whether the £35 fee was lawfully imposed in the first place.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# DISP — DISP INTRO 1 Introduction

## DISP INTRO 1.4

Chapters 2 - 4: The Financial Ombudsman ServiceChapters 2, 3 and 4 set out how the Financial Ombudsman Service (operated by FOS Ltd) considers unresolved complaints.Chapter 2 sets out the scope of the Financial Ombudsman Service's two jurisdictions:• the Compulsory Jurisdiction; and• the Voluntary Jurisdiction.The scope of the two jurisdictions is defined by: the type of activity to which the complaint relates; the place where the activity took place; the eligibility of the complainant; and the time limits for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.Chapter 3 sets out the procedures of the Financial Ombudsman Service, including consideration and determination of complaints and how the Financial Ombudsman Service deals with information received.Chapter 4 sets out the terms under which VJ participants participate in the Voluntary Jurisdiction.

## DISP INTRO 1.5

Appendix 1: FCA’s guidance on handling mortgage-endowment complaintsThis appendix contains the FCA's guidance to firms on handling complaints relating to mortgage endowments.

## DISP INTRO 1.6

Appendix 3: FCA’s rules and guidance on handling payment protection insurance complaintsThis appendix sets out the approach which firms should use when handling complaints relating to the sale of payment protection contracts.

## DISP INTRO 1.7

Financial Ombudsman Service feesThe rules on fees charged in respect of the Financial Ombudsman Service are in Chapter 5 of the Fees manual.

metadata.section_name: DISP INTRO 1 Introduction

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.7 (Rules)

(1) If a complaint relates to the sale of an endowment policy for the purpose of achieving capital repayment of a mortgage and the complainant receives a letter from a firm or a VJ participant warning that there is a high risk that the policy will not, at maturity, produce a sum large enough to repay the target amount then, subject to (2), (3), (4) and (5):

(a) time for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service starts to run from the date the complainant receives the letter; and

(b) ends three years from that date ('the final date').

(2) Paragraph (1)(b) applies only if the complainant also receives within the three year period mentioned in (1)(b) and at least six months before the final date an explanation that the complainant's time to refer such a complaint would expire at the final date.

(3) If an explanation is given but is sent outside the period referred to in (2), time for referring a complaint will run until a date specified in such an explanation which must not be less than six months after the date on which the notice is sent.

(4) A complainant will be taken to have complied with the time limits in (1) to (3) above if in any case he refers the complaint to the firm or VJ participant within those limits and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received.

(5) Paragraph (1) does not apply if the Ombudsman is of the opinion that, in the circumstances of the case, it is appropriate for DISP 2.8.2R (2) to apply.

# FEES — FEES 5.5E Representative case fees (for financial years beginning on or after 1 April 2026)

## FEES 5.5E.1 (Rules)

FEES 5.5E applies to a complainant representative in relation to a complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service which is closed on or after 1 April 2026.

## FEES 5.5E.2 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E does not apply to the Voluntary Jurisdiction.

## FEES 5.5E.3 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E sets out when a complainant representative that is representing a complainant must pay fees in respect of complaints referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## FEES 5.5E.4 (Rules)

A complainant representative will not be liable for a representative case fee under FEES 5.5E if it is acting entirely pro bono in relation to the complaint.

## FEES 5.5E.5 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E.4R applies where a complainant representative is representing the complainant without any fees, charges or other form of remuneration becoming payable by the complainant in any circumstance.

## FEES 5.5E.6 (Rules)

Where a complaint is closed by the Financial Ombudsman Service during a financial year in circumstances where:(1) the complaint was referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or after 1 April 2025; and(2) a category B complainant representative was (at any time) representing the complainant in relation to that complaint, the category B complainant representative which was first representing the complainant (even if it is no longer doing so at the time the complaint is closed) must pay to the FOS Ltd:(3) the reduced representative case fee specified in FEES 5 Annex 5R Part 2, if the complaint was closed as a change in outcome in favour of the complainant; or(4) the standard representative case fee specified in FEES 5 Annex 5R Part 2, if the complaint was closed other than as a change in outcome in favour of the complainant.

metadata.section_name: FEES 5.5E Representative case fees (for financial years beginning on or after 1 April 2026)

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules)

The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if:

(1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or

(2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint:

(a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or

(b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme:

(a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or

(b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme;

unless:

(4) the respondent consents and:

(a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and

(b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint.

## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules)

Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

# CONC — CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

## CONC 5C.2.1 (Rules)

(1) A firm must not:

(a) enter into an agreement with a customer that provides for an arranged overdraft charge or an unarranged overdraft charge; or

(b) impose on a customer an arranged overdraft charge or an unarranged overdraft charge,

unless the conditions in (2) to (7) are satisfied.

(2) The charge must be a rate of interest expressed as a percentage applied on an annual basis to the relevant balance of arranged overdraft or unarranged overdraft (as the case may be).

(3) The rate of interest that applies to any given balance of arranged overdraft relating to a personal current account must either be zero or the same as the rate of interest that applies to any other balance of arranged overdraft in respect of that personal current account.

(4) The rate of interest that applies to any given balance of unarranged overdraft relating to a personal current account must either be zero or the same as the rate of interest that applies to any other balance of unarranged overdraft in respect of that personal current account.

(5) A firm must not require a customer to pay more than one arranged overdraft charge or more than one unarranged overdraft charge arising out of the same event.

(6) Where a customer has an arranged overdraft, in relation to a personal current account, to which a rate of interest above zero applies, any unarranged overdraft charge imposed on the customer in relation to that personal current account must also consist of a rate of interest computed, structured and presented in an identical manner (although the level of the rate of interest that applies to the unarranged overdraft may be lower).

metadata.section_name: CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

# CONC — CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

(5) A firm is not prevented from providing in the terms and conditions of the overdraft that no interest is payable in respect of arranged overdraft balances or unarranged overdraft balances of up to specified amounts (sometimes described as “fee-free amounts” or “buffer zones”) where permitted by CONC 5C.2.1R. The purpose of CONC 5C.2.1R(7) is to prevent firms from offering fee-free amounts or buffer zones that are free only in certain circumstances. An example of a buffer zone that is not permitted is where no interest is payable if an unarranged overdraft balance does not exceed the upper threshold of the buffer zone, but where interest becomes payable in respect of the entire balance (including the part of the balance in the buffer zone) if the customer exceeds the threshold.

(6) A firm is not prevented from waiving or reducing overdraft charges (in whole or in part) in appropriate circumstances (for example, where the firm is treating a customer with forbearance in line with other rules in this sourcebook).

(7) CONC 5C.2.1R does not prohibit the level of the single, uniform contractual rate of interest from differing from customer to customer, or between personal current accounts for the same customer.

(8)

(a) The definitions of an arranged overdraft charge and an unarranged overdraft charge are broad.

(b) These definitions capture any charges that arise because a customer has used an overdraft, or that are triggered by - or the size of which are affected by - the fact that the personal current account has entered, remains in, or extended, a debit position.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?
