claims.reasoning: Section 7.4 states: 'Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must: 1. Be issued within eight weeks of the date the complaint was received.'

claims.claim: If the firm is unable to issue a final response within eight weeks, it will write to the customer to explain why and confirm their options.
claims.reasoning: Section 7.5 states that in a written response issued at eight weeks, if the firm has not yet been able to issue a final response, it must explain the reason for the delay and confirm the FOS referral right.

claims.claim: If the customer remains dissatisfied with the final response, or has not received a final response within eight weeks, they have the right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.1R confirms the Ombudsman can consider a complaint after a final response or after eight weeks have elapsed. The 'free of charge' element is not explicitly stated in the retrieved provisions, though it is a well-known feature of the FOS.

claims.claim: The FOS can be contacted at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk and telephone 0800 023 4567.
claims.reasoning: Section 7.5 of the internal procedure specifies these exact FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567).

claims.claim: Once the final response is issued, the customer will have six months from the date of that letter to refer the complaint to the FOS.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.2R(1) states the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if referred more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with how my complaint was handled. Three weeks ago, I contacted your bank about an incorrect fee of £45 charged to my current account. Your staff member called me back the same day and offered to refund the fee, which I accepted over the phone. I then received a short letter saying the complaint was resolved, but the letter did not mention the Financial Ombudsman Service at all — no website address, no information about my right to refer the matter if I changed my mind, nothing. I have since looked into this further and I believe the fee was actually part of a wider pattern of overcharging on my account going back several months. I am no longer satisfied with the resolution and wish to escalate this matter. I want the bank to investigate the full pattern of charges and I want to know why I was not properly informed of my rights when the complaint was first closed.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct issues that must be handled separately.

**Issue 1: Missing FOS referral information in the summary resolution communication**
The customer is correct that they should have been informed of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service. Under DISP 1.6.2R (as reflected in Crest Bank's own procedure at sections 7.4 and 7.5), any final response or resolution communication must notify the complainant of their FOS referral right, including the FOS contact details and the six-month time limit to refer. The short letter sent after the phone resolution did not include this. This is a procedural failing you should acknowledge.

Note on the six-month clock: Under DISP 2.8.2R, the six-month period for the customer to refer to the FOS is only triggered by a response that qualifies as a final response, redress determination, or summary resolution communication — and DISP 2.8.3G confirms that the response must actually tell the complainant about the six-month time limit for it to start running. Because the letter omitted the FOS information, it is arguable the six-month clock has not yet been validly triggered. You should flag this to the Head of Customer Operations and seek legal/compliance input before treating the original letter as a valid final response.

**Issue 2: New/escalated complaint about a wider pattern of overcharging**
The customer is now raising a materially different and broader complaint — a pattern of alleged overcharging over several months — which goes beyond the original £45 fee. This should be treated as a new complaint (or a significant escalation of the original), logged in the CMS, and investigated in accordance with the full complaints procedure under DISP 1 and Crest Bank's internal procedure (sections 7.1–7.4). The eight-week clock for a final response will run from the date this escalation was received.

Given that: (a) the original resolution communication was defective; (b) the customer is now raising a broader financial complaint that may involve redress; and (c) there is a question about whether the original complaint was validly closed, **human review is required before any response is sent**. The Head of Customer Operations should be involved. If the wider investigation reveals financial redress of £500 or more, countersignature will also be required before despatch (per section 7.4 of the internal procedure).

**Assumptions to confirm before sending the draft below:**
1. Confirm the date the escalation/new complaint was received, so the eight-week clock can be correctly set.
2. Confirm whether the original short letter was intended as a 'summary resolution communication' or a 'final response' — and whether it met the formal requirements for either.
3. Confirm whether the customer is to be treated as having an open complaint from the original date or a new complaint from the escalation date — compliance/legal input is advisable.
4. The draft below does not commit to any specific redress or outcome on the wider overcharging allegation, as this has not been investigated.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for getting back in touch with us. I am sorry to hear that you are not satisfied with how your complaint was handled, and I want to address both of the concerns you have raised.

**Your right to refer to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

You are right to raise this. When we wrote to you to confirm that your complaint had been resolved, we should have included information about your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service if you remained dissatisfied. That information was not included in our letter, and I apologise for that oversight.

To put this right: if you are dissatisfied with any aspect of how we have handled your complaint, you have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. You can contact them at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567. The time limit for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service is six months from the date of our final response letter — we will make sure that any further response we send you clearly sets out this time limit.

**Your wider concern about a pattern of charges**

We have noted that you are now raising a broader concern about a pattern of charges on your account over recent months. We take this seriously. We are treating this as a complaint requiring full investigation, and we will review your account records and the relevant charges in detail.

We will write to you with the outcome of our investigation. We aim to provide you with a full response within eight weeks of the date we received your escalation. If for any reason we are unable to do so within that timeframe, we will write to you to explain why and to confirm your options at that stage.

Your complaint reference number is [REFERENCE NUMBER]. If you have any questions in the meantime, please contact [Handler Name] at [contact details].

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

Sets the six-month time limit within which a complainant must refer a complaint to the FOS after receiving a final response; relevant to whether the original defective letter validly triggered this clock.

Indicates that the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that actually tells the complainant about the six-month limit, supporting the view that the defective letter may not have started the clock running.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.1 (Rules)

The Ombudsman can only consider a complaint if:

(1) the respondent has already sent the complainant its final response or summary resolution communication; or

(2) in relation to a complaint that is not an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint, eight weeks have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(2A) in relation to a complaint that is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint:

(a) 15 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint and the complainant has not received a holding response as described in DISP 1.6.2A R(2)(a); or

(b) where the complainant has received a holding response, 35 business days have elapsed since the respondent received the complaint; or

(3) in relation to a complaint the subject matter of which falls to be dealt with (or has properly been dealt with) under a consumer redress scheme:

(a) the respondent has already sent the complainant its redress determination under the scheme; or

(b) the respondent has failed to send a redress determination in accordance with the time limits specified under the scheme;

unless:

(4) the respondent consents and:

(a) the Ombudsman has informed the complainant that the respondent must deal with the complaint within eight weeks (or for EMD complaints and PSD complaints 15 business days or, in exceptional circumstances, 35 business days) and that it may resolve the complaint more quickly than the Ombudsman; and

(b) the complainant nevertheless wishes the Ombudsman to deal with the complaint.

## DISP 2.8.1A (Rules)

Where a respondent has chosen to treat a complaint in its entirety in accordance with DISP 1.6.2AR, notwithstanding that parts of it fall outside DISP 1.6.2AR, DISP 2.8 will apply as if the whole complaint were an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint.

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 7. Procedures

### 7.1 Definition and Receipt of Complaints

A complaint is any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, from or on behalf of an eligible complainant, as defined in DISP 2.7, about Crest Bank's provision of, or failure to provide, a financial service or a redress determination.

All customer-facing staff must recognise expressions of dissatisfaction and log them in the CMS on the same business day they are received. Staff must not attempt to resolve a complaint informally without logging it, unless the complaint is resolved by close of business on the next business day (the "next business day rule" under DISP 1.5). Where a complaint is resolved under this rule, it must still be recorded in the CMS with the resolution outcome noted.

### 7.2 Complaint Logging and Acknowledgement

Upon receipt of a complaint, the assigned complaints handler must:

1. Log the complaint in the CMS, capturing: customer name, account number, channel of receipt, date and time of receipt, nature of the complaint, and product or service affected.
2. Assign a unique complaint reference number and communicate this to the customer.
3. Send a written acknowledgement to the customer within **five business days** of receipt, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The acknowledgement must confirm: the complaint reference number, the name and contact details of the handler, and the expected resolution timescale.

### 7.3 Investigation Process

The complaints handler must conduct a fair and impartial investigation, which will include:

1. Reviewing all relevant account records, transaction data, and customer communications held in Crest Bank's systems.
2. Obtaining written input from the relevant business area (e.g., Product, Technology, Credit) within **five business days** of the request being made.
3. Considering whether the complaint indicates a potential data breach, financial crime, or systemic product failure

# FEES — FEES 5.5E Representative case fees (for financial years beginning on or after 1 April 2026)

## FEES 5.5E.1 (Rules)

FEES 5.5E applies to a complainant representative in relation to a complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service which is closed on or after 1 April 2026.

## FEES 5.5E.2 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E does not apply to the Voluntary Jurisdiction.

## FEES 5.5E.3 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E sets out when a complainant representative that is representing a complainant must pay fees in respect of complaints referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## FEES 5.5E.4 (Rules)

A complainant representative will not be liable for a representative case fee under FEES 5.5E if it is acting entirely pro bono in relation to the complaint.

## FEES 5.5E.5 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5E.4R applies where a complainant representative is representing the complainant without any fees, charges or other form of remuneration becoming payable by the complainant in any circumstance.

## FEES 5.5E.6 (Rules)

Where a complaint is closed by the Financial Ombudsman Service during a financial year in circumstances where:(1) the complaint was referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or after 1 April 2025; and(2) a category B complainant representative was (at any time) representing the complainant in relation to that complaint, the category B complainant representative which was first representing the complainant (even if it is no longer doing so at the time the complaint is closed) must pay to the FOS Ltd:(3) the reduced representative case fee specified in FEES 5 Annex 5R Part 2, if the complaint was closed as a change in outcome in favour of the complainant; or(4) the standard representative case fee specified in FEES 5 Annex 5R Part 2, if the complaint was closed other than as a change in outcome in favour of the complainant.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.

# DISP — DISP INTRO 1 Introduction

## DISP INTRO 1.4

Chapters 2 - 4: The Financial Ombudsman ServiceChapters 2, 3 and 4 set out how the Financial Ombudsman Service (operated by FOS Ltd) considers unresolved complaints.Chapter 2 sets out the scope of the Financial Ombudsman Service's two jurisdictions:• the Compulsory Jurisdiction; and• the Voluntary Jurisdiction.The scope of the two jurisdictions is defined by: the type of activity to which the complaint relates; the place where the activity took place; the eligibility of the complainant; and the time limits for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.Chapter 3 sets out the procedures of the Financial Ombudsman Service, including consideration and determination of complaints and how the Financial Ombudsman Service deals with information received.Chapter 4 sets out the terms under which VJ participants participate in the Voluntary Jurisdiction.

## DISP INTRO 1.5

Appendix 1: FCA’s guidance on handling mortgage-endowment complaintsThis appendix contains the FCA's guidance to firms on handling complaints relating to mortgage endowments.

## DISP INTRO 1.6

Appendix 3: FCA’s rules and guidance on handling payment protection insurance complaintsThis appendix sets out the approach which firms should use when handling complaints relating to the sale of payment protection contracts.

## DISP INTRO 1.7

Financial Ombudsman Service feesThe rules on fees charged in respect of the Financial Ombudsman Service are in Chapter 5 of the Fees manual.

# FEES — FEES 5.5C Representative case fees (for financial years ending before 1 April 2026)

## FEES 5.5C.9 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5C.8R applies, for example, where the complaint is closed by the Financial Ombudsman Service with an outcome that is more favourable for the complainant compared to when the complaint was referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## FEES 5.5C.10 (Rules)

A complainant representative must pay to the FOS Ltd any representative case fee which it is liable to pay under FEES 5.5C and which is invoiced by the FOS Ltd within 30 calendar days of the date when the invoice is issued by the FOS Ltd.

## FEES 5.5C.11 (Rules)

If, at the end of the financial year, the amount standing in credit to the complainant representative under FEES 5.5C.8R exceeds the amounts invoiced under FEES 5.5C.10R which remain unpaid (including any interest or administrative fee due under FEES 5.5C.12R), the FOS Ltd will repay the difference between the 2 amounts to the complainant representative by credit transfer within 30 calendar days of the complainant representative notifying the FOS Ltd of its account details.

## FEES 5.5C.11A (Rules)

In respect of any complaint which was not closed before 1 April 2026, the FOS Ltd will credit the amount of £250 to the complainant representative and FEES 5.5E will instead apply to that complainant representative in respect of that complaint.

# FEES — FEES 5.5C Representative case fees (for financial years ending before 1 April 2026)

## FEES 5.5C.5 (Rules)

(1) Subject to FEES 5.5C.6R, a complainant representative must pay to the FOS Ltd a representative case fee of £250 in respect of a complaint which is referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or after 1 April 2025. (2) A representative case fee payable pursuant to paragraph (1) must be paid: (a) at the time a complaint is referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service if the complainant representative is representing the complainant at the time the complaint is referred; or (b) subject to paragraph (3) below, at the time a complainant representative begins to represent the complainant in respect of a complaint that has already been referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service. (3) A complainant representative will not be liable for the representative case fee under paragraph (1) above if: (a) the representative case fee in relation to the complaint has been paid by a complainant representative who was previously representing the complainant in respect of the same complaint; or (b) the complainant representative is acting entirely pro bono in relation to the complaint.

## FEES 5.5C.6 (Rules)

A complainant representative will, in any financial year, only be liable for, and the FOS Ltd will only invoice for, the representative case fee under FEES 5.5C.5R in respect of the 11th and subsequent complaints that are referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## FEES 5.5C.7 (Guidance)

FEES 5.5C.5R(3)(b) applies where a complainant representative is representing the complainant without any fees, charges or other form of remuneration becoming payable by the complainant in any circumstance.

## FEES 5.5C.8 (Rules)

In relation to any complaint which is closed by the Financial Ombudsman Service as a change in outcome in favour of the complainant, the FOS Ltd will credit the amount of £175 to the complainant representative.

However, on the second and more substantive issue (the wider pattern of overcharging), no decision is stated — the firm says it will investigate but provides no outcome. The response does not use clear 'uphold/partially uphold/reject' language overall, and the main financial complaint remains unresolved. This is partial at best.

verdicts.reasoning: The customer draft mentions the right to refer to the FOS and provides contact details. However, this is presented in the context of the original procedural failing rather than as a formal notification attached to this final response. The letter also states 'we will make sure that any further response we send you clearly sets out this time limit,' implying this letter itself is not the final response for the wider complaint. claims.claim: Under DISP 1.6.2R, any final response or resolution communication must notify the complainant of their FOS referral right, including the FOS contact details and the six-month time limit to refer.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure at section 7.5 states that in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their FOS referral right, include FOS contact details, and state the six-month time limit.

claims.claim: Crest Bank's own procedure at sections 7.4 and 7.5 requires FOS referral information in final response or resolution communications.
claims.reasoning: Section 7.4 point 4 requires informing the complainant of their FOS referral right, and section 7.5 elaborates on this requirement.

claims.claim: Under DISP 2.8.2R, the six-month period for the customer to refer to the FOS is only triggered by a response that qualifies as a final response, redress determination, or summary resolution communication.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.2R(1) states the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint referred more than six months after the respondent sent its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication. DISP 2.8.3G confirms the six-month time limit is only triggered by such a response.

claims.claim: DISP 2.8.3G confirms that the response must actually tell the complainant about the six-month time limit for it to start running.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance) explicitly states: 'The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.'

claims.claim: Because the letter omitted the FOS information, it is arguable the six-month clock has not yet been validly triggered.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.3G states the six-month time limit is only triggered by a response that tells the complainant about the six-month time limit. If the letter omitted this, the guidance supports the argument that the clock was not triggered.

claims.claim: The new/escalated complaint about a wider pattern of overcharging should be treated as a new complaint, logged in the CMS, and investigated in accordance with the full complaints procedure under DISP 1 and Crest Bank's internal procedure (sections 7.1–7.4).
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure at 7.1 requires logging complaints in the CMS and 7.2-7.3 set out the investigation process. However, the specific determination that a broader complaint must be treated as a 'new complaint' rather than an escalation of the existing one is not explicitly stated in the retrieved provisions.

claims.claim: The eight-week clock for a final response will run from the date this escalation was received.
claims.reasoning: Section 7.4 states the final response must be issued within eight weeks of the date the complaint was received. However, the specific determination that the clock runs from the escalation date (rather than the original complaint date) is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the retrieved provisions.

claims.claim: If the wider investigation reveals financial redress of £500 or more, countersignature will also be required before despatch (per section 7.4 of the internal procedure).
claims.reasoning: Section 7.4 explicitly states: 'All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more... must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.'

claims.claim: When we wrote to you to confirm that your complaint had been resolved, we should have included information about your right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service.
claims.reasoning: Section 7.5 requires that FOS referral rights be included in the final response letter, and section 7.4 point 4 also requires this.

claims.claim: You have the right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: The retrieved provisions confirm the right to refer to the FOS, but none of the retrieved provisions explicitly state that the service is 'free of charge' to the complainant, though this is a well-known fact about the FOS.

claims.claim: You can contact them at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 023 4567.
claims.reasoning: Section 7.5 explicitly states that communications must include the FOS contact details: 'website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567'.

claims.claim: The time limit for referring a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service is six months from the date of our final response letter.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.2R(1) states the complainant must refer within six months of the date the respondent sent its final response. The internal procedure at section 7.5 also confirms the six-month time limit from the date of the final response.

claims.claim: We are treating this as a complaint requiring full investigation, and we will review your account records and the relevant charges in detail.
claims.reasoning: Section 7.3 requires the complaints handler to conduct a fair and impartial investigation including reviewing all relevant account records and transaction data.

claims.claim: We aim to provide you with a full response within eight weeks of the date we received your escalation.
claims.reasoning: Section 7.4 requires the final response to be issued within eight weeks of the date the complaint was received. The claim is consistent with this requirement, though the characterisation of the start date as the 'escalation' date is an interpretation.

claims.claim: If for any reason we are unable to do so within that timeframe, we will write to you to explain why and to confirm your options at that stage.
claims.reasoning: Section 7.5 states that in a written response issued at eight weeks, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, it must explain the reason for the delay and confirm the FOS referral right.

claims.claim: Complex or multi-area complaints must be escalated to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator.
claims.reasoning: The procedure explicitly states: 'Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.'

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with the final response letter I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about unauthorised overdraft charges totalling £340 applied to my current account between June and September this year. Your letter rejected my complaint, stating the charges were applied correctly under the account terms and conditions. However, the letter did not include any information about the Financial Ombudsman Service, no leaflet was enclosed, no website address was provided, and there was no mention of any time limits for me to refer my complaint elsewhere. I feel I have been left completely in the dark about my options. I believe the charges were unfair as I was never notified that my account had gone overdrawn before the charges were applied, and I want a full refund. I also want to know what my rights are now, since your so-called 'final response' told me nothing about where I can escalate this matter.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This complaint raises two distinct issues that must be addressed separately.

**Issue 1: Defective final response letter (procedural — clear regulatory breach)**
The final response letter dated 15 November is defective under DISP 1.6.2R. That rule requires a final response to: (a) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (b) provide the FOS website address; (c) inform the complainant they may refer the complaint to the FOS if dissatisfied; and (d) include the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R regarding the six-month time limit for FOS referral. None of these were included. This is a clear breach of DISP 1.6.2R.

Critically, under DISP 2.8.3G, the six-month time limit for the customer to refer to the FOS is only triggered by a response that qualifies as a valid final response — and that response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit. Because the 15 November letter did not do this, the six-month clock has not yet started running for this customer. You will need to issue a remedying letter that constitutes a proper final response, including all required FOS information and the Annex 3R wording. The customer draft below is drafted on that basis — please confirm this is the intended approach before sending.

Note: Crest Bank's internal procedure (section 7.5) also requires FOS contact details (www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; 0800 023 4567) to be included in all final response letters. The remedying letter should include these.

**Issue 2: Substantive complaint — unarranged overdraft charges of £340**
The customer alleges the charges were unfair, citing lack of prior notification. The retrieved provisions (CONC 5C.2.1R and CONC 5C.3.2R) are relevant: CONC 5C.2.1R requires that overdraft charges on personal current accounts must be expressed as a rate of interest (not flat fees), and CONC 5C.3.2R provides that if an unarranged overdraft charge is imposed in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R, it is unenforceable and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid. However, the retrieved provisions do not cover the specific allegation of failure to notify before charges were applied, and the substantive merits of the complaint (whether the charges were correctly applied, whether they comply with CONC 5C requirements, and whether any refund is due) require a factual investigation of the account records and charge structure.

**Recommended action:**
1. Investigate whether the £340 in charges were applied as interest rates (as required by CONC 5C.2.1R) or as flat fees/other charges, and whether the rate applied to the unarranged overdraft complied with CONC 5C.3.1R.
2. Issue a remedying final response letter that corrects the procedural deficiency and addresses the substantive complaint on its merits once investigated.
3. This complaint **must be reviewed by a human** before any response is sent: (a) it involves potential financial redress (£340); (b) the original final response was defective and a regulatory deadline question arises; (c) the substantive CONC 5C position requires factual investigation; and (d) under Crest Bank's internal procedure, financial redress complaints of £500 or more require Head of Customer Operations sign-off — while this is below £500, the regulatory significance of the defective final response may still warrant escalation.

**Assumption flagged:** The customer draft below treats the new letter as the remedying final response that starts the six-month FOS clock. If the bank's position is instead to treat this as an interim response while investigation continues, the letter must be amended accordingly and must not include the Annex 3R prescribed wording (which is for final responses only). Please confirm the intended approach.

The Annex 3R wording used in the draft below is option (1) from DISP 1 Annex 3R (respondent does not consent to waive the six-month time limit), on the assumption the bank is not consenting to waive. If the bank wishes to consent to waive, the wording must be changed.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer name],

Thank you for writing to us. We are sorry that our letter of 15 November did not provide you with the information you needed about your options following our response to your complaint. We recognise this was not good enough, and we apologise.

**Your complaint**

You have complained about unarranged overdraft charges totalling £340 applied to your current account between June and September this year. You have told us that you were not notified that your account had gone overdrawn before the charges were applied, and you are asking for a full refund.

**Our position on the charges**

We have reviewed your complaint carefully. [INSERT OUTCOME OF SUBSTANTIVE INVESTIGATION HERE — e.g. whether the charges are upheld, partially upheld, or rejected, with reasons, and any redress offered and how it has been calculated.]

**Your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge. We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet, which explains how the service works. You can also find information on their website at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk, or by calling them on 0800 023 4567.

You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge – but you must do so within six months of the date of this letter. If you do not refer your complaint in time, the Ombudsman will not have our permission to consider your complaint and so will only be able to do so in very limited circumstances. For example, if the Ombudsman believes that the delay was as a result of exceptional circumstances.

Yours sincerely,

[Handler name]
[Job title]
Crest Bank
[Date]

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires a final response to enclose the FOS explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, inform the complainant of their right to refer to the FOS, and include the Annex 3R time limit wording — none of which were included in the 15 November letter.
Sets out the prescribed wording that must be included in a final response where the respondent does not consent to waive the six-month time limit for FOS referral.
Establishes the six-month time limit within which a complainant must refer a complaint to the FOS after receiving a final response, and the underlying time limits that apply to the complaint itself.

Indicates that the six-month time limit for FOS referral is only triggered by a valid final response that tells the complainant about the time limit — meaning the defective 15 November letter did not start the clock.
Indicates that FOS information required in responses under the complaints time limit rules should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently within the text.

Requires that overdraft charges on personal current accounts must be expressed as a rate of interest applied on an annual basis — relevant to assessing whether the £340 in charges were lawfully imposed.

Provides that if an unarranged overdraft charge is imposed in contravention of the interest rate cap rule, it is unenforceable and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid — relevant to the refund claim if the charges are found to be non-compliant.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

# DISP — DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

## DISP 1.6.4 (Rules)

DISP 1.6.2 R does not apply if the complainant has already indicated in writing acceptance of a response by the respondent, provided that the response:

(1) informed the complainant how to pursue his complaint with the respondent if he remains dissatisfied;

(2) referred to the ultimate availability of the Financial Ombudsman Service if he remains dissatisfied with the respondent's response;

(3) enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service standard explanatory leaflet;

(4) provided the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service; and

(5) indicated whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R (Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?) by including the appropriate wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3 R.

## DISP 1.6.4A (Guidance)

DISP 1.6.4R does not affect the requirements imposed by DISP 1.6.2AR. Where a complaint is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint and DISP 1.6.2AR applies a final response must always be sent unless DISP 1.5.1R applies.

## DISP 1.6.6A (Guidance)

The information regarding the Financial Ombudsman Service, required to be provided in responses sent under the complaints time limit rules (DISP 1.6.2 R, DISP 1.6.2AR and DISP 1.6.4 R), should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently within the text of those responses.

[Note: article 13 of the ADR Directive]

# CONC — CONC 5C.3 Interest rates for unarranged overdrafts to be no more than the interest rates for arranged overdrafts

## CONC 5C.3.2 (Rules)

If a firm imposes an unarranged overdraft charge in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b), the obligation to pay the charge is unenforceable against the customer and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid by, or on behalf of, the customer under the obligation imposed.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

## CONC 5C.2.1 (Rules)

(1) A firm must not:

(a) enter into an agreement with a customer that provides for an arranged overdraft charge or an unarranged overdraft charge; or

(b) impose on a customer an arranged overdraft charge or an unarranged overdraft charge,

unless the conditions in (2) to (7) are satisfied.

(2) The charge must be a rate of interest expressed as a percentage applied on an annual basis to the relevant balance of arranged overdraft or unarranged overdraft (as the case may be).

(3) The rate of interest that applies to any given balance of arranged overdraft relating to a personal current account must either be zero or the same as the rate of interest that applies to any other balance of arranged overdraft in respect of that personal current account.

(4) The rate of interest that applies to any given balance of unarranged overdraft relating to a personal current account must either be zero or the same as the rate of interest that applies to any other balance of unarranged overdraft in respect of that personal current account.

(5) A firm must not require a customer to pay more than one arranged overdraft charge or more than one unarranged overdraft charge arising out of the same event.

(6) Where a customer has an arranged overdraft, in relation to a personal current account, to which a rate of interest above zero applies, any unarranged overdraft charge imposed on the customer in relation to that personal current account must also consist of a rate of interest computed, structured and presented in an identical manner (although the level of the rate of interest that applies to the unarranged overdraft may be lower).

metadata.section_name: CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.2 (Rules)

The Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint if the complainant refers it to the Financial Ombudsman Service:

(1) more than six months after the date on which the respondent sent the complainant its final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication ; or

(2) more than:

(a) six years after the event complained of; or (if later)

(b) three years from the date on which the complainant became aware (or ought reasonably to have become aware) that he had cause for complaint;

unless the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Ombudsman within that period and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received;

unless:

(3) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(4) the Ombudsman is required to do so by the Ombudsman Transitional Order; or

(5) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R have expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act ).

## DISP 2.8.2A (Rules)

If a respondent consents to the Ombudsman considering a complaint in accordance with DISP 2.8.2 R (5), the respondent may not withdraw consent.

## DISP 2.8.3 (Guidance)

The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication . The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

## DISP 2.8.4 (Guidance)

An example of exceptional circumstances might be where the complainant has been or is incapacitated.

metadata.section_name: DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

retrieved_chunks.content:
# CONC — CONC 5C.2 Charges for overdrafts to be interest rates

(5) A firm is not prevented from providing in the terms and conditions of the overdraft that no interest is payable in respect of arranged overdraft balances or unarranged overdraft balances of up to specified amounts (sometimes described as “fee-free amounts” or “buffer zones”) where permitted by CONC 5C.2.1R. The purpose of CONC 5C.2.1R(7) is to prevent firms from offering fee-free amounts or buffer zones that are free only in certain circumstances. An example of a buffer zone that is not permitted is where no interest is payable if an unarranged overdraft balance does not exceed the upper threshold of the buffer zone, but where interest becomes payable in respect of the entire balance (including the part of the balance in the buffer zone) if the customer exceeds the threshold.

(6) A firm is not prevented from waiving or reducing overdraft charges (in whole or in part) in appropriate circumstances (for example, where the firm is treating a customer with forbearance in line with other rules in this sourcebook).

(7) CONC 5C.2.1R does not prohibit the level of the single, uniform contractual rate of interest from differing from customer to customer, or between personal current accounts for the same customer.

(8)

(a) The definitions of an arranged overdraft charge and an unarranged overdraft charge are broad.

(b) These definitions capture any charges that arise because a customer has used an overdraft, or that are triggered by - or the size of which are affected by - the fact that the personal current account has entered, remains in, or extended, a debit position.

claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R(1)(f) requires the response to indicate whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits by including the appropriate wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3R.

claims.claim: Under DISP 2.8.3G, the six-month time limit for the customer to refer to the FOS is only triggered by a response that qualifies as a valid final response, and that response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.3G states: 'The six-month time limit is only triggered by a response which is a final response, redress determination or summary resolution communication. The response must tell the complainant about the six-month time limit that the complainant has to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.'

claims.claim: Because the 15 November letter did not include the required FOS information, the six-month clock has not yet started running for this customer.
claims.reasoning: DISP 2.8.3G makes clear the six-month time limit is only triggered by a valid final response that tells the complainant about the time limit. If the letter failed to do this, the clock has not started.

claims.claim: Crest Bank's internal procedure (section 7.5) requires FOS contact details (www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; 0800 023 4567) to be included in all final response letters.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure section 7.5 states that all communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567).

claims.claim: CONC 5C.2.1R requires that overdraft charges on personal current accounts must be expressed as a rate of interest (not flat fees).
claims.reasoning: CONC 5C.2.1R(2) states the charge must be a rate of interest expressed as a percentage applied on an annual basis to the relevant balance.

claims.claim: CONC 5C.3.2R provides that if an unarranged overdraft charge is imposed in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R, it is unenforceable and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid.
claims.reasoning: CONC 5C.3.2R explicitly states that if a firm imposes an unarranged overdraft charge in contravention of CONC 5C.3.1R(1)(b), the obligation to pay is unenforceable and the customer is entitled to recover any sum paid.

claims.claim: Under Crest Bank's internal procedure, financial redress complaints of £500 or more require Head of Customer Operations sign-off.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure states: 'All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.'

claims.claim: The Annex 3R wording used in the draft is option (1) from DISP 1 Annex 3R (respondent does not consent to waive the six-month time limit).
claims.reasoning: DISP 1 Annex 3R option (1) is the wording for when the respondent does not consent to waive the six-month time limit in DISP 2.8.2R(1), and the text in the provision matches the wording used in the draft.

claims.claim: If you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R(1)(e) requires informing the complainant that if he remains dissatisfied, he may refer his complaint to the FOS. The Annex 3R wording also states 'free of charge'.

claims.claim: We have enclosed a copy of their explanatory leaflet.
claims.reasoning: DISP 1.6.2R(1)(d) requires enclosing a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet.

claims.claim: You can also find information on their website at www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk, or by calling them on 0800 023 4567.
claims.reasoning: The internal procedure section 7.5 requires FOS contact details including website (www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk) and telephone (0800 023 4567). DISP 1.6.2R(1)(da) also requires the website address.

claims.claim: You have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge – but you must do so within six months of the date of this letter. If you do not refer your complaint in time, the Ombudsman will not have our permission to consider your complaint and so will only be able to do so in very limited circumstances. For example, if the Ombudsman believes that the delay was as a result of exceptional circumstances.

structure-titan.scenario_text: I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with the final response I received from your bank dated 15 November regarding my complaint about unauthorised transactions on my current account. Your letter rejected my complaint, stating that I had failed to keep my PIN secure. However, I believe this conclusion is unfair as I have never shared my PIN with anyone and I reported the transactions within hours of them occurring. Your final response letter did not include a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, nor did it provide their website address. It also failed to tell me whether you consent to waive any time limits if I refer my complaint to the Ombudsman. I feel I have been treated unfairly and I want to know what my options are now. I am a pensioner on a fixed income and the loss of £1,200 from these transactions has left me unable to pay my heating bills this winter.
structure-titan.handler_answer:
This is a complaint about a rejected unauthorised transaction claim, where the customer is also alleging that the final response letter of 15 November was deficient under DISP 1.6.2R. There are several important points to address:

**Deficiencies in the 15 November final response letter:**
Under DISP 1.6.2R, a final response must: (d) enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (da) provide the FOS website address; and (f) indicate whether or not the bank consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2R or DISP 2.8.7R by including the appropriate wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R. The customer is correct that all three of these elements appear to have been omitted from the 15 November letter. This is a breach of DISP 1.6.2R and must be remedied.

**Recommended action:**
You should issue a new letter to the customer that: (1) acknowledges the omissions from the 15 November letter; (2) encloses the FOS standard explanatory leaflet; (3) provides the FOS website address (www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk); (4) includes the DISP 1 Annex 3R wording on time limit waiver; and (5) confirms the customer's right to refer to the FOS. The internal policy (section 7.5) also requires FOS contact details including the telephone number 0800 023 4567 to be included. The customer has six months from the date of the final response to refer to the FOS — but note the assumption below.

**Important assumptions and uncertainties requiring human review:**
1. **Vulnerability:** The customer has explicitly stated they are a pensioner on a fixed income and that the £1,200 loss has left them unable to pay heating bills. This is a clear indicator of potential financial vulnerability and must be flagged for appropriate handling.
2. **Financial redress:** The complaint involves a potential loss of £1,200. Under the internal policy (section 7.4), any complaint involving financial redress of £500 or more must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.
3. **Substance of the rejection:** The customer disputes the bank's finding that they failed to keep their PIN secure. The retrieved provisions do not cover the substantive rules on unauthorised transactions (e.g. Payment Services Regulations / BCOBS). You should not re-open or re-adjudicate the substantive complaint in this letter without a proper review — this draft only addresses the procedural deficiency and signposts the customer's options.
4. **Six-month referral window:** The internal policy states the customer has six months from the date of the final response to refer to the FOS. The 15 November letter is the final response. Whether that six-month clock is already running (and how much time remains) must be confirmed before sending. The draft below refers to the right to refer but does not state a specific deadline, as the exact remaining time cannot be confirmed from the information provided.
5. **DISP 1 Annex 3R wording:** The exact prescribed wording for the time limit waiver indication (DISP 1 Annex 3R) is not in the retrieved provisions. You must insert the correct prescribed wording from DISP 1 Annex 3R — a placeholder is included in the draft. Do not send without completing this.
6. **This letter does not constitute a new final response** unless the bank intends to re-open the substantive complaint. If it is intended only to remedy the procedural omissions, that should be made clear internally and the letter should be framed accordingly. If the bank wishes to re-open the substantive complaint, a full reinvestigation is required.

**This response must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch**, given the financial redress threshold and vulnerability indicators.
structure-titan.customer_draft:
Dear [Customer Name],

Thank you for writing to us. I am sorry to hear about the difficulties you have experienced, and I want to address the concerns you have raised about our letter of 15 November.

**An important correction to our previous letter**

Having reviewed our final response letter of 15 November, we recognise that it did not include all of the information we are required to provide. Specifically, it did not enclose a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's explanatory leaflet, did not provide their website address, and did not state our position on whether we consent to waive any relevant time limits should you wish to refer your complaint to the Ombudsman. We apologise for these omissions and are writing now to put this right.

**Your right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service**

Our position on your complaint, as set out in our letter of 15 November, remains that we are unable to uphold it. However, if you remain dissatisfied with our response, you have the right to refer your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service, free of charge.

The Financial Ombudsman Service can be contacted at:
- Website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk
- Telephone: 0800 023 4567

We have enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service's standard explanatory leaflet, which explains how to refer a complaint and the time limits that apply.

**Our position on time limits**

[INSERT DISP 1 ANNEX 3R PRESCRIBED WORDING ON TIME LIMIT WAIVER HERE]

**We take your situation seriously**

We understand that this matter has caused you significant concern. If you would like to discuss your complaint further with us before deciding whether to refer it to the Financial Ombudsman Service, please do not hesitate to contact us at [contact details].

Yours sincerely,

[Handler Name]
[Job Title]
Crest Bank

cited_provisions.relevance: Requires the final response to enclose the FOS explanatory leaflet, provide the FOS website address, and indicate whether the respondent consents to waive relevant time limits — all of which were omitted from the 15 November letter and must now be remedied.

Indicates that information regarding the Financial Ombudsman Service required in responses under the complaints time limit rules should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently within the text of the response.

Requires all communications informing customers of FOS referral rights to include the FOS contact details (website and telephone number) and to state that the customer has six months from the date of the final response to refer to the FOS.

Requires all final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more to be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch — this complaint involves a potential loss of £1,200.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

Where a complaint is complex or involves multiple business areas, the complaints handler must escalate to the Head of Customer Operations, who will appoint a lead investigator and set an internal resolution target.

### 7.4 Final Response Letters

Crest Bank must issue a final response letter to the complainant in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R. The final response must:

1. Be issued within **eight weeks** of the date the complaint was received.
2. State clearly whether Crest Bank upholds, partially upholds, or rejects the complaint, with reasons.
3. Where the complaint is upheld or partially upheld, set out the redress or remedy offered, including any financial compensation and the basis on which it has been calculated.
4. Inform the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service if they remain dissatisfied, in accordance with DISP 1.6.2R (see section 7.5).
5. Be written in plain English, free from jargon, and accessible to the customer.

All final response letters for complaints involving financial redress of £500 or more, or any complaint assessed as having potential regulatory significance, must be reviewed and countersigned by the Head of Customer Operations before despatch.

### 7.5 FOS Referral Rights

In accordance with DISP 1.6.2R, Crest Bank must notify the complainant of their right to refer the complaint to the FOS:

- **In the final response letter**, regardless of whether the complaint is upheld or rejected.
- **In a written response issued at eight weeks**, if Crest Bank has not yet been able to issue a final response, explaining the reason for the delay and confirming the FOS referral right.

All communications informing customers of FOS referral rights must include the FOS contact details (website: www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk; telephone: 0800 023 4567) and must state that the customer has **six months** from the date of the final response to refer the complaint to the FO

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 2.8 Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?

## DISP 2.8.9 (Rules)

(1) In addition to DISP 2.8.1R and DISP 2.8.2R, unless one or more of the conditions in (2) below is met, the Ombudsman cannot consider a complaint which:

(a) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that took place on or before 29 August 2017; and

(b) expresses dissatisfaction about the sale, or matters related to the sale, including where there is a rejection of claims on the grounds of ineligibility or exclusion (but not matters unrelated to the sale, such as delays in claims handling or administrative matters such as taking the incorrect amount of premium).

(2) The conditions are that:

(a) the complainant referred the complaint to the respondent or to the Financial Ombudsman Service on or before 29 August 2019 and has a written acknowledgement or some other record of the complaint having been received; or

(b) in the view of the Ombudsman, the failure to comply with the time limit in (2)(a) was as a result of exceptional circumstances; or

(c) the respondent has consented to the Ombudsman considering the complaint where the time limit in (2)(a) has expired (but this does not apply to a “relevant complaint” within the meaning of section 404B(3) of the Act); or

(d) the complaint:

(i) is made on or after 29 August 2019;

(ii) relates to the sale of a payment protection contract that was live as at 29 August 2017;

(iii) is made following a full or partial rejection of a claim on or after 29 August 2017 on the grounds of ineligibility, exclusion or limitation

and this condition applies only to the extent that the complaint relates to those grounds of rejection.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

## DISP 1.6.4 (Rules)

DISP 1.6.2 R does not apply if the complainant has already indicated in writing acceptance of a response by the respondent, provided that the response:

(1) informed the complainant how to pursue his complaint with the respondent if he remains dissatisfied;

(2) referred to the ultimate availability of the Financial Ombudsman Service if he remains dissatisfied with the respondent's response;

(3) enclosed a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service standard explanatory leaflet;

(4) provided the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service; and

(5) indicated whether or not the respondent consents to waive the relevant time limits in DISP 2.8.2 R or DISP 2.8.7 R (Was the complaint referred to the Financial Ombudsman Service in time?) by including the appropriate wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 3 R.

## DISP 1.6.4A (Guidance)

DISP 1.6.4R does not affect the requirements imposed by DISP 1.6.2AR. Where a complaint is an EMD complaint or a PSD complaint and DISP 1.6.2AR applies a final response must always be sent unless DISP 1.5.1R applies.

## DISP 1.6.6A (Guidance)

The information regarding the Financial Ombudsman Service, required to be provided in responses sent under the complaints time limit rules (DISP 1.6.2 R, DISP 1.6.2AR and DISP 1.6.4 R), should be set out clearly, comprehensibly, in an easily accessible way and prominently within the text of those responses.

[Note: article 13 of the ADR Directive]

metadata.section_name: DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

retrieved_chunks.content:
# DISP — DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

## DISP 1.6.6B (Guidance)

A respondent may, where relevant, in a response sent under the complaints time limits rules (DISP 1.6.2R and DISP 1.6.4R) refer to the availability of the Pensions Ombudsman, in addition to the Financial Ombudsman Service, by including the wording set out in DISP 1 Annex 4G.

## DISP 1.6.7 (Guidance)

It is expected that within eight weeks of their receipt, almost all complaints to a respondent will have been substantively addressed by it through a final response or response as described in DISP 1.6.4 R.

## DISP 1.6.8 (Guidance)

When assessing a respondent's response to a complaint, the

FCA

may have regard to a number of factors, including, the quality of response, as against the complaints resolution rules, as well as the speed with which it was made.

retrieved_chunks.content:
# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 4. Regulatory Context

| Regulatory Source | Relevance to This Policy |
|---|---|
| **DISP 1** (Dispute Resolution: Complaints) | Sets out the FCA's detailed requirements for complaint handling, including acknowledgement and resolution timescales, final response content, and record-keeping obligations. |
| **DISP 2** | Defines the scope of the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) jurisdiction and the conditions under which customers may refer complaints to the FOS. |
| **PRIN 6** (Customers' interests) | Requires Crest Bank to pay due regard to the interests of its customers and treat them fairly throughout the complaints process. |
| **PRIN 12** (Consumer Duty) | Requires Crest Bank to act to deliver good outcomes for retail customers; complaint handling is a key mechanism for identifying and remedying poor outcomes. |
| **SYSC 9.1** | Requires Crest Bank to maintain orderly records, including records of complaints and the steps taken to investigate and resolve them. |
| **BCOBS 5** (Banking: Conduct of Business) | Sets specific conduct standards applicable to payment accounts and savings products offered by Crest Bank. |

---

# Customer Complaints Handling Procedure

## 5. Policy Statement

1. Crest Bank treats every expression of dissatisfaction from a customer as a complaint and will handle it with fairness, diligence, and transparency, in accordance with DISP 1.
2. Crest Bank will acknowledge all complaints promptly and resolve them within the timescales prescribed by DISP 1, communicating clearly with the customer at every stage.
3. Crest Bank will investigate each complaint impartially, considering all relevant evidence before reaching a conclusion, and will not allow commercial considerations to influence the outcome.
4. Crest Bank will inform all complainants of their right to refer unresolved complaints to the Financial Ombudsman Service, in the manner and at the times required by DISP 1.
5. Crest Bank will use complaint data to identify systemic issues and root causes, and will take proportionate remedial action to prevent recurrence, in support of its obligations under PRIN 12.
6. Crest Bank will maintain complete and accurate complaint records for a minimum of five years from the date of receipt, in accordance with SYSC 9.1.
7. Crest Bank will report complaint management information (MI) to the relevant governance bodies on a regular basis and will submit regulatory returns to the FCA as required by DISP 1.

---

# DISP — DISP 1.6 Complaints time limit rules

(c) encloses a copy of the Financial Ombudsman Service standard explanatory leaflet; and

(d) provides the website address of the Financial Ombudsman Service.

[Note: article 13 of the ADR Directive]

# DISP — DISP 1 Annex 1AA Notes on completing electronic money and payment services complaints return form

The contextual information for account information services should be the number of customers that have used the firm’s account information services (AIS) in the reporting period. For authorised PIs this figure should be the same as that provided by payment and e-money institutions in Q80 of the Authorised Payment Institution Capital Adequacy Return and for authorised EMIs, in Q76 of the Authorised Electronic Money Institution Questionnaire.

Complaints relating to alleged authorised push payment fraud (Table 4)

Information on complaints relating to alleged authorised push payment fraud should be provided in Table 4. Data in this table should not be included in any total complaint figures as these complaints should already be reported in the preceding tables under the appropriate product/service groupings (for example, under ‘Credit transfer’).

…

DISP 1 Annex 1R

This return (Payment Services Complaints Return) only relates to complaints made in relation to payment services or electronic money. All complaints should be reported in DISP 1 Annex 1R.

Transparency

To improve consumer awareness and to help payment service providers compare their performance against their peers, the FCA may publish aggregated and anonymised complaints data.

The FCA may also publish respondent level complaints data where it has the respondent’s consent. If the respondent ticks the ‘Yes’ box in this report it is consenting to the FCA publishing the complaints data.
